**State the BATNEEC principle. Why is it important in selecting an EOP technology?**

**Model Answer:**
**BATNEEC** stands for *Best Available Technology Not Entailing Excessive Cost.* It is the regulatory principle used by pollution control boards while granting consent to industries.

Importance:
- Ensures the industry adopts **modern, efficient** technology (the "Best Available" part).
- Recognizes that the technology must be **economically feasible** (the "Not Entailing Excessive Cost" part).
- Balances **environmental protection with industrial development** — a key KTU Module 1 outcome.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Skipping sub-parts (a) or (b) leads to direct loss of 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **typical wastewater End-of-Pipe treatment train** for a chemical process industry. List the function of each unit.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Unit | Function |
|---|---|
| **Bar Screen** | Removes large floating debris (rags, sticks) to protect downstream pumps. |
| **Grit Chamber** | Settles heavy inorganic solids (sand, gravel). |
| **Equalization Tank** | Dampens fluctuations in flow and pollutant concentration. |
| **Primary Clarifier** | Removes 50–70% of suspended solids and 25–40% of BOD by gravity settling (Stokes' law). |
| **Secondary Biological Unit** | Activated sludge or trickling filter; oxidizes dissolved organics using microorganisms. |
| **Secondary Clarifier** | Separates biological flocs (sludge) from clarified water. |
| **Tertiary Treatment** | Polishing step — filtration, activated carbon, RO, nutrient (N, P) removal. |
| **Disinfection** | UV or chlorination to kill pathogens before discharge. |
| **Sludge Treatment Line** | Thickening → Dewatering → Disposal (landfill/incineration/compost). |

The complete treatment train converts a *highly polluted* effluent into a stream that meets **CPCB / KSPCB** discharge standards (e.g., BOD < 30 mg/L, TSS < 100 mg/L for inland surface water).

**(b)** A wet scrubber treats a flue gas stream of **10,000 Nm³/hr** containing **1500 mg/Nm³ of SO₂**. The scrubber achieves a removal efficiency of **92%**.
   Calculate:
   (i) Outlet SO₂ concentration in mg/Nm³
   (ii) Mass of SO₂ removed per day in kg
   (iii) Mass of SO₂ discharged per day in kg

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 10{,}000\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$
- $C_{in} = 1500\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$
- $\eta = 92\% = 0.92$

**(i) Outlet concentration:**

$$
C_{out} = C_{in}(1 - \eta) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.92)
$$

$$
C_{out} = 1500 \times 0.08 = 120\ \text{mg/Nm}^3
$$

**(ii) Mass of SO₂ removed per day:**

Mass flow rate in:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = Q_g \times C_{in} = 10{,}000\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr} \times 1500\ \text{mg/Nm}^3
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = 1.5 \times 10^7\ \text{mg/hr} = 15\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

Mass removed per day:

$$
m_{removed} = \eta \times \dot{m}_{in} \times 24\ \text{hr}
$$

$$
m_{removed} = 0.92 \times 15 \times 24 = 331.2\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**(iii) Mass discharged per day:**

$$
m_{discharged} = (1 - \eta) \times \dot{m}_{in} \times 24
$$

$$
m_{discharged} = 0.08 \times 15 \times 24 = 28.8\ \text{kg/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Valuation Pitfall):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg to kg** ($1\ \text{kg} = 10^6\ \text{mg}$). If a student writes "$331.2 \times 10^6$ kg/day" instead of $331.2$ kg/day, they lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Not multiplying by 24 hours** — KTU numericals are per *day*, not per *hour*. Always check the unit requested.
> 3. **Mixing up "removed" and "discharged"** — they sum to the total inlet mass. Verify: $331.2 + 28.8 = 360\ \text{kg/day} = 15 \times 24$ ✓.

---

#### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP). Discuss how plate area, gas flow rate, and migration velocity influence collection efficiency.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Deutsch–Anderson equation is:

$$
\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency
- $w$ = migration velocity of charged particles (m/s) — depends on particle size, charge, and electric field
- $A$ = total collection plate area (m²)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)

**Influence of parameters:**

1. **Effect of plate area $A$:** Efficiency increases with $A$, but **non-linearly (exponentially)**. Doubling the plate area does NOT double the efficiency; it asymptotically approaches 100%. Going from 90% to 99% requires more area than going from 0% to 90%.

2. **Effect of gas flow rate $Q$:** Higher $Q$ *reduces* efficiency because particles spend less residence time in the electric field. ESPs are sensitive to gas velocity — typical design value is 1–2 m/s.

3. **Effect of migration velocity $w$:** $w$ is a function of the particle's electrical charge and the field strength. Larger particles and stronger fields give higher $w$ and thus higher $\eta$. *Particle resistivity* is critical — high-resistivity dust (e.g., fly ash > 10¹⁰ Ω·cm) causes back-corona and severely reduces $w$.

**Numerical illustration:**
For $w = 0.1$ m/s, $A = 5000$ m², $Q = 50$ m³/s:

$$
\eta = 1 - e^{-(0.1 \times 5000 / 50)} = 1 - e^{-10} \approx 0.99995 = 99.995\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows why ESPs are the preferred EOP for coal-fired boilers in India — they can achieve > 99.9% efficiency on fly ash.

**(b)** A cyclone separator receives dust-laden air at **30,000 m³/hr** with an inlet dust concentration of **25 g/m³**. The cyclone removes particles at an efficiency of **85%** for particles larger than 10 µm, and 40% for particles below 10 µm. The mass fraction of particles above 10 µm in the inlet is **60%**.
   Calculate:
   (i) Total dust removed per hour
   (ii) Outlet dust loading in g/m³
   (iii) Overall removal efficiency

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 30{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$
- $C_{in} = 25\ \text{g/m}^3$
- $\eta_1 = 85\%$ for fraction $f_1 = 0.60$ (particles > 10 µm)
- $\eta_2 = 40\%$ for fraction $f_2 = 0.40$ (particles < 10 µm)

**Step 1: Inlet mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = Q \times C_{in} = 30{,}000 \times 25 = 7.5 \times 10^5\ \text{g/hr} = 750\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2: Mass removed from each fraction:**

Coarse fraction removed:

$$
\dot{m}_{1,removed} = f_1 \times \eta_1 \times \dot{m}_{in} = 0.60 \times 0.85 \times 750
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{1,removed} = 382.5\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

Fine fraction removed:

$$
\dot{m}_{2,removed} = f_2 \times \eta_2 \times \dot{m}_{in} = 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 750
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{2,removed} = 120\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3: Total removed (i):**

$$
\dot{m}_{removed} = 382.5 + 120 = 502.5\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4: Outlet mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = 750 - 502.5 = 247.5\ \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 5: Outlet concentration (ii):**

$$
C_{out} = \frac{\dot{m}_{out}}{Q} = \frac{247.5 \times 10^3\ \text{g/hr}}{30{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

$$
C_{out} = 8.25\ \text{g/m}^3
$$

**Step 6: Overall removal efficiency (iii):**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{\dot{m}_{removed}}{\dot{m}_{in}} = \frac{502.5}{750} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = 67\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using a simple average** $\eta = (85 + 40)/2 = 62.5\%$ — this is WRONG. The correct weighted average is $67\%$. Students lose 2 marks for skipping the mass-fraction weighting.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** from g/m³ to kg/hr (divide by 1000) when reporting mass flow.
> 3. **Confusing inlet and outlet** — if a student reports $C_{out} = 16.75$ g/m³ (using $1 - 0.67$), they have computed the **outlet efficiency contribution**, not the **outlet concentration**. The full $C_{in}$ minus $C_{out}$ relationship must be applied.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-night-before-exam revision sheet.**

✅ **End-of-Pipe (EOP) Treatment** = pollution control applied **after** generation, **before** discharge.

✅ **Pollution Control Hierarchy (Memorize):** *E-R-R-E-D* = **E**liminate → **R**educe → **R**ecover → **E**nd-of-pipe → **D**isposal.

✅ **Removal Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ — the **single most-used equation** in Module 1.

✅ **EOP does not destroy pollutants; it transfers them** (gas→liquid→solid). Hence, sludge/waste management is part of EOP design.

✅ **Three media, three EOP trains:**
- Air → Cyclone, ESP, Baghouse, Scrubber, Carbon adsorber.
- Water → Primary, Secondary, Tertiary treatment.
- Solid → Landfill, Incineration, Stabilization.

✅ **Deutsch–Anderson (ESP):** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ — exponential, asymptotic to 100%.

✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18\mu}$ — settling velocity is **proportional to $d_p^2$** (square of particle diameter).

✅ **BATNEEC** = *Best Available Technology Not Entailing Excessive Cost* — used by SPCB/CPCB for consent decisions.

✅ **EOP is "additive" not "subtractive"** — remember this for essay-type questions.

✅ **Common numerical traps:** unit conversion (mg ↔ kg), 24-hr scaling, mass-fraction weighting for mixed particles.

✅ **Real-world Kerala context (for 14-mark answers):** Always cite FACT Kochi, Cochin Refinery, or Hindustan Newsprint as case studies for full marks.

✅ **Acts to remember (Indian context):** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, Hazardous Waste Rules 2008.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Reduction and Waste Minimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source Reduction and Waste Minimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Source Reduction** (also called *Pollution Prevention* or *P2*) is defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) as any practice which:

- Reduces the amount of any hazardous substance, pollutant, or contaminant entering any waste stream or otherwise released into the environment (including fugitive emissions) **prior to recycling, treatment, or disposal**; and
- Reduces the hazards to public health and the environment associated with the release of such substances, pollutants, or contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Source reduction is the engineering and management philosophy that prioritizes preventing the generation of waste at the point of origin, rather than controlling, treating, or disposing of waste after it has been created. It is the most preferred and economically attractive element of the "Integrated Waste Management Hierarchy."*

**Waste Minimization** is the broader umbrella term that includes source reduction **plus** environmentally sound recycling and treatment practices that reduce the volume or toxicity of waste that must be finally disposed of.

### 1.2 The Integrated Waste Management Hierarchy (The "4R" Principle)

In KTU examination language, source reduction sits at the apex of the pollution control hierarchy. The standard 4R hierarchy (from most to least preferred) is:

1. **REDUCE** (Source Reduction) — Highest priority
2. **REUSE** — Direct reuse without major processing
3. **RECYCLE** — Reprocessing into new raw materials
4. **RECOVER** (Energy Recovery) — Incineration with energy capture
5. **DISPOSAL** (Secure Landfill / Deep Well Injection) — Last resort

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer scripts, always represent the hierarchy in a **top-down pyramid** and explicitly mention that the order is governed by both **environmental priority and economic efficiency**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Leaking Tap" Analogy:**
> Imagine a tap that is leaking water (this is *pollution*). There are two ways to handle the leak:
> - **End-of-Pipe Solution (Conventional Approach):** Place a bucket below the tap to collect the water, then find a way to dispose of it (treatment).
> - **Source Reduction Solution (Modern Approach):** *Tighten the tap washer* so the leak stops at its origin. No waste is generated at all.
>
> Source reduction is essentially "tightening the tap washer" of an industrial process — preventing pollution before it is born, instead of managing the consequences afterwards.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Units in **Bold**)

The following metrics are universally accepted in process industry pollution control:

- **Waste Generation Rate (WGR)** — measured in **kg of waste per tonne of product** or **kg/hr**
- **Waste Minimization Ratio (WMR)** — dimensionless
- **Source Reduction Efficiency (η_SR)** — expressed as a **percentage (%)**
- **Pollution Prevention Index (P2I)** — dimensionless ranking metric

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Source Reduction

Source reduction operates on a **cause-to-effect reversal** philosophy. The control logic is:

- **Step 1 (Root Cause Identification):** Conduct a *Waste Audit* — material balance across every process unit.
- **Step 2 (Substitution):** Replace toxic/hazardous raw materials with benign alternatives (e.g., water-based instead of solvent-based paints).
- **Step 3 (Process Modification):** Redesign the process to improve yield, selectivity, and atom economy.
- **Step 4 (Good Housekeeping):** Eliminate spills, leaks, fugitive emissions, and improper storage.
- **Step 5 (Inventory Management):** Just-in-time (JIT) procurement to avoid material degradation and overstocking.
- **Step 6 (Product Modification):** Redesign the end product to have a longer life, be recyclable, or use fewer hazardous components.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework: Material Balance Approach

The theoretical foundation of waste minimization is the **Law of Conservation of Mass** applied across a control volume (process unit, plant, or unit operation).

For a steady-state process with no accumulation:

$$
\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Consumption}
$$

Where waste streams are explicitly identified as part of the "Output" term. A waste audit aims to minimize these output terms.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Practical Engineering Drivers

| Driver Category | Engineering "Why" | Operational "How" |
|:----------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **Economic** | Treatment & disposal costs are rising | Reduce raw material purchase cost |
| **Regulatory** | Stricter effluent norms (PCB/CPCB) | Avoid non-compliance penalties |
| **Liability** | Long-term Superfund liability | Reduce hazardous waste generation |
| **Public Image** | Corporate social responsibility (CSR) | Achieve ISO 14001 certification |
| **Resource** | Depleting virgin raw materials | Improve atom economy |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the exam-critical equations. Memorize symbols and units.**

| Formula Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Description / Engineering Meaning | Standard Units |
|:---------------|:------------------------|:----------------------------------|:---------------|
| $\text{WGR}$ | $\dfrac{M_{\text{waste}}}{M_{\text{product}}}$ | Waste Generation Rate | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}$ |
| $\eta_{SR}$ | $\dfrac{W_{\text{before}} - W_{\text{after}}}{W_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Source Reduction Efficiency | **%** |
| $\text{WMR}$ | $\dfrac{V_{\text{waste, final}}}{V_{\text{waste, initial}}}$ | Waste Minimization Ratio (must be $\leq 1$) | dimensionless |
| $\text{AF}$ | $\dfrac{M_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{reactants used}}}$ | Atom Economy / Atom Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\text{SR}_{\text{load}}$ | $\dfrac{C_i \times Q_i - C_f \times Q_f}{C_i \times Q_i} \times 100$ | Specific Pollutant Load Reduction | **%** |
| $\text{C}_{\text{opt}}$ | $\text{C}_{\text{treatment}} - \text{C}_{\text{raw material saved}}$ | Net Cost Saving (annualized) | $\text{INR} \cdot \text{yr}^{-1}$ |
| $\text{MPY}$ | $\dfrac{Y_{\text{actual}}}{Y_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Mass Yield Percentage | **%** |
| $\text{RR}$ | $\dfrac{R_{\text{reused}}}{R_{\text{total consumed}}} \times 100$ | Recycle/Reuse Ratio | **%** |

> **Critical Note:** In all ratio-style metrics above, the sign convention must show *reduction* as a **positive percentage**, never negative. This is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Source reduction is the cornerstone of:

- **Green Chemistry** (Anastas & Warner, 12 Principles)
- **Process Intensification** (PI) — combining multiple unit operations
- **Circular Economy** — closing material loops at design stage
- **Cleaner Production (CP)** — UNEP/UNIDO global program
- **ISO 14001 Environmental Management Systems (EMS)**

In petrochemical, pharmaceutical, electroplating, and pulp & paper industries, source reduction typically achieves **30–70% waste reduction** before any end-of-pipe technology is even considered.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Source Reduction Efficiency (η_SR)

We start with the formal definition of efficiency in process engineering.

**Step 1: Define the baseline (before intervention) waste generation rate.**

Let $W_{\text{before}}$ be the mass of pollutant or waste generated per unit of product output **before** implementing source reduction measures (e.g., a process change or material substitution).

**Step 2: Define the post-intervention waste generation rate.**

Let $W_{\text{after}}$ be the mass of waste generated per unit of product output **after** the source reduction measures are implemented.

**Step 3: Compute the absolute reduction in waste.**

The amount of waste eliminated is the simple arithmetic difference:

$$
\Delta W = W_{\text{before}} - W_{\text{after}}
$$

This gives the *absolute* mass of waste that no longer enters the environment. It is a useful metric, but it does not account for the relative magnitude of the original waste.

**Step 4: Normalize by the baseline.**

To obtain a dimensionless (or percentage) efficiency, we divide the absolute reduction by the original baseline:

$$
\eta_{SR} = \dfrac{W_{\text{before}} - W_{\text{after}}}{W_{\text{before}}}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage form for reporting.**

Multiplying by 100 gives the conventional percentage efficiency:

$$
\eta_{SR} \; (\%) = \left( \dfrac{W_{\text{before}} - W_{\text{after}}}{W_{\text{before}}} \right) \times 100
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A value of $\eta_{SR} = 80\%$ means that 80% of the original waste stream has been eliminated at the source. Higher values are always better.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass-Balance Based Waste Audit Equation

**Step 1: Draw the control volume around a single process unit.**

Consider a reactor with input streams $F_{\text{in}}$ and output streams $F_{\text{out}}$, where waste is one of the outputs.

**Step 2: Apply the steady-state mass balance.**

At steady state, accumulation is zero. Hence:

$$
\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}
$$

Expanding with explicit waste term:

$$
F_{\text{raw material}} + F_{\text{recycle}} + F_{\text{water}} = F_{\text{product}} + F_{\text{waste}} + F_{\text{losses}}
$$

**Step 3: Isolate the waste term.**

Rearranging the equation to solve for the waste stream flow rate:

$$
F_{\text{waste}} = F_{\text{raw material}} + F_{\text{recycle}} + F_{\text{water}} - F_{\text{product}} - F_{\text{losses}}
$$

**Step 4: Define the source reduction target.**

The waste minimization goal is to reduce $F_{\text{waste}}$. This is achieved by:
- Decreasing $F_{\text{raw material}}$ (through higher selectivity)
- Decreasing $F_{\text{water}}$ (through closed-loop recycling)
- Increasing $F_{\text{product}}$ (through higher conversion)
- Decreasing $F_{\text{losses}}$ (through good housekeeping)

**Step 5: Express the Waste Minimization Ratio (WMR).**

$$
\text{WMR} = \dfrac{F_{\text{waste, after}}}{F_{\text{waste, before}}}
$$

For a successful source reduction program, the WMR must satisfy:

$$
\text{WMR} \leq 1
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A dye intermediate manufacturing unit generates **450 kg/day** of mother liquor (waste stream containing organic contaminants). After implementing a source reduction program involving solvent substitution and process modification, the waste generation is reduced to **120 kg/day**. Calculate: (a) Absolute waste reduction, (b) Source reduction efficiency, and (c) Waste minimization ratio.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Absolute Reduction:**

$$
\Delta W = 450 - 120 = 330 \; \text{kg/day}
$$

*Result: 330 kg/day of waste has been eliminated at the source.*

**Part (b) — Source Reduction Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{SR} = \left( \dfrac{450 - 120}{450} \right) \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{330}{450} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{SR} = 73.33 \; \%
$$

*Result: The source reduction program achieved 73.33% efficiency.*

**Part (c) — Waste Minimization Ratio:**

$$
\text{WMR} = \dfrac{120}{450} = 0.2667
$$

*Result: WMR = 0.2667, which is significantly less than 1, confirming successful waste minimization.*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show units in intermediate steps. A 14-mark question in Part B typically awards 2–3 marks just for the unit consistency check.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Economic Payback for Source Reduction

**Step 1: Identify capital cost of source reduction equipment/process change.**

Let $C_{\text{cap}}$ be the one-time capital investment (in **INR**).

**Step 2: Identify annualized operating savings.**

The savings arise from two sources:
- Reduced raw material cost: $C_{\text{raw, saved}}$
- Reduced waste treatment/disposal cost: $C_{\text{treat, saved}}$

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = C_{\text{raw, saved}} + C_{\text{treat, saved}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Simple Payback Period (SPP).**

$$
\text{SPP} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cap}}}{S_{\text{annual}}} \quad \text{(years)}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Annualized Benefit.**

$$
B_{\text{net}} = S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{OM}}
$$

where $C_{\text{OM}}$ is the annual operation and maintenance cost of the new process.

> A source reduction option is considered *economically attractive* by KTU examiners when:
>
> $$\text{SPP} \leq 2 \text{ to } 3 \; \text{years}$$
>
> Anything beyond 5 years is generally considered economically unattractive without subsidy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 4R Waste Management Hierarchy (Pyramid Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REDUCE - Source Reduction - HIGHEST PRIORITY"] --> B["REUSE - Direct Application"]
    B --> C["RECYCLE - Reprocess into New Material"]
    C --> D["RECOVER - Energy Recovery Incineration"]
    D --> E["DISPOSAL - Secure Landfill - LAST RESORT"]
    F["MOST PREFERRED"] -.-> A
    G["LEAST PREFERRED"] -.-> E
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Source Reduction Program

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Plant Wide Waste Audit"] --> B1["Material Balance Calculation"]
    B1 --> C1["Identify Waste Hotspots"]
    C1 --> D1["Option Generation Brainstorm"]
    D1 --> E1["Technical Screening"]
    E1 --> F1["Economic Feasibility Analysis"]
    F1 --> G1["Implementation of Source Reduction"]
    G1 --> H1["Monitoring and Verification"]
    H1 --> I1["Continuous Improvement Loop"]
    I1 --> D1
    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Source Reduction Decision Tree for Process Industries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start - Waste Stream Identified"] --> B0{"Is Raw Material Substitution Possible?"}
    B0 -- YES --> C0["Substitute with Benign Material - Example Water Based Solvents"]
    B0 -- NO --> D0{"Can Process Yield Be Improved?"}
    D0 -- YES --> E0["Process Modification - Better Catalyst"]
    D0 -- NO --> F0{"Is Closed Loop Recycling Feasible?"}
    F0 -- YES --> G0["Install Recycle Loop - Counter Current Washing"]
    F0 -- NO --> H0{"Apply Good Housekeeping and Spill Prevention"]
    H0 --> I0["End of Source Reduction Options - Apply End of Pipe Treatment"]
    style C0 fill:#98FB98
    style E0 fill:#98FB98
    style G0 fill:#98FB98
    style H0 fill:#FFE4B5
    style I0 fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.4 Comparative Flow: End-of-Pipe vs Source Reduction Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Conventional End of Pipe Approach"]
        P1["Process Unit"] --> W1["Waste Generation"]
        W1 --> T1["Treatment Unit"]
        T1 --> D1["Safe Disposal"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Source Reduction Approach"]
        P2["Modified Process Unit"] --> W2["Minimized Waste"]
        W2 --> D2["Safe Disposal Reduced Volume"]
        M2["Material Substitution and Process Redesign"] --> P2
    end
    style S1 fill:#FFE4E1
    style S2 fill:#E0FFE0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define *source reduction* and explain how it differs from *waste treatment*.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Source Reduction** is the practice of preventing the generation of waste at its point of origin, before it is created, by modifying raw materials, processes, or products.

**Difference from Waste Treatment:**

| Aspect | Source Reduction | Waste Treatment |
|:-------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| Philosophy | Prevention at source | Control after generation |
| Stage of intervention | Pre-generation | Post-generation |
| Cost economics | Saves raw material cost | Adds treatment cost |
| Regulatory weight | Top of the 4R hierarchy | Bottom of the 4R hierarchy |

In short, source reduction *eliminates* the problem, while waste treatment *manages* the problem.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State the **Integrated Waste Management Hierarchy** in order of environmental priority.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Integrated Waste Management Hierarchy, in decreasing order of environmental priority, is:

1. **Reduce** (Source Reduction) — most preferred
2. **Reuse** — using the waste again for the same or different purpose
3. **Recycle** — processing waste into new raw material or product
4. **Recover** — energy recovery through incineration
5. **Disposal** — secure landfilling, the least preferred option

The hierarchy prioritizes prevention over cure, and the order is determined by both environmental sustainability and economic efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **techniques of source reduction** applicable to process industries, with at least **two real-world industrial examples** for each technique. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electroplating unit uses 1,200 kg/day of a nickel plating solution. After implementing a **rinse-water recycling and drag-out minimization** program, the solution consumption drops to 480 kg/day. Calculate:
   (i) Absolute reduction in solution usage
   (ii) Source reduction efficiency (η_SR)
   (iii) Waste minimization ratio (WMR) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Source Reduction Techniques:**

The major categories of source reduction techniques used in process industries are:

1. **Inventory Management:**
   - Just-in-Time (JIT) procurement reduces material degradation.
   - *Example:* FIFO (First-In-First-Out) in pharmaceutical warehouses to avoid expired raw materials.
   - *Example:* Computerized inventory in paint industries to prevent solvent evaporation losses.

2. **Material Substitution:**
   - Replacing toxic/hazardous raw materials with benign ones.
   - *Example:* Replacing cyanide-based zinc plating with alkaline non-cyanide zinc plating.
   - *Example:* Using water-based coatings instead of solvent-based coatings in the automotive industry.

3. **Process Modification:**
   - Improving catalyst selectivity, conversion, and atom economy.
   - *Example:* Switching from a batch to a continuous reactor in petrochemical refineries.
   - *Example:* Using microreactors in pharmaceutical API manufacturing to reduce by-product formation.

4. **Good Housekeeping:**
   - Spill prevention, leak detection, proper storage.
   - *Example:* Bunded flooring in chemical warehouses to prevent soil contamination.
   - *Example:* Vapor recovery systems in petrol pump stations.

5. **Recycling/Reuse Within Process:**
   - Counter-current rinsing, closed-loop water systems.
   - *Example:* Counter-current rinsing in electroplating.
   - *Example:* Solvent recovery via distillation in pharmaceutical industries.

6. **Product Redesign:**
   - Designing products for longer life and easier recyclability.
   - *Example:* Modular mobile phones with replaceable batteries.
   - *Example:* Refillable printer cartridges.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $W_{\text{before}} = 1200 \; \text{kg/day}$ (nickel solution used initially)
- $W_{\text{after}} = 480 \; \text{kg/day}$ (after source reduction)

**(i) Absolute reduction:**

$$
\Delta W = 1200 - 480 = 720 \; \text{kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Source Reduction Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{SR} = \left( \dfrac{1200 - 480}{1200} \right) \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{720}{1200} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{SR} = 60 \; \%
$$

**(iii) Waste Minimization Ratio:**

$$
\text{WMR} = \dfrac{480}{1200} = 0.40
$$

**Conclusion:** The electroplating unit has achieved a 60% source reduction efficiency and the waste minimization ratio is 0.40, indicating a successful pollution prevention intervention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **methodology of conducting a waste audit** for source reduction in a process industry. Include a material balance flow diagram in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process generates 800 kg/hr of a hazardous organic waste stream. The treatment cost is ₹50/kg, and the disposal cost is an additional ₹30/kg. The proposed source reduction option requires a one-time capital investment of ₹12,00,000 and yields an annual operating saving of ₹6,00,000. Calculate the simple payback period and comment on the economic feasibility. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Waste Audit Methodology:**

A waste audit is a systematic, top-down procedure comprising the following steps:

1. **Pre-Audit Planning:** Define the audit scope, plant boundaries, and waste streams of interest. Gather process flow diagrams (PFDs) and Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs).

2. **Data Collection:**
   - Quantify every input (raw material, water, energy).
   - Quantify every output (product, by-product, waste, emission).
   - Sampling and analysis of each waste stream for composition, pH, COD, BOD, heavy metals, etc.

3. **Material Balance Construction:**
   - Build a Sankey-type material balance for the entire plant.
   - For each unit operation, write:

$$
\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation (if unsteady)}}
$$

   - Identify *unaccounted losses* (fugitive emissions, leaks).

4. **Waste Hotspot Identification:**
   - Rank waste streams by **mass × toxicity**.
   - Apply the Pareto principle (80/20 rule): 20% of the sources generate 80% of the waste.

5. **Option Generation:**
   - Brainstorm source reduction options for each hotspot.
   - Categorize as: substitution, modification, housekeeping, recycling.

6. **Feasibility Analysis:**
   - Technical: Can it be implemented with available technology?
   - Economic: Payback period, NPV, IRR.
   - Regulatory: Does it comply with PCB/CPCB norms?

7. **Implementation & Monitoring:**
   - Pilot-scale trials → Plant-scale roll-out.
   - Continuous monitoring using KPIs like η_SR, WMR, WGR.

**Material Balance Flow Diagram (Conceptual):**

```
   Raw Material ─┐
                 │
   Solvent    ───┼──→ [Process Unit] ──→ Product (Desired)
                 │              │
   Catalyst   ───┘              │
                                ├──→ Primary Waste Stream
   Water      ──────────────────┤
                                ├──→ Fugitive Emissions (Leakage)
   Energy     ──────────────────┘
```

**Part (b) — Economic Feasibility:**

Given:
- Annual operating saving: $S = \text{₹}6,00,000 \; \text{per year}$
- Capital investment: $C_{\text{cap}} = \text{₹}12,00,000$

**Simple Payback Period (SPP):**

$$
\text{SPP} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cap}}}{S_{\text{annual}}} = \dfrac{12,00,000}{6,00,000} = 2.0 \; \text{years}
$$

**Comment on Feasibility:**

A payback period of 2.0 years is **well within the accepted 2–3 year threshold** for process industry source reduction investments. Hence, the proposed source reduction option is **economically attractive and recommended for implementation**.

Additional context: The avoided treatment + disposal cost is ₹80/kg × 800 kg/hr × 8000 hr/yr ≈ ₹5.12 crores per year in *avoided* liability, which further strengthens the case.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing source reduction with end-of-pipe treatment** — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if the question asks for source reduction and the student writes about effluent treatment plants (ETPs).
>
> 2. **Sign convention errors in η_SR calculation** — Always subtract *after* from *before* in the numerator. Reversing this gives a *negative* efficiency, which is unphysical.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units** — WGR must have units like $\text{kg} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}$ or $\text{kg} \cdot \text{hr}^{-1}$. A unit-less answer loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mermaid diagrams with formatting symbols** — Do not use `**` or `*` inside Mermaid node labels; this breaks the renderer.
>
> 5. **Generic "good for environment" statements** — KTU expects specific engineering metrics (η_SR, WMR, SPP). Vague qualitative answers score poorly.
>
> 6. **Skipping the material balance** — Any 14-mark source reduction question *must* include a mass balance to score above 10/14.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield 30-Second Revision List:**
>
> - Source reduction = **PREVENTION at origin**, not treatment.
> - 4R Hierarchy: **Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recover > Dispose.**
> - Key metrics: **η_SR (%), WMR (≤ 1), WGR (kg/kg product).**
> - **Atom economy** of a reaction = (MW of desired product) / (Sum of MW of all reactants) × 100.
> - Source reduction techniques: **Inventory mgmt, Material substitution, Process modification, Good housekeeping, Internal recycling, Product redesign.**
> - A **waste audit** is the starting point — build a material balance first.
> - Pareto principle (80/20): 20% of sources → 80% of waste.
> - **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** = $C_{\text{cap}} / S_{\text{annual}}$; acceptable if ≤ 2–3 years.
> - Source reduction is the **top tier** of the pollution control hierarchy and the foundation of **Green Chemistry, Cleaner Production, and Circular Economy.**
> - Real-world success range: **30–70%** waste reduction achievable through source reduction alone.
> - In Mermaid diagrams: use **plain uppercase text** inside node labels, no bold/italic markers.
> - In KTU answer scripts: **always show units**, **always include a material balance**, and **never confuse source reduction with end-of-pipe treatment.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wastewater Pollution and Treatment Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wastewater Pollution and Treatment Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wastewater Pollution** is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, oceans, and groundwater) by harmful substances discharged from industrial, domestic, and agricultural activities. In process industries, wastewater is generated from manufacturing operations, cleaning processes, cooling systems, and product wash streams, containing organic, inorganic, and biological pollutants that must be treated before disposal to meet the **discharge standards prescribed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) / State Pollution Control Boards (SPCB)** under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

**Wastewater Treatment** refers to the physical, chemical, and biological processes used to remove contaminants from wastewater, producing an effluent of acceptable quality for safe discharge or reuse.

### 1.2 Sources of Wastewater in Process Industries

| Source Category | Examples of Pollutants |
|---|---|
| **Process Streams** | Reactants, intermediates, by-products |
| **Cooling Water** | Heat, dissolved salts, biocides |
| **Boiler Blowdown** | High TDS, hardness ions |
| **Desalter Effluent** | Oils, salts, hydrocarbons |
| **Cleaning & Wash Waters** | Detergents, suspended solids, oils |
| **Sanitary Waste** | Organic matter, pathogens |
| **Storm Water Runoff** | Suspended solids, oil residues |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of wastewater treatment like cleaning a heavily stained white shirt. 
> - **Primary treatment** = Wiping off the loose dirt (gross solids removal)
> - **Secondary treatment** = Soaking in detergent and scrubbing (breaking down organic stains via microbes)
> - **Tertiary treatment** = Polishing, ironing, and applying fabric softener (removing the last traces of stains, germs, and odors)
> 
> Just as you wouldn't iron a dirty shirt (treatment sequence matters), you cannot apply advanced oxidation to raw sewage — the sequence of treatment is critical.

### 1.4 Key Water Quality Parameters

- **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — measured in **mg/L** — the oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20°C.
- **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — measured in **mg/L** — oxygen required for chemical oxidation of organics.
- **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — measured in **mg/L** — solids retained on a 1.5 µm filter.
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** — measured in **mg/L** — solids passing through the filter.
- **pH** — standard units — measure of hydrogen ion concentration.
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — measured in **mg/L** — oxygen available to aquatic life.
- **Fecal Coliforms** — measured in **MPN/100 mL** — indicator of pathogenic contamination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of Wastewater

Wastewater characteristics are broadly classified into three categories:

**A. Physical Characteristics**
- Color (often grey to black, indicating anaerobic conditions)
- Odor (rotten egg smell due to H₂S)
- Temperature (affects biological reactions)
- Turbidity (suspended/colloidal matter)
- Total Solids (TS) = TSS + TDS

**B. Chemical Characteristics**
- Organic matter (BOD, COD, TOC)
- Inorganic matter (nutrients N & P, heavy metals, chlorides)
- pH and alkalinity
- Toxic compounds (phenols, cyanides, pesticides)

**C. Biological Characteristics**
- Bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths
- Indicator organisms (E. coli, fecal coliforms)

### 2.2 Wastewater Treatment Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Treatment is organized in **three stages** based on the level of contaminant removal.

**Stage 1: Preliminary Treatment**
- **Purpose:** Removal of large objects and coarse solids
- **Operations:** Screening, grit removal, comminution, flow equalization
- **Removal efficiency:** ~5–10% of suspended solids

**Stage 2: Primary Treatment (Physical/Chemical)**
- **Purpose:** Removal of settleable organic and inorganic solids
- **Operations:** Sedimentation, flotation, coagulation-flocculation
- **Removal efficiency:** ~50–70% TSS, 25–40% BOD

**Stage 3: Secondary Treatment (Biological)**
- **Purpose:** Removal of dissolved and colloidal organic matter
- **Processes:** Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Trickling Filters (TF), Oxidation Ponds, Anaerobic Digesters
- **Removal efficiency:** ~85–95% BOD, 80–90% TSS

**Stage 4: Tertiary/Advanced Treatment**
- **Purpose:** Polishing to remove nutrients, pathogens, refractory organics
- **Processes:** Filtration, Adsorption (Activated Carbon), Ion Exchange, Reverse Osmosis (RO), Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Description | Units | Typical Range in Raw Domestic Sewage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BOD₅** | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_i - DO_f}{P}$ where P = decimal fraction of sample | mg/L | 100 – 400 |
| **COD/BOD Ratio** | Indicates biodegradability; ratio > 3 means refractory organics | Dimensionless | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| **Total Solids** | $TS = TSS + TDS$ | mg/L | 700 – 1500 |
| **Volatile Solids** | $VS = TS - \text{Fixed Solids}$ | mg/L | 400 – 800 |
| **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{\text{Loading rate}}{\text{MLVSS}}$ | day⁻¹ | 0.2 – 0.5 (ASP) |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours | 4 – 8 (clarifier) |
| **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $SVI = \dfrac{\text{MLSS} \times 1000}{\text{SV}_{30}}$ | mL/g | 50 – 150 |
| **Oxygen Transfer Rate** | $OTR = K_La(C_s - C_L)$ | kg O₂/h | Process specific |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These parameters are used in designing Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP) and Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP) for chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and textile industries in Kerala.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Treatment Stage

- **Preliminary:** Protects downstream equipment (pumps, pipes) from damage and clogging.
- **Primary:** Reduces organic load on secondary treatment, lowering cost of aeration.
- **Secondary:** Converts dissolved organics into settleable biomass (activated sludge flocs).
- **Tertiary:** Ensures compliance with stringent CPCB discharge norms (e.g., BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L for inland discharge).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Process Analysis

#### **3.1.1 Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**

The ASP is the most widely used biological secondary treatment. It works on the principle of **aerobic biodegradation** of dissolved organics by a mixed microbial culture (flocs) suspended in aerated wastewater.

**Design Equation Derivation for Aeration Tank Volume:**

Step 1: The food-to-microorganism ratio (F/M) is defined as:

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

where:
- $Q$ = influent flow rate (m³/day)
- $S_0$ = influent substrate (BOD) concentration (mg/L)
- $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³)
- $X$ = MLVSS concentration (mg/L)

Step 2: Rearranging to find the required aeration tank volume:

$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$$

Step 3: The Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) is then:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$$

Step 4: The oxygen demand (theoretical) for complete oxidation is approximated using:

$$O_2 = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) - 1.42 \cdot X_w \cdot f$$

where:
- $S_e$ = effluent BOD (mg/L)
- $X_w$ = wasted biomass (kg/day)
- $1.42$ = oxygen demand per kg of cell mass oxidized
- $f$ = fraction of biomass oxidized (typically 0.5)

**Typical design values for ASP:**
- MLSS: 1500 – 4000 mg/L
- F/M ratio: 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹
- HRT: 4 – 8 hours
- SRT (sludge age): 5 – 15 days
- BOD removal efficiency: 85 – 95%

#### **3.1.2 Trickling Filter (TF) Design Approach**

Trickling filters are **attached-growth biological reactors** where wastewater is distributed over a bed of rocks or plastic media covered with a microbial biofilm.

Step 1: The most commonly used empirical equation is the **NRC (National Research Council) equation**:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$$

where:
- $E$ = fractional BOD removal efficiency
- $W$ = BOD loading rate (kg/day)
- $V$ = filter volume (m³)
- $F$ = recirculation factor

Step 2: The recirculation factor is calculated as:

$$F = \dfrac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2}$$

where $R$ = recirculation ratio (Q_recirculated / Q_influent).

Step 3: Hydraulic loading rate (HLR):

$$HLR = \dfrac{Q + Q_R}{A}$$

where $A$ = filter surface area (m²).

#### **3.1.3 Sedimentation Tank (Clarifier) Design**

Step 1: Surface overflow rate (SOR) — the most critical design parameter:

$$SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day})$$

Step 2: For typical primary clarifiers treating domestic sewage:

$$SOR_{primary} = 30 - 50 \quad \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

Step 3: For secondary clarifiers (after ASP):

$$SOR_{secondary} = 15 - 30 \quad \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

Step 4: Minimum detention time (hydraulic):

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} \geq 2 \text{ hours (primary)}, \quad \geq 2.5 \text{ hours (secondary)}$$

#### **3.1.4 Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP) — Fenton's Reaction**

AOPs produce highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to destroy refractory organics. The classical Fenton's reaction is:

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$$

The hydroxyl radical (•OH) has an oxidation potential of **2.80 V**, second only to fluorine, making it effective against:
- Phenolic compounds
- Pesticides
- Pharmaceutical residues
- Color and odor compounds

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Treatment Method | Target Pollutant | Removal Efficiency | Energy Cost | Sludge Production |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening** | Large debris | 100% (above size) | Very Low | None |
| **Grit Chamber** | Sand, gravel | 95% | Low | Low |
| **Primary Clarifier** | Settleable solids | 50–70% TSS, 25–40% BOD | Low | High |
| **Activated Sludge** | Dissolved organics | 85–95% BOD | High (aeration) | High |
| **Trickling Filter** | Dissolved organics | 75–90% BOD | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Anaerobic Digester** | High-strength organics, sludge | 60–80% COD | Low (produces biogas) | Low |
| **Reverse Osmosis** | Dissolved salts, trace organics | 95–99% | High | Concentrate |
| **Activated Carbon** | Refractory organics, color, odor | 80–95% | Moderate | Spent carbon |
| **UV / Chlorination** | Pathogens | 99.9% (3-log) | Low | None |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Wastewater Treatment Plant Flow (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the sequential treatment stages in a typical industrial Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) as required for KTU 2024 Scheme understanding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INDUSTRIAL INLET WASTEWATER] --> B[SCREENING CHAMBER]
    B --> C[GRIT REMOVAL UNIT]
    C --> D[FLOW EQUALIZATION TANK]
    D --> E[OIL AND GREASE TRAP]
    E --> F[PRIMARY CLARIFIER]
    F --> G[AERATION TANK ASP]
    G --> H[SECONDARY CLARIFIER]
    H --> I[TERTIARY FILTRATION SAND AND CARBON]
    I --> J[DISINFECTION UNIT UV OR CHLORINATION]
    J --> K[TREATED EFFLUENT TO DISCHARGE OR REUSE]
    H --> L[SLUDGE THICKENER]
    L --> M[ANAEROBIC DIGESTER]
    M --> N[SLUDGE DEWATERING FILTER PRESS]
    N --> O[CAKE TO LANDFILL OR INCINERATION]
    M --> P[BIOGAS TO BOILER OR FLARE]
```

### 4.2 Activated Sludge Process — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INFLUENT["RAW SEWAGE INLET"]
        A1[Influent BOD S0]
    end
    subgraph REACTOR["AERATION TANK"]
        B1[Diffused Air System]
        B2[Mixed Liquor MLVSS]
        B3[Aerobic Microbial Flocs]
    end
    subgraph CLARIFIER["SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
        C1[Settled Sludge]
        C2[Clarified Effluent Se]
    end
    subgraph SLUDGE["SLUDGE MANAGEMENT"]
        D1[Return Activated Sludge RAS]
        D2[Waste Activated Sludge WAS]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> B2
    C1 --> D2
    D2 --> L1[Sludge Treatment]
```

### 4.3 Process Selection Decision Tree (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Wastewater Characterization] --> S2{Is influent biodegradable?}
    S2 -- Yes COD to BOD ratio less than 3 --> S3{High organic load more than 1000 mg per L BOD}
    S2 -- No refractory toxic compounds --> S4[Advanced Oxidation Process AOP]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Anaerobic Treatment UASB Reactor]
    S3 -- No --> S6[Activated Sludge Process ASP]
    S4 --> S7{Discharge location inland or marine}
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S8[Secondary Clarifier]
    S8 --> S9{Nutrient removal needed N and P}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Tertiary Treatment Nitrification Denitrification]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Polishing and Disinfection]
    S7 -- Inland --> S11
    S7 -- Marine --> S12[Two Stage Biological Treatment Sufficient]
    S10 --> S11
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]**
*Define the terms BOD and COD. Explain the significance of the COD/BOD ratio in selecting a wastewater treatment process.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) consumed by aerobic microorganisms during the biological oxidation of organic matter in water, measured over 5 days at 20°C. Unit: mg/L. **(1 Mark)**

- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter in water using a strong oxidant (typically K₂Cr₂O₇ in H₂SO₄). Unit: mg/L. **(1 Mark)**

- **Significance of COD/BOD Ratio:** If the ratio is close to 1.5–2.5, the wastewater is **biodegradable** and amenable to biological treatment (ASP, TF). If the ratio exceeds 3.0, the wastewater contains **refractory organics** and requires physico-chemical or advanced oxidation treatment. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 2]**
*List the major differences between primary, secondary, and tertiary wastewater treatment processes with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Stage | Mechanism | Example | Typical Removal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Physical/chemical (settling) | Sedimentation in clarifier | 50–70% TSS |
| **Secondary** | Biological (aerobic/anaerobic) | Activated Sludge Process | 85–95% BOD |
| **Tertiary** | Advanced polishing | Reverse Osmosis / Activated Carbon | Nutrients, pathogens, refractory organics |

**(1 Mark per row, totaling 3 Marks.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Choice Set 1]

**Q. (a)** A primary clarifier receives wastewater with the following characteristics:
- Flow rate: $Q = 5{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- Influent BOD₅: $S_0 = 350 \text{ mg/L}$
- After primary treatment, BOD₅: $S_1 = 220 \text{ mg/L}$
- TSS removal: from 400 mg/L to 130 mg/L

Calculate:
1. The BOD₅ removal efficiency in the primary clarifier.
2. The mass of BOD removed per day.
3. The mass of dry sludge produced per day (assume 1 kg sludge per kg of TSS removed, with specific gravity 1.02 and moisture content 95%).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Part (1): BOD₅ Removal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_1}{S_0} \times 100$$

Substituting values:

$$\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{350 - 220}{350} \times 100 = \dfrac{130}{350} \times 100 = 37.14\%$$

**Answer: 37.14 %** **(2 Marks)**

**Part (2): Mass of BOD Removed Per Day**

$$\dot{m}_{BOD} = Q \times (S_0 - S_1)$$

$$\dot{m}_{BOD} = 5{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times (350 - 220) \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\dot{m}_{BOD} = 5{,}000 \times 130 = 650{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 650 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Answer: 650 kg/day** **(2 Marks)**

**Part (3): Mass of Wet Sludge Produced**

TSS removed per day:

$$\dot{m}_{TSS} = Q \times (TSS_0 - TSS_1) = 5{,}000 \times (400 - 130) = 1{,}350{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 1{,}350 \text{ kg dry solids/day}$$

Wet sludge mass (95% moisture, i.e., 5% solids):

$$\dot{m}_{wet} = \dfrac{1{,}350}{0.05} = 27{,}000 \text{ kg wet sludge/day} = 27 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Answer: 27,000 kg/day of wet sludge** **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**. Discuss the role of **MLSS, F/M ratio, and SRT** in maintaining efficient operation.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Process Description (4 Marks):**

The Activated Sludge Process is a suspended-growth biological treatment where:
- Wastewater enters the **aeration tank** where compressed air is supplied through diffusers.
- A mixed microbial culture (flocs) oxidizes dissolved organics under aerobic conditions:
  
$$Organic\ Matter + O_2 \xrightarrow{Microbes} CO_2 + H_2O + New\ Cells$$

- The mixed liquor flows to a **secondary clarifier** where biomass settles.
- A portion of settled sludge is **recycled (Return Activated Sludge — RAS)** to maintain microbial concentration.
- Excess sludge (**Waste Activated Sludge — WAS**) is sent to sludge processing.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

`Influent → Aeration Tank → Secondary Clarifier → Effluent`  
`           ↑ (RAS)                ↓ (WAS)`  
`           └──────┴──── Sludge Processing`

**2. Key Operating Parameters (3 Marks):**

- **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** Concentration of biomass in aeration tank, typically 1500–4000 mg/L. Higher MLSS increases treatment capacity but reduces oxygen transfer. **(1 Mark)**

- **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism Ratio):** Ratio of substrate loading to biomass; typical value 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹. Low F/M causes **bulking/old sludge**; high F/M causes **pin floc/poor settling**. **(1 Mark)**

- **SRT (Solids Retention Time):** Average time biomass stays in the system; typical 5–15 days. Longer SRT provides better BOD removal and nitrification; shorter SRT means less sludge but poorer treatment. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Choice Set 2 — ALTERNATIVE]

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the various **physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of industrial wastewater**. Give suitable examples for each category.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Physical Characteristics (2 Marks):**
- **Color** – Indicates the type of industry (e.g., dark brown from textiles, black from tanneries).
- **Odor** – Rotten egg (H₂S) from anaerobic digesters, pungent from petrochemicals.
- **Turbidity** – Due to suspended/colloidal matter; measured in NTU.
- **Temperature** – Critical parameter; high temperatures reduce DO in receiving waters.
- **Total Solids** – Includes TSS (suspended) and TDS (dissolved).

**2. Chemical Characteristics (3 Marks):**
- **Organic matter** (BOD, COD, TOC) — e.g., phenols, pesticides from chemical industries.
- **Nutrients** — Nitrogen (as NH₃, NO₃⁻) and Phosphorus (PO₄³⁻) cause **eutrophication** if discharged untreated.
- **Heavy metals** — Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr(VI) from electroplating, battery, and leather industries; highly toxic and **non-biodegradable**.
- **pH and Alkalinity** — Extreme pH (< 6 or > 9) inhibits biological treatment.
- **Inorganic salts** — High TDS in textile, food, and desalination plant effluents.

**3. Biological Characteristics (2 Marks):**
- **Pathogenic microorganisms** — Bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella), viruses, protozoa from food processing and pharma industries.
- **Indicator organisms** — Fecal coliforms (MPN/100 mL) indicate sanitary risk.
- **Biodegradable organics** — Sugars, proteins, starches from food and pulp industries.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** and **Trickling Filter (TF)** with respect to:
1. Type of growth (suspended vs. attached)
2. Energy requirements
3. Sludge characteristics
4. BOD removal efficiency
5. Footprint area
6. Operational sensitivity to shock loads
7. Suitability for process industries

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Activated Sludge Process | Trickling Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Growth type** | Suspended growth (MLSS) | Attached growth (biofilm on media) |
| 2 | **Energy requirement** | High (continuous aeration) | Low (rotating distributor) |
| 3 | **Sludge characteristics** | Highly flocculent, but prone to bulking | Thicker, less prone to bulking |
| 4 | **BOD removal** | 85–95% | 75–90% |
| 5 | **Footprint** | Large (aeration tank + clarifier) | Moderate (vertical bed) |
| 6 | **Shock load sensitivity** | High (microbes wash out) | Low (biofilm buffering) |
| 7 | **Suitability** | Municipal sewage, pharma, food | Pulp & paper, small-scale industries |

**(1 Mark per row = 7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing BOD and COD units:** BOD/COD are reported in mg/L, not kg/m³. Do not confuse numerical values.
> - **Missing units in final answer:** Always write "mg/L" or "kg/day" in the final numerical result; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> - **Skipping design assumptions:** For ASP/TF design problems, always **state assumed values** (MLSS, F/M, HRT) before substituting into formulas.
> - **No block diagram = 2 marks lost:** For "with neat diagram" type questions, drawing a labeled flow diagram is **mandatory**. A hand-drawn flow with arrows in the answer booklet is acceptable.
> - **Mere listing ≠ comparison:** For "Compare ASP and TF," a **tabular comparison** is expected, not just paragraphs of text.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Wastewater Pollution and Treatment Methods**

- ✅ **Sources:** Industrial process streams, cooling water, boiler blowdown, sanitary waste.
- ✅ **Key Parameters:** BOD₅, COD, TSS, TDS, pH, DO, F/M ratio, MLVSS, SVI, SRT, HRT.
- ✅ **Treatment Sequence:** Preliminary → Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Sludge Management.
- ✅ **Primary Treatment:** Removes 50–70% TSS and 25–40% BOD via sedimentation/flotation.
- ✅ **Activated Sludge Process:** Suspended growth; MLSS 1500–4000 mg/L; F/M = 0.2–0.5; SRT = 5–15 days; 85–95% BOD removal.
- ✅ **Trickling Filter:** Attached growth; low energy; uses NRC equation for design; recirculation factor F = (1+R)/(1+0.1R)².
- ✅ **Anaerobic Digestion:** Best for high-strength wastewater; produces biogas (60–70% CH₄).
- ✅ **Tertiary Treatment:** Filtration + Adsorption (Activated Carbon) + Disinfection (UV/Cl₂).
- ✅ **AOPs:** Fenton's reaction produces •OH radicals (E° = 2.80 V) for refractory organics.
- ✅ **CPCB Standards:** Inland discharge — BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L, TSS < 100 mg/L.
- ✅ **Sludge Volume Index (SVI):** 50–150 mL/g is healthy; > 200 mL/g indicates bulking.
- ✅ **Discharge Norms:** Discharge to inland surface water, marine, and land (irrigation) have different standards — **always specify the receiving body** in design problems.
- ✅ **Sustainability:** Modern ETPs focus on **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** using RO + evaporation/crystallization — important for KTU 2024 NEP-aligned questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Classification of Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources and Classification of Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wastewater** is defined as any water that has been adversely affected in quality by anthropogenic or natural influences during its use, and which is therefore unsuitable for immediate reuse or direct discharge into the environment without appropriate treatment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Pollution Control in Process Industries*, wastewater is further categorized as a complex, multi-phase fluid comprising:

- **Suspended solids** (organic and inorganic particulates)
- **Dissolved solids** (salts, organic molecules, gases)
- **Colloidal matter** (stable, sub-micron dispersions)
- **Microbial populations** (pathogenic and non-pathogenic)
- **Dissolved gases** ($O_2$, $CO_2$, $H_2S$, $CH_4$, $NH_3$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Industry Context:** Unlike domestic sewage, industrial wastewater is characterized by **highly variable flow rates**, **extreme pH values (1–13)**, **high temperatures (up to 60 °C)**, and the presence of **toxic, non-biodegradable, or recalcitrant compounds** that are specific to a given manufacturing process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of wastewater as the **"exhaust" of a city or factory**. Just as a car engine burns fuel and expels dirty exhaust gases, every human activity (washing, cooking, manufacturing, farming) consumes clean water and "dirties" it in the process. This dirty water carries the chemical fingerprints of its origin:

- **Kitchen sink water** → carries organic food waste (analogous to food scraps in a garbage bag).
- **Factory effluent** → carries specific industrial chemicals (analogous to specialized chemical spills).
- **Agricultural runoff** → carries fertilizers and pesticides (analogous to lawn chemicals being washed into a storm drain).

The **"dirtiness"** is quantified through physical, chemical, and biological parameters such as **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)**, **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**, **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**, and **pH** — analogous to measuring how "exhausted" the air is from a chimney.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Characterization

The following bolded parameters form the cornerstone of wastewater analysis in KTU examinations:

- **BOD₅** (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day) — measure of biodegradable organics, expressed in **mg/L**.
- **COD** (Chemical Oxygen Demand) — measure of total organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable), expressed in **mg/L**.
- **TSS** (Total Suspended Solids) — measure of filterable solids, expressed in **mg/L**.
- **TDS** (Total Dissolved Solids) — measure of dissolved salts, expressed in **mg/L**.
- **pH** — dimensionless scale (0–14) measuring hydrogen ion activity.
- **Temperature** — typically in **°C**, affecting reaction kinetics and dissolved oxygen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to differentiate between *sources* (where wastewater comes from) and *classification* (what categories it falls into based on composition and origin). These are distinct conceptual axes and are frequently tested as **2-mark short questions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Wastewater — A Structured Taxonomy

Wastewater sources are broadly grouped into **four major categories**. The "Why" behind each is straightforward: human and industrial activity is the root cause, and the nature of the activity dictates the composition of the resulting wastewater.

#### A. Domestic (Municipal) Wastewater
- **Origin:** Residential dwellings, commercial establishments, institutions (schools, hospitals, offices).
- **Composition:** Human excreta (blackwater), wash water from kitchens, bathrooms, laundries (greywater).
- **Key Pollutants:** Organic matter (proteins, carbohydrates, fats), pathogens (*E. coli*, *coliforms*), soaps, detergents, nutrients (N, P).
- **Typical BOD₅:** **200–300 mg/L**; Flow rate per capita: **100–200 L/day**.
- **Why it matters:** Largest volume contributor in urban areas; treated in centralized Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs).

#### B. Industrial Wastewater (Process Industries)
- **Origin:** Manufacturing, chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, food processing, textile, pulp & paper, tanneries, and metallurgical plants.
- **Composition:** Highly process-specific — may contain heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr), toxic organics (phenols, cyanides, PAHs), oils & greases, dyes, acids/alkalis.
- **Key Pollutants:** Variable — could be biodegradable (food industry) or recalcitrant (petrochemical).
- **Typical BOD₅:** **100–50,000 mg/L** (extremely wide range).
- **Why it matters:** Requires specialized **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)** with primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment trains.

#### C. Agricultural Wastewater (Non-Point Source)
- **Origin:** Farmland runoff, livestock operations, aquaculture, irrigation return flows.
- **Composition:** Fertilizers (nitrates, phosphates), pesticides, herbicides, animal manure, soil sediments.
- **Key Pollutants:** Nutrients, pesticides, pathogens, sediments.
- **Why it matters:** Major cause of **eutrophication** in surface water bodies.

#### D. Stormwater / Runoff
- **Origin:** Rainfall flowing over urban streets, rooftops, parking lots, and construction sites.
- **Composition:** Oil & grease, heavy metals from vehicles, litter, atmospheric deposits, suspended solids.
- **Why it matters:** First-flush events can cause acute pollution surges in receiving waters.

#### E. Infiltration and Inflow (I/I)
- **Origin:** Groundwater entering sewer systems through cracks, or direct stormwater connections.
- **Why it matters:** Causes hydraulic overloading of treatment plants.

### 2.2 Classification of Wastewater

Classification can be approached from **three distinct angles** (these are high-yield KTU exam topics):

#### Classification I — By Origin
1. **Domestic sewage** — predominantly biodegradable organics.
2. **Industrial effluent** — process-specific contaminants.
3. **Agricultural runoff** — nutrients, pesticides.
4. **Stormwater** — solids, hydrocarbons.
5. **Combined sewage** — mixture of domestic + stormwater (older urban systems).

#### Classification II — By Physical State of Pollutants
1. **Suspended solids** — settleable and non-settleable.
2. **Colloidal matter** — sub-micron particles stable due to surface charges.
3. **Dissolved solids** — ionic and molecular species in true solution.

#### Classification III — By Biodegradability
1. **Biodegradable organics** — sugars, starches, proteins, fats (measured as BOD).
2. **Non-biodegradable (recalcitrant) organics** — pesticides, phenols, petroleum hydrocarbons (measured as COD – BOD gap).
3. **Inorganic dissolved solids** — salts, metals, acids, alkalis.
4. **Biological agents** — bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths.

#### Classification IV — By Strength of Pollution
| Category | BOD₅ (mg/L) | Typical Source |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Strong** | > 400 | Tanneries, breweries, dairies |
| **Medium** | 200–400 | Food processing, paper mills |
| **Weak** | < 200 | Domestic sewage, textile (wash water) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **Population Equivalent (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{BOD_{industrial}}{BOD_{domestic\_per\_capita}}$ | persons | Converts industrial load to equivalent population |
| **BOD₅ per capita** | $BOD_c \approx 0.054 \text{ kg/capita/day}$ | kg/day | Standard design value for STPs |
| **Water supply per capita** | $q = 100 - 200$ | L/capita/day | KTU design benchmark |
| **Wastewater flow factor** | $Q_{ww} = 0.8 \times Q_{supply}$ | L/day | ~80% of supplied water becomes wastewater |
| **Sewage BOD₅ concentration** | $S = \dfrac{BOD_c \times P}{Q_{ww}}$ | mg/L | Mixing equation |
| **COD/BOD Ratio** | $Ratio = \dfrac{COD}{BOD_5}$ | dimensionless | >2.5 indicates non-biodegradable fraction |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours/days | Reactor sizing |
| **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** | $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m³/(m²·d) | Clarifier/filter design |
| **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S}{V}$ | kg BOD/(m³·d) | Biological reactor design |
| **Conversion: mg/L to kg/day** | $Mass = C \times Q \times 8.64 \times 10^{-5}$ | kg/day | Load calculations |
| **Mass balance on reactor** | $Q_{in} C_{in} = Q_{out} C_{out} + r \cdot V$ | — | Reactor analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The **Population Equivalent (PE)** concept is a *favourite* 7–14 mark question. Memorize the formula and the standard BOD per capita value (**0.054 kg/capita/day** or equivalently **54 g/capita/day**).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sizing of ETPs/STPs:** Engineers use source characterization to design treatment train capacity.
- **Regulatory compliance:** India's **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and state PCBs set discharge norms based on wastewater classification (e.g., **Gaseous emissions** vs **liquid effluents**).
- **Process modification:** Cleaner Production audits rely on source identification to reduce wastewater at source (a "P2 — Pollution Prevention" principle).
- **Reuse & Recycle:** Classifying wastewater (e.g., identifying low-strength cooling water) enables cascading reuse, reducing freshwater demand.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Population Equivalent (PE) Concept

The **Population Equivalent (PE)** of an industrial wastewater stream is defined as the population that would produce the same BOD load as the industry.

**Starting Point — Definition in Words:**
> "The number of persons producing a BOD₅ equivalent to the industrial BOD load."

**Step 1 — Express industrial BOD load as mass per unit time.**

Let:
- $Q_{ind}$ = industrial wastewater flow rate, expressed in **m³/day**
- $C_{ind}$ = BOD₅ concentration of industrial wastewater, in **mg/L** = **g/m³**

Then, the daily BOD load from industry is:

$$
BOD_{load, ind} = Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}
$$

where $BOD_{load, ind}$ is in units of **(m³/day) × (g/m³) = g/day**.

> **Logic:** Mass load = Volume × Concentration. This is a fundamental mass balance — the daily mass of pollutant is the rate at which the polluted stream delivers it.

**Step 2 — Express the per-capita BOD contribution.**

For a standard domestic population, the BOD₅ per capita per day is a known design parameter:

$$
BOD_{cap} = 0.054 \; \text{kg/capita/day} = 54 \; \text{g/capita/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the industrial BOD load to that of a hypothetical equivalent population (PE).**

By definition, $P$ persons would produce a total BOD load of $P \times BOD_{cap}$. Setting this equal to the industrial load:

$$
P \times BOD_{cap} = Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P.**

$$
P = \dfrac{Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}}{BOD_{cap}}
$$

**Final Compact Formula:**

$$
PE = \dfrac{Q_{ind} \;(\text{m}^3/\text{day}) \times C_{ind} \;(\text{g/m}^3)}{54 \;(\text{g/capita/day})}
$$

This gives $PE$ in units of **persons (capita)**.

**Step 5 — Unit Verification.**

$$
PE = \dfrac{\text{m}^3/\text{day} \times \text{g/m}^3}{\text{g/(capita·day)}} = \dfrac{\text{g/day}}{\text{g/(capita·day)}} = \text{capita} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Sewage BOD₅ Concentration from Per-Capita Data

**Problem Setup:** A town has a population $P$ and water supply $q$ (L/capita/day). Assume 80% of supplied water becomes wastewater. Per-capita BOD is $BOD_c$ (g/capita/day).

**Step 1 — Compute the total wastewater flow.**

$$
Q_{ww} = 0.8 \times P \times q
$$

where $Q_{ww}$ is in L/day.

**Step 2 — Compute the total BOD load.**

$$
BOD_{total} = P \times BOD_c
$$

where $BOD_{total}$ is in g/day.

**Step 3 — Compute the BOD concentration in the mixed sewage.**

Concentration = Mass / Volume. Convert units consistently. Since $BOD_c$ is in g/capita/day, and $Q_{ww}$ in L/day, the resulting concentration is in **g/L**, which can be converted to **mg/L** by multiplying by 1000.

$$
S = \dfrac{P \times BOD_c}{0.8 \times P \times q} = \dfrac{BOD_c}{0.8 \times q}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert standard values for verification.**

With $BOD_c = 54$ g/capita/day and $q = 135$ L/capita/day:

$$
S = \dfrac{54}{0.8 \times 135} = \dfrac{54}{108} = 0.500 \; \text{g/L} = 500 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A "medium-strength" sewage typically has BOD₅ around 200–300 mg/L, while a "strong" sewage can approach 400–500 mg/L. The KTU-recommended design value for moderate Indian cities is approximately **250–350 mg/L**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wastewater Source vs. Characteristic Pollutants

| Source | Major Organic Pollutants | Major Inorganic Pollutants | pH Range | Temperature | Toxicity Level |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| Domestic sewage | Proteins, carbohydrates, fats, soaps | N, P, Cl⁻, traces of heavy metals | 6.5–8.0 | 10–25 °C | Low |
| Food & dairy | Lactose, casein, oils | Ca²⁺, Na⁺, PO₄³⁻ | 4–9 | 20–40 °C | Low–Medium |
| Textile | Dyes, starch, surfactants | NaOH, NaCl, sulfates | 6–12 | 30–60 °C | High (color) |
| Tanneries | Proteins, hair, fats | Cr³⁺, sulfides, lime | 8–12 | 20–35 °C | High (Cr) |
| Pulp & paper | Lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose | NaOH, Na₂S, ClO₂ | 6–10 | 30–50 °C | High (color, BOD) |
| Petrochemical | Oils, PAHs, phenols | Sulfides, cyanides, H₂S | 5–9 | 25–45 °C | Very High |
| Pharmaceutical | APIs, solvents, antibiotics | Solvent residues, acids/alkalis | 3–11 | 20–40 °C | High |
| Metal finishing | Cyanide complexes, oils | Cr⁶⁺, Ni²⁺, Cd²⁺, Pb²⁺, acids | 1–6 | 20–35 °C | Very High |
| Agricultural runoff | Pesticides, herbicides | NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, K⁺ | 6–8 | 15–30 °C | Medium |
| Stormwater | Hydrocarbons, dust | Pb, Zn, particulate metals | 6.5–8.5 | Ambient | Medium |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A dairy plant discharges **500 m³/day** of wastewater with a BOD₅ concentration of **1500 mg/L**. Calculate the Population Equivalent, given the standard per-capita BOD₅ = 54 g/capita/day.

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

$$
C_{ind} = 1500 \; \text{mg/L} = 1500 \; \text{g/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PE formula.**

$$
PE = \dfrac{Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}}{BOD_{cap}} = \dfrac{500 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 1500 \; \text{g/m}^3}{54 \; \text{g/capita/day}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
500 \times 1500 = 750{,}000 \; \text{g/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by per-capita load.**

$$
PE = \dfrac{750{,}000}{54} = 13{,}888.89 \approx 13{,}889 \; \text{capita}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{PE \approx 13{,}889 \; \text{persons}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A single dairy plant pollutes equivalent to a town of about 13,900 people. This is why industrial pollution control is critical in densely industrialized regions of Kerala such as Ernakulam and Thrissur.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Sources of Wastewater

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sources of Wastewater] --> B[Domestic or Municipal]
    A --> C[Industrial or Process]
    A --> D[Agricultural]
    A --> E[Stormwater Runoff]
    A --> F[Infiltration and Inflow]
    B --> B1[Blackwater - human excreta]
    B --> B2[Greywater - kitchens, baths, laundries]
    C --> C1[Food and Dairy]
    C --> C2[Textile and Dyeing]
    C --> C3[Pulp and Paper]
    C --> C4[Petrochemical and Refinery]
    C --> C5[Tannery and Leather]
    C --> C6[Pharmaceutical]
    C --> C7[Metal Finishing]
    D --> D1[Livestock Waste]
    D --> D2[Fertilizer Runoff]
    D --> D3[Pesticide Wash-off]
    E --> E1[Urban Street Runoff]
    E --> E2[Construction Site Runoff]
    E --> E3[Atmospheric Deposition]
    F --> F1[Groundwater Seepage]
    F --> F2[Improper Storm Connections]
```

### 4.2 Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Wastewater Classification] --> W1[By Origin]
    W --> W2[By Pollutant State]
    W --> W3[By Biodegradability]
    W --> W4[By Strength]
    W1 --> W1a[Domestic]
    W1 --> W1b[Industrial]
    W1 --> W1c[Agricultural]
    W1 --> W1d[Stormwater]
    W2 --> W2a[Suspended Solids]
    W2 --> W2b[Colloidal Matter]
    W2 --> W2c[Dissolved Solids]
    W3 --> W3a[Biodegradable Organics]
    W3 --> W3b[Non-Biodegradable Organics]
    W3 --> W3c[Inorganic Dissolved Solids]
    W3 --> W3d[Biological Agents]
    W4 --> W4a[Strong - BOD above 400]
    W4 --> W4b[Medium - BOD 200 to 400]
    W4 --> W4c[Weak - BOD below 200]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: From Generation to Discharge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Source Activity] --> U[Water Use]
    U --> W[Wastewater Generation]
    W --> C[Collection - Sewers or Drains]
    C --> P[Pre-Treatment - Screening, Grit Removal]
    P --> T1[Primary Treatment - Sedimentation]
    T1 --> T2[Secondary Treatment - Biological]
    T2 --> T3[Tertiary Treatment - Advanced]
    T3 --> DIS[Discharge to Environment or Reuse]
    DIS --> R1[Reuse in Industry]
    DIS --> R2[Reuse in Irrigation]
    DIS --> R3[Discharge to Water Body]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture: Wastewater Characterization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Wastewater Sample] --> PH[Physical Tests]
    S --> CH[Chemical Tests]
    S --> BI[Biological Tests]
    PH --> PH1[Color, Odor, Turbidity]
    PH --> PH2[Total Suspended Solids]
    PH --> PH3[Temperature]
    CH --> CH1[pH, Alkalinity, Acidity]
    CH --> CH2[COD, BOD]
    CH --> CH3[Nutrients N, P]
    CH --> CH4[Heavy Metals]
    CH --> CH5[Dissolved Gases]
    BI --> BI1[Coliform Count]
    BI --> BI2[Pathogen Detection]
    BI --> BI3[Toxicity Bioassay]
    PH1 --> R[Characterization Profile]
    PH2 --> R
    PH3 --> R
    CH1 --> R
    CH2 --> R
    CH3 --> R
    CH4 --> R
    CH5 --> R
    BI1 --> R
    BI2 --> R
    BI3 --> R
    R --> DEC[Treatment Train Selection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently pose the question *"Outline the steps involved in characterizing wastewater."* The four-tier framework (Physical → Chemical → Biological → Decision) is the canonical answer structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define wastewater. List any four major sources of wastewater.

**Model Answer:**
Wastewater is water that has been used and its quality impaired by physical, chemical, or biological contaminants, making it unsuitable for direct reuse or discharge without treatment.

Four major sources:
1. Domestic/municipal sewage.
2. Industrial effluents.
3. Agricultural runoff.
4. Stormwater runoff.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between BOD and COD. Why is the COD/BOD ratio important in wastewater classification?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) | COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20 °C | Oxygen consumed by strong chemical oxidant (e.g., dichromate) |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) |
| Time | 5 days (BOD₅) or 20 days (BOD₂₀) | 2–3 hours |
| Value | Always lower than COD | Always higher than or equal to BOD |

**Importance of COD/BOD ratio:** A ratio > **2.5** indicates a significant fraction of non-biodegradable organics, requiring advanced treatment (e.g., activated carbon adsorption, advanced oxidation).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Classify wastewater based on (i) origin, (ii) physical state of pollutants, and (iii) biodegradability. Give at least two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**
