> **(b)** A textile industry discharges 800 m³/day of wastewater with a BOD₅ concentration of 2000 mg/L. Compute the population equivalent of this industry. State its significance in environmental engineering. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Wastewater (7 marks):**

**(i) By Origin:**
1. **Domestic sewage** — e.g., municipal blackwater, greywater.
2. **Industrial effluent** — e.g., textile dye wastewater, dairy effluent.
3. **Agricultural runoff** — e.g., fertilizer-laden irrigation return flow.
4. **Stormwater** — e.g., urban street runoff carrying oils and sediments.

**(ii) By Physical State of Pollutants:**
1. **Suspended solids** — e.g., paper fibres in pulp mill effluent.
2. **Colloidal matter** — e.g., emulsified oil droplets in refinery wastewater.
3. **Dissolved solids** — e.g., sodium chloride ions in textile dye bath.

**(iii) By Biodegradability:**
1. **Biodegradable organics** — e.g., sugars, proteins, fats in food industry effluent.
2. **Non-biodegradable (recalcitrant) organics** — e.g., pesticides, PAHs, phenols in petrochemical effluent.
3. **Inorganic dissolved solids** — e.g., heavy metals, acids, alkalis in metal-finishing effluent.
4. **Biological agents** — e.g., *E. coli*, *Salmonella* in domestic sewage.

**(b) Population Equivalent Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**
- $Q_{ind} = 800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $C_{ind} = 2000 \; \text{mg/L} = 2000 \; \text{g/m}^3$
- $BOD_{cap} = 54 \; \text{g/capita/day}$ (KTU standard value)

**Step 2 — Apply the PE formula.**

$$
PE = \dfrac{Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}}{BOD_{cap}} = \dfrac{800 \times 2000}{54}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
PE = \dfrac{1{,}600{,}000}{54} = 29{,}629.6 \approx 29{,}630 \; \text{persons}
$$

**Step 4 — State the significance (3–4 sentences).**
The population equivalent of 29,630 means that the textile industry contributes a BOD load equivalent to a town of ~30,000 people. This metric is used by pollution control boards to:
1. Size the common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP) for industrial estates.
2. Levy pollution cess on industries based on their PE.
3. Compare the polluting strength of different industries on a common scale.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the major sources of wastewater in a typical urban-industrial region. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics used to characterize industrial wastewater. Give at least two parameters under each category. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Sources of Wastewater — Flow Diagram (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Master Block Diagram)** of these notes. The student should produce a flowchart showing the four to five major source categories with sub-categories:

1. **Domestic:** Residential complexes, commercial buildings, hospitals, schools.
2. **Industrial:** Process industries (chemical, textile, food, pharma, metal).
3. **Agricultural:** Livestock farms, fertilizer/pesticide runoff.
4. **Stormwater:** Urban runoff, roof drainage, street drainage.
5. **Infiltration/Inflow:** Groundwater seepage, illegal storm connections.

**Explanation (3–4 marks):** Briefly describe each source with example industries and typical pollutant classes.

**(b) Wastewater Characterization (7 marks):**

**(i) Physical Characteristics:**
1. **Color and Odor** — Indicator of fresh vs. septic wastewater; e.g., black color with H₂S odor indicates anaerobic septic sewage.
2. **Turbidity** — Measure of suspended/colloidal matter; measured in **NTU** (Nephelometric Turbidity Units).
3. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — Dried residue after filtration; expressed in **mg/L**.
4. **Temperature** — Affects biological activity and dissolved oxygen solubility.

**(ii) Chemical Characteristics:**
1. **pH** — Indicates acidic/alkaline nature; industrial effluents can range from pH 1 to 13.
2. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — Measure of biodegradable organics; standard at 5 days, 20 °C.
3. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — Measure of total organics; useful when toxic compounds inhibit BOD test.
4. **Total Nitrogen and Phosphorus** — Cause eutrophication; critical in agricultural runoff.
5. **Heavy Metals** — Lead, cadmium, mercury, chromium — toxic and non-biodegradable.

**(iii) Biological Characteristics:**
1. **Total Coliform Count** — Indicator of pathogenic contamination; measured in **MPN/100 mL** (Most Probable Number).
2. **Fecal Streptococci** — Specific indicator of fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals.
3. **Pathogens** — *E. coli*, *Salmonella typhi*, *Vibrio cholerae*, viruses, helminth eggs.
4. **Biodiversity Index** — Used for toxicity assessment and ecological evaluation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Source" with "Classification":** Many students interchange these. *Sources* = where wastewater comes from; *Classification* = how it is categorized. These carry **separate marks**.
> 2. **Unit Mistakes in PE Calculation:** Forgetting to convert mg/L to g/m³ (multiply by 1, since 1 mg/L = 1 g/m³) leads to a factor-of-1000 error. Always write units explicitly.
> 3. **Standard Value Memory:** The per-capita BOD₅ of **54 g/capita/day** and per-capita water supply of **135 L/capita/day** are the KTU-favoured design values. Using incorrect values leads to 1–2 mark deductions.
> 4. **Diagram Neatness:** A 7-mark source/classification question *always* requires a diagram. A well-labelled Mermaid-style or hand-drawn flowchart fetches full marks; a textual-only answer is capped at 5 marks.
> 5. **Avoid generic lists:** Examiners reward specific industrial examples (e.g., *"tannery effluent with Cr³⁺ at pH 10"*) over vague statements (*"industrial wastewater has chemicals"*).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sources and Classification of Wastewater**

- **Wastewater definition:** Water whose quality has been impaired by use; requires treatment before discharge or reuse.
- **Major sources (5):** Domestic, Industrial, Agricultural, Stormwater, Infiltration/Inflow.
- **Classification axes (4):** By Origin, By Physical State, By Biodegradability, By Strength.
- **Physical state categories:** Suspended, Colloidal, Dissolved.
- **Biodegradability categories:** Biodegradable, Recalcitrant, Inorganic, Biological.
- **Strength benchmark:** Strong > 400 mg/L BOD; Medium 200–400 mg/L; Weak < 200 mg/L.
- **Standard design values:** BOD per capita = **54 g/capita/day**; Water supply = **100–200 L/capita/day**; Wastewater flow = **80% of water supplied**.
- **Population Equivalent (PE) formula:** $PE = \dfrac{Q_{ind} \times C_{ind}}{BOD_{cap}}$ — units: $PE$ in persons.
- **Unit conversion:** **1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = 1 ppm** (for dilute aqueous solutions).
- **BOD vs COD:** BOD measures biodegradable; COD measures total organics. **COD ≥ BOD always**.
- **COD/BOD ratio significance:** > 2.5 implies recalcitrant organics needing tertiary treatment.
- **Process industries focus:** Highly variable flow, pH, temperature; presence of toxic, non-biodegradable compounds.
- **KTU focus industries:** Tanneries (Cr), Pulp & paper (color), Textile (dyes), Petrochemical (phenols, PAHs), Metal finishing (heavy metals), Food & dairy (high BOD).
- **Key Indian regulatory body:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — sets discharge norms for BOD, COD, TSS, pH.
- **Diagram must-haves:** A typical 7-mark answer on sources requires a labelled flowchart with at least 3 source categories and 2 sub-categories each.
- **Memorize the PE worked example:** It is reused across multiple modules of this course.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OECHT613 Pollution Control in Process Industries**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Water Pollutants and Their Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Water Pollutants and Their Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Water pollution* is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, oceans, aquifers, and groundwater) with harmful substances in sufficient concentrations that degrade water quality, render it unfit for its intended beneficial use, or adversely affect aquatic ecosystems and human health. A **water pollutant** is any physical, chemical, biological, or radiological substance introduced into a water body in quantities that exceed natural assimilation capacity, producing measurable detrimental ecological or health effects.

According to the **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA)** and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 learning outcomes, water pollutants are broadly classified into **eight major categories**:

| S.No | Pollutant Class | Typical Examples |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pathogens | *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Vibrio cholerae*, Rotavirus |
| 2 | Organic Wastes (Biodegradable) | Sewage, animal manure, food-processing effluent |
| 3 | Chemical Pollutants (Inorganic) | Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), acids, alkalis, cyanides |
| 4 | Synthetic Organic Compounds | Pesticides, PCBs, dioxins, detergents, plastics |
| 5 | Nutrients | Nitrates (NO₃⁻), Phosphates (PO₄³⁻), Ammonia (NH₃) |
| 6 | Sediments & Suspended Solids | Silt, clay, soil runoff |
| 7 | Thermal Pollutants | Heated cooling water from power plants/industries |
| 8 | Radioactive Substances | Strontium-90, Iodine-131, Cesium-137, Uranium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Units in Water Pollution Engineering:**
> - Concentration: **mg/L** (milligrams per litre) ≡ **ppm** (parts per million) for dilute aqueous solutions
> - BOD/COD: **mg O₂/L** (mass of oxygen consumed per litre of sample)
> - pH: dimensionless, scale **0–14**
> - Hardness: **mg/L as CaCO₃**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Bloodstream"**
> Think of a river as the **bloodstream of the Earth**. Just as a human body suffers when foreign chemicals (alcohol, drugs, viruses) enter the bloodstream, a river "suffers" when pollutants enter it. The river has a natural **kidney-like self-purification capacity** (microbial degradation, dilution, sunlight UV disinfection, sedimentation). However, when the pollutant load exceeds this natural "kidney function," the river becomes "diseased" — it loses dissolved oxygen, kills aquatic life, and eventually poisons the humans who depend on it.

**Why should a Chemical Engineer study this?** Every process industry (petroleum refinery, fertilizer plant, pulp & paper mill, tannery, textile, pharmaceutical) generates wastewater containing a *specific fingerprint* of pollutants. Identifying the *type* of pollutant is the **first step** in designing the right treatment train. For instance:

- **Heavy metals** → require **precipitation, ion-exchange, or membrane separation**
- **Biodegradable organics** → require **biological treatment** (activated sludge, trickling filter)
- **Heat** → require **cooling towers or cooling ponds**

---

## 1.3 Detailed Classification Tree of Water Pollutants

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["WATER POLLUTANTS"] --> B["BIOLOGICAL"]
    A --> C["CHEMICAL"]
    A --> D["PHYSICAL"]
    A --> E["RADIOLOGICAL"]
    
    B --> B1["Bacteria e.g. E.coli"]
    B --> B2["Viruses e.g. Hepatitis A"]
    B --> B3["Protozoa e.g. Giardia"]
    B --> B4["Helminths Parasitic Worms"]
    
    C --> C1["Inorganic"]
    C --> C2["Organic"]
    C1 --> C1a["Heavy Metals Pb Hg Cd Cr"]
    C1 --> C1b["Acids and Alkalis pH shift"]
    C1 --> C1c["Salts TDS Hardness"]
    C1 --> C1d["Cyanides CN-"]
    C2 --> C2a["Pesticides DDT Endosulfan"]
    C2 --> C2b["Detergents Surfactants"]
    C2 --> C2c["PCBs Dioxins POPs"]
    C2 --> C2d["Petroleum Hydrocarbons"]
    
    D --> D1["Thermal Heat"]
    D --> D2["Suspended Solids Turbidity"]
    D --> D3["Color and Odor"]
    D --> D4["Oil and Grease"]
    
    E --> E1["Natural Radionuclides U-238 Rn-222"]
    E --> E2["Anthropogenic Sr-90 Cs-137 I-131"]
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Category-Wise Mechanistic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Biological Pollutants (Pathogens)

Pathogens are **disease-causing microorganisms** originating primarily from **domestic sewage, animal husbandry, hospital effluents, and food-processing discharges**.

**Mechanism of harm:**
- **Bacteria** → release toxins, invade intestinal walls (typhoid, cholera)
- **Viruses** → replicate inside host cells (Hepatitis A, Polio)
- **Protozoa** → form cysts resistant to chlorination (Giardia, *Cryptosporidium*)
- **Helminths** → eggs/larvae ingested (roundworm, tapeworm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indicator Organism:** *E. coli* (faecal coliform) is used as the standard **indicator** because it is abundant in human faeces, easy to culture, and its absence reliably indicates the absence of other pathogens. KTU exam favourite!

### 2.1.2 Biodegradable Organic Wastes

**The "Why" — Oxygen Depletion Mechanism:**
Microorganisms consume dissolved oxygen (DO) to oxidize organic matter:

$$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Biomass}$$

When the rate of oxygen consumption exceeds the rate of re-aeration, the river becomes **anaerobic**, producing H₂S (rotten-egg smell), CH₄, and dead fish.

**Key Metric — Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD):**
BOD is the **mass of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microbes** to oxidize biodegradable organic matter in a water sample over a specified time (standard: **5 days at 20°C**, written as BOD₅).

> [!NOTE]
> **BOD₅ Ultimate = BOD₅ × 1.46** (first-order kinetics assumption for typical municipal sewage)

### 2.1.3 Chemical Pollutants — Inorganic

**(a) Heavy Metals:**
Heavy metals (atomic density > **5 g/cm³**) are **non-biodegradable** and undergo **bioaccumulation** (concentration in an organism over time) and **biomagnification** (concentration increasing up the food chain).

| Metal | Symbol | Toxicity Threshold | Major Source |
|-------|--------|--------------------|--------------|
| Lead | Pb | 0.05 mg/L (drinking) | Battery, smelting |
| Mercury | Hg | 0.001 mg/L | Chlor-alkali, coal plants |
| Cadmium | Cd | 0.005 mg/L | Electroplating, mining |
| Chromium (VI) | Cr⁶⁺ | 0.05 mg/L | Tanneries, metal finishing |
| Arsenic | As | 0.01 mg/L | Pesticides, mining |

**(b) Acids/Alkalis:** Sudden pH shocks kill aquatic life. Industrial effluents from pickling, electroplating, and fertilizer plants are major sources.

### 2.1.4 Chemical Pollutants — Synthetic Organic Compounds

These include **Pesticides, PCBs, Dioxins, Detergents, Plastics** — often called **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)**. They are:
- **Resistant to microbial degradation** (persistent in environment for decades)
- **Lipophilic** (fat-soluble → accumulate in animal fat tissue)
- **Toxic at very low concentrations** (µg/L range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DDT — Classic KTU Example of Biomagnification:**
> Water: 0.003 ppm → Zooplankton: 0.04 ppm → Small Fish: 0.5 ppm → Large Fish: 2.0 ppm → Fish-eating Birds: 25 ppm
> This **8,300× magnification** is why DDT caused eggshell thinning in bald eagles.

### 2.1.5 Nutrients (Eutrophication)

Excess nitrogen and phosphorus cause **algal blooms** → light blockage → death of submerged plants → bacterial decomposition of dead algae → oxygen depletion → **hypoxic/anoxic "dead zones"** (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, Baltic Sea).

### 2.1.6 Suspended Solids & Sediments

Cause **turbidity** → reduce light penetration → smother fish eggs → carry adsorbed pollutants (pesticides, heavy metals).

### 2.1.7 Thermal Pollution

Power plants and refineries discharge cooling water **5–10°C warmer** than receiving water. Effects:
- **Reduces DO solubility** (warm water holds less oxygen)
- **Accelerates metabolic rates** of fish → increased oxygen demand
- **Disrupts spawning cycles**

The mathematical relationship (critical for KTU):

$$DO_{sat}(T) = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$$

### 2.1.8 Radioactive Substances

Sources: nuclear power plant cooling water, uranium mining, medical/industrial isotopes. Effects include **genetic mutations, cancer, and bioaccumulation in bone tissue** (Strontium-90 mimics calcium).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — Master these for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_{initial} - DO_{final}}{\text{Dilution Factor}}$ | Standard 5-day BOD | mg O₂/L |
| 2 | $COD = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N \times 8000}{V_{sample}}$ | Dichromate COD | mg O₂/L |
| 3 | $BOD_u = \dfrac{L_0}{1 + e^{-kt}}$ | BOD at time *t* (first-order) | mg/L |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = -kL$ | BOD exertion rate | mg/(L·d) |
| 5 | $DO_{sat}(T) = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$ | DO saturation vs. temperature | mg/L |
| 6 | $Hardness_{CaCO_3} = [Ca^{2+}] \times \dfrac{100}{40} + [Mg^{2+}] \times \dfrac{100}{24.3}$ | Total Hardness | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 7 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Acidity/Alkalinity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{mg \text{ solute}}{L \text{ solution}}$ | ppm = mg/L (dilute aq.) | mg/L |
| 9 | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | L/kg |
| 10 | $DO_{deficit} = DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$ | Dissolved oxygen deficit | mg/L |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) Design:** Identifying pollutant type dictates the **sequence of treatment units**. A textile effluent (high color, COD, salts) → coagulation → biological → RO. A refinery effluent (oil, phenols, heavy metals) → API oil separator → DAF → biological.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Mandatory under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986 (India)** and KTU-aligned industry practice.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** IS 2490 (tolerance limits for industrial effluents), CPCB norms — students must know parameter-wise permissible limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order BOD Reaction Kinetics

The biochemical oxidation of organic matter follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining oxygen-demanding substrate.

### Step 1: Define Variables

Let:
- $L_0$ = Ultimate (total) BOD at $t = 0$ (mg/L)
- $L_t$ = Remaining oxygen-demanding load at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = First-order reaction rate constant (day⁻¹, typically 0.1–0.3 per day at 20°C)
- $t$ = Time in days

The rate of BOD exertion is proportional to the remaining unoxidized organic matter:

$$\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Rearranging to integrate from $t=0$ to $t=t$:

$$\dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \, dt$$

### Step 3: Integration

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

Applying the limits:

$$\ln(L_t) - \ln(L_0) = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Exponentiation

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

### Step 5: Definition of BOD at time *t*

The **BOD exerted** up to time *t* is the difference between the ultimate BOD and the remaining load:

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_t$$

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

$$\boxed{BOD_t = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k t})}$$

### Step 6: Verification for Standard BOD₅

At $t = 5$ days, $k = 0.23$ day⁻¹ (typical domestic sewage):

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}) = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.15})$$

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \cdot (1 - 0.3166) = 0.6834 \, L_0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BOD_5 \approx 0.68 \, L_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_0 \approx 1.46 \, BOD_5}$$

This 1.46 factor is the **standard conversion** universally used in environmental engineering and is a **KTU favourite**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hardness as CaCO₃ Equivalent

Water hardness comes from dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts. Standard practice expresses hardness as **equivalent mg/L of CaCO₃**.

### Step 1: Equivalent Weight of CaCO₃

$$E_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{M_{CaCO_3}}{2} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

### Step 2: Equivalent Weight of Ca²⁺

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = \dfrac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ g/eq}$$

### Step 3: Multiplying Factor (Ca²⁺ → CaCO₃)

$$\text{Factor}_{Ca} = \dfrac{E_{CaCO_3}}{E_{Ca^{2+}}} = \dfrac{50}{20} = 2.5$$

Or equivalently: $\dfrac{100/2}{40/2} = \dfrac{100}{40} = 2.5$

### Step 4: Multiplying Factor (Mg²⁺ → CaCO₃)

Atomic weight of Mg = 24.3 g/mol; valence = 2:

$$E_{Mg^{2+}} = \dfrac{24.3}{2} = 12.15 \text{ g/eq}$$

$$\text{Factor}_{Mg} = \dfrac{50}{12.15} = 4.12 \approx \dfrac{100}{24.3}$$

### Step 5: General Expression

$$\boxed{Hardness_{as \, CaCO_3} = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] \, (mg/L) + 4.12 \times [Mg^{2+}] \, (mg/L)}$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

A water sample contains Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L and Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L. Calculate total hardness as CaCO₃.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$H_{Ca} = 2.5 \times 80 = 200 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

$$H_{Mg} = 4.12 \times 24 = 98.88 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

$$H_{total} = 200 + 98.88 = 298.88 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \approx 299 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification:** 0–60 (soft), 61–120 (moderately hard), 121–180 (hard), >180 (very hard). Sample above is **very hard**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pollutant Categories

| Pollutant Class | Persistence | Primary Health/Eco Effect | Treatment Method | KTU Question Weightage |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Pathogens | Days–months | Cholera, Typhoid, Hepatitis | Chlorination, UV, Ozonation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| BOD/COD | Days–weeks | Oxygen depletion, fish kills | Activated sludge, trickling filter | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Heavy Metals | Indefinite | Cancer, kidney damage, neurotoxicity | Precipitation, ion-exchange, RO | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Nutrients (N, P) | Weeks | Eutrophication, algal blooms | Nitrification/denitrification, P-precipitation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| POPs (Pesticides/PCBs) | Years–decades | Biomagnification, endocrine disruption | GAC adsorption, advanced oxidation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Suspended Solids | Hours–days | Turbidity, sediment smothering | Sedimentation, filtration | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Thermal | Hours | Reduced DO, ecosystem stress | Cooling towers, cooling ponds | ⭐⭐ |
| Radioactive | Decades–millennia | Cancer, genetic mutation | Ion-exchange, evaporation, vitrification | ⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Water Pollution Sources & Pathways

The diagram below illustrates the **interconnected flows** of major water pollutant categories from their sources through environmental compartments to their effects.

```mermaid
graph TD
    source1["DOMESTIC SEWAGE"] --> path1["Municipal Outfalls"]
    source2["INDUSTRIAL EFFLUENTS"] --> path2["Factory Discharge Pipes"]
    source3["AGRICULTURAL RUNOFF"] --> path3["Field Drainage"]
    source4["URBAN STORMWATER"] --> path4["Storm Drains"]
    source5["POWER PLANTS"] --> path5["Cooling Water Discharge"]
    
    path1 --> WB["RECEIVING WATER BODY River Lake Estuary"]
    path2 --> WB
    path3 --> WB
    path4 --> WB
    path5 --> WB
    
    WB --> eff1["Pathogen Infection"]
    WB --> eff2["Dissolved Oxygen Depletion"]
    WB --> eff3["Bioaccumulation in Food Chain"]
    WB --> eff4["Eutrophication and Dead Zones"]
    WB --> eff5["Thermal Stress"]
    WB --> eff6["Sediment Contamination"]
    
    eff1 --> hum["HUMAN HEALTH IMPACT"]

    eff4 --> aqu
    eff5 --> aqu
    eff6 --> aqu
    
    hum --> resp["Disease Burden"]
    hum --> eco["Economic Loss"]
    aqu --> bio["Biodiversity Loss"]
    aqu --> eco
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Eutrophication Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Excess N and P from Fertilizers Sewage Detergents"] --> B["Nutrient Loading to Lake Estuary"]
    B --> C["Rapid Algal Growth Algal Bloom"]
    C --> D["Light Blockage Below Surface"]
    D --> E["Death of Submerged Aquatic Plants"]
    E --> F["Aerobic Bacterial Decomposition of Dead Biomass"]
    F --> G["Severe Dissolved Oxygen Depletion Hypoxia Anoxia"]
    G --> H["Fish Kills and Aquatic Dead Zones"]
    H --> I["Loss of Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Biomagnification Pathway of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1["Water Containing DDT 0.003 ppm"] --> L2["Phytoplankton and Algae 0.04 ppm"]
    L2 --> L3["Zooplankton and Small Fish 0.5 ppm"]
    L3 --> L4["Predator Fish 2.0 ppm"]
    L4 --> L5["Fish Eating Birds 25 ppm"]
    L5 --> L6["Reproductive Failure Eggshell Thinning Population Collapse"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Integrated Wastewater Treatment Train

This block diagram maps the **treatment unit selection logic** based on pollutant category, which is the heart of any pollution-control engineer's decision-making.

```mermaid
graph TD
    influent["Raw Industrial Effluent"] --> screening["Stage 1 Preliminary Screening and Grit Removal"]
    screening --> primary["Stage 2 Primary Clarifier Removal of Suspended Solids"]
    primary --> equal["Equalization Basin pH and Flow Neutralization"]
    equal --> chemical["Stage 3 Chemical Treatment Coagulation Flocculation Precipitation for Heavy Metals"]
    chemical --> biological["Stage 4 Biological Treatment Activated Sludge or Trickling Filter for BOD COD Reduction"]
    biological --> tertiary["Stage 5 Tertiary Treatment Filtration Activated Carbon Nitrification Denitrification Nutrient Removal"]
    tertiary --> dis["Disinfection Chlorination UV or Ozonation"]
    dis --> reuse["Treated Effluent Reuse or Safe Discharge"]
    
    chemical -.-> sludge1["Chemical Sludge to Hazardous Waste Handling"]
    biological -.-> sludge2["Biological Sludge to Digesters"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the term "Biochemical Oxygen Demand" (BOD). Why is it considered an important indicator of water pollution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter present in a water sample under standard conditions of **5 days at 20°C**. It is measured in **mg O₂/L** (or ppm).

**Importance as pollution indicator:**
1. Directly measures the **oxygen-depleting potential** of biodegradable organics — the principal cause of river/lake "death."
2. Quantifies **strength of sewage/industrial wastewater** — used to size biological treatment units (activated sludge aeration tanks, trickling filters).
3. Acts as a **regulatory parameter** under IS 2490 and CPCB norms.
4. BOD₅ of unpolluted river water < **1 mg/L**; domestic sewage ~ **200–400 mg/L**; strong industrial waste > **1000 mg/L**.

*(Word count ~ 80; typical 3-mark answer length)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between BOD and COD. When would COD be preferred over BOD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BOD | COD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Full form | Biochemical Oxygen Demand | Chemical Oxygen Demand |
| Mechanism | Microbial oxidation (5 days) | Chemical oxidation (K₂Cr₂O₇, 2–3 hrs) |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) |
| Time | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Oxygen source | Dissolved O₂ | Strong oxidizing acid (H₂SO₄ + Cr₂O₇²⁻) |

**COD is preferred when:**
- The sample contains **toxic substances** that would kill the microbes used in BOD (e.g., heavy metals, phenols, antibiotics).
- A **rapid result** is needed for process control (within hours vs. 5 days).
- The sample has **high non-biodegradable organic content** (petroleum, pesticides) which BOD would underestimate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Classify the major types of water pollutants with suitable examples. Discuss the sources and effects of **heavy metal pollution** in water bodies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **eutrophication**. Discuss its causes, mechanism, and the consequences on aquatic ecosystems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Classification of Water Pollutants (3 marks):**
Water pollutants are broadly classified into 8 categories:

1. **Biological pollutants** – bacteria (e.g., *E. coli*, *Salmonella typhi*), viruses (Hepatitis A), protozoa (*Giardia*), helminths (roundworms). Source: domestic sewage, hospital waste, animal husbandry.

2. **Biodegradable organic wastes** – sewage, food processing effluent, pulp & paper waste. Measured by BOD/COD. Source: municipal sewage, food industries.

3. **Inorganic chemical pollutants** – heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr, As), acids, alkalis, cyanides, fluorides. Source: electroplating, mining, fertilizer, tanneries.

4. **Synthetic organic compounds** – pesticides (DDT, BHC), PCBs, dioxins, detergents, plastics. Source: agriculture, chemical industries.

5. **Nutrients** – nitrates, phosphates, ammonia. Source: fertilizers, detergents, sewage.

6. **Sediments/suspended solids** – silt, clay, soil. Source: construction, deforestation, agricultural runoff.

7. **Thermal pollutants** – heated cooling water. Source: thermal power plants, nuclear plants, refineries.

8. **Radioactive substances** – U-238, Sr-90, Cs-137, I-131. Source: nuclear plants, hospitals, mining.

**Heavy Metal Pollution — Sources and Effects (4 marks):**

**Sources:**
- **Mining and ore processing** → acid mine drainage carrying dissolved metals.
- **Electroplating industries** → Cr, Ni, Cd, Zn in rinse water.
- **Battery manufacturing** → lead (Pb).
- **Chlor-alkali plants** (mercury cell process) → Hg.
- **Pesticide manufacture** → As, Hg.
- **Tanneries** → Cr⁶⁺ from chrome tanning.
- **Atmospheric deposition** → from coal combustion, smelters.

**Effects:**
1. **Non-biodegradable** — accumulate indefinitely in sediments and organisms.
2. **Bioaccumulation & biomagnification** — e.g., mercury reaches 10⁶× concentration in top predator fish.
3. **Minamata disease** — methyl mercury poisoning in Japan (1956); caused neurological damage, death.
4. **Itai-Itai disease** — cadmium poisoning in Japan; caused kidney failure and bone demineralization.
5. **Lead poisoning** in children → neurodevelopmental disorders, lowered IQ, anaemia.
6. **Chromium (VI)** → carcinogenic (lung cancer), mutagenic, corrosive to aquatic life.
7. **Long biological half-life** — Hg in human body ≈ 70–80 days; Pb ≈ 30 years in bone.
8. **Synergistic toxicity** — combinations (e.g., As + Cd) are more toxic than individual metals.

**Treatment methods (mention briefly):**
- Chemical precipitation as hydroxides/sulphides
- Ion-exchange resins
- Reverse osmosis
- Electrocoagulation
- Adsorption on activated carbon/ biosorbents

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Eutrophication — Explanation (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of a water body with nutrients (especially nitrogen and phosphorus), leading to **accelerated growth of algae and aquatic plants**, followed by oxygen depletion and degradation of water quality.

**Causes (2 marks):**
- **Agricultural runoff** containing NPK fertilizers.
- **Domestic sewage** rich in urea, ammonia, phosphates from detergents.
- **Industrial effluents** from fertilizer plants, food processing.
- **Aquaculture and animal husbandry** waste.
- **Atmospheric deposition** of nitrogen oxides.
- **Septic tank leakage** in rural areas.

**Mechanism (Sequential — 2 marks):**
1. Nutrient influx → rapid algal bloom.
2. Algal mat blocks sunlight → submerged plants cannot photosynthesize → die.
3. Dead biomass sinks → aerobic bacteria decompose it.
4. Bacterial respiration consumes dissolved oxygen → **hypoxia (< 2 mg/L DO)** → anoxia.
5. Anaerobic conditions → H₂S, CH₄ production → foul smell.
6. Fish kills → collapse of food web → biodiversity loss.
7. Repeated cycle forms a "**dead zone**" — e.g., Gulf of Mexico (≈ 22,000 km²).

**Consequences on Aquatic Ecosystems (1 mark):**
- Loss of biodiversity (only pollution-tolerant species survive).
- Economic loss to fisheries.
- Drinking water treatment problems (algal toxins, taste, odour).
- Recreational loss (no swimming, boating).
- Eutrophication reversal requires decades even after nutrient source removal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** What are **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)**? Explain with two examples their sources, persistence, and **biomagnification** in the food chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the **sources, mechanism, and consequences of thermal pollution** of water. Discuss any **two cooling methods** to control it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Definition (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** are organic chemical substances that:
> 1. Remain intact in the environment for **long periods** (resistant to chemical, biological, and photolytic degradation).
> 2. Are **lipophilic** (fat-soluble) → bioaccumulate in fatty tissues.
> 3. Undergo **long-range transport** via air/water across continents.
> 4. Are **highly toxic** to humans and wildlife (endocrine disruption, carcinogenicity).

**Examples — DDT and PCBs (5 marks):**

**DDT (Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane):**
- **Source:** Used extensively as agricultural insecticide post-WWII; also in malaria control.
- **Persistence:** Half-life in soil ≈ **2–15 years**; in water, decades.
- **Biomagnification in food chain:**
  - Water: 0.003 ppm
  - Zooplankton: 0.04 ppm
  - Small fish: 0.5 ppm
  - Large fish: 2.0 ppm
  - Fish-eating birds (e.g., bald eagle): 25 ppm
  - **Magnification factor ≈ 8000×**
- **Effect:** Eggshell thinning in raptors → reproductive failure → near-extinction of bald eagle, peregrine falcon. Banned in 1972 (US) and 2004 (Stockholm Convention globally).

**PCBs (Polychlorinated Biphenyls):**
- **Source:** Used as dielectric fluids in transformers/capacitors, hydraulic fluids, plasticizers. Released via leaks, improper disposal.
- **Persistence:** Half-life **> 10 years in soil/sediment**.
- **Biomagnification:** Magnification factor up to **10⁶×** from water to top predator.
- **Effect:** Endocrine disruption, immune suppression, carcinogen (Yusho disease in Japan, 1968; rice oil contaminated with PCBs).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Thermal Pollution — Concept (1.5 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Thermal pollution is the degradation of water quality by any process that changes the **ambient temperature** of a water body. Typically caused by industrial facilities using water as a **cooling medium** and returning it at a higher temperature.

**Sources (1.5 marks):**
- Thermal and nuclear power plants (largest single source — ~ 80% of cooling water discharge).
- Petroleum refineries.
- Steel mills and metallurgical plants.
- Pulp and paper mills.
- Coal-fired power plants.
- Desalination plants (reject brine is also hot).

**Mechanism of Harm (1.5 marks):**
1. **Reduced DO solubility** — warm water holds less oxygen.
   $$DO_{sat}(30°C) \approx 7.6 \text{ mg/L}$$
   $$DO_{sat}(10°C) \approx 11.3 \text{ mg/L}$$
   → A 20°C rise reduces DO capacity by ~33%.
2. **Increased metabolic rate** of fish → higher oxygen demand at the very moment supply drops → asphyxiation.
3. **Disruption of reproductive cycles** — many fish spawn in temperature-specific seasons; thermal shifts cause reproductive failure.
4. **Invasion of warm-water species** displacing native cold-water species → biodiversity loss.
5. **Acceleration of chemical reactions** → increased toxicity of pollutants (e.g., ammonia becomes more toxic with temperature).

**Consequences (1 mark):**
- Massive fish kills (e.g., once common downstream of thermal plants).
- Loss of cold-water fisheries (salmon, trout).
- Ecosystem simplification to pollution-tolerant species.
- Enhanced algal growth (compounds with nutrient pollution).

**Two Cooling Methods (1.5 marks):**

**1. Cooling Towers (Wet/Dry):**
- **Wet cooling tower:** Heated water is sprayed over packing; counter-current air evaporates a portion (~2–3% by mass), removing latent heat. Water is recirculated back to the condenser.
- **Dry cooling tower:** Air-cooled heat exchangers; no evaporation; lower efficiency but no water loss.
- Reduces inlet-outlet ΔT from ~ 10°C (direct discharge) to < 3°C.

**2. Cooling Ponds / Spray Ponds:**
- Heated water held in large surface ponds; heat dissipated by **convection, evaporation, and radiation**.
- Simpler and cheaper than towers but requires large land area (≈ 1 hectare per MW for typical plant).
- Effective in hot, dry climates.

*(Other valid methods: once-through cooling with diffuser design, multiport diffusers in deep water.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating BOD and COD** — many students treat them as identical. They are NOT; understand the *biodegradable vs. total* distinction clearly.
> 2. **Wrong hardness factor** — using atomic weight instead of equivalent weight; using 2.5 vs. 4.12. Always show the derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the 5-day, 20°C standard** in BOD definition — **direct 1-mark cut** if omitted.
> 4. **Confusing bioaccumulation and biomagnification** — they are NOT synonyms. Bioaccumulation is in *one organism over time*; biomagnification is *across trophic levels*.
> 5. **Missing examples** — never write "heavy metals" without naming Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr. Examiners expect at least 2 specific examples per category.
> 6. **Numerical errors in CaCO₃ equivalent** — don't forget the valence: hardness factor for Ca = 100/(40) = 2.5, not 100/20.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in thermal pollution questions** — even a simple cooling-tower sketch gets full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Water Pollutants**

- ✅ **Eight pollutant classes:** Pathogens, Biodegradable organics, Inorganic chemicals, Synthetic organics, Nutrients, Sediments, Thermal, Radioactive.
- ✅ **BOD definition** must include "**5 days, 20°C**" and units **mg O₂/L**.
- ✅ **COD vs. BOD:** COD ≥ BOD always; COD = BOD + (non-biodegradable organics).
- ✅ **BOD₅ = 0.68 L₀**; **L₀ = 1.46 × BOD₅** — standard conversion.
- ✅ **Hardness factor:** Ca = **2.5**, Mg = **4.12** (multiplied by mg/L concentration).
- ✅ **Biomagnification:** DDT magnified **~8000×** from water to fish-eating birds.
- ✅ **Eutrophication** = nutrient-driven algal bloom → DO depletion → dead zones.
- ✅ **Thermal pollution** reduces DO; cooling towers and cooling ponds are key control methods.
- ✅ **Heavy metals are non-biodegradable** and undergo biomagnification; specific diseases: **Minamata (Hg)**, **Itai-Itai (Cd)**.
- ✅ **Indicator organism** for faecal contamination = ***E. coli*** (faecal coliform).
- ✅ **Indian regulatory standards:** IS 2490 (industrial effluents), IS 10500 (drinking water).
- ✅ **Stockholm Convention (2001, enforced 2004):** Global treaty to eliminate 12 initial POPs ("dirty dozen") — DDT, PCBs, dioxins, etc.
- ✅ **Permissible BOD for discharge:** ≤ **30 mg/L** (inland surface water); COD ≤ **250 mg/L**.
- ✅ **pH of natural water:** 6.5–8.5; industrial effluent before discharge: **5.5–9.0**.
- ✅ **DO saturation formula** is a favourite — memorize the cubic polynomial.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OECHT613 Pollution Control in Process Industries (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Treatment Methods (Brief Overview - Diagram, Principle and Working of Equipment Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Treatment Methods in Process Industries

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT613), **Treatment Methods** refer to the systematic physical, chemical, and biological unit operations employed to remove, transform, or neutralize pollutants from industrial effluents (liquid, gaseous, and solid streams) before their safe discharge into the environment. These methods form the engineering backbone of **End-of-Pipe (EOP) pollution control** and are designed to comply with statutory discharge standards such as those prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module introduces students to the **unit operations and unit processes** that constitute a complete Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) or Air Pollution Control (APC) system. The treatment train is always designed as a **sequence of stages** — preliminary → primary → secondary → tertiary.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Treatment Methods

Pollution control treatment methods are broadly classified based on the **phase of the pollutant** and the **underlying separation mechanism**.

| Stream Type | Primary Treatment Methods | Secondary Treatment Methods | Tertiary / Polishing |
|-------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **Liquid (Wastewater)** | Screening, Grit removal, Sedimentation | Activated Sludge, Trickling Filter, Anaerobic Digestion | Reverse Osmosis, Adsorption, Ion Exchange |
| **Gaseous (Flue Gas)** | Cyclone Separators, Settling Chambers | Scrubbers, ESP, Bag Filters | Catalytic Converters, Biofilters |
| **Solid (Sludge/Waste)** | Thickening, Dewatering | Stabilization, Composting | Incineration, Landfilling |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Water Filter" Analogy:** Think of a treatment plant exactly like the **multi-stage water purifier** you have at home.
> - The **mesh screen** at the inlet is the *preliminary stage* (removes large debris).
> - The **sediment catcher** is the *primary stage* (lets heavy particles settle).
> - The **activated charcoal layer** is the *secondary stage* (biological/chemical action).
> - The **UV lamp or RO membrane** is the *tertiary stage* (polishing).
>
> An industrial treatment plant is simply a **giant, engineered, and instrumented version** of this same multi-stage logic — handling flows of thousands of cubic meters per day.

## 1.4 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering parameters govern the design of treatment equipment:

- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $\theta = V/Q$ where $V$ is reactor volume (m³) and $Q$ is flow rate (m³/h). Standard HRT ranges from **2–24 hours** for biological reactors.
- **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $v_o = Q/A$ where $A$ is the surface area of the settling tank. Typical values: **24–48 m³/m²/day** for primary clarifiers.
- **Removal Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ where $C$ is the pollutant concentration.
- **Pressure Drop (ΔP):** Critical parameter for air pollution control devices; must remain within **0.5–2.5 kPa** for efficient operation without excessive fan power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Unit Convention:** Concentrations are universally reported as **mg/L** (liquid) or **mg/Nm³** (gas at Normal conditions: 1 atm, 273.15 K).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Preliminary Treatment Methods

Preliminary treatment removes **large suspended solids, grit, oil & grease** that could damage downstream equipment. It is purely a **physical** stage.

### 2.1.1 Bar Screens
- **Principle:** Size exclusion — wastewater passes through parallel bars; solids larger than the bar spacing are retained.
- **Types:** *Manually cleaned* (bar spacing 25–50 mm) and *mechanically cleaned* (bar spacing 6–25 mm).
- **Velocity Criterion:** Approach velocity must be between **0.6–1.2 m/s** to prevent grit carryover and screen blinding.

### 2.1.2 Grit Chambers
- **Principle:** Gravity settling based on **Stokes' Law** — heavy inorganic particles (specific gravity ≈ 2.65) settle while organic matter remains suspended.
- **Types:** *Horizontal flow* (rectangular) and *Aerated* (spiral roll pattern induced by diffused air).
- **Design Criterion:** Detention time of **45–90 seconds**.

### 2.1.3 Oil & Grease Skimmers
- **Principle:** Density-based separation (oil density < water density).
- **Mechanism:** Air flotation introduces fine bubbles that attach to oil droplets, accelerating their rise to the surface.

## 2.2 Primary Treatment Methods

### 2.2.1 Primary Sedimentation / Clarifiers
- **Principle:** Quiescent settling under low horizontal velocity ($v_h < 0.3$ m/min).
- **Equipment:** Circular (radial flow) or rectangular (horizontal flow) tanks fitted with **rotating scrapers**.
- **Removal:** 50–70% of suspended solids (SS); 25–40% of BOD.

### 2.2.2 Coagulation–Flocculation Tanks
- **Principle:** Destabilization of colloidal particles by addition of electrolytes (e.g., **Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O]**, **Ferric Chloride [FeCl₃]**, or **Poly-Aluminium Chloride [PAC]**).
- **Mechanism:** Charge neutralization → microflocs → bridging flocculation → settleable macroflocs.

## 2.3 Secondary (Biological) Treatment Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Secondary treatment exploits **microbial metabolism** to convert dissolved organic matter into biomass (sludge) and end-products (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄). It is the heart of any ETP.

### 2.3.1 Activated Sludge Process (ASP)
- **Principle:** Suspended-growth aerobic process. Microbes form **flocs** that consume dissolved organics in the presence of dissolved oxygen (DO > 2 mg/L).
- **Key Equipment:** Aeration tank, **diffused aerator** or **mechanical surface aerator**, and a **Secondary Clarifier** for sludge recycle.
- **F/M Ratio (Food to Microorganism Ratio):** Controls process stability.

### 2.3.2 Trickling Filter
- **Principle:** Attached-growth aerobic process. Wastewater trickles over a **bed of rock/plastic media** coated with a **biofilm**.
- **Mechanism:** Organic matter diffuses into the biofilm → microbial oxidation → sloughing of excess biomass.

### 2.3.3 Anaerobic Digester / Biogas Plant
- **Principle:** Oxygen-free degradation by **acidogenic** and **methanogenic** archaea.
- **End Products:** Biogas (≈ 60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂) and stabilized sludge.

## 2.4 Air Pollution Control (APC) Equipment

| Equipment | Principle | Typical Efficiency | Key Application |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Cyclone Separator** | Centrifugal force (inertial separation) | 70–90% (for > 10 µm) | Pre-cleaner in process industries |
| **Wet Scrubber** | Gas-liquid mass transfer (absorption) | 80–95% | SO₂, HCl, particulate removal |
| **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | Corona discharge → particle charging → collection on electrodes | 99%+ | Coal-fired boilers, cement kilns |
| **Bag House / Fabric Filter** | Surface and depth filtration through fabric | 99%+ | Fine particulates, pharmaceutical dust |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\theta = V/Q$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | hours |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Removal Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $v_o = Q/A$ | Surface Overflow Rate | m³/m²·day |
| 4 | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18\mu}$ | Stokes' Settling Velocity (laminar) | m/s |
| 5 | $F/M = \dfrac{S_o}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food to Microorganism Ratio | day⁻¹ (0.2–0.5 typical) |
| 6 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | Sludge Volume Index (settleability) | mL/g (50–150 good) |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot v^2}{2g \cdot D}$ | Pressure drop (ducting) | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Fan/pump power for APC | kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the **Stokes' Law assumption** — laminar flow, spherical particle, $Re_p < 1$. Outside this range, use the **Newton's Law regime** (turbulent settling).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** API separators → DAF → ASP → Multimedia filters.
- **Dye & Textile Industries:** Equalization → Coagulation → Electrocoagulation → Activated Carbon adsorption.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** MBR (Membrane Bioreactor) → RO → UV disinfection.
- **Cement & Steel:** Cyclone → ESP → Bag House for particulate control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)

The terminal settling velocity is the single most important parameter in the design of **clarifiers, grit chambers, and cyclones**.

**Starting Force Balance** on a spherical particle in a viscous fluid at terminal velocity (zero net force):

$$F_{gravity} = F_{buoyancy} + F_{drag}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the forces.**

$$F_{gravity} = \rho_p \cdot V_p \cdot g = \rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g$$

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g$$

$$F_{drag} = 3\pi \mu d_p v_s \quad \text{(Stokes' drag, } Re_p < 1 \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the force balance.**

$$\rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g = \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot g + 3\pi \mu d_p v_s$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $v_s$.**

$$\dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_w) g = 3\pi \mu d_p v_s$$

$$v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_w) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form.**

$$\boxed{v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18 \mu}}$$

**Interpretation of the Result:**
- Settling velocity is **proportional to the square of particle diameter** → halving the particle size reduces settling velocity by 4×.
- Settling velocity is **inversely proportional to viscosity** → cold wastewater (higher $\mu$) settles slower.
- It is valid only in the **laminar regime** ($Re_p < 1$). For larger particles, transition to Newton's law.

## 3.2 Derivation: Removal Efficiency of a Cyclone Separator

Cyclones remove particulates using centrifugal force. The **theoretical cut-size diameter ($d_{50}$)** is defined as the particle size collected with **50% efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Radial (centrifugal) velocity at radius $r$.**

$$v_r = \dfrac{Q}{A_{in}} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r h}$$

**Step 2 — Settling distance in radial direction** (time to reach the wall).

$$t = \dfrac{B}{v_r} = \dfrac{2\pi r h B}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal settling velocity in radial direction** (driven by centrifugal acceleration $v_t^2/r$).

$$v_{s,radial} = \dfrac{d_{50}^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) v_t^2}{18 \mu r}$$

**Step 4 — Equate radial settling distance to travel width $B$** at terminal velocity.

$$v_{s,radial} \cdot t = B$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the cut-size expression.**

$$\boxed{d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_t (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}}$$

Where:
- $B$ = cyclone inlet width (m)
- $N_e$ = number of effective turns (typically 5)
- $v_t$ = tangential inlet velocity (m/s)
- $\rho_g$ = gas density (kg/m³)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Treatment Method vs. Pollutant Type

| Pollutant Type | Particle Size / Nature | Recommended Method | Equipment Used |
|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Large debris | > 25 mm | Screening | Bar screens |
| Suspended solids | 0.1–10 mm | Gravity settling | Primary clarifier |
| Colloidal matter | 0.001–0.1 µm | Coagulation-flocculation | Flash mixer, flocculator |
| Dissolved organics | Molecular | Biological oxidation | ASP, Trickling filter |
| Dissolved inorganics | Ionic | Chemical precipitation | Lime soda softener |
| Oil & grease | Free floating | Skimming, DAF | API separator, DAF unit |
| Volatile organics | Gaseous | Air stripping, adsorption | Stripping tower, AC column |
| Fine particulates (< 10 µm) | Solid in gas | ESP / Bag filter | Electrostatic precipitator |
| Acidic/Basic gases | Gaseous | Absorption | Packed-bed scrubber |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
