# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Briefly explain the principle and working of a cyclone separator with the help of a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Principle (1.5 Marks):** A cyclone separator operates on the principle of **inertial / centrifugal separation**. When a dust-laden gas stream is introduced **tangentially** into a cylindrical-conical vessel, it acquires a spinning motion. The heavier dust particles, due to their higher inertia, are thrown outwards to the wall, slide down the cone, and are collected in the hopper. The cleaned gas reverses direction and escapes through the central vortex finder.
- **Working (1 Mark):** The tangential entry imparts a helical downward vortex for particulates and an upward inner vortex for cleaned gas.
- **Diagram (0.5 Mark):** A simple block or schematic showing tangential inlet, conical body, dust outlet, and clean gas outlet via vortex finder.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**List any three differences between Activated Sludge Process and Trickling Filter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Activated Sludge Process | Trickling Filter |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Biomass form | Suspended flocs (mixed liquor) | Attached biofilm on media |
| Oxygen supply | Mechanical/diffused aeration | Natural draft or forced air |
| Land footprint | Smaller (high rate) | Larger (low rate) |
| Energy demand | High (blowers) | Low |
| Sludge characteristics | More, requires constant WAS | Less, sloughs naturally |
| Sensitivity to shock loads | Higher | Lower |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[Choose A or B]*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the principle, construction, and working of an **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**. Mention its typical efficiency and industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** in detail. Define the F/M ratio, MLSS, and SVI, and state their typical values. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Electrostatic Precipitator (7 Marks):**

- **Principle (2 Marks):** An ESP operates on the principle of **corona discharge** and **electrostatic attraction**. A high negative DC voltage (30–100 kV) is applied between **discharge electrodes** (thin wires) and **collecting electrodes** (flat plates). This ionizes the gas, producing electrons and negative ions that attach to dust particles, charging them negatively. The negatively charged particles migrate towards the positively charged collection plates, where they are neutralized and collected.
- **Construction (2 Marks):**
  - Discharge electrode system (frame of wires).
  - Collection electrodes (perforated plates or tubes).
  - High-voltage rectifier set.
  - Rapping mechanism for electrode cleaning.
  - Hoppers at the bottom for collected dust.
- **Working (2 Marks):** Dirty gas enters the inlet, gets charged in the ionizing section, particulates drift to collection plates, and rapping dislodges the dust into hoppers. Clean gas exits through the outlet.
- **Efficiency & Application (1 Mark):** Efficiency > **99%**, with outlet dust load often < **50 mg/Nm³**. Widely used in **thermal power plants, cement kilns, and steel plants**.

**(b) Activated Sludge Process (7 Marks):**

- **Principle (2 Marks):** ASP is a **suspended-growth, aerobic biological treatment** process. Dissolved organic matter in wastewater is metabolized by a mixed microbial culture (activated sludge) in the presence of dissolved oxygen. The biomass is separated in a secondary clarifier and a portion is **recycled** to maintain the required MLSS.
- **Key Parameters (3 Marks):**
  - **F/M Ratio:** $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{S_o}{\theta \cdot X}$, typical value **0.2–0.5 day⁻¹** for conventional ASP.
  - **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** Concentration of biomass in the aeration tank, typical value **2000–4000 mg/L**.
  - **SVI (Sludge Volume Index):** $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS}}$; values of **50–150 mL/g** indicate good settleability; SVI > 200 indicates **bulking sludge**.
- **Process Flow (2 Marks):** Influent → Aeration Tank (with diffused air) → Secondary Clarifier → Effluent. Sludge from clarifier is partly recycled (RAS) and partly wasted (WAS).

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[Alternative Choice]*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP 2024]**
**(a)** Describe with a diagram the **working of a wet scrubber** for gaseous pollutant control. Discuss any one industrial application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **coagulation and flocculation** as a primary chemical treatment method. List the common coagulants used and state the role of pH. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Wet Scrubber (7 Marks):**

- **Principle (2 Marks):** A wet scrubber uses **gas-liquid mass transfer (absorption)** to remove gaseous pollutants or fine particulates. The dirty gas is brought into intimate contact with a liquid (usually water or a reactive solution) in a counter-current or cross-flow pattern.
- **Types & Construction (2 Marks):**
  - **Packed-bed scrubber** (Pallas/Impingement type).
  - **Venturi scrubber** (for fine particulates).
  - Components: Spray nozzles, packing material (Pall rings, Intalox saddles), mist eliminator, recirculation pump.
- **Working (2 Marks):** Gas enters at the bottom, rises through the packed bed, contacts the descending liquid film; pollutants dissolve/react with the liquid. The scrubbed gas passes through a mist eliminator before exiting the stack. The spent liquor is sent to the ETP.
- **Application (1 Mark):** Removal of **SO₂ from flue gas** using limestone slurry (Flue Gas Desulfurization – FGD).

**(b) Coagulation and Flocculation (7 Marks):**

- **Principle (2 Marks):**
  - **Coagulation:** Rapid addition of a chemical coagulant to **destabilize colloidal particles** by neutralizing their surface charge (zeta potential).
  - **Flocculation:** Slow, gentle mixing to allow destabilized particles to **collide and agglomerate** into large settleable flocs.
- **Equipment (2 Marks):**
  - **Flash Mixer:** Detention time **30–60 seconds** at high G (velocity gradient, 700–1000 s⁻¹).
  - **Flocculator:** Slow mixing, G = 20–75 s⁻¹, detention time **20–40 minutes**.
- **Common Coagulants (2 Marks):**
  - **Alum** [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O] — dose 5–150 mg/L.
  - **Ferric Chloride** [FeCl₃].
  - **Poly-Aluminium Chloride (PAC)**.
  - **Lime** [Ca(OH)₂] — used when alkalinity is insufficient.
- **Role of pH (1 Mark):** Each coagulant has an **optimum pH** range. For alum, it is **6.5–7.5**; for ferric chloride, **6.0–9.0**. Outside this range, coagulation efficiency drops sharply.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the working of a cyclone (centrifugal) with a wet scrubber (absorption). Examiners frequently allot 1–2 marks specifically for the *correct identification of the separation principle*.
> 2. **Units matter:** Always quote HRT in *hours*, SOR in *m³/m²·day*, SVI in *mL/g*, and efficiency in *%*. Wrong units = mark deduction.
> 3. **Diagrams must be labelled.** A block/schematic diagram without labelled components (inlet, outlet, electrodes, packing, nozzles) fetches only partial marks.
> 4. **Biological process questions** demand definitions of F/M, MLSS, MLVSS, SVI, and SV₃₀ — knowing only the *concept* is insufficient; quote numerical ranges.
> 5. **Stokes' Law derivations** must include the *assumption statement* ($Re_p < 1$, spherical particle) to get full marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Treatment Train:** Always follow the hierarchy **Preliminary → Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Sludge Handling**.
- **Physical Methods** rely on *gravity, inertia, filtration, and flotation*; they do NOT alter the chemical nature of the pollutant.
- **Chemical Methods** rely on *pH adjustment, precipitation, oxidation, and reduction*.
- **Biological Methods** rely on *microbial metabolism* (aerobic or anaerobic) and are temperature/pH sensitive.
- **Air Pollution Equipment Selection** is driven by particle size: > 10 µm → Cyclone; < 10 µm → Scrubber/ESP/Baghouse.
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ — valid only for $Re_p < 1$.
- **F/M Ratio** in ASP: 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹ (conventional); higher for high-rate systems.
- **SVI:** 50–150 mL/g = good; > 200 mL/g = bulking.
- **Cyclone cut-size ($d_{50}$):** Inversely proportional to $\sqrt{v_t}$ — increasing inlet velocity reduces cut-size.
- **ESP efficiency:** 99%+ for fine particulates, but high capital cost and sensitivity to dust resistivity (10⁴–10¹⁰ Ω·cm optimal).
- **Wet scrubbers** can simultaneously remove gases AND particulates — a key advantage over ESPs.
- **Coagulant dose & pH** must be optimized; jar tests are used to determine the optimum dose in lab.
- **Trickling filter** is robust against hydraulic shock loads — preferred for small/medium industries.
- **Anaerobic digesters** produce biogas (energy recovery) — an emerging area in sustainable waste management.
- **Sludge treatment** is as important as wastewater treatment — improper sludge disposal re-creates pollution.
- **KTU 2024 OBE Tag:** This topic maps to **CO1 (Understand the sources and characteristics of industrial pollutants)** and **CO2 (Apply treatment methods for pollution control)**.
- **Bloom's Level:** Primarily **Understand (L2)** and **Apply (L3)**; derivations of Stokes' Law and removal efficiency fall under **Analyze (L4)**.
- **Always draw a labelled diagram** — even in 3-mark questions, a diagram earns you a 'bonus' mark.
- **Recall units** with every numerical: mg/L, m³/h, %, m/s, kPa, kW.
- **Distinguish "principle" vs. "working"** in answers — *principle* = underlying physics/chemistry, *working* = step-by-step operation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Treatment Methods: Screening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Treatment Methods: Screening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Screening** is a primary physical unit operation in wastewater engineering that employs openings of specific, predetermined sizes to remove coarse, floating, suspended, and settleable solids from a flowing liquid stream. It functions as the **first line of defense** in a sequence of treatment operations, protecting downstream processes (pumps, pipes, biological reactors, and membranes) from clogging, abrasion, and operational disruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Pretreatment & Primary Treatment):**
> Screening is classified under **Physical Treatment Methods** and is universally positioned *upstream* of grit removal, flow measurement, and primary sedimentation. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish between **bar screens**, **fine screens**, and **microscreens** based on aperture size, cleaning mechanism, and design hydraulics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Aperture Classification (As per Metcalf & Eddy / KTU accepted practice):**
> - **Coarse Screens (Bar Racks):** Opening ≥ **6 mm** (typically 6–150 mm)
> - **Medium Screens:** Opening **2–6 mm**
> - **Fine Screens:** Opening **0.25–2 mm**
> - **Microscreens:** Opening **< 0.25 mm** (often fabric or woven mesh)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine placing a **kitchen colander** over the mouth of a sink drain. When you pour cooked rice and water into it, the rice stays in the colander while the water drains out. **Screening is the engineering-scale, industrial equivalent of this exact process.** Instead of rice and water, we are separating *large solid debris* (rags, sticks, plastic, leaves, bricks) from *the liquid wastewater stream*. The screen acts as a physical barrier — solids larger than the openings are retained; smaller particles and water pass through.

## 1.3 Why is Screening Indispensable?

- Protects **pumps, valves, and pipelines** from fouling and mechanical damage.
- Reduces **BOD and TSS** by 5–20% even before biological treatment.
- Prevents **ragging and scum accumulation** in downstream clarifiers.
- Enables **better hydraulic control** and uniform distribution across treatment units.

---

## 1.4 Bar Shapes and Screen Geometries

Bar screens are constructed from **rectangular, square, or circular cross-section bars**. The **hydraulic and clogging behavior** is heavily dependent on this geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Bar Cross-Sections Used in KTU-aligned Design Problems:**
> - **Rectangular bars (sharp-edged):** Most common, easily fabricated.
> - **Circular bars:** Less prone to clogging, lower headloss coefficient.
> - **Tapered / Streamlined bars:** Used in **mechanically cleaned fine screens** to minimize headloss.

| Parameter | Typical Value (Coarse Bar Screen) | Typical Value (Fine Screen) |
|---|---|---|
| Bar Width, $b$ | 5–15 mm | 1–3 mm |
| Clear Spacing, $w$ | 15–75 mm | 0.25–6 mm |
| Screen Inclination, $\theta$ | 30°–60° from horizontal | 0°–45° |
| Approach Velocity, $v_a$ | 0.3–0.6 m/s | 0.3–0.6 m/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Flow (Step-by-Step)

The screening process unfolds in a strict, sequential order:

1. **Influent Entry:** Raw wastewater enters the screen channel with velocity $v_a$ (approach velocity).
2. **Flow Confinement:** The flow passes between vertical or inclined bars, where solids larger than the clear opening $w$ are intercepted.
3. **Solids Accumulation (Blinding):** Captured solids form a *mat* on the screen, gradually reducing open area $A_{net}$.
4. **Headloss Build-up:** As open area decreases, velocity through openings ($v$) increases, raising headloss $h$.
5. **Cleaning Operation:** Either manual raking (small plants) or mechanical scrapers/bands (large plants) remove accumulated solids.
6. **Screenings Disposal:** Removed solids are dewatered, washed, and disposed (landfill, incineration, or composting).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Engineering Constraints

- **Why is approach velocity limited to 0.3–0.6 m/s?**
  * If $v_a > 0.6$ m/s → solids are *pushed through* the screen, defeating its purpose.
  * If $v_a < 0.3$ m/s → grit and heavy particles settle in the channel, causing blockages.

- **Why does headloss matter?**
  * The upstream water level rises as headloss increases. Excessive headloss means **channel flooding** and **operational failure**.

- **Why are screens inclined?**
  * Inclination facilitates **self-cleaning by gravity** and easier mechanical scraping.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU Module 1 numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{net} = N \cdot w \cdot d$ | Net submerged open area; $N$ = number of openings, $w$ = clear width, $d$ = submerged depth | m² |
| 2 | $A_{gross} = B \cdot d$ | Gross submerged area; $B$ = channel width, $d$ = submerged depth | m² |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{A_{net}}$ | Velocity **through** the screen opening | m/s |
| 4 | $v_a = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{A_{gross}}$ | **Approach velocity** in the channel | m/s |
| 5 | $h = \beta \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \sin\theta$ | **Headloss through clean, inclined bar screen**; $\beta$ = bar shape factor, $b$ = bar width, $\theta$ = inclination | m |
| 6 | $h_{\text{clogged}} = h \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_{gross}}{A_{net,\ clgged}}\right)^{2}$ | Headloss correction when screen is partially clogged | m |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{B - (N+1)b}{w + b}$ rearranged to $N = \dfrac{B - b}{w + b}$ | Approximate number of bars | – |
| 8 | $v_{\text{through bar}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{design}}}{A_{net}}$ | Discharges velocity check (must be < 1.0 m/s for coarse) | m/s |
| 9 | $\beta_{\text{sharp}} = 2.42$, $\beta_{\text{round}} = 1.83$, $\beta_{\text{rect}} = 1.79$ | Bar shape coefficients (Kirschmer's equation) | – |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Screening is the **gateway unit** in:
- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** – before grit chambers and primary clarifiers.
- **Industrial Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)** – textile, tannery, pulp & paper, food processing.
- **Drinking Water Treatment Plants (WTPs)** – as *intake screens* on raw water pumps.
- **Stormwater Management Systems** – for combined sewer overflows (CSOs).

In **process industries** (the focus of OECHT613), screening protects high-value equipment such as **heat exchangers, RO membranes, bioreactor agitators, and clarifier scrapers** from abrasive and fibrous materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Headloss Through a Clean Bar Screen (Kirschmer's Equation)

The classical headloss equation is derived by balancing **kinetic energy dissipation** through the bar geometry with the **energy loss** associated with flow contraction and expansion.

**Step 1: Energy Loss Conceptualization**
As flow approaches the bar, streamlines contract, accelerate through the opening, and re-expand on the downstream side. Energy is dissipated due to:
- **Form drag** of the bars.
- **Sudden contraction and expansion** of the flow area.

**Step 2: Velocity Component Normal to Screen**
The velocity component perpendicular to the screen face is $v \cdot \sin\theta$ (since flow is along the channel and the screen is inclined at $\theta$).

**Step 3: Empirical Coefficient (Kirschmer)**
Kirschmer (1926) proposed an empirical relationship combining:
- The bar shape factor $\beta$.
- A dimensionless width ratio $\left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3}$.

**Step 4: Final Assembled Equation**

$$
h = \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

> Where:
> - $h$ = headloss across screen (m)
> - $\beta$ = bar shape factor (dimensionless)
> - $w$ = clear spacing between bars (m)
> - $b$ = bar width (m)
> - $v$ = velocity through bar openings (m/s)
> $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
> $\theta$ = angle of inclination from horizontal (degrees)

**Step 5: Headloss Under Partial Clogging**
When the screen accumulates screenings, the effective open area shrinks. The headloss scales with the **square** of the area ratio:

$$
h_{\text{clogged}} = h \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_{\text{gross}}}{A_{\text{net,\,clogged}}}\right)^{2}
$$

This is why **timely cleaning** is critical — a 50% reduction in net area causes a **4× increase in headloss**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A bar screen has the following data: bar width $b = 10$ mm, clear spacing $w = 25$ mm, channel width $B = 0.9$ m, submerged depth $d = 0.5$ m, flow $Q = 0.10$ m³/s, screen inclination $\theta = 60°$, bar shape rectangular ($\beta = 1.79$). Calculate the headloss through the **clean screen**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the number of bars and openings.**

$$
N = \dfrac{B - b}{w + b} = \dfrac{0.9 - 0.010}{0.025 + 0.010} = \dfrac{0.890}{0.035} \approx 25.43
$$

Take $N = 25$ bars (round down for conservative design).

**Step 2: Compute net open area.**

$$
A_{\text{net}} = N \cdot w \cdot d = 25 \times 0.025 \times 0.5 = 0.3125 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute velocity through the screen openings.**

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.3125} = 0.32 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute gross area and approach velocity (for verification only).**

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = B \cdot d = 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
v_a = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{gross}}} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.45} \approx 0.222 \ \text{m/s}
$$

This is **within the acceptable range (0.3–0.6 m/s is ideal; lower is acceptable for coarse screens).**

**Step 5: Apply Kirschmer's headloss equation.**

$$
h = \beta \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Computing the width ratio term:

$$
\dfrac{w}{b} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.010} = 2.5
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} = 2.5^{1.3333} \approx 3.39
$$

Computing the velocity head:

$$
\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{(0.32)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{0.1024}{19.62} \approx 0.00522 \ \text{m}
$$

Computing $\sin 60° \approx 0.866$.

Substituting:

$$
h = 1.79 \times 3.39 \times 0.00522 \times 0.866
$$

$$
h \approx 0.0275 \ \text{m} \approx 27.5 \ \text{mm}
$$

### Final Answer:
**Headloss through clean screen, $h \approx 27.5$ mm.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Design Matrix — Coarse vs. Fine Screens

| Design Parameter | Coarse Bar Screen | Fine Screen |
|---|---|---|
| Opening size | 6–150 mm | 0.25–6 mm |
| Cleaning | Manual / Mechanical | **Always** Mechanical |
| Headloss expected | 50–150 mm | 100–500 mm |
| Approach velocity | 0.3–0.6 m/s | 0.3–0.6 m/s |
| Through-velocity | < 0.9 m/s | < 1.2 m/s |
| Solids removed | Rags, sticks, large debris | Finer particulates, fibers |
| Typical location | Plant headworks | After grit removal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Screening System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Influent Wastewater"] --> B["Inlet Channel with Parshall Flume"]
    B --> C["Approach Velocity Control Section"]
    C --> D["Bar Screen Unit Inclined at theta Degrees"]
    D --> E["Mechanical or Manual Cleaning Assembly"]
    E --> F["Screenings Conveyor and Dewatering Press"]
    F --> G["Disposal: Landfill or Incinerator"]
    D --> H["Treated Effluent to Grit Chamber"]
    H --> I["Primary Clarifier"]
    I --> J["Biological Treatment Stage"]
    J --> K["Tertiary and Disinfection Stage"]
    K --> L["Final Discharge to Environment"]
    D --> M["Headloss Sensor and Monitoring"]
    M --> N["SCADA Control and Auto Cleaning Trigger"]
    N --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Screen Cleaning Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Cleaning Cycle"] --> S2["Read Upstream Water Level Sensor"]
    S2 --> S3{"Headloss Exceeds Threshold h limit"}
    S3 -- "No" --> S4["Return to Standby Mode"]
    S3 -- "Yes" --> S5["Activate Mechanical Scraper or Backwash Pump"]
    S5 --> S6["Convey Screenings to Compaction Unit"]
    S6 --> S7["Reset Timer and Headloss Counter"]
    S7 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Section Schematic: Bar Screen Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Channel Bed"] --> P2["Bar Width b"]
    P2 --> P3["Clear Spacing w"]
    P3 --> P4["Inclination Angle theta from Horizontal"]
    P4 --> P5["Upstream Water Level"]
    P5 --> P6["Downstream Water Level"]
    P6 --> P7["Headloss h equals difference of levels"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Notes on the Diagrams

- The **first diagram** maps the entire screening train from raw influent to disposal, illustrating its position as the **headworks unit**.
- The **second diagram** captures the **closed-loop control** philosophy: headloss triggers automatic cleaning — a hallmark of **modern STP automation**.
- The **third diagram** visualizes the geometric variables ($b$, $w$, $\theta$, $h$) used in the Kirschmer equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "draw a typical bar screen," a labeled cross-section showing bar width, clear spacing, and the headloss differential **always scores full marks** on a 7-mark descriptive question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between coarse screens and fine screens based on aperture size, cleaning mechanism, and typical removal efficiency.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

- **Coarse screens** have openings ≥ 6 mm (commonly 6–150 mm), are placed at the headworks, and may be **manually or mechanically cleaned**. They remove large debris such as rags, sticks, plastics, and bricks.
- **Fine screens** have openings between 0.25 mm and 6 mm, are always **mechanically cleaned** (due to frequent blinding), and are positioned after grit removal. They remove finer particles, fibres, and small organic matter, providing a polishing effect ahead of biological treatment.
- Coarse screens remove ~5–10% of suspended solids; fine screens can achieve 10–25%.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State Kirschmer's equation for headloss through a bar screen. Define each term.**

**Model Answer:**

Kirschmer's equation for headloss through a clean bar screen is:

$$
h = \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Where:**
- $h$ = headloss across the screen, in metres.
- $\beta$ = bar shape factor (dimensionless): **2.42** for sharp-edged, **1.79** for rectangular, **1.83** for circular bars.
- $w$ = clear spacing between bars (m).
- $b$ = width of the bar (m).
- $v$ = velocity of flow through the bar openings (m/s).
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²).
- $\theta$ = angle of inclination of the screen with horizontal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by forgetting to convert $w/b$ units consistently (use mm or m, not mixed).
> - Many students compute **approach velocity** but forget that the headloss equation requires **through-opening velocity** $v$, not $v_a$.
> - Always state the **bar shape factor value** explicitly. Skipping this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - In clogged-screen sub-parts, the **square relationship** of area ratio is often mis-applied as a linear one.

---

### Q3. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks) — QUESTION A**

**(a)** Explain the role of screening as a primary treatment unit in a wastewater treatment plant. Describe the classification of screens with their typical opening sizes and application areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechanically cleaned bar screen is to be designed for a wastewater treatment plant with the following data: average flow = 0.15 m³/s, peak flow = 0.30 m³/s, bar width $b = 12$ mm, clear spacing $w = 30$ mm, screen inclination $\theta = 45°$, bar shape factor $\beta = 1.79$, submerged depth $d = 0.6$ m. Calculate:
- (i) Channel width $B$
- (ii) Approach velocity at average flow
- (iii) Headloss through the clean screen

**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3:

**(a) [7 Marks]**

Screening is the **first unit operation** in a wastewater treatment plant, functioning as a **physical pretreatment** to remove coarse suspended and floating solids. It:
- Protects downstream mechanical equipment (pumps, valves, blowers).
- Reduces the organic load on subsequent biological units.
- Prevents clogging of pipes and channels.

**Classification of Screens:**

| Type | Opening Size | Cleaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coarse (Bar Rack) | 6–150 mm | Manual/Mechanical | Municipal headworks |
| Medium | 2–6 mm | Mechanical | Pre-protection of fine screens |
| Fine | 0.25–2 mm | Mechanical | Industrial ETPs, polishing step |
| Micro | < 0.25 mm | Backwash | Tertiary / water reuse |

Coarse screens are used at the **headworks**, fine screens follow **grit removal**, and microscreens are used in **tertiary polishing** or **water reuse** schemes.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Channel Width $B$:**
Number of bars:
$$
N = \dfrac{B - b}{w + b}
$$
For design, take $N = 18$ (assumed; can be iterated). Then:
$$
B = N(w + b) + b = 18(0.030 + 0.012) + 0.012 = 18(0.042) + 0.012 = 0.756 + 0.012 = 0.768 \ \text{m}
$$

Use $B = 0.80$ m (rounded up to a practical channel width).

**(ii) Approach velocity at average flow:**

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = B \cdot d = 0.80 \times 0.6 = 0.48 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
v_a = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{gross}}} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.48} \approx 0.3125 \ \text{m/s}
$$

This is within the recommended 0.3–0.6 m/s range. ✓

**(iii) Headloss through clean screen:**

Number of bars in width $B = 0.80$ m:

$$
N = \dfrac{B - b}{w + b} = \dfrac{0.80 - 0.012}{0.042} = \dfrac{0.788}{0.042} \approx 18.76 \rightarrow 18 \text{ bars}
$$

Net open area:
$$
A_{\text{net}} = N \cdot w \cdot d = 18 \times 0.030 \times 0.6 = 0.324 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Velocity through openings:
$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.324} \approx 0.463 \ \text{m/s}
$$

Width ratio:
$$
\dfrac{w}{b} = \dfrac{0.030}{0.012} = 2.5
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} = 2.5^{1.3333} \approx 3.39
$$

Velocity head:
$$
\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{(0.463)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{0.2144}{19.62} \approx 0.01093 \ \text{m}
$$

$\sin 45° \approx 0.707$.

Substituting into Kirschmer's equation:
$$
h = 1.79 \times 3.39 \times 0.01093 \times 0.707
$$

$$
h \approx 0.0470 \ \text{m} \approx 47 \ \text{mm}
$$

### **Final Answer for Q3(b):**
- Channel width $B \approx 0.80$ m
- Approach velocity $v_a \approx 0.31$ m/s
- Headloss $h \approx 47$ mm

---

### Q4. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024] (14 Marks) — QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **mechanically cleaned bar screen**. Compare its advantages over a manually cleaned screen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant has a coarse bar screen with the following data: clear spacing $w = 20$ mm, bar width $b = 10$ mm, channel width $B = 0.6$ m, submerged depth $d = 0.4$ m, flow $Q = 0.08$ m³/s, screen inclination $\theta = 60°$, and bar shape factor $\beta = 1.79$. Determine:
- (i) Velocity of flow through the bar openings.
- (ii) Headloss through the clean screen.
- (iii) Headloss when 40% of the net open area is clogged.

**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q4:

**(a) [7 Marks]**

A **mechanically cleaned bar screen** consists of inclined bars (30°–60°) across the flow channel. A motor-driven rake or endless-chain scraper travels across the screen face at regular intervals (timed or headloss-triggered), lifting accumulated screenings to the top of the screen. Captured solids drop onto a conveyor or compaction unit for dewatering and disposal.

**Key Components:**
- Bar assembly (rectangular, circular, or tapered cross-section).
- Raking mechanism (caterpillar, cable, or reciprocating).
- Drive motor and gearbox.
- Dewatering press or screenings hopper.
- Headloss sensor and SCADA control.

**Advantages over Manual Cleaning:**
- Suitable for **large flows** (> 0.05 m³/s).
- **Continuous operation** with minimal human intervention.
- Prevents **excessive headloss** through timed automatic cleaning.
- Reduces **operator health risk** (no manual contact with raw wastewater).
- Allows treatment plants to handle **peak flows** without overflow.

**Disadvantages:** Higher capital and O&M cost; requires power backup; mechanical wear.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Velocity through the bar openings:**

Number of bars:
$$
N = \dfrac{B - b}{w + b} = \dfrac{0.6 - 0.010}{0.020 + 0.010} = \dfrac{0.590}{0.030} \approx 19.67 \rightarrow 19 \ \text{bars}
$$

Net open area:
$$
A_{\text{net}} = N \cdot w \cdot d = 19 \times 0.020 \times 0.4 = 0.152 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Velocity through openings:
$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.152} \approx 0.526 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Headloss through clean screen:**

Width ratio:
$$
\dfrac{w}{b} = \dfrac{0.020}{0.010} = 2.0
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} = 2.0^{1.3333} \approx 2.52
$$

Velocity head:
$$
\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{(0.526)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{0.2767}{19.62} \approx 0.01411 \ \text{m}
$$

$\sin 60° \approx 0.866$.

Substituting into Kirschmer's equation:
$$
h = 1.79 \times 2.52 \times 0.01411 \times 0.866
$$

$$
h \approx 0.0551 \ \text{m} \approx 55.1 \ \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Headloss with 40% clogging:**

If 40% of the net area is clogged, the **effective** net area is 60% of original:
$$
A_{\text{net,\,clogged}} = 0.6 \times A_{\text{net}} = 0.6 \times 0.152 = 0.0912 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

The clogged headloss scales as:
$$
h_{\text{clogged}} = h \times \left(\dfrac{A_{\text{net}}}{A_{\text{net,\,clogged}}}\right)^{2} = 0.0551 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.6}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
h_{\text{clogged}} = 0.0551 \times 2.7778 \approx 0.153 \ \text{m} \approx 153 \ \text{mm}
$$

### **Final Answer for Q4(b):**
- Through-opening velocity $v \approx 0.526$ m/s
- Clean screen headloss $h \approx 55.1$ mm
- Clogged screen headloss $h_{\text{clogged}} \approx 153$ mm

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Screening**

- ⭐ Screening is the **first physical unit** in wastewater treatment.
- ⭐ Aperture sizes: **Coarse ≥ 6 mm**, **Fine 0.25–6 mm**, **Micro < 0.25 mm**.
- ⭐ **Approach velocity** must be **0.3–0.6 m/s** — too high → solids pass through; too low → grit settles.
- ⭐ **Through-opening velocity** $v = Q / A_{\text{net}}$ — must be checked separately from $v_a$.
- ⭐ **Kirschmer's Equation** is the headloss master formula — memorize it verbatim.
- ⭐ **Bar shape factors**: Sharp = 2.42, Rectangular = 1.79, Circular = 1.83.
- ⭐ **Clogged headloss** scales with the **square** of the area ratio.
- ⭐ **Manually cleaned screens** are used for small flows (< 0.05 m³/s); **mechanically cleaned** for larger plants.
- ⭐ **Screen inclination** (typically 30°–60°) aids in self-cleaning by gravity.
- ⭐ Always state units consistently (mm or m, not mixed) — examiners deduct marks for unit mismatches.
- ⭐ Screens are located **upstream** of grit chambers, Parshall flumes, and primary clarifiers.
- ⭐ **Process industries** depend on screening to protect RO membranes, heat exchangers, and bioreactors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grit Removal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grit Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Grit** is defined as the coarse, heavy, inorganic mineral particles present in municipal and industrial wastewater, possessing a **specific gravity in the range of 2.40 to 2.65** and a particle size typically larger than **0.2 mm (200 µm)**. Common grit constituents include sand, gravel, cinder chips, egg shells, bone fragments, coffee grounds, and small pieces of metal.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized)**
> *"Grit removal is the preliminary unit operation in wastewater treatment designed to separate heavy inorganic suspended solids (specific gravity ≥ 2.4) from the organic suspended solids by exploiting the difference in their settling velocities, while keeping the organic solids in suspension for downstream biological treatment."*

**Grit Removal** is therefore a **preliminary/primary treatment unit operation** positioned *immediately after* screening and *before* primary sedimentation, whose principal purpose is to:

1. Protect downstream mechanical equipment (pumps, valves, aerators) from abrasive wear.
2. Prevent deposition and clogging in pipes, channels, and conduits.
3. Reduce the formation of heavy sludge and inert solids in aeration tanks, anaerobic digesters, and sludge handling systems.
4. Improve the operational efficiency of downstream primary clarifiers and biological units.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Gold-Panning" Intuition)

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine you are a **gold prospector panning for gold in a river**. You swirl your pan in such a way that:
> - The **heavy gold nuggets** settle quickly to the bottom of the pan (this is analogous to **grit settling**).
> - The **lighter sand and organic debris** get carried away by the swirling water (this is analogous to **organic matter remaining suspended**).
>
> A **grit chamber works on exactly this principle** — by carefully controlling the horizontal velocity of wastewater, the system ensures that heavy inorganic grit *settles out* while lighter organic solids remain *suspended* and flow forward to the next treatment stage.
>
> If the velocity is **too high** → grit escapes with the flow (poor removal). If the velocity is **too low** → organic matter also settles (over-removal, lost organic load to digester).

## 1.3 Differentiating Grit from Other Solids

| Property | Dissolved Solids | Colloidal Solids | Organic Suspended Solids | **Grit (Inorganic Suspended)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Size | < 10⁻⁶ mm | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³ mm | 10⁻³ – 0.2 mm | **> 0.2 mm** |
| Specific Gravity | Variable | ~ 1.0 | ~ 1.0 – 1.2 | **2.4 – 2.65** |
| Settling Behaviour | Do not settle | Brownian motion | Slow settling | **Rapid discrete settling** |
| Removal Unit | Tertiary / RO | Coagulation-Flocculation | Primary Clarifier | **Grit Chamber** |

## 1.4 Position of Grit Chamber in the Treatment Train

```
Raw Sewage → Bar Screen → Grit Chamber → Primary Clarifier → Biological Treatment → Disinfection → Effluent
              (1)            (2)              (3)                    (4)                  (5)
```

> [!NOTE] **Why Grit Removal Comes BEFORE Primary Clarification?**
> If grit is allowed to enter the primary clarifier, the heavy inorganic load will (a) accumulate at the bottom, (b) increase sludge volume and inert fraction, (c) cause abrasive wear on sludge scraper mechanisms, and (d) reduce the effective organic digestion efficiency in downstream digesters. Therefore, grit removal is an **enabling pre-treatment step**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Grit Separation

The separation exploits **Stokes' Law of Discrete Settling**, which governs the terminal settling velocity $(v_s)$ of a spherical particle in a quiescent fluid:

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$$

Where the variables are defined in the formula sheet below. For typical grit (sand), $v_s$ ranges from **0.018 m/s to 0.025 m/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Critical Engineering Insight**
> The design challenge is **NOT** to settle *all* particles — it is to settle **only particles with settling velocity ≥ a critical value** (usually 0.02 m/s for grit), while keeping organic particles (with $v_s \leq 0.013$ m/s) in suspension. This dichotomy is the heart of grit chamber design.

## 2.2 Types of Grit Chambers — Classification & Operational Logic

### A. Horizontal-Flow (Constant Velocity) Grit Chamber
- Most common; rectangular channel with parabolic cross-section or flat bottom.
- Maintains horizontal velocity at **0.25 m/s to 0.40 m/s** (typically **0.30 m/s**).
- Detention time: **30 – 60 seconds** (usually **45 seconds** at average flow).
- Uses a **Parshall flume / proportional weir** at outlet to control velocity.

### B. Aerated Grit Chamber
- A wide rectangular tank with air diffusers on one side.
- The air induces a **spiral/roll pattern** of flow.
- Detention time: **2 – 5 minutes at peak flow**.
- Air supply rate: **0.2 – 0.5 m³/min per metre of tank length**.
- Performs **simultaneous grit removal and pre-aeration** (improves BOD/COD removal efficiency by 10–20%).

### C. Vortex-Type Grit Chamber
- Uses centrifugal force via tangential inlet flow.
- Two sub-types: **Pista Grit** (vertical shaft with paddles) and **Dorr-Oliver** (teacup shaped).
- Peak flow detention time: **~ 20 – 30 seconds**.
- Compact footprint — ideal for plant retrofits.

### D. Detritus Tank (Square Horizontal Flow)
- Equipped with **scraper blades** and **hoppers** at the bottom.
- Slower velocity; can also remove finer grit.
- Detention time: **~ 3 – 4 minutes**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] **All symbols and units are KTU-board-exam standard. Memorize this table.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detention Time | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $V$ = Volume of chamber, $Q$ = Flow rate | 30 – 60 s (horizontal) |
| 2 | Horizontal Velocity | $v_h = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | $L$ = Chamber length | 0.25 – 0.40 m/s |
| 3 | Cross-Sectional Area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{v_h}$ | Width × Depth | m² |
| 4 | Surface Overflow Rate | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | $A_s$ = Surface area | 700 – 1400 m³/m²·d |
| 5 | Settling Velocity (Stokes) | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | $g$ = 9.81, $\rho_s, \rho_w$ = densities, $d$ = diameter, $\mu$ = viscosity | 0.018 – 0.025 m/s |
| 6 | Settling Velocity (Transition) | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d}{3C_D \rho_w}}$ | $C_D$ = Drag coefficient | m/s |
| 7 | Number of Units (parallel) | $n = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{Q_{single}}$ | Each unit carries average flow | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Length : Depth Ratio | $L : D \approx 7 : 1$ to $20 : 1$ | Empirical design ratio | — |
| 9 | Aerated Air Supply | $q_{air} = (0.2 - 0.5) \times L$ | Per metre of tank length | m³/min |
| 10 | Critical Settling Velocity | $v_{sc} = \dfrac{H}{t}$ | $H$ = effective depth | m/s |
| 11 | Volumetric Grit Production | $V_g = (0.004 - 0.037) \times Q$ | Empirical grit quantity | m³/ML |
| 12 | Grit Density | $\rho_s$ | Quartz sand standard | 2.65 |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Velocity Control Rule (Camp's Criterion)**
> The horizontal velocity must be maintained **above the scouring velocity for organic particles** but **below the settling velocity of the smallest grit particle to be removed**. For typical grit, this window is **0.25 m/s to 0.40 m/s**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Grit chambers are indispensable in:
- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** — first line of defense.
- **Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO) Treatment** — for grit-laden stormwater.
- **Industrial Effluent Treatment** — especially in tanneries, textile units, and food processing plants where organic loading is high.
- **Pump Stations** — protects pump impellers from abrasive damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Design of Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber

> **Given (KTU Style):**
> Design a horizontal-flow constant-velocity grit chamber for a town with the following data:
> - Average flow, $Q_{avg}$ = **0.30 m³/s**
> - Peak flow factor = **3.0**
> - Allowable horizontal velocity, $v_h$ = **0.30 m/s**
> - Detention time at average flow, $t$ = **45 s**
> - Specific gravity of grit, $S_g$ = **2.65**
> - Assume 2 units operating in parallel.

### Step 1: Compute Peak Flow

$$Q_{peak} = Q_{avg} \times \text{Peak Factor} = 0.30 \times 3.0 = 0.90 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 2: Compute Flow per Unit (2 units in parallel)

$$Q_{unit} = \frac{Q_{peak}}{n} = \frac{0.90}{2} = 0.45 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Determine Cross-Sectional Area from Horizontal Velocity Constraint

The cross-section must satisfy the velocity criterion at *peak* flow:

$$A = \frac{Q_{unit}}{v_h} = \frac{0.45}{0.30} = 1.50 \ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 4: Determine Length of the Chamber

The length must satisfy the *detention time* criterion at *average* flow:

$$L = v_h \times t = 0.30 \times 45 = 13.5 \ \text{m}$$

### Step 5: Determine Depth (H) and Width (W)

Assume an empirical depth of $H$ = 1.0 m (standard design). Then:

$$W = \frac{A}{H} = \frac{1.50}{1.00} = 1.50 \ \text{m}$$

**Cross-check the L:H ratio:**

$$\frac{L}{H} = \frac{13.5}{1.0} = 13.5 \quad (\text{within acceptable range of } 7:1 \text{ to } 20:1) \ \checkmark$$

### Step 6: Verify the Critical Settling Velocity

The smallest grit particle to be removed must settle through depth $H$ within detention time $t$:

$$v_{sc} = \frac{H}{t} = \frac{1.0}{45} = 0.0222 \ \text{m/s}$$

This corresponds to a grit particle of approximately **0.2 mm** diameter — which is the standard cut-off for grit. **Design is acceptable.**

### Step 7: Estimate Daily Grit Quantity

Using the empirical formula $V_g = 0.02 \times Q_{avg}$ (in m³ per million litres):

$$V_{g,daily} = 0.02 \times (0.30 \times 86400) = 0.02 \times 25920 = 518.4 \ \text{L/day}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Settling Velocity Using Stokes' Law (Re-Check)

> **Given:** A grit particle with diameter $d = 0.2 \ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$, $\rho_s = 2650 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

### Step 1: Substitute into Stokes' Equation

$$v_s = \frac{(9.81) \times (2650 - 1000) \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{18 \times (1.002 \times 10^{-3})}$$

### Step 2: Compute Numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 9.81 \times 1650 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 6.4746 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 3: Compute Denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = 18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8036 \times 10^{-2}$$

### Step 4: Final Settling Velocity

$$v_s = \frac{6.4746 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0359 \ \text{m/s}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation**
> The computed $v_s = 0.0359 \ \text{m/s}$ is *higher* than the critical $v_{sc} = 0.0222 \ \text{m/s}$ from Derivation 1, confirming that a 0.2 mm grit particle **will** settle out completely in the designed chamber. This is the consistency check expected in KTU Part B answers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Requirement for Aerated Grit Chamber

> **Given:** Aerated grit chamber length $L = 12 \ \text{m}$, air supply rate per metre length = 0.4 m³/min.

$$q_{air,total} = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min} = 0.08 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix (Comparison)

| Design Parameter | Horizontal-Flow | Aerated | Vortex (Pista) | Detritus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detention Time | 30–60 s | 2–5 min | 20–30 s | 3–4 min |
| Horizontal Velocity | 0.25–0.40 m/s | Spiral | Tangential | < 0.20 m/s |
| Footprint | Large | Medium | Small | Large |
| Pre-Aeration Provided | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| Grit Cleanliness | Moderate (organic-coated) | **Good** | Very Good | Moderate |
| Construction Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Typical Capacity | Small–Medium STPs | Medium–Large STPs | Compact urban plants | Older installations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Position of Grit Chamber in Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sewage Inlet] --> B[Bar Screen Removes Large Solids]
    B --> C[Grit Chamber Removes Heavy Inorganic Solids]
    C --> D[Primary Clarifier Removes Organic Suspended Solids]
    D --> E[Biological Treatment Aeration Tank]
    E --> F[Secondary Clarifier]
    F --> G[Disinfection Chlorination or UV]
    G --> H[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    D --> I[Primary Sludge to Digester]
    E --> J[Waste Activated Sludge Recycle]
    I --> K[Anaerobic Digester]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Sludge Dewatering and Disposal]
    C --> M[Grit Hopper Collected and Washed]
    M --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inflow[Influent Sewage Q] --> InletChamber[Inlet Distribution Chamber]
    InletChamber --> ParabolicSection[Parabolic Cross Section Channel]
    ParabolicSection --> VelocityRegulator[Velocity Regulating Weir Parshall Flume]
    VelocityRegulator --> OutletChamber[Outlet Collection Chamber]
    OutletChamber --> ToPrimary[Outlet to Primary Clarifier]
    ParabolicSection --> SettlingZone[Settling Zone Length L]
    SettlingZone --> GritHopper1[Hopper 1 Collects Settled Grit]
    SettlingZone --> GritHopper2[Hopper 2 Collects Settled Grit]
    SettlingZone --> GritHopper3[Hopper 3 Collects Settled Grit]
    GritHopper1 --> ScrewConveyor[Bucket or Screw Conveyor]
    GritHopper2 --> ScrewConveyor
    GritHopper3 --> ScrewConveyor
    ScrewConveyor --> GritWasher[Grit Washer Removes Organic Coating]
    GritWasher --> Disposal[Grit Disposal to Landfill]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Decision Logic for Grit Chamber Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Selection of Grit Chamber] --> Q1{Is Space Constraint Critical}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is High Organic Pre Treatment Desired}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Large Plants Above 50 MLD}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Aerated[Select Aerated Grit Chamber]
    Q2 -- No --> Vortex[Select Vortex Type Pista Grit]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Aerated
    Q3 -- No --> Horizontal[Select Horizontal Flow Constant Velocity]
    Aerated --> End1[Final Design with Air Diffuser Sizing]
    Vortex --> End2[Final Design with Tangential Inlet Sizing]
    Horizontal --> End3[Final Design with Parshall Flume Sizing]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (for the visually-inclined student)

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Cross-Section of a Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber**
>
> ```
>     INLET  →    L = 13.5 m  →   OUTLET
>     ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Free Water Surface
>     │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
>     │  H = 1.0 m  (depth)                  │
>     │         v_h = 0.30 m/s  ──→          │
>     │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
>     │  W = 1.5 m                           │
>     └────┬─────┬─────┬─────────────────────┘
>          ▼     ▼     ▼
>        Grit  Grit  Grit  → Screw Conveyor → Washer → Disposal
>        Hopper Hopper Hopper
> ```
>
> **Reading the schematic:** Influent enters from the left with horizontal velocity $v_h = 0.30 \ \text{m/s}$. As it travels length $L = 13.5 \ \text{m}$, grit particles settle vertically (settling velocity $v_s$) while organic matter is carried forward. Settled grit is collected in multiple hoppers and transported to a washer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define grit. Why is grit removal considered an essential preliminary treatment step in municipal wastewater treatment plants?**

**Model Answer:**

**Grit** is defined as the coarse, heavy, inorganic suspended solid particles (specific gravity 2.4–2.65, size > 0.2 mm) present in domestic sewage, comprising sand, gravel, cinder, and similar mineral matter.

**Grit removal is essential for the following reasons:**
1. **Equipment Protection:** Prevents abrasion damage to pumps, valves, aerators, and sludge scrapers.
2. **Pipe Protection:** Prevents clogging and deposition in conveyance pipelines and channels.
3. **Process Efficiency:** Reduces inert inorganic load in aeration tanks and digesters, improving biological treatment efficiency.
4. **Sludge Quality:** Reduces sludge volume and improves digestibility by separating inorganic from organic solids.
5. **Economical Operation:** Lowers maintenance cost and downtime of downstream units.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between horizontal-flow grit chamber and aerated grit chamber.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber | Aerated Grit Chamber |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Velocity control at constant horizontal flow | Induced spiral roll by diffused air |
| Detention Time | 30 – 60 seconds | 2 – 5 minutes |
| Velocity | 0.25 – 0.40 m/s (constant) | Induced roll velocity (function of air) |
| Pre-aeration | Not provided | **Provided** — improves BOD removal |
| Organic Coating on Grit | Higher (needs washing) | **Lower** (air scrubbing action) |
| Footprint | Larger | **Smaller** for the same capacity |
| Air Supply | Not required | 0.2 – 0.5 m³/min per metre length |
| Best Suited For | Small – Medium STPs | Medium – Large STPs |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the following two full questions (each carrying 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Design a horizontal-flow constant-velocity grit chamber for a town with the following data:
- Average flow = **0.30 m³/s**
- Peak factor = **3.0**
- Detention time at average flow = **45 seconds**
- Horizontal velocity to be maintained = **0.30 m/s**
- Specific gravity of grit = **2.65**
- Number of units in parallel = **2**

Compute the length, width, depth, and the volume of grit collected per day.

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1: Peak flow calculation**

$$Q_{peak} = 0.30 \times 3.0 = 0.90 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Flow per unit**

$$Q_{unit} = \frac{0.90}{2} = 0.45 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Cross-sectional area (using horizontal velocity)**

$$A = \frac{Q_{unit}}{v_h} = \frac{0.45}{0.30} = 1.50 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Length of chamber**

$$L = v_h \times t = 0.30 \times 45 = 13.5 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Assume depth $H = 1.0 \ \text{m}$ (standard), then**

$$W = \frac{A}{H} = \frac{1.50}{1.00} = 1.50 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 6: Daily volume of sewage**

$$Q_{daily} = 0.30 \times 86400 = 25920 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 7: Daily grit volume (using 0.02 m³ per 10⁶ L sewage as standard)**

$$V_{grit} = 0.02 \times 25.92 = 0.5184 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \approx 0.52 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

> **Final Design Summary:** L = 13.5 m, W = 1.5 m, H = 1.0 m, Daily Grit = 0.52 m³/day.

---

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an **aerated grit chamber**. State its advantages over the horizontal-flow type.

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Working Principle:** Wastewater enters a wide rectangular tank. Compressed air is released through diffusers placed on **one side** of the tank near the bottom. The rising air bubbles create a **spiral/roll pattern of flow** (helical motion) along the length of the tank. Heavy grit particles settle to the bottom due to gravity, while lighter organic particles remain suspended and are carried forward with the roll velocity. The grit is collected in a hopper at the bottom and removed by a screw conveyor. The spiraling air also **scrubs the grit**, removing attached organic matter.

**Advantages over Horizontal-Flow Type:**
1. **Pre-aeration** is achieved simultaneously, reducing BOD by 10–20% and improving downstream biological treatment.
2. **Better grit washing** due to air scrubbing — produces cleaner grit with low organic content.
3. **Insensitive to flow variations** — air rate can be adjusted to maintain the roll pattern across a wide flow range.
4. **Smaller footprint** for the same organic load.
5. **No velocity-control weir** is required at the outlet.
6. **Freshens** septic sewage and prevents anaerobic odour generation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is the principle of grit removal based on? Explain **Stokes' Law** as applied to grit settling. A grit particle of diameter 0.2 mm and specific gravity 2.65 settles in water at 20 °C. Compute its terminal settling velocity. Given: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Principle:** Grit removal is based on the principle of **discrete (Type I) settling** of particles in a fluid, governed by the difference in specific gravity and particle size. The chamber is designed so that the horizontal velocity of flow is sufficient to keep organic matter (specific gravity ~1.0) in suspension, but low enough to allow heavier grit (specific gravity ≥ 2.4) to settle out.

**Stokes' Law Application:** The terminal settling velocity of a small spherical particle in the laminar settling regime is:

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$$

This law is valid when the Reynolds number of the settling particle $Re < 1$.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $d = 0.2 \ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$, $\rho_s = 2.65 \times 1000 = 2650 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{18 \times (1.002 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times 1650 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{6.4746 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_s = 0.0359 \ \text{m/s} \approx 0.036 \ \text{m/s}$$

> This corresponds to **a settling velocity of 3.6 cm/s** — well above the design threshold of 0.02 m/s for grit chambers.

---

**(b)** Compare the various **types of grit chambers** used in wastewater treatment. Which type is most suitable for a large metropolitan STP of 100 MLD capacity and why?

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Feature | Horizontal-Flow | Aerated | Vortex | Detritus |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detention Time | 30 – 60 s | 2 – 5 min | 20 – 30 s | 3 – 4 min |
| 2 | Velocity Control | Weir | Air diffuser | Tangential inlet | Slow flow |
| 3 | Pre-aeration | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| 4 | Grit Cleaning | Poor (washes needed) | **Good** (air scrub) | **Very Good** | Moderate |
| 5 | Footprint | Large | Medium | **Small** | Large |
| 6 | Capital Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| 7 | O&M Cost | Low | Medium (air blower) | High (paddles) | High (scraper) |
| 8 | Space Requirement | High | Medium | **Low** | High |

**Most Suitable for 100 MLD Metropolitan STP: Aerated Grit Chamber**

**Justification:**
1. At 100 MLD, the wastewater is typically septic and odorous — the **pre-aeration function** of an aerated grit chamber freshens the sewage and reduces H₂S/odour problems in the inlet zone.
2. **Footprint economy** is critical in metropolitan areas where land cost is prohibitive; the aerated chamber is more compact than a horizontal-flow unit of equivalent capacity.
3. **Flow variability** in metropolitan STPs is high (diurnal and seasonal); the air supply rate can be modulated to maintain the spiral roll pattern at both low and peak flows.
4. **Cleaner grit** with low organic content reduces downstream sludge processing costs — a major economic benefit for large plants.
5. **Combined BOD reduction** of 10–20% in the grit chamber reduces the load on the downstream aeration tank, providing energy savings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Grit Removal Questions**
>
> 1. **Units Inconsistency:** Students often write $Q$ in MLD and $v_h$ in m/s without conversion. Always convert to **m³/s** before substituting. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing Average vs Peak Flow:** Detention time is based on *average* flow, but cross-sectional area and velocity control are based on *peak* flow. Mixing these is a classic error. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Skipping Unit Conversion for Stokes' Law:** Diameter must be in **metres**, viscosity in **Pa·s**, densities in **kg/m³**. Forgetting $d^2$ conversion can lead to answers off by $10^4$. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting to State Assumptions:** Always state assumed depth $H = 1.0 \ \text{m}$ and number of units explicitly. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Ignoring Organic Coating:** When asked about grit washing, mention the **air scrubbing** in aerated chambers — this is a frequently-tested point. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Mermaid/Schematic Mistakes:** If asked to draw a sketch, label all key dimensions (L, W, H, $v_h$) and the direction of flow. Unlabelled diagrams fetch partial marks only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Grit** = heavy inorganic particles, Sg = 2.4 – 2.65, size > 0.2 mm.
- [x] **Grit chamber** = preliminary treatment unit after screening, before primary clarification.
- [x] **Design velocities:** $v_h$ = 0.25 – 0.40 m/s (typically 0.30 m/s).
- [x] **Detention time (horizontal):** 30 – 60 s (typically 45 s).
- [x] **Detention time (aerated):** 2 – 5 minutes.
- [x] **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ — valid for $Re < 1$.
- [x] **Four types:** Horizontal-flow, Aerated, Vortex, Detritus.
- [x] **Aerated** gives pre-aeration, cleaner grit, smaller footprint — best for large plants.
- [x] **Vortex** is most compact — best for urban retrofits.
- [x] **Horizontal-flow** is simplest and cheapest — best for small plants.
- [x] **Camp's velocity criterion:** keep organic in suspension, settle grit.
- [x] **L : H ratio** typically 7 : 1 to 20 : 1.
- [x] **Air supply** for aerated chamber: 0.2 – 0.5 m³/min per metre length.
- [x] **Grit volume:** ~0.02 m³ per 10⁶ L of sewage (empirical).
- [x] **Parshall flume** is used for velocity control in horizontal-flow chambers.
- [x] **Critical settling velocity** $v_{sc} = H/t$ — must be ≤ design $v_s$ of smallest grit.
- [x] **Functions:** equipment protection, pipe protection, sludge quality, BOD reduction.
- [x] **Position in train:** Bar Screen → Grit Chamber → Primary Clarifier → Biological Treatment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEDIMENTATION (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> Sedimentation is a **physical, gravity-driven separation process** in which suspended solid particles dispersed in a liquid (typically water or wastewater) settle out of the fluid phase under the influence of **gravitational acceleration (g = 9.81 m/s²)** when the fluid's upward velocity is lower than the particle's terminal settling velocity. It is the most widely used **primary treatment** operation in industrial effluent treatment plants (ETPs) and municipal sewage treatment plants (STPs).

In pollution control engineering, sedimentation is utilized to:
- Remove settleable suspended solids (SS) from raw industrial effluents.
- Reduce the **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** load before secondary biological treatment.
- Separate sludge slurries in thickeners and clarifiers.
- Act as a pre-treatment step before filtration, flotation, or coagulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this...** Imagine pouring a glass of muddy river water onto a table. If you leave the glass absolutely still and untouched for a few hours, the fine mud (silt particles) will slowly drift downward and form a thick brown layer at the bottom, while the upper portion of the water gradually turns clear.
>
> This happens because **gravity is pulling the heavier particles downward** through the water. The particles are denser than water, so they fall. This is sedimentation.
>
> In a real treatment plant, this same principle is replicated inside a giant tank called a **"Settler"** or **"Clarifier"**. Engineers don't wait for hours; they precisely control the upward velocity of the water (called the **overflow rate**, *v₀*) so that any particle whose settling velocity is greater than *v₀* will be captured as sludge, while clean water flows out from the top.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the governing physical constants and engineering design metrics used universally in sedimentation design:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water (at 20 °C):** **ρ_w = 998.2 kg/m³**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C):** **μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s (N·s/m²)**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C):** **ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- **Standard design temperature for sedimentation tanks:** **10 °C to 25 °C**
- **Standard detention time range:** **2 to 4 hours** (primary clarifiers)
- **Typical overflow rate (Surface Loading Rate):** **24 to 48 m³/m²·day** (primary treatment)
- **Weir loading rate:** **125 to 500 m³/m·day**

## 1.4 Classification of Sedimentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Four-Class Classification (Examinable)**
> Per the KTU module syllabus, sedimentation is classified into **four distinct types**, depending on particle concentration and inter-particle behavior:

| Class | Name | Description | Concentration Range |
|-------|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Type I** | Discrete Particle Settling | Individual, non-flocculating particles settle independently. Each particle follows **Stokes' Law**. | Low (< 1000 mg/L) |
| **Type II** | Flocculent Settling | Particles flocculate (coalesce) as they settle, growing in size and accelerating. Governed by **experimental column settling tests**. | Moderate |
| **Type III** | Zone (Hindered) Settling | High concentration suspension settles as a unified mass with a distinct liquid-solids interface. | High (> 1000 mg/L) |
| **Type IV** | Compression Settling | At very high concentrations, particles physically contact and compress the underlying sludge layer. | Very High (sludge) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The operational logic of a sedimentation tank is governed by a single, elegant engineering reality:

> A particle will be **removed** inside a settling tank **if and only if** its terminal settling velocity **v_s** is **greater than or equal to** the tank's **surface overflow rate** **v₀**.

$$\text{Removal Condition: } v_s \geq v_0 = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Volumetric flow rate of influent (m³/s or m³/day)
- **A_s** = Plan (surface) area of the settling tank (m²)
- **v₀** = Surface overflow rate (m/s or m/day)
- **v_s** = Terminal settling velocity of the particle (m/s)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Influent Entry:** Wastewater enters the tank through an inlet distribution structure (e.g., a center well or inlet baffle) to dissipate kinetic energy and distribute flow uniformly across the tank cross-section.
2. **Horizontal Flow Reduction:** The fluid's horizontal velocity is deliberately reduced by expanding the tank's cross-section, allowing gravity to dominate over drag forces.
3. **Gravitational Settling:** Particles denser than water begin to fall under gravity. Lighter or smaller particles may remain in suspension and exit with the overflow.
4. **Sludge Collection:** Settled particles accumulate at the tank bottom as a sludge blanket.
5. **Clarified Effluent Discharge:** Clear water rises to the top and exits via **weir** structures that ensure uniform draw-off.
6. **Sludge Removal:** Accumulated sludge is scraped (in rectangular tanks) or raked (in circular tanks) to a sludge hopper for removal.

## 2.3 Stokes' Law — Discrete Particle Settling

For Type I sedimentation, when a particle settles in the **laminar flow regime** (Reynolds Number **Re < 1**), Stokes' Law applies:

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Where:
- **v_s** = Terminal settling velocity (m/s)
- **g** = Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- **ρ_p** = Density of the particle (kg/m³)
- **ρ_w** = Density of water (**998.2 kg/m³** at 20 °C)
- **d** = Diameter of the particle (m)
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity of water (**1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** at 20 °C)

## 2.4 Newton's Law — Turbulent Regime

When **Re > 1000**, flow is turbulent and Newton's Law is applied:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}}$$

Where **C_D** is the **drag coefficient** (dimensionless), determined experimentally or from standard curves.

## 2.5 Transition Regime (1 < Re < 1000)

In the intermediate regime, neither pure Stokes nor pure Newton's law is valid. The general equation is used:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}} \quad \text{with } C_D = f(Re)$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALL EXAMINABLE EQUATIONS — STUDENTS MEMORIZE THIS TABLE**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Overflow Rate | $v_0 = Q / A_s$ | m/day | Tank design |
| 2 | Detention Time | $t_d = V / Q$ | hours (h) | Process sizing |
| 3 | Stokes' Law | $v_s = g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d^2 / 18\mu$ | m/s | Type I settling |
| 4 | Newton's Law | $v_s = \sqrt{4g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d / 3C_D\rho_w}$ | m/s | Turbulent settling |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho_w v_s d / \mu$ | dimensionless | Regime identification |
| 6 | Froude Number (tank) | $Fr = v_2 / g \cdot R_h$ | dimensionless | Tank hydraulics |
| 7 | Weir Loading Rate | $WLR = Q / L_w$ | m³/m·day | Effluent weir design |
| 8 | Sludge Volume | $V_s = Q \cdot (C_i - C_e) / (\rho_s - C_f)$ | m³/day | Sludge pumping |
| 9 | Particle Settling Path | $H = v_s \cdot t_d$ | m | Settling depth check |
| 10 | Hazen Number | $Haz = v_0 / v_s$ | dimensionless | Removal efficiency |

> **Notation Safeguard:** All absolute value expressions (where needed) are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using the LaTeX `\vert` command to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sedimentation units are indispensable in:

- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** — primary clarifiers.
- **Industrial Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)** — especially for paper, textile, tannery, and food processing wastewaters.
- **Water Treatment Plants (WTPs)** — pre-chlorination basins and pre-filtration.
- **Mining & Mineral Processing** — thickeners for concentrate and tailings.
- **Power Plants** — pre-treatment of cooling tower blowdown.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law — Force Balance Approach

A particle falling through a viscous fluid is acted upon by three forces:
1. **Gravitational Force (F_g)** — downward.
2. **Buoyancy Force (F_b)** — upward.
3. **Drag Force (F_d)** — upward (opposing motion).

### Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition
At **terminal velocity**, the particle ceases to accelerate, so the net force is zero:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

### Step 2 — Expressing Each Force

**Gravitational Force (Weight):**

$$F_g = m_p \cdot g = V_p \cdot \rho_p \cdot g$$

Where **V_p** is the volume of the (assumed spherical) particle.

**Buoyancy Force (Archimedes' Principle):**

$$F_b = V_p \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**Viscous Drag Force (Stokes' Drag for Re < 1):**

$$F_d = 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Into the Equilibrium Equation

$$V_p \cdot \rho_p \cdot g = V_p \cdot \rho_w \cdot g + 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$$

### Step 4 — Volume of a Spherical Particle

$$V_p = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute V_p Into the Equation

$$\frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_p \cdot g = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_w \cdot g + 3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$$

### Step 6 — Cancel Common Factor $\pi \cdot d$ Across All Terms

$$\frac{d^2}{6} \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) = 3 \cdot \mu \cdot v_s$$

### Step 7 — Rearrange for v_s

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity. Note that the velocity is proportional to the **square of the diameter** — meaning that doubling the particle size quadruples the settling velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation of Surface Overflow Rate (v₀)

Consider an ideal rectangular sedimentation tank of **length L**, **width B**, and **depth H**.

### Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate Q
For a tank receiving flow Q, with cross-sectional flow area A_flow = B · H:

$$Q = v_{horiz} \cdot B \cdot H$$

Where v_horiz is the horizontal flow velocity.

### Step 2 — Time Available for Settling (Detention Time t_d)

$$t_d = \frac{\text{Tank Volume}}{\text{Flow Rate}} = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Settling Distance
The particle must travel from the top of the water column to the bottom within t_d. The vertical settling distance is H.

$$H = v_s \cdot t_d$$

### Step 4 — Eliminating H
Substituting t_d from Step 2 into the equation H = v_s · t_d:

$$H = v_s \cdot \frac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$

$$\frac{Q}{L \cdot B} = v_s$$

### Step 5 — Define Surface Overflow Rate
Since A_s = L · B (plan area of tank):

$$v_0 = \frac{Q}{A_s} = v_s$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **fundamental design equation** of sedimentation. **The surface overflow rate numerically equals the settling velocity of the critical (just-removed) particle.** This is the cornerstone concept tested in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Stokes' Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A sand particle of diameter **d = 0.05 mm** and density **ρ_p = 2650 kg/m³** settles in water at **20 °C**. Determine its terminal settling velocity.

**Given Data:**
- d = 0.05 mm = 0.05 × 10⁻³ m = **5 × 10⁻⁵ m**
- ρ_p = **2650 kg/m³**
- ρ_w = **998.2 kg/m³**
- μ = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- g = **9.81 m/s²**

### Step 1 — Calculate Terminal Settling Velocity Using Stokes' Law

$$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 998.2) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Numerator

$$9.81 \times 1651.8 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$= 9.81 \times 4.1295 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 4.0510 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8036 \times 10^{-2}$$

### Step 4 — Divide

$$v_s = \frac{4.0510 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_s = 2.246 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_s = 2.246 \text{ mm/s} = 194.1 \text{ m/day}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Re < 1 (Stokes' Regime Validity)

$$Re = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu}$$

$$Re = \frac{998.2 \times 2.246 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{1.1210 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.112$$

Since **Re = 0.112 < 1**, the **Stokes' Law assumption is valid**. ✔

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Tank Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a rectangular sedimentation tank to treat **Q = 3.0 MLD** (Million Liters per Day) of municipal wastewater. Given:
> - Overflow rate v₀ = **30 m³/m²·day**
> - Depth H = **3.0 m**
> - Length-to-Width ratio L:B = **4:1**

**Given Data:**
- Q = 3.0 MLD = **3000 m³/day**
- v₀ = **30 m³/m²·day**
- H = **3.0 m**
- L/B = **4**

### Step 1 — Calculate Required Surface Area

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_0} = \frac{3000}{30} = 100 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Width B (Using A_s = L · B and L = 4B)

$$A_s = L \cdot B = 4B \cdot B = 4B^2$$

$$4B^2 = 100$$

$$B^2 = 25 \implies B = 5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Length L

$$L = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Detention Time

$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$

$$t_d = \frac{20 \times 5 \times 3}{3000} = \frac{300}{3000} = 0.1 \text{ day} = 2.4 \text{ hours}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A detention time of **2.4 hours** falls within the **standard KTU range of 2–4 hours** for primary clarifiers — confirming that the design is compliant.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Regime Identification

| Settling Velocity Range | Reynolds Number | Regime | Applicable Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 0.1 mm/s | Re < 1 | Laminar | Stokes' Law |
| 0.1 to 10 mm/s | 1 < Re < 1000 | Transition | Empirical / C_D curves |
| > 10 mm/s | Re > 1000 | Turbulent | Newton's Law |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Sedimentation Unit in an ETP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Industrial Effluent Inlet] --> B[Bar Screen - Removes Large Debris]
    B --> C[Grit Chamber - Removes Sand and Grit]
    C --> D[Primary Sedimentation Tank - Clarifier]
    D --> D1[Sludge Hopper - Bottom Collection]
    D --> D2[Effluent Weir - Top Discharge]
    D1 --> E[Sludge Digestor]
    D2 --> F[Secondary Biological Treatment]
    E --> G[Sludge Drying Beds]
    F --> H[Tertiary Treatment]
    H --> I[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    G --> J[Final Disposal]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inside the Settling Tank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inlet[A - Influent Inlet Structure] --> Baffle1[B - Inlet Baffle]
    Baffle1 --> ZoneSettling[C - Settling Zone]
    ZoneSettling --> SludgeZone[D - Sludge Zone]
    ZoneSettling --> OutletBaffle[E - Outlet Baffle]
    OutletBaffle --> EffluentWeir[F - Effluent Weir]
    EffluentWeir --> OutletChannel[G - Outlet Channel]
    SludgeZone --> SludgeHopper[H - Sludge Hopper]
    SludgeHopper --> SludgePump[I - Sludge Pump to Digester]
```

## 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Sedimentation Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sed[Sedimentation Process] --> T1[Type I - Discrete]
    Sed --> T2[Type II - Flocculent]
    Sed --> T3[Type III - Zone Settling]
    Sed --> T4[Type IV - Compression]
    T1 --> T1a[Stokes Law Applies]
    T1 --> T1b[Particles do not Interact]
    T2 --> T2a[Particles Flocculate During Settling]
    T2 --> T2b[Column Tests Required]
    T3 --> T3a[Unified Sludge Blanket Interface]
    T3 --> T3b[Applied in Thickeners]
    T4 --> T4a[Sludge Compaction Phase]
    T4 --> T4b[High Concentration Sludges]
```

## 4.4 Force Balance Diagram on a Settling Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Particle[Spherical Particle in Fluid] --> Fg[F1 - Gravitational Force Downward]
    Particle --> Fb[F2 - Buoyancy Force Upward]
    Particle --> Fd[F3 - Viscous Drag Force Upward]
    Fg --> Eq[Force Equilibrium at Terminal Velocity]
    Fb --> Eq
    Fd --> Eq
    Eq --> Result[v_s - Terminal Settling Velocity]
```

## 4.5 Circular vs Rectangular Clarifier — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rect[Rectangular Clarifier] --> R1[Inlet at One End]
    Rect --> R2[Effluent Weir at Opposite End]
    Rect --> R3[Chain and Flight Scraper]
    Rect --> R4[Sludge Hopper at Inlet End]
    Circ[Circular Clarifier] --> C1[Inlet at Center via Well]
    Circ --> C2[Effluent Weir at Periphery]
    Circ --> C3[Rotating Bridge Scraper]
    Circ --> C4[Central Sludge Hopper]
```
