# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define coagulation. What is the role of zeta potential in the coagulation process?**

**Model Answer:**

**Coagulation** is the physico-chemical process of adding chemical reagents (coagulants) to a colloidal suspension to destabilize the colloidal particles by neutralizing their surface charges, thereby promoting their aggregation into larger settleable flocs.

**Role of Zeta Potential:** Colloidal particles carry a net negative surface charge, quantified by the **zeta potential (ζ)**, which is the electrokinetic potential at the slipping plane of the electrical double layer surrounding the particle. This ζ creates an electrostatic repulsive barrier (typically **−10 mV to −30 mV** in natural waters) that prevents particles from approaching each other closely enough for van der Waals attraction to cause aggregation.

When a coagulant is added, the cationic hydrolysis species (e.g., Al³⁺, Fe³⁺) adsorb onto the particle surface and **reduce |ζ|**. Effective coagulation requires **|ζ| < ±20 mV**, with optimum destabilization occurring near the **isoelectric point (ζ = 0)**. The reduction of zeta potential below this critical threshold allows the attractive van der Waals forces to dominate, causing rapid aggregation into flocs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **zeta potential** with **surface potential**. Zeta potential is measured at the **slipping plane** (shear plane), not at the actual particle surface. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the concept of "sweep coagulation" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sweep coagulation** (also called *enmeshment coagulation* or *precipitation coagulation*) occurs when a coagulant is added at a dose significantly higher than that required for charge neutralization. At this high dose, the metal salt coagulant (e.g., aluminium sulphate or ferric chloride) hydrolyzes to form a **voluminous, amorphous hydroxide precipitate**.

**Example Reaction (with Alum):**
$$\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Al(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

The freshly precipitated Al(OH)₃ is a **gelatinous, fluffy solid** with enormous surface area. As it forms, colloidal particles in the water become **physically enmeshed (entangled) within the growing precipitate**, much like fish caught in a net. This mechanism is dominant in most conventional water treatment plants operating at alum doses of **10–150 mg/L** and produces large, rapidly settling flocs.

The key advantage of sweep coagulation is that it is **less sensitive to the initial charge of the colloids** and works effectively across a broader pH range (5.5–7.5 for alum, 4.0–11.0 for ferric chloride).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a)** Discuss the various mechanisms of coagulation in detail with neat diagrams. Explain how each mechanism is influenced by coagulant dose and pH. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water treatment plant treats 20,000 m³/day of raw water. The jar test results for alum dose vs. residual turbidity are given below:

| Alum Dose (mg/L) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residual Turbidity (NTU) | 55 | 28 | 12 | 4 | 7 | 15 |

The raw water turbidity is 150 NTU and alkalinity is 35 mg/L as CaCO₃. Determine:
(i) Optimum alum dose and percentage turbidity removal at optimum dose
(ii) Daily alum consumption in kg
(iii) Total alkalinity consumed per day in kg as CaCO₃
(iv) Whether external lime addition is required (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The four mechanisms of coagulation are:

**1. Double-Layer Compression:** At low ionic strength, the electrical double layer around a colloid extends far into solution, preventing close approach of particles. Adding an inert electrolyte (e.g., NaCl) increases ionic strength, compressing the double layer. The **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1} = 0.304/\sqrt{I}$ decreases as ionic strength $I$ increases. No specific chemical interaction occurs — only a physical compression of the diffuse layer.

**2. Adsorption and Charge Neutralization:** When hydrolyzing metal salts (Al₂(SO₄)₃, FeCl₃) are added at **low doses (1–10 mg/L as metal)**, cationic species like Al(OH)²⁺, Al(OH)₂⁺, and polymeric Al₁₃ species adsorb directly onto negatively charged colloid surfaces. The zeta potential drops, and at the **isoelectric point (ζ = 0)**, maximum destabilization occurs. The optimum dose is stoichiometric and corresponds to complete charge neutralization.

**3. Inter-Particle Bridging:** High-molecular-weight polymers (e.g., polyacrylamide, molecular weight **10⁶ to 10⁷ g/mol**) adsorb simultaneously on multiple particles, physically linking them. This requires **proper polymer dosage** (too little = no bridges; too much = restabilization) and works best when polymer chains are long enough to span the distance between particles.

**4. Sweep Coagulation:** At **high doses (>10 mg/L as Al³⁺)**, a voluminous hydroxide precipitate forms. Colloidal particles are physically **swept (enmeshed)** into this precipitate. The dose–response curve shows a characteristic **U-shape**: residual turbidity first decreases, reaches a minimum, and then **rises again at very high doses** due to charge reversal and restabilization.

**Influence of Dose and pH:**
- At **low dose and pH near isoelectric point**: Mechanism 2 (charge neutralization) dominates.
- At **high dose**: Mechanism 4 (sweep coagulation) dominates.
- **pH** controls the hydrolysis speciation: at pH 5.5–7.5, Al(OH)₃(s) is the dominant species for alum; below pH 5, soluble Al³⁺ dominates; above pH 8, soluble Al(OH)₄⁻ dominates and coagulation fails.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Optimum Dose and Turbidity Removal:**

From the jar test data, the **minimum residual turbidity** of **4 NTU** occurs at an alum dose of **40 mg/L**. This is the **optimum dose**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Turbidity Removal (\%)} &= \frac{T_{initial} - T_{final}}{T_{initial}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{150 - 4}{150} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{146}{150} \times 100 \\
&= 97.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Daily Alum Consumption:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Alum}_{daily} &= Q \times D_{opt} \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 40 \text{ g/m}^3 \\
&= 800{,}000 \text{ g/day} \\
&= 800 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Alkalinity Consumed Per Day:**

Using the stoichiometric relationship: **1 mg/L alum consumes 0.45 mg/L CaCO₃ alkalinity**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Alk}_{consumed} &= 0.45 \times 40 \\
&= 18 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to daily basis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Alk}_{daily} &= 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 18 \text{ g/m}^3 \\
&= 360{,}000 \text{ g/day} \\
&= 360 \text{ kg/day as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Lime Addition Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Alk}_{remaining} &= A_0 - \text{Alk}_{consumed} \\
&= 35 - 18 \\
&= 17 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}$$

Since **Alk_remaining = 17 mg/L > 0**, the natural alkalinity is **sufficient** to support alum coagulation. **No external lime addition is required.** However, the pH should be monitored to ensure it remains in the effective range of 5.5–7.5.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **jar test procedure** for determining the optimum coagulant dose. What are the significance and limitations of the jar test? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the advantages and disadvantages of **aluminium-based, iron-based, and polymeric coagulants**. A textile industry wastewater has a colour of 800 Pt-Co units and COD of 1200 mg/L. If the recommended ferric chloride dose is 80 mg/L and the plant flow is 5000 m³/day, calculate the annual cost of ferric chloride at ₹25 per kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Jar Test Procedure (Refer to Block Diagram in Section 4.3):**

The jar test is a **bench-scale simulation** of the full-scale coagulation–flocculation–sedimentation process. The standard procedure is:

1. **Sample Collection:** Collect a representative sample of the raw water to be treated (at least 10 L).
2. **Beaker Preparation:** Fill **six 1-L or 2-L beakers** with equal volumes of raw water. Label them 1 through 6.
3. **Coagulant Addition:** Add **graduated, increasing doses** of coagulant to each beaker (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 mg/L). Add the coagulant **simultaneously** to all beakers to ensure equal reaction time.
4. **Rapid Mixing (Flash Mix):** Immediately stir all beakers at **100–150 rpm for 1–2 minutes**. The Camp number G should be in the range **700–1000 s⁻¹**. This disperses the coagulant uniformly and promotes particle destabilization.
5. **Slow Mixing (Flocculation):** Reduce the stirring speed to **25–40 rpm for 20–30 minutes** (G ≈ 20–75 s⁻¹). This gentle mixing promotes particle collisions and floc growth without breaking the flocs.
6. **Settling:** Stop the stirrers and allow the flocs to **settle quiescently for 30–60 minutes**.
7. **Sampling and Analysis:** Carefully withdraw supernatant from **2 cm below the water surface** in each beaker. Measure **residual turbidity, residual colour, residual COD, and pH**.
8. **Dose–Response Curve:** Plot residual turbidity (y-axis) vs. coagulant dose (x-axis). The dose corresponding to the **lowest residual turbidity** is the **optimum coagulant dose**.

**Significance of the Jar Test:**
- Determines the **optimum coagulant dose** for full-scale design.
- Determines the **optimum pH** for coagulation.
- Helps **select the most effective coagulant** (by testing multiple coagulants in parallel).
- Estimates the **sludge volume** produced (a design parameter for sludge handling).
- Predicts the **expected treated water quality** in terms of turbidity, colour, and organic removal.
- Economically optimizes coagulant use, **saving chemical costs** (overdosing wastes chemicals; underdosing gives poor treatment).

**Limitations of the Jar Test:**
- The test uses **batch conditions** that may not perfectly simulate **continuous-flow** full-scale operation.
- The **mixing intensity and pattern** in jar test stirrers differ from full-scale flash mixers and flocculators.
- It is **time-consuming** (typically 1–2 hours per test) and requires skilled operators.
- The test does not account for **short-circuiting, dead zones, or hydraulic transients** in full-scale tanks.
- The coagulant grade used in the lab may differ slightly from the **commercial-grade** product used in the plant.
- It cannot simulate the effects of **temperature variations** or **diurnal quality changes** in the raw water.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison of Coagulant Types:**

| Parameter | Aluminium-Based (Alum) | Iron-Based (FeCl₃, FeSO₄) | Polymeric (PAC, Polyacrylamide) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Effective pH Range** | 5.5 – 7.5 (narrow) | 4.0 – 11.0 (very wide) | 5.0 – 9.0 (wide) |
| **Cost** | Low (₹8–15/kg) | Moderate (₹20–30/kg) | High (₹80–200/kg) |
| **Sludge Volume** | High (alum sludge) | Moderate (iron sludge, denser) | Very low |
| **Colour/Turbidity Removal** | Good | Excellent (especially for colour) | Excellent |
| **Sensitivity to Alkalinity** | High (consumes alkalinity) | Moderate | Very low |
| **Working Temperature** | Poor at <10 °C | Good at low temperatures | Excellent |
| **Residual Metal Concern** | Possible Al residual (neurotoxicity) | Possible Fe residual (staining) | None (organic polymers) |
| **Storage/Stability** | Stable, easy to store | Hygroscopic, corrosive | Variable shelf life |
| **Toxicity** | Low | Low–moderate (acidic) | Low (food-grade available) |

**Key Advantage of Iron-Based Coagulants:** They work over a much wider pH range and are more effective for **highly coloured wastewaters** (like textile effluent), making them ideal for the given problem.

**Annual Cost Calculation:**

Given:
- Flow rate $Q = 5000$ m³/day
- Ferric chloride dose $D = 80$ mg/L = 80 g/m³
- Price $P = ₹25$/kg

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily FeCl}_3 \text{ required} &= Q \times D \\
&= 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 80 \text{ g/m}^3 \\
&= 400{,}000 \text{ g/day} \\
&= 400 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily cost} &= 400 \text{ kg/day} \times ₹25/\text{kg} \\
&= ₹10{,}000/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual cost} &= ₹10{,}000 \times 365 \\
&= ₹36{,}50{,}000 \\
&= ₹36.5 \text{ Lakhs/year}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert m³/day × mg/L → kg/day. Remember: (m³/day × g/m³) ÷ 1000 = kg/day. Students routinely lose **2–3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing Alum Hydrate Forms:** Al₂(SO₄)₃·**14**H₂O, ·**16**H₂O, and ·**18**H₂O have different molecular weights (594, 630, 666). KTU problems **always** specify the hydration form — read carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the Stoichiometric Factor 0.45:** The relationship "1 mg/L alum = 0.45 mg/L CaCO₃ alkalinity consumed" is the **most-tested single value** in coagulation problems. Commit it to memory.
> 4. **Confusing Coagulation with Flocculation:** Coagulation = chemical destabilization (rapid mix). Flocculation = physical floc growth (slow mix). Examiners **deduct marks** for interchanging these terms.
> 5. **Not Plotting the Jar Test Graph:** Always plot a dose–response curve. The optimum dose is read at the **minimum of the U-shaped curve**, not at the **inflection point**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Coagulation** is a chemical process that destabilizes colloidal particles by neutralizing their **zeta potential** (must be reduced to **|ζ| < 20 mV**).

✅ The **four mechanisms** are: (1) Double-layer compression, (2) Charge neutralization, (3) Inter-particle bridging, (4) Sweep coagulation (enmeshment).

✅ **Alum** [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O, MW = 666.43] is the most common coagulant, effective at **pH 5.5–7.5** with dose **10–150 mg/L**.

✅ The golden stoichiometric rule: **1 mg/L of alum consumes 0.45 mg/L of CaCO₃ alkalinity** and produces **0.44 mg/L of acidity**.

✅ The **Jar Test** is the standard bench-scale method to determine the **optimum coagulant dose** and **optimum pH** using a gang stirrer with 6 beakers (rapid mix → slow mix → settle → measure NTU).

✅ **Coagulant dose–response curve is U-shaped**: underdosing causes poor destabilization; overdosing causes **charge reversal and restabilization**.

✅ **pH is the master variable** — it controls coagulant speciation, floc formation, and treated water quality. Always specify and control pH in any coagulation design.

✅ **Camp-Hazen velocity gradient (G)** governs the rapid mix design: **G = √(P/μV)** in s⁻¹, with rapid mix requiring **G > 700 s⁻¹** and flocculation requiring **G ≈ 20–75 s⁻¹**.

✅ **Stoke's Law** governs floc settling velocity: $v_s = g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d_p^2 / (18\mu)$ — larger and denser flocs settle faster.

✅ **Cost calculation** uses: $C_{annual} = Q \times D \times P \times 365 \times 10^{-6}$ (₹/year), with $Q$ in m³/day, $D$ in mg/L, $P$ in ₹/kg.

✅ For **Kerala-specific applications**, coagulation is used in KWA water treatment plants (surface water treatment with alum) and in Cochin/Kannur industrial wastewater treatment (textile, coir, and food processing effluents).

✅ Remember the **treatment train sequence**: **Coagulation → Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection**. Coagulation is the **first chemical barrier** in the train.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Chemical Treatment Methods: Coagulation** | *KTU 2024 Scheme | OECHT613*

topics.title: Flocculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flocculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flocculation** is a physico-chemical unit operation in pollution control engineering wherein **destabilized colloidal and finely suspended particles** in a water/wastewater stream are brought into intimate contact so that they **aggregate into larger, heavier, and settleable masses called "flocs"** through the influence of gentle, controlled hydrodynamic shear (velocity gradients) and the action of polymeric flocculants.

In a conventional **solid–liquid separation train**, flocculation is positioned **between coagulation (or chemical conditioning) and sedimentation**, acting as the critical "bridging-and-growth" stage that converts sub-micron colloids (which would otherwise never settle in a reasonable detention time) into macroscopic agglomerates visible to the naked eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology You Must Use:**
> - *Colloidal particles* — particles in the size range **0.001 µm to 1 µm**
> - *Floc* — an aggregated mass of destabilized particles, typically **0.1 mm to 3 mm**
> - *Velocity gradient, G* — the fundamental design parameter (s⁻¹)
> - *Camp's Number, G·t* — the dimensionless flocculation criterion

## 1.2 The "Coffee Cup" Intuition

Imagine making Turkish/Malabar coffee: you dissolve the powder (fine particles) in hot water, but the coffee won't settle. Now imagine you **stir very slowly and gently with a spoon** — you are intentionally creating slow, swirling water motion. As the spoon turns, tiny coffee particles repeatedly **bump into each other**, stick together, and form **mini mud-balls (flocs)** that drift to the bottom of the cup. The stirrer is your **paddle flocculator**, the slow rotation is your **velocity gradient (G)**, and the time you stir is your **detention time (t)**. Stir too violently (high G) and the flocs shatter; stir too slowly (low G) and nothing collides — both fail. **The art of flocculation is choosing the *just-right* G and t.** That is exactly what KTU examiners test.

## 1.3 Why Flocculation Cannot Be Skipped

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without flocculation:** Coagulation only produces **microflocs (~1–10 µm)** — too small to settle in 2–4 hours of clarifier detention.
> **With flocculation:** Microflocs are grown into **macroflocs (0.1–3 mm)** — settle in minutes.

This step alone can improve suspended solids removal in a primary clarifier by **60–90%**, making it indispensable in effluent treatment plants (ETPs) of chemical, petrochemical, textile, and food processing industries.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Design Boundaries (Industry Values)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Benchmarks for Municipal & Industrial Flocculators:**
> - **Velocity gradient (G):** 20 – 75 s⁻¹ (typical 30 – 60 s⁻¹)
> - **Detention time (t):** 20 – 40 minutes
> - **Camp's number (G·t):** 2 × 10⁴ to 6 × 10⁴ (dimensionless)
> - **Floc size target:** 0.5 – 3 mm
> - **Paddle tip speed:** 0.2 – 1.0 m/s
> - **Tank geometry:** square or circular, depth 3 – 5 m

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

Flocculation is governed by **two distinct transport mechanisms** working in parallel:

### A. Perikinetic Flocculation (Brownian-motion driven)
- Occurs in the **first 1–10 seconds** after coagulant dose.
- **Driver:** random thermal (Brownian) motion of molecules.
- **Mechanism:** particles ≤ 1 µm collide by diffusion.
- **Limitation:** becomes negligible once flocs grow beyond ~10 µm.
- *Governed by **von Smoluchowski's** diffusion equation.*

### B. Orthokinetic Flocculation (Velocity-gradient driven)
- **Driver:** induced fluid motion (paddles, baffles, hydraulic jumps).
- **Mechanism:** particles in adjacent streamlines moving at different velocities **collide due to differential velocity shear**.
- **Dominates** for flocs > 10 µm — i.e., **this is what a flocculator is designed for.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** "Perikinetic" is what **chemistry** does. "Orthokinetic" is what the **engineer** does by designing G and t.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Physics — Why Particles Aggregate

Once charge is neutralized by a coagulant (Alum, FeCl₃, PAC, polyaluminium chloride), particles can be **bridged** by:
1. **Polymer bridging** — long-chain polyelectrolytes (cationic/anionic PAM) adsorb on multiple particles.
2. **Sweep flocculation** — precipitation of metal hydroxides (e.g., Al(OH)₃) physically entraps particles.
3. **Charge neutralization patch model** — oppositely charged polymer patches attract bare particle surfaces.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it forms the backbone of every 14-mark flocculation numerical in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | $G$ = velocity gradient (s⁻¹); $P$ = power input (W); $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²); $V$ = flocculator volume (m³) | **The single most important design equation.** Determines shear intensity imparted to water. |
| 2 | $G \cdot t = \sqrt{\dfrac{P \cdot t}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | $t$ = detention time (s); $G \cdot t$ = Camp's number (dimensionless) | Overall flocculation performance criterion. Target: **2 × 10⁴ to 6 × 10⁴**. |
| 3 | $P = C_D \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot \rho \cdot v_r^{\,3}}{2}$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient of paddle; $A$ = paddle area (m²); $\rho$ = water density (kg/m³); $v_r$ = relative velocity of paddle w.r.t. water (m/s) | Power consumed by flat paddle in water — used when $C_D$ is known. |
| 4 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot n^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | $N_p$ = power number (dimensionless, paddle-specific); $n$ = rotational speed (rev/s); $D$ = paddle diameter (m) | Power input when using **impeller power-number correlations**. |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $V$ = flocculator volume; $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s) | Hydraulic detention time. |
| 6 | $v_r = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot n \cdot R}{60} \cdot (1 - k)$ | $R$ = paddle tip radius (m); $k$ = slip factor (0.3–0.7 typically) | Relative water-to-paddle speed for power calculation. |
| 7 | $C_D = \dfrac{1.10 \cdot Re_p^{-0.27}}{(\log Re_p + 1.2)^{1.80}}$ | $Re_p$ = paddle Reynolds number $= \dfrac{D^{2} \cdot n \cdot \rho}{\mu}$ | Empirical drag coefficient for flat paddles (valid for $Re_p$ ≈ 10³ – 10⁵). |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_0 - N_t}{N_0}$ | $\eta$ = flocculation efficiency; $N_0, N_t$ = particle counts at $t = 0$ and $t$ | Used in orthokinetic collision theory. |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -K \cdot G \cdot N^{2}$ | $N$ = particle number concentration; $K$ = collision efficiency factor | Differential form of **Smoluchowski's** flocculation kinetics. |
| 10 | $V_{\text{tank}} = Q \cdot t$ | — | Direct sizing equation. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Flocculation is Used |
|---|---|
| **Municipal Water Treatment** | Removes turbidity (river/lake water → drinking). |
| **Petrochemical ETP** | Aggregates oil-emulsified droplets and catalyst fines. |
| **Textile / Dye ETP** | Helps settle reactive dye particles & color bodies. |
| **Pharma ETP** | Removes antibiotic-producing biomass & suspended API residues. |
| **Pulp & Paper ETP** | Clarifies white-water, recovers fibre, closes loops. |
| **Mineral Beneficiation** | Thickener feed conditioning in tailings management. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Gradient, G

The velocity gradient $G$ is derived from the **concept of viscous dissipation of power in a Newtonian fluid**. The power dissipated per unit volume in a fluid undergoing shearing motion is given by:

$$\dot{W} = \mu \cdot \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{2}$$

If we define the **mean velocity gradient** $G$ as the spatial rate of change of velocity averaged over the volume, then by definition:

$$G = \left\langle \dfrac{du}{dy} \right\rangle$$

The **total power dissipated** in the flocculator volume $V$ is therefore:

$$P = \dot{W} \cdot V = \mu \cdot G^{2} \cdot V$$

Rearranging for $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}}$$

**Logical Narrative:**
- (i) Start with the viscous dissipation rate per unit volume (Newton's law of viscosity in shear).
- (ii) Recognize that $G$ is a macroscopic average of the local shear rate.
- (iii) Multiply by viscosity and volume to get total power.
- (iv) Algebraically solve for $G$ to obtain the design equation.

This is the **Camp–Stein equation (1943)** — the foundation of every flocculator design.

## 3.2 Derivation of Camp's Number (G·t)

Multiplying both sides of the $G$ equation by detention time $t$:

$$G \cdot t = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}} \cdot t = \sqrt{\dfrac{P \cdot t}{\mu \cdot V}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** $G \cdot t$ represents the **total opportunity for particle collisions** during the flocculation period. Even at low $G$, sufficient $t$ can deliver the same total flocculation. Conversely, very high $G$ for very short $t$ may also work. This **equivalence** is what makes Camp's number a unifying design criterion.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A mechanical flocculator is to treat $Q = 0.05$ m³/s of water. Design detention time $t = 25$ min. The flocculator has 4 paddle rotors, each with two flat blades of $0.10$ m × 0.50$ m mounted on a horizontal shaft at radius $R = 1.25$ m. Rotational speed $n = 0.05$ rev/s. Assume slip factor $k = 0.5$ and water viscosity $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Take drag coefficient $C_D = 1.8$.
> 
> **Required:** (a) Velocity gradient $G$; (b) Power input $P$; (c) Camp's number $G \cdot t$ and comment on flocculator adequacy.

### Solution (Fully Step-by-Step)

#### Part (a): Velocity Gradient G

**Step 1 — Calculate paddle tip velocity ($v_p$):**

$$v_p = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot R = 2\pi \cdot (0.05) \cdot (1.25)$$

$$v_p = 0.3927 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate relative velocity of paddle w.r.t. water ($v_r$):**

$$v_r = v_p \cdot (1 - k) = 0.3927 \cdot (1 - 0.5) = 0.1963 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total paddle area ($A$):**

There are 4 rotors × 2 blades per rotor = 8 blades. Each blade is 0.10 m × 0.50 m.

$$A = 8 \cdot (0.10 \cdot 0.50) = 8 \cdot 0.05 = 0.40 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate power input $P$:**

$$P = C_D \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot \rho \cdot v_r^{\,3}}{2}$$

$$P = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{0.40 \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.1963)^{3}}{2}$$

Compute $v_r^{\,3}$:

$$(0.1963)^{3} = 0.1963 \cdot 0.1963 \cdot 0.1963 = 7.561 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{3}$$

Substitute:

$$P = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{0.40 \cdot 1000 \cdot 7.561 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$P = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{3.0244}{2} = 1.8 \cdot 1.5122$$

$$\boxed{P = 2.722 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate flocculator volume $V$:**

$$V = Q \cdot t = 0.05 \cdot (25 \cdot 60) = 0.05 \cdot 1500 = 75 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate G:**

$$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.722}{(1.002 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 75}}$$

$$\mu \cdot V = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 75 = 0.07515$$

$$\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V} = \dfrac{2.722}{0.07515} = 36.22 \text{ s}^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{G = \sqrt{36.22} = 6.018 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

#### Part (b): Power Input (already computed in Step 4)

$$\boxed{P = 2.722 \text{ W}}$$

**Power per unit volume (sanity check):**

$$\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{2.722}{75} = 0.0363 \text{ W/m}^{3}$$

This is a **very low** value — typical ETP flocculators operate at **5–50 W/m³**.

#### Part (c): Camp's Number and Adequacy

$$G \cdot t = 6.018 \cdot (25 \cdot 60) = 6.018 \cdot 1500$$

$$\boxed{G \cdot t = 9.027 \times 10^{3}}$$

**Comment:**
The recommended Camp's number is **2 × 10⁴ to 6 × 10⁴**. Our value of **9.03 × 10³ is too low** → flocculation is **inadequate**.

**Remedial Design Action:** Increase paddle area, rotational speed, or both. Since $G \propto \sqrt{P}$ and $P \propto v_r^{\,3}$, increasing $n$ by 50% would triple the power, raising $G$ to ~10.4 s⁻¹ and $G \cdot t$ to ~1.56 × 10⁴ — still low. A better approach is to **reduce flocculator volume** (shorter t is acceptable if G is high) or **add more rotors/blades**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary for KTU

| Feature | Perikinetic | Orthokinetic |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver** | Brownian motion | Velocity gradient (shear) |
| **Dominant for** | Particles < 1 µm | Particles > 10 µm |
| **Time scale** | Seconds to minutes | 20–40 min |
| **Controllable?** | No (passive) | Yes (by $G$, $t$) |
| **Math basis** | Diffusion equation (Fick's 2nd law) | Smoluchowski collision model |
| **KTU weightage** | Theory question | Numerical (always in ESE) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Flocculation Train in an ETP

The following block-level flow illustrates how flocculation is integrated within a complete pollution-control process train.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Effluent / Influent] --> B[Equalization Tank]
    B --> C[Rapid Mix Coagulation]
    C --> D[Flash Mixer G 700 to 1000 s-1 t 1 min]
    D --> E[Flocculator G 30 to 60 s-1 t 20 to 40 min]
    E --> F[Clarifier Sedimentation]
    F --> G[Thickened Sludge to Sludge Handling]
    F --> H[Clarified Effluent to Filtration]
    H --> I[Treated Water to Disinfection or Discharge]
    G --> J[Sludge Dewatering Belt Press or Centrifuge]
    J --> K[Cake to Landfill or Incinerator]
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79
    style E fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00
    style F fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235
    style I fill:#D5E3F0,stroke:#2E75B6
```

## 4.2 Mechanical Paddle Flocculator — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FlocTank[Flocculation Basin]
        direction TB
        I1[Inlet] --> MixZone
        MixZone[Mixing Zone G 30 to 60 s-1] --> SettleZone
        SettleZone[Settling Zone Low Velocity]
        SettleZone --> O1[Outlet to Clarifier]
    end
    subgraph DriveUnit[Drive Assembly]
        Motor[Electric Motor with VFD] --> Gearbox
        Gearbox[Gear Reducer] --> Shaft
    end
    Shaft[Vertical or Horizontal Shaft] --> PaddleArray
    PaddleArray[Paddle Cluster on Both Sides] --> MixZone
    style MixZone fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00
    style DriveUnit fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
    style FlocTank fill:#F2F2F2,stroke:#7F7F7F
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selection of Flocculator Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Flocculator] --> QCheck{Flow Rate?}
    QCheck -- Small Q less than 50 m3 per hr --> Hyd[Hydraulic Baffled Flocculator]
    QCheck -- Medium to Large Q --> Mech[Mechanical Paddle Flocculator]
    Mech --> ConfigCheck{Site Constraint?}
    ConfigCheck -- Long narrow --> HorizShaft[Horizontal Shaft Paddle]
    ConfigCheck -- Compact circular --> VertShaft[Vertical Shaft Paddle]
    Hyd --> Final[Design G 30 to 60 s-1 t 20 to 40 min]
    HorizShaft --> Final
    VertShaft --> Final
    style Start fill:#FFD966,stroke:#BF8F00
    style Final fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#548235
```

## 4.4 Hydraulic vs Mechanical Flocculator — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HydraulicType[Hydraulic Baffled Flocculator]
        H1[Around the End Baffles] --> H2[Over and Under Baffles]
        H2 --> H3[Sloped Baffles]
    end
    subgraph MechanicalType[Mechanical Paddle Flocculator]
        M1[Flat Paddle Rotor] --> M2[Pitched Blade Turbine]
        M2 --> M3[Reel Type Paddle]
    end
    HydraulicType --> Compare[Power Input Sources]
    MechanicalType --> Compare
    Compare --> P1[Hydraulic: Gravity Head Loss]
    Compare --> P2[Mechanical: Electric Motor Gearbox]
    style HydraulicType fill:#DDEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
    style MechanicalType fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **coagulation** and **flocculation**. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Coagulation | Flocculation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Purpose** | Destabilize colloidal charges | Aggregate destabilized particles into flocs |
> | **Mechanism** | Chemical (charge neutralization) | Physical (collision + bridging) |
> | **Energy input** | High mixing ($G = 700 - 1000$ s⁻¹) | Gentle mixing ($G = 30 - 60$ s⁻¹) |
> | **Time** | 30 s – 1 min | 20 – 40 min |
> | **Equipment** | Flash mixer | Paddle / baffled flocculator |
> | **Output** | Microflocs (1–10 µm) | Macroflocs (0.1–3 mm) |

### Q2. Define **velocity gradient (G)** and state its typical range for flocculator design. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:** Velocity gradient $G$ is the mean temporal rate of change of velocity across a small fluid element, representing the shear intensity imparted to the fluid by the paddle. It is defined mathematically as $G = \sqrt{P / (\mu V)}$, where $P$ is the power dissipated (W), $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²), and $V$ is the flocculator volume (m³). **Typical design range is 20 – 75 s⁻¹, with optimum 30 – 60 s⁻¹** for orthokinetic flocculation of municipal and industrial effluents.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A horizontal-shaft mechanical flocculator treats $Q = 0.10$ m³/s of industrial effluent in two compartments. Each compartment has 3 paddle rotors on a common shaft. Each rotor carries 4 flat blades of dimensions 0.15 m × 0.40 m, mounted at a radius $R = 1.50$ m. Shaft speed $n = 0.04$ rev/s. Slip factor $k = 0.6$. $C_D = 1.8$, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m². Detention time $t = 30$ min. **Compute (i) power input $P$, (ii) velocity gradient $G$, (iii) Camp's number $G \cdot t$. Comment on adequacy.** (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a mechanical paddle flocculator** and explain the role of **polyelectrolyte flocculants** in enhancing floc growth. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Number of blades:** 2 compartments × 3 rotors × 4 blades = **24 blades**.

**Step 2 — Total paddle area:**
$$A = 24 \cdot (0.15 \cdot 0.40) = 24 \cdot 0.06 = 1.44 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Tip velocity:**
$$v_p = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot R = 2\pi \cdot 0.04 \cdot 1.50 = 0.3770 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Relative velocity:**
$$v_r = v_p \cdot (1 - k) = 0.3770 \cdot 0.40 = 0.1508 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Power input:**
$$P = C_D \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot \rho \cdot v_r^{\,3}}{2}$$
$$v_r^{\,3} = (0.1508)^{3} = 3.430 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$P = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{1.44 \cdot 998 \cdot 3.430 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{4.929}{2} = 1.8 \cdot 2.464$$
$$\boxed{P = 4.436 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 6 — Flocculator volume:**
$$V = Q \cdot t = 0.10 \cdot (30 \cdot 60) = 180 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 7 — Velocity gradient:**
$$\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V} = \dfrac{4.436}{(10^{-3}) \cdot 180} = \dfrac{4.436}{0.18} = 24.64$$
$$G = \sqrt{24.64} = \boxed{4.964 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Step 8 — Camp's number:**
$$G \cdot t = 4.964 \cdot 1800 = \boxed{8.935 \times 10^{3}}$$

**Adequacy comment:** Recommended $G \cdot t$ range is $2 \times 10^{4}$ to $6 \times 10^{4}$. Our value is below this — **flocculation is inadequate**. Increase $G$ (raise $n$ or add rotors) to bring $G \cdot t$ into the design window.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

- **Constructional features of paddle flocculator:** (i) Concrete/steel basin, (ii) Horizontal or vertical shaft, (iii) Flat/pitched blades, (iv) Electric motor with variable frequency drive (VFD), (v) Gearbox speed reducer, (vi) Baffles on walls to break swirl.
- **Working principle:** Motor rotates shaft at low speed; paddles drag water, creating velocity gradient $G$. Particles repeatedly collide; flocs grow.
- **Role of polyelectrolytes:** (i) Long-chain polymers **bridge** multiple particles, (ii) dramatically increase floc size and strength, (iii) reduce coagulant dose, (iv) produce **larger, denser, faster-settling flocs**, (v) particularly effective in textile/dye ETPs where colloidal dye is hard to settle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **theory of orthokinetic flocculation** and derive the design equation $G = \sqrt{P / (\mu V)}$ from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions involved. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A flocculation basin treats $Q = 0.25$ m³/s of wastewater. Design detention time is $t = 25$ min. Required $G = 50$ s⁻¹. Taking $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², **determine (i) basin volume, (ii) required power input, (iii) Camp's number and comment.** (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid is Newtonian (viscosity $\mu$ constant).
2. Shear is uniform throughout the volume (turbulent eddies assumed homogenized).
3. No slip between paddle and water (or accounted for by slip factor $k$).
4. Power dissipated is entirely by viscous action (negligible inertial losses).

**Derivation (re-derivation as in Section 3.1, expanded):**
- Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
- Power dissipated per unit volume: $\dot{W} = \tau \cdot G = \mu \cdot G^{2}$
- Total power: $P = \mu \cdot G^{2} \cdot V$
- Solving: $G = \sqrt{P / (\mu V)}$

**Orthokinetic theory (Smoluchowski):**
- Collision rate between particles of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$:
$$J = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot G \cdot (r_1 + r_2)^{3} \cdot n_1 \cdot n_2$$
- For a monodisperse system: $J = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot G \cdot r^{3} \cdot N^{2}$
- Integrated form: $N_t = N_0 / (1 + K \cdot G \cdot t)$

This **predicts that** the residual particle count $N_t$ depends on $G \cdot t$, validating Camp's criterion.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Basin volume:**
$$V = Q \cdot t = 0.25 \cdot (25 \cdot 60) = 0.25 \cdot 1500 = \boxed{375 \text{ m}^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Required power:**
From $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu V)}$:
$$P = G^{2} \cdot \mu \cdot V = (50)^{2} \cdot (10^{-3}) \cdot 375$$
$$P = 2500 \cdot 0.375 = \boxed{937.5 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 3 — Camp's number:**
$$G \cdot t = 50 \cdot (25 \cdot 60) = 50 \cdot 1500 = \boxed{7.5 \times 10^{4}}$$

**Comment:** $G \cdot t = 7.5 \times 10^{4}$ is **slightly above the upper recommended limit** of $6 \times 10^{4}$, indicating **slight risk of floc breakup**. Reduce $G$ marginally to ~45 s⁻¹ or shorten $t$ to ~22 min. Power per unit volume = $937.5 / 375 = 2.5$ W/m³ — typical for industrial ETPs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Flocculation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in $G \cdot t$ calculation (e.g., using $t = 25$ instead of $t = 1500$) — costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing relative velocity $v_r$ with tip velocity $v_p$** — never use $v_p$ directly in the power equation; always apply slip factor.
> 3. **Paddle area blunder** — counting blades on **one** side only. A rotor with 4 blades has **4 blades total**, not 2.
> 4. **Using $C_D = 1.0$ by default** — if problem states $C_D = 1.8$, USE IT. Do not assume.
> 5. **Forgetting density $\rho$** in the power equation when using the drag-coefficient form.
> 6. **Mixing up coagulation and flocculation G values** — coagulation $G$ is 700–1000 s⁻¹; flocculation $G$ is 30–60 s⁻¹. Examiners check this.
> 7. **Skipping units** in final answer — write s⁻¹ for $G$, W for $P$, m³ for $V$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLOCULATION — TOPIC CHEAT SHEET (Pin this!)**
> - **Definition:** Aggregation of destabilized colloids into settleable flocs under gentle shear.
> - **Position in treatment train:** Coagulation → **Flocculation** → Sedimentation.
> - **Two mechanisms:** Perikinetic (Brownian, < 1 µm) and Orthokinetic (G-driven, > 10 µm).
> - **Master equation:** $G = \sqrt{P / (\mu V)}$ — Camp-Stein equation (1943).
> - **Performance metric:** Camp's number $G \cdot t$, target $2 \times 10^{4}$ to $6 \times 10^{4}$.
> - **Typical design values:** $G = 30 - 60$ s⁻¹, $t = 20 - 40$ min, floc size 0.5–3 mm.
> - **Power equation (paddle):** $P = C_D \cdot A \cdot \rho \cdot v_r^{3} / 2$.
> - **Power equation (impeller):** $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot n^{3} \cdot D^{5}$.
> - **Flocculator types:** Hydraulic (baffled) and Mechanical (paddle/turbine).
> - **Chemical aids:** Cationic/anionic polyacrylamide (PAM), polyaluminium chloride (PAC), bioflocculants.
> - **Kinetic basis:** Smoluchowski's collision theory: $dN/dt = -K \cdot G \cdot N^{2}$.
> - **Influencing factors:** $G$, $t$, coagulant type & dose, pH, temperature, mixing intensity, particle concentration.
> - **Industrial uses:** ETP for textile, petrochemical, pharma, food, pulp & paper, mineral processing.
> - **Common mistakes:** Using tip velocity instead of relative velocity, wrong unit conversion, wrong paddle count.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Precipitation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Precipitation** is a physico-chemical separation process in which dissolved ionic contaminants in a waste stream are converted into **insoluble solid compounds** by the addition of a chemical reagent (precipitant), followed by their physical removal via sedimentation, filtration, or flotation. It is governed fundamentally by the **Solubility Product Principle (Ksp)** and the **Common Ion Effect**. In pollution control engineering, precipitation is the **workhorse unit operation** for removing heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cr³⁺, Ni²⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺, As³⁺/⁵⁺), hardness ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), fluoride, phosphate, and sulfate from industrial effluents before discharge or reuse. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trap-the-Ion" Mechanism > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a prison yard full of escaped "criminal ions" (dissolved metal ions like Pb²⁺ or Cd²⁺) swimming freely in a pool of water. They are dangerous and invisible. When a "police reagent" (like NaOH or lime) is sprinkled in, the ions are instantly handcuffed together with the reagent's anion to form a heavy, insoluble "ball and chain" compound (e.g., Pb(OH)₂, Cd(OH)₂). This heavy compound cannot remain dissolved — it sinks to the bottom as a thick sludge that can be scooped out, leaving the water clean. **Why does it work?** Water can only "hold" a limited amount of any salt. Once that limit (the **solubility product, Ksp**) is crossed, the system forces the excess to crash out of solution as a solid. The engineer manipulates pH and reagent dose to deliberately exceed this limit. ## 1.3 Classification of Precipitation | Type | Mechanism | Typical Reagent | Application | |------|-----------|-----------------|-------------| | **Hydroxide Precipitation** | Adjust pH to form M(OH)ₙ | Lime [Ca(OH)₂], NaOH | Heavy metals, mixed metal streams | | **Sulfide Precipitation** | Add S²⁻ source to form MSₙ | Na₂S, FeS, H₂S | Hg, Pb, Cd (very low solubilities) | | **Carbonate Precipitation** | Add CO₃²⁻ source | Na₂CO₃, CaCO₃ | Ca, Mg hardness, lead | | **Phosphate Precipitation** | Add PO₄³⁻ source | H₃PO₄, Na₃PO₄ | Pb, Zn, Cd polishing | | **Co-precipitation / Adsorptive** | Carrier precipitation | Fe(OH)₃, Al(OH)₃ flocs | Trace metals, As, F⁻ | | **Crystalline Precipitation** | Slow, ordered growth | Ca(OH)₂, CaCO₃ | Water softening | ## 1.4 Key Physical/Chemical Constants Used > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Ksp (Solubility Product Constant):** Product of ion concentrations at saturation (mol/L) > - **Ksp relationship:** For MₐXᵦ ⇌ aMⁿ⁺ + bXᵐ⁻, Ksp = [Mⁿ⁺]ᵃ[Xᵐ⁻]ᵇ > - **Molar Mass of Common Precipitants:** > - **Ca(OH)₂ = 74.09 g/mol** (lime, low cost) > - **NaOH = 40.00 g/mol** (caustic soda, soluble) > - **Na₂CO₃ = 105.99 g/mol** (soda ash) > - **Na₂S = 78.04 g/mol** (soluble sulfide) > - **FeCl₃ = 162.20 g/mol** (co-precipitant) > - **Standard state:** 25 °C, 1 atm ## 1.5 Precipitation Sequence Diagram (Block-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Wastewater
with Dissolved Metals] --> B[Equalization
and pH Adjustment Tank] B --> C[Rapid Mix
Reactor with Precipitant] C --> D[Slow Mix
Flocculation Tank] D --> E[Clarifier /
Thickener / Sedimentation] E --> F[Sludge to
Filter Press / Dewatering] E --> G[Treated Clarified
Effluent] G --> H[Polishing Filter
Sand or Membrane] H --> I[Final Discharge
or Reuse] ``` # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Solubility Product Principle For a sparingly soluble salt MₐXᵦ in equilibrium with its saturated solution: $$M_aX_b \rightleftharpoons aM^{n+} + bX^{m-}$$ The **thermodynamic solubility product** is: $$K_{sp} = [M^{n+}]^a \cdot [X^{m-}]^b$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Precipitation occurs when the **Ion Activity Product (IAP)** exceeds the Ksp: > - If **IAP < Ksp** → solution is **undersaturated** (no precipitate forms) > - If **IAP = Ksp** → solution is at **saturation** (equilibrium) > - If **IAP > Ksp** → solution is **supersaturated** → **precipitate forms** until IAP = Ksp ## 2.2 Common Ion Effect (The Engineer's Lever) When a soluble salt containing an ion already present in the dissolving salt is added, **solubility decreases**. This is the fundamental lever in precipitation engineering — by adding excess precipitant, we depress the residual metal concentration far below what pure water solubility would allow. **Example:** For Pb(OH)₂, Ksp = 1.42 × 10⁻²⁰ - In pure water: [Pb²⁺] ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ M (i.e., ~31 mg/L — non-compliant) - At pH 9.5, [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁴·⁵ M, then [Pb²⁺] = Ksp / [OH⁻]² = 1.42 × 10⁻²⁰ / 10⁻⁹ ≈ 1.42 × 10⁻¹¹ M (i.e., 0.003 mg/L — compliant!) ## 2.3 Why pH Controls Hydroxide Precipitation For metal hydroxide M(OH)ₙ: $$M(OH)_n \rightleftharpoons M^{n+} + nOH^-$$ $$K_{sp} = [M^{n+}][OH^-]^n$$ Taking the log and converting [OH⁻] to pH: $$[M^{n+}] = \frac{K_{sp}}{[OH^-]^n} = \frac{K_{sp} \cdot 10^{14n}}{[H^+]^n}$$ In logarithmic form (the famous "pH-residual metal diagram"): $$\log[M^{n+}] = \log K_{sp} - 14n + n \cdot pH$$ This is a **linear equation** of slope n when plotted as log[Mⁿ⁺] vs pH. ## 2.4 The Amphoteric Problem > [!WARNING] > Many metal hydroxides **redissolve at high pH** by forming soluble hydroxo-complexes: > - Zn(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻ → [Zn(OH)₄]²⁻ > - Al(OH)₃ + OH⁻ → [Al(OH)₄]⁻ > - Cr(OH)₃ + OH⁻ → [Cr(OH)₄]⁻ > > **Engineering consequence:** There is an **optimum pH window** for each metal — too low and the metal stays soluble, too high and it redissolves. This window is the heart of every design problem. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CHEAT SHEET — KTU Exam Must-Knows** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $K_{sp} = [M^{n+}]^a \cdot [X^{m-}]^b$ | Solubility product | (mol/L)^(a+b) | | 2 | $[M^{n+}] = \dfrac{K_{sp}}{[OH^-]^n}$ | Residual metal at pH | mol/L | | 3 | $pH = 14 - pOH = 14 + \log[OH^-]$ | pH–pOH relation | dimensionless | | 4 | $\log[M^{n+}] = \log K_{sp} - 14n + n \cdot pH$ | Linear pH-residual form | dimensionless | | 5 | $M_{ppt} = \dfrac{C \cdot V \cdot M_w}{1000 \cdot n}$ | Mass of precipitant | g | | 6 | $Dose = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{Purity \cdot 10}$ | Reagent dose (kg/d) | kg/d | | 7 | $\% R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_f}{C_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | | 8 | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | Stokes settling velocity | m/s | | 9 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$ | Velocity gradient (mixing) | s⁻¹ | | 10 | $G \cdot t$ criterion | Flocculation parameter | dimensionless (10⁴–10⁵) | > **Where:** C = concentration (mg/L), V = volume (m³), Mw = molecular weight, n = valence, g = 9.81 m/s², μ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), ρp, ρw = particle and water densities (kg/m³), d = particle diameter (m), P = power input (W). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Precipitation is the **most widely applied heavy-metal removal process** in: - **Electroplating rinse waters** (Zn, Cr, Ni, Cu) - **Battery manufacturing effluent** (Pb) - **Mining and smelter waste** (As, Hg) - **Steel pickling baths** (Fe, Cr) - **Pulp and paper** (color, lignin via coagulants) - **Petrochemical** (sulfide, mercury) - **Pharmaceutical** (residual catalysts) It is **economical**, requires **simple equipment**, and handles **mixed-metal streams** with selective pH control. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Solubility from Ksp **Statement:** For a salt MX₂ in equilibrium: MX₂(s) ⇌ M²⁺ + 2X⁻ **Step 1 — Write the Ksp expression:** $$K_{sp} = [M^{2+}] \cdot [X^-]^2$$ **Step 2 — Define molar solubility:** Let **s** be the molar solubility (mol/L). Then: $$[M^{2+}] = s \quad \text{and} \quad [X^-] = 2s$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$K_{sp} = (s)(2s)^2 = 4s^3$$ **Step 4 — Solve for s:** $$s = \left(\frac{K_{sp}}{4}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Step 5 — Generalize:** For a generic salt MₐXᵦ, with solubility s: $$[M^{n+}] = as, \quad [X^{m-}] = bs, \quad \therefore K_{sp} = a^a \cdot b^b \cdot s^{(a+b)}$$ $$\boxed{s = \left(\frac{K_{sp}}{a^a \, b^b}\right)^{\dfrac{1}{a+b}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation II — Residual Metal Concentration as a Function of pH **Statement:** For M(OH)ₙ(s) ⇌ Mⁿ⁺ + n OH⁻, derive [Mⁿ⁺] in terms of pH. **Step 1 — Ksp expression:** $$K_{sp} = [M^{n+}] \cdot [OH^-]^n$$ **Step 2 — Isolate [Mⁿ⁺]:** $$[M^{n+}] = \frac{K_{sp}}{[OH^-]^n}$$ **Step 3 — Use water autoprotolysis: Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴ at 25 °C** $$[OH^-] = \frac{K_w}{[H^+]} = \frac{10^{-14}}{[H^+]}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute [OH⁻]:** $$[M^{n+}] = \frac{K_{sp} \cdot [H^+]^n}{K_w^n} = K_{sp} \cdot 10^{14n} \cdot [H^+]^n$$ **Step 5 — Take logarithm base 10:** $$\log[M^{n+}] = \log K_{sp} + 14n + n \cdot \log[H^+]$$ **Step 6 — Recognize pH = −log[H⁺]:** $$\boxed{\log[M^{n+}] = \log K_{sp} + 14n - n \cdot pH}$$ This shows that on a log[Mⁿ⁺] vs pH plot, the **slope is −n** and the **intercept is log Ksp + 14n**. ## 3.3 Numerical Example 1 — Solubility of CaF₂ **Given:** Ksp(CaF₂) = 3.9 × 10⁻¹¹ at 25 °C. Calculate the molar solubility in **pure water**. **Step 1 — Dissociation:** CaF₂ ⇌ Ca²⁺ + 2F⁻ **Step 2 — Setup:** Let s = [Ca²⁺] and 2s = [F⁻] **Step 3 — Ksp expression:** $$K_{sp} = [Ca^{2+}] \cdot [F^-]^2 = s \cdot (2s)^2 = 4s^3$$ **Step 4 — Solve:** $$4s^3 = 3.9 \times 10^{-11}$$ $$s^3 = 9.75 \times 10^{-12}$$ $$s = (9.75 \times 10^{-12})^{1/3} = 2.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mg/L of CaF₂** (Mw = 78.07 g/mol): $$C = 2.14 \times 10^{-4} \times 78.07 \times 1000 = 16.7 \text{ mg/L as CaF}_2$$ **Answer:** Solubility in pure water ≈ 2.14 × 10⁻⁴ mol/L or 16.7 mg/L. ## 3.4 Numerical Example 2 — Common Ion Effect on CaF₂ **Given:** Same Ksp, but the solution already contains **0.10 M F⁻** (e.g., from NaF). **Step 1 — Setup:** [F⁻] ≈ 0.10 + 2s ≈ 0.10 (since s is now very small) **Step 2 — Ksp:** $$K_{sp} = [Ca^{2+}] \cdot (0.10)^2 = 3.9 \times 10^{-11}$$ **Step 3 — Solve:** $$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{3.9 \times 10^{-11}}{0.01} = 3.9 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 4 — Compare to pure water:** Solubility dropped from 2.14 × 10⁻⁴ to 3.9 × 10⁻⁹ mol/L — a reduction of **~55,000×**. > [!NOTE] > **This is the engineer's hammer — adding excess precipitant drives the residual metal to vanishingly small values.** ## 3.5 Numerical Example 3 — Lime Dose for Cd Removal **Problem:** A plating waste contains **50 mg/L Cd²⁺**, flow 100 m³/d, pH = 2. The target is **0.1 mg/L Cd²⁺** in the treated effluent. Lime [Ca(OH)₂] is 90% pure, density 2200 kg/m³. **Reaction:** Cd²⁺ + Ca(OH)₂ → Cd(OH)₂↓ + Ca²⁺ Ksp(Cd(OH)₂) = 7.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ **Step 1 — Required [OH⁻] for 0.1 mg/L residual Cd:** $$[Cd^{2+}]_{target} = \frac{0.1}{112.4} \times 1000 = 8.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$ $$[OH^-]^2 = \frac{K_{sp}}{[Cd^{2+}]} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-15}}{8.9 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.09 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$[OH^-] = 8.99 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L} \implies pOH = 4.05 \implies pH \approx 9.95$$ **Step 2 — pH must be raised from 2 to ~10 (use lime)** Assume current [H⁺] = 10⁻² M (pH=2). Target [H⁺] = 10⁻¹⁰ M (pH=10). H⁺ to neutralize per litre: $$\Delta[H^+]_{neutralized} \approx 10^{-2} - 10^{-10} \approx 10^{-2} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 3 — Total lime for neutralization + precipitation:** Moles of Ca(OH)₂ needed for H⁺ neutralization = ½ × moles of H⁺ = 0.5 × 10⁻² = 5 × 10⁻³ mol/L (since Ca(OH)₂ gives 2 OH⁻) Moles of Ca(OH)₂ for Cd precipitation = 8.9 × 10⁻⁷ mol/L (1:1 stoichiometry) **Total Ca(OH)₂ = 5 × 10⁻³ + 8.9 × 10⁻⁷ ≈ 5 × 10⁻³ mol/L** **Step 4 — Mass per day:** $$M = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L} \times 74.09 \text{ g/mol} \times 10^3 \text{ L/m}^3 \times 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} = 37.05 \text{ kg/d of pure lime}$$ **Step 5 — Accounting for 90% purity:** $$\text{Commercial lime required} = \frac{37.05}{0.90} = 41.16 \text{ kg/d}$$ > **Answer: ~41.2 kg/d of commercial lime** to treat this stream. ## 3.6 Numerical Example 4 — Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law) **Given:** Cd(OH)₂ floc particle diameter d = 50 μm, ρp = 2480 kg/m³, ρw = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. **Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law:** $$v_s = \frac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$$ **Step 2 — Plug in values (d = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m):** $$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2480 - 1000) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times 1480 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.01804}$$ $$v_s = \frac{3.63 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01804} = 2.01 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to m/h:** $$v_s = 2.01 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 7.25 \text{ m/h}$$ **Step 4 — Check Reynolds number** (to confirm laminar flow, Re < 0.3): $$Re = \frac{\rho_w v_s d}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.01 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.10$$ **✓ Stokes' Law is valid (Re < 0.3).** A 2 m deep clarifier requires **~17 minutes** to settle. ## 3.7 Numerical Example 5 — Sludge Volume and Disposal **Given:** From Example 3, 50 mg/L Cd²⁺ in 100 m³/d = 5 kg/d Cd removed as Cd(OH)₂ (Mw = 146.43 g/mol, Cd = 112.4). **Step 1 — Mass of Cd(OH)₂ sludge:** $$\text{Cd(OH)}_2 = 5 \text{ kg Cd} \times \frac{146.43}{112.4} = 6.51 \text{ kg/d dry solid}$$ **Step 2 — Wet sludge at 2% solids:** $$\text{Wet sludge volume} = \frac{6.51}{0.02} \times \frac{1}{1000} \text{ m}^3 = 0.326 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} = 326 \text{ L/d}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** Sludge handling costs often **exceed** the chemical cost. Always design with sludge minimization (e.g., sludge recycle, filter press) in mind. ## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Precipitant | Criterion | Lime [Ca(OH)₂] | NaOH | Na₂CO₃ | Na₂S | |-----------|---------------|------|--------|------| | Cost per kg | **Low** | High | Medium | Medium | | Solubility | Low (slurry) | High | High | High | | Sludge volume | **High** | Low | Medium | Low | | pH control ease | Moderate | **Easy** | Easy | Moderate | | Treated water hardness | **High** | Low | Medium | Low | | Amphoteric issue at high pH | **Severe** | Severe | Mild | Mild | | Residual achievable | ~1 mg/L | ~0.5 mg/L | ~1 mg/L | **<0.01 mg/L** | ## 3.9 Worked Example 6 — pH Selection for Mixed Metals > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multi-metal streams** (e.g., Cu, Zn, Ni from a brass mill) require a **compromise pH** because each metal has a different optimum. Sequential precipitation is often used: > - Fe, Cr, As at pH 4–6 > - Cu, Zn at pH 7–9 > - Mn, Ni at pH 9.5–10.5 > - Cd, Pb at pH 10–11 # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete Industrial Precipitation Treatment Train ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INLET[Influent Characterization] INF[Raw Process Wastewater
Contains Dissolved Metals and Acids] end subgraph EQ[Stage 1: Equalization] EQT[Equalization Basin
Flow and Load Dampening] pHM[pH / ORP Monitoring Station] end subgraph STAGE2[Stage 2: Reagent Addition and Rapid Mix] CHEM[Chemical Feed System
Lime or NaOH Storage] RM[Rapid Mix Reactor
G Greater Than 700 per s
Detention 1 to 5 min] end subgraph STAGE3[Stage 3: Flocculation] FLOC[Slow Mix Flocculator
G Equals 20 to 75 per s
Detention 20 to 30 min] POLY[Polymer Coagulant Aid Injection] end subgraph STAGE4[Stage 4: Solid Liquid Separation] CLAR[Clarifier or Thickener
Surface Overflow Less Than 25 m per d] FLT[Multimedia or Sand Filter] end subgraph STAGE5[Stage 5: Sludge Handling] SLG[Sludge Hopper] FP[Filter Press or Centrifuge] CAKE[Cake to Secure Landfill] end subgraph OUTLET[Stage 6: Effluent Polishing] EFF[Clarified Treated Water] UV[UV or Carbon Polishing] DIS[Compliant Discharge to ETP Outfall] end INF --> EQT EQT --> pHM pHM --> RM CHEM --> RM RM --> FLOC POLY --> FLOC FLOC --> CLAR CLAR --> FLT CLAR --> SLG SLG --> FP FP --> CAKE FLT --> EFF EFF --> UV UV --> DIS ``` ## 4.2 pH-Log[Residual Metal] Solubility Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LEFT[Acidic Region pH 1 to 5] direction TB L1[Fe3plus Soluble as Fe3plus] L2[Al3plus Soluble] L3[Zn2plus Soluble] L4[Cr3plus Soluble] end subgraph MID[Precipitation Window pH 7 to 10] direction TB M1[Fe3plus Hydroxide Precipitates at pH 3] M2[Al3plus Hydroxide at pH 5] M3[Cu2plus Hydroxide at pH 6] M4[Zn2plus Hydroxide at pH 8] M5[Ni2plus Hydroxide at pH 9] M6[Cd2plus Hydroxide at pH 10] end subgraph RIGHT[Alkaline Region pH 11 to 14] direction TB R1[Zn Redissolves as ZnOH4 2minus] R2[Al Redissolves as AlOH4 minus] R3[Cr Redissolves as CrOH4 minus] end LEFT --> MID --> RIGHT ``` ## 4.3 Reactor Configuration Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Reactor Selection Logic] A --> B{High Flow Low Concentration} B -->|Yes| C[Complete Mix CSTR
Back Mix Reactor] B -->|No| D{Tight Effluent Spec} D -->|Yes| E[Plug Flow PFR
with Multiple Feed Points] D -->|No| F[Series CSTR Cascade
2 to 4 Stages] C --> G[Final pH Adjust and Polishing] E --> G F --> G ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree for Precipitant Selection ```mermaid flowchart TB S[Select Precipitant] S --> P1{Target Residual Less Than 0.1 mg per L} P1 -->|Yes| P2[Tight Spec or Toxic Metal] P2 -->|Yes| P3[Use Na2S or FeS] P2 -->|No| P4[Sequential pH Precipitation with NaOH] P1 -->|No| P5{High Volume Sludge OK} P5 -->|Yes| P6[Use Lime Ca OH 2
Lowest Cost] P5 -->|No| P7[Use NaOH with Sludge Thickening] P3 --> Z[Recommended Process] P4 --> Z P6 --> Z P7 --> Z ``` ## 4.5 Sludge Management Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR SL[Spent Precipitate Sludge] SL --> R1{Stabilization Required} R1 -->|Yes| R2[Stabilize with Cement Kiln Dust or Fly Ash] R1 -->|No| R3[Thicken and Dewater] R2 --> R4[Filter Press] R3 --> R4 R4 --> R5{Recovery Possible} R5 -->|Yes| R6[Metal Recovery Pyrometallurgy] R5 -->|No| R7[Secure Hazardous Landfill Disposal] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define the term "solubility product" (Ksp) and explain its significance in the precipitation process.** **Model Answer:** The **solubility product (Ksp)** of a sparingly soluble ionic salt MₐXᵦ is the equilibrium constant for the dissolution reaction MₐXᵦ(s) ⇌ aMⁿ⁺ + bXᵐ⁻, defined as: $$K_{sp} = [M^{n+}]^a \cdot [X^{m-}]^b$$ evaluated at saturation. **Significance in precipitation:** 1. It sets the **maximum concentration** of ions that can coexist in solution. 2. By comparing the **Ion Activity Product (IAP)** to Ksp, we predict whether precipitation will occur (IAP > Ksp), whether the system is at equilibrium (IAP = Ksp), or whether dissolution will occur (IAP < Ksp). 3. It is the **fundamental design equation** — engineers calculate the required pH and reagent dose from Ksp to achieve a target residual metal concentration. 4. Smaller Ksp ⇒ lower residual metal ⇒ better effluent quality. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: What is meant by the "common ion effect"? How is it exploited in hydroxide precipitation of heavy metals?** **Model Answer:** The **common ion effect** is the suppression of the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt when a soluble compound containing one of its ions is added to the solution. It arises directly from Le Chatelier's principle and the Ksp constraint. **Exploitation in hydroxide precipitation:** - When precipitating a metal Mⁿ⁺ as M(OH)ₙ by adding NaOH or Ca(OH)₂, the [OH⁻] in solution becomes very high. - Because Ksp = [Mⁿ⁺][OH⁻]ⁿ is constant, an increase in [OH⁻] forces [Mⁿ⁺] to drop dramatically. - For example, residual Pb²⁺ at pH 9.5 is ~10⁻¹¹ M, ~10⁵ times lower than its solubility in pure water. - **Thus, by simply raising pH, the engineer reduces metal concentration to sub-ppb levels without sophisticated equipment.** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** A wastewater stream contains **100 mg/L Cu²⁺** (M = 63.5, Ksp of Cu(OH)₂ = 4.8 × 10⁻²⁰). Calculate: > (i) the molar solubility of Cu(OH)₂ in pure water, > (ii) the pH at which the residual Cu²⁺ is **0.5 mg/L**, and > (iii) the mass of NaOH (pure) required per m³ of wastewater to achieve this pH, assuming the initial pH is 7.0. > **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **complete process flow diagram** of a typical chemical precipitation plant for heavy-metal removal from a plating industry effluent. Discuss the function of **each unit** and the typical design parameters. #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Step 1 — Molar solubility in pure water:** For Cu(OH)₂ ⇌ Cu²⁺ + 2OH⁻, let s = [Cu²⁺] and 2s = [OH⁻]: $$K_{sp} = s \cdot (2s)^2 = 4s^3 = 4.8 \times 10^{-20}$$ $$s^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-20} \implies s = 2.29 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 2 — pH for 0.5 mg/L residual Cu:** $$[Cu^{2+}] = \frac{0.5}{63.5} \times 1000 = 7.87 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$ $$[OH^-]^2 = \frac{K_{sp}}{[Cu^{2+}]} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-20}}{7.87 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.10 \times 10^{-18}$$ $$[OH^-] = 2.47 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mol/L} \implies pOH = 8.61 \implies pH = 5.39$$ **Step 3 — Mass of NaOH per m³ to raise pH from 7 to 5.39:** At pH 7 → [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ M. At pH 5.39 → [OH⁻] = 2.47 × 10⁻⁹ M. The pH must be **decreased** (current pH is more basic). Hence, we need to **add acid** or, in hydroxide-precipitation, we add NaOH only to **ensure excess OH⁻**. **Recalculating rigorously** (proper interpretation): - The Cu²⁺ will react with OH⁻ to consume 2 OH⁻ per Cu²⁺ removed. - Moles of Cu²⁺ to remove per m³ = 100/63.5 × 1/1000 × 1000 = 1.575 mol. - OH⁻ consumed in precipitation = 2 × 1.575 = 3.15 mol. - Additionally, [OH⁻] must end at 2.47 × 10⁻⁹ × 1000 = 2.47 × 10⁻⁶ mol per m³. - **Plus** we must also account for **acidic pH** in real wastewater (often pH 2–4 for plating rinse). Here the problem states initial pH = 7 (neutral), so no neutralization H⁺ exists. - Total NaOH needed = 3.15 + 2.47 × 10⁻⁶ ≈ **3.15 mol per m³** $$\text{Mass of NaOH} = 3.15 \text{ mol} \times 40 \text{ g/mol} = \textbf{126 g/m}^3$$ #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Plant Description** (refer to **Section 4.1** flow diagram): 1. **Collection and Equalization Tank** — dampens flow variation; pH probe + lime/NaOH feed for pre-conditioning. 2. **Rapid Mix Chamber** — residence time 30–60 s; G > 700 s⁻¹; introduces NaOH to raise pH to the target (often 9–10 for Cu). 3. **Slow-Mix Flocculator** — residence 20–30 min; G = 20–75 s⁻¹; polymer flocculant added to grow settleable flocs. 4. **Clarifier/Thickener** — surface overflow rate 15–25 m³/m²·d; separates the precipitate sludge. 5. **Sludge Handling** — thickened to 2–5% solids, then dewatered in a **filter press**; cake to hazardous-waste landfill. 6. **Polishing Sand Filter** — removes residual flocs; final pH adjustment to 6.5–7.5 before discharge. 7. **Monitoring** — pH, TSS, residual metal, and flow recorded continuously. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **omit the sludge handling line** entirely, losing 2–3 marks. A complete process must show **sludge to filter press → cake to disposal** as a parallel branch. Also, students forget to mention **pH adjustment of the final effluent to 6.5–7.5** before discharge. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **hydroxide precipitation, sulfide precipitation, and carbonate precipitation** for the removal of heavy metals from industrial wastewater. Tabulate the relative advantages, limitations, and the specific application niche of each. Discuss why **sulfide precipitation** is preferred for mercury (Hg²⁺) and lead (Pb²⁺). > **(b) [7 Marks]** An effluent contains **200 mg/L Pb²⁺** at pH = 3. Using **lime Ca(OH)₂ (90% pure)** as the precipitant, calculate: > (i) the theoretical lime dose (kg/m³) to raise pH to **9.5**, > (ii) the theoretical residual Pb²⁺ at this pH (Ksp of Pb(OH)₂ = 1.42 × 10⁻²⁰, M(Pb) = 207.2, M(Ca(OH)₂) = 74.09). > (iii) If the clarifier has a surface overflow rate of 20 m³/m²·d, design its diameter for a flow of 500 m³/d. #### **Model Solution to (a):** | Feature | Hydroxide | Sulfide | Carbonate | |---------|-----------|---------|-----------| | Reagent | NaOH, Ca(OH)₂ | Na₂S, FeS, H₂S | Na₂CO₃, CaCO₃ | | pH range | 8–11 | 6–9 | 8–10 | | Sludge volume | High (with lime) | **Low** | Medium | | Ksp range (typical) | 10⁻¹⁴ to 10⁻²⁵ | **10⁻²⁵ to 10⁻⁵³** | 10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻¹⁴ | | Toxicity | Low | **High (H₂S)** | Low | | Residual achievable | 0.1–1 mg/L | **<0.01 mg/L** | 0.5–5 mg/L | | Cost | Lowest (lime) | High | Medium | | Best for | **Mixed streams, bulk removal** | **Hg, Pb, As polishing** | **Water softening, Pb recovery** | **Why sulfide for Hg and Pb:** - The **Ksp values are extremely small** (HgS: 10⁻⁵³, PbS: 10⁻²⁸), giving theoretical residuals in the **ppt** (parts per trillion) range. - Hydroxide precipitates redissolve if pH drifts; sulfides are **insoluble across a wide pH range (1–12)**, making them operationally robust. - Sulfide sludge is **dense, low-volume, and stable**, ideal for toxic metal disposal. - **Caveat:** Strict **H₂S safety** is mandatory — scrubbers, enclosed reactors, and gas monitors are essential. #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Raise pH from 3 to 9.5:** At pH 3: [H⁺] = 10⁻³ M; at pH 9.5: [H⁺] = 10⁻⁹·⁵ ≈ 3.16 × 10⁻¹⁰ M. The OH⁻ to be added = (10⁻³ − 3.16 × 10⁻¹⁰) × 1000 L/m³ ≈ **1.0 mol/m³** of OH⁻ (effectively neutralizing the H⁺). Since Ca(OH)₂ provides 2 OH⁻: moles of Ca(OH)₂ for neutralization = 0.5 mol/m³. **Step 2 — OH⁻ for precipitation of Pb²⁺:** Moles of Pb²⁺ = (200 / 207.2) × (1 / 1000) × 1000 = 0.965 mol/m³. OH⁻ consumed = 2 × 0.965 = 1.93 mol/m³. Moles of Ca(OH)₂ for precipitation = 1.93 / 2 = 0.965 mol/m³. **Step 3 — Total Ca(OH)₂ = 0.5 + 0.965 = 1.465 mol/m³** $$M_{pure} = 1.465 \times 74.09 = 108.5 \text{ g/m}^3 = 0.1085 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Commercial lime (90% pure):** $$M_{commercial} = \frac{0.1085}{0.90} = \textbf{0.121 kg/m}^3$$ **Step 5 — Residual Pb²⁺ at pH 9.5:** [OH⁻] at pH 9.5 = 10⁻⁴·⁵ = 3.16 × 10⁻⁵ M. $$[Pb^{2+}] = \frac{K_{sp}}{[OH^-]^2} = \frac{1.42 \times 10^{-20}}{(3.16 \times 10^{-5})^2} = \frac{1.42 \times 10^{-20}}{10^{-9}} = 1.42 \times 10^{-11} \text{ M}$$ $$C_{Pb} = 1.42 \times 10^{-11} \times 207.2 \times 1000 = 2.94 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mg/L} \approx \textbf{3 microgram per L}$$ **Step 6 — Clarifier diameter:** $$A = \frac{Q}{SOR} = \frac{500}{20} = 25 \text{ m}^2$$ $$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 25}{\pi}} = \sqrt{31.83} = \textbf{5.64 m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > 1. Many students forget to **account for the reagent purity** (90%) — losing 1 mark. > 2. The **two contributions** to lime demand (neutralization + precipitation) must be **both computed** — do not skip one. > 3. Clarifier design: use the formula **A = Q/SOR**, not volume. Diameter is **not** just from detention time. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST** - ✅ **Precipitation** converts dissolved ionic pollutants into insoluble solids using a precipitant, then separates them. - ✅ **Solubility Product Ksp** = [cation]^a [anion]^b at saturation. - ✅ **Precipitation occurs when Ion Activity Product > Ksp.** - ✅ **Common ion effect** is the engineer's main lever — adding excess precipitant drops the residual metal concentration dramatically. - ✅ **pH controls hydroxide precipitation** because [OH⁻] = 10^(pH−14). - ✅ The **log[Mⁿ⁺] vs pH plot** is a straight line of slope −n. - ✅ **Amphoteric hydroxides** (Zn, Al, Cr, Pb) redissolve at high pH → optimum pH window exists. - ✅ **Lime is cheapest** but generates large sludge; **NaOH is cleanest** but costlier; **Na₂S gives lowest residuals** but is toxic. - ✅ **Order of metal precipitation with pH:** Fe³⁺ (3) < Al³⁺ (5) < Cu²⁺ (6) < Zn²⁺ (8) < Ni²⁺ (9) < Cd²⁺ (10). - ✅ **Sulfide precipitation** preferred for Hg, Pb, As, Cd because of ultra-low Ksp. - ✅ **Stokes' Law** governs settling: $v_s = g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d^2 / 18\mu$ — valid only for Re < 0.3. - ✅ **Flocculation criterion:** G·t between 10⁴ and 10⁵. - ✅ **Sludge handling** is often the **lifetime cost driver** — always design for sludge minimization. - ✅ **Common precipitant formulae:** - Lime: Ca(OH)₂, Mw = 74.09 - Caustic: NaOH, Mw = 40.00 - Soda ash: Na₂CO₃, Mw = 105.99 - Sodium sulfide: Na₂S, Mw = 78.04 - ✅ **Design must always include:** pH adjustment → rapid mix → flocculation → clarification → sludge dewatering → effluent polishing.

topics.title: Biological Treatment Methods: Activated Sludge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Biological Treatment Methods: Activated Sludge # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment process** in which a **mixed microbial consortium (biomass/flocs)** is kept in continuous contact with organic substrate in an **aerated reactor**, after which the biomass is separated from the treated effluent in a **secondary settling tank (clarifier)** and a portion is **recycled (Return Activated Sludge, RAS)** to maintain the required **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)** concentration in the aeration tank. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Activated Sludge is a *core* secondary biological treatment process used when influent BOD is moderate to high and land area is limited. It is one of the most frequently asked topics in KTU ESE. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Microbial Restaurant"** > > Think of the aeration tank as a **self-service buffet** for bacteria. Wastewater (the food) flows in continuously. Compressed air (the energy source, like electricity for a restaurant) is supplied to keep things moving and the bacteria (the customers) alive. The bacteria consume the dissolved organic matter (food) and multiply. The "excess customers" (waste activated sludge) are removed, while a fixed number of "regulars" (return sludge) are sent back to keep the restaurant full. The clarified water (clean effluent) leaves the system. > > - **Food (Substrate) →** BOD / COD in wastewater > - **Customers (Biomass) →** MLVSS (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids) > - **Buffet (Reactor) →** Aeration Tank > - **Waiter clearing tables (Clarifier) →** Secondary Settling Tank > - **Regulars coming back (RAS) →** Return Activated Sludge ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology The following metrics form the **backbone of KTU numerical questions** on this topic: | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids | **MLSS** | 1500 – 4000 | mg/L | | Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids | **MLVSS** | 1200 – 3200 | mg/L | | Food to Microorganism Ratio | **F/M** | 0.2 – 0.5 | day⁻¹ | | Mean Cell Residence Time (Sludge Age) | **θc / SRT** | 5 – 15 | days | | Hydraulic Retention Time | **HRT / θ** | 4 – 8 | hours | | Sludge Volume Index | **SVI** | 80 – 150 | mL/g | | Recycle Ratio | **R** | 0.25 – 0.75 | dimensionless | | Dissolved Oxygen in Aeration Tank | **DO** | 1.5 – 2.0 | mg/L | | BOD Removal Efficiency | **η** | 85 – 95 | % | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Engineering Constants used in ASP design:** > - **Y (Yield coefficient)** = 0.4 – 0.8 mg VSS/mg BOD₅ > - **Kd (Endogenous decay coefficient)** = 0.04 – 0.075 day⁻¹ > - **Oxygen demand per unit BOD removed** ≈ **1.42** kg O₂/kg BOD₅ (theoretical, for C₅H₇NO₂ cell formula) --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The activated sludge process is built on **four sequential functional units**: 1. **Influent Distribution & Primary Clarification** - Raw sewage enters; large settleable solids and grit are removed in a primary clarifier. - This *reduces the organic load* on the biological stage. 2. **Aeration Tank (Bioreactor)** - **Aeration** is provided by either surface aerators (mechanical) or diffused air systems (blowers + diffusers). - Functions of aeration: - Supply **O₂** for aerobic metabolism. - Provide **mixing** to keep biomass in suspension. - Strip dissolved CO₂ and prevent anoxic zones. - Microbial reactions (simplified): $$\text{Organic matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New cells (sludge)}$$ 3. **Secondary Clarifier (Settling Tank)** - Biomass–water mixture flows from the aeration tank to a quiescent clarifier. - **Sludge flocs settle** under gravity; clarified supernatant forms the **treated effluent**. - The settled sludge is split into two streams: - **RAS (Return Activated Sludge):** pumped back to the aeration tank. - **WAS (Waste Activated Sludge):** bled off to maintain steady-state MLSS. 4. **Sludge Handling** - WAS is sent to thickeners, digesters, and dewatering units. - The recycled RAS is the "seed culture" — it keeps the system biologically active. ## 2.2 Why and How: Microbial Metabolism | Pathway | Electron Acceptor | End Product | Occurs When | |---|---|---|---| | Aerobic respiration | O₂ | CO₂ + H₂O | DO > 1.5 mg/L | | Anoxic (denitrification) | NO₃⁻ | N₂ gas | DO ≈ 0 | | Anaerobic (fermentation) | Organic matter | CH₄, CO₂, VFAs | No O₂, no NO₃⁻ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The activated sludge process is designed to operate predominantly in the AEROBIC regime.** Facultative bacteria dominate the floc structure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | Aeration tank design | | 2 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (Sludge Age) | Process control | | 3 | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | Reactor volume from HRT | Sizing | | 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | Sizing | | 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD removal efficiency | Performance | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{X \cdot \theta} - K_d$ | Substrate utilization (Lawrence-McCarty) | Design / Effluent prediction | | 7 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y (S_0 - S_e)}{\theta (1 + K_d \theta_c)}$ | Biomass concentration in aerator | Effluent + SRT given | | 8 | $P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q (S_0 - S_e)$ | Sludge production rate (kg/d) | WAS calculation | | 9 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + K_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | Sludge production | | 10 | $\text{RAS ratio } R = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | Recycle ratio | Clarifier mass balance | | 11 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30}}{X_{MLSS}}$ (mL/g) | Sludge Volume Index | Settleability check | | 12 | $O_2 = 1.42 Q (S_0 - S_e) + 4.57 Q (NO_3^- \text{ produced})$ | Oxygen requirement (kg/d) | Blower sizing | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > - $Q$ = influent flow (m³/d) > - $S_0, S_e$ = influent & effluent soluble BOD₅ (mg/L) > - $X$ = MLVSS in aeration tank (mg/L) > - $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³) > - $X_r$ = RAS concentration (mg/L) > - $Q_w, X_w$ = waste sludge flow & concentration; $Q_e, X_e$ = effluent flow & solids ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Municipal Sewage Treatment:** Activated sludge is the **workhorse of urban STPs** across Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Thiruvananthapuram municipal plants). - **Industrial Effluents:** Used in **petrochemical, refinery, food & beverage, pharmaceutical, and pulp & paper** industries for organic load reduction before discharge. - **Pre-treatment** to advanced processes (membrane bioreactors, tertiary RO/UV). - **Resource recovery:** Modern ASP variants (e.g., **A²/O, MBBR, SBR**) integrate **biological nutrient removal (BNR)** — simultaneous N and P removal. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Substrate Mass Balance on Aeration Tank (Steady-State, Completely Mixed) **Assumptions:** Steady state, completely mixed flow regime, no biomass in influent or effluent (X₀ = Xₑ = 0), biomass loss only via WAS and effluent. **General Mass Balance:** $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption} $$ At steady state, **Accumulation = 0**. Writing for substrate (S, in BOD units): $$ 0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e - V \left(\frac{dS}{dt}\right)_{utilization} $$ The rate of substrate utilization by Monod-type kinetics (substrate-limited) is: $$ \left(\frac{dS}{dt}\right)_{utilization} = \frac{\mu_{max} S \cdot X}{Y(K_s + S)} $$ At high $S \gg K_s$ (typical for ASP), the rate simplifies to a first-order-in-biomass form: $$ \left(\frac{dS}{dt}\right)_{utilization} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y} X = k X $$ where $k$ is the specific substrate removal rate (day⁻¹). **Therefore:** $$ 0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e - V k X $$ Rearranging: $$ Q (S_0 - S_e) = k X V $$ **Dividing both sides by $X V$:** $$ \frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \cdot \theta} = k $$ This is the famous **specific substrate utilization rate (U)**: $$ U = \frac{F/M}{\eta} = k $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** At steady state, the rate at which each unit of biomass removes substrate equals a constant $k$ — the *operational kinetic constant*. KTU problems often give $k$ and ask to find aeration volume. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Biomass Mass Balance & SRT Equation Biomass (active MLVSS, $X$) mass balance on the aeration tank: $$ 0 = Q X_0 + V \left(\frac{dX}{dt}\right)_{growth} - Q X_e - Q_w X_r $$ For an activated sludge system, $X_0 \approx 0$ and $X_e \approx 0$ (clarifier removes biomass). Net growth rate: $$ \left(\frac{dX}{dt}\right)_{net} = -K_d X + Y \left(\frac{dS}{dt}\right)_{utilization} $$ Substituting the substrate utilization term $kX$: $$ \left(\frac{dX}{dt}\right)_{net} = -K_d X + Y k X = X (Y k - K_d) $$ The mass balance becomes: $$ 0 = V X (Y k - K_d) - Q_w X_r $$ Solving for the sludge age (SRT) $\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{Q_w X_r}$: $$ \boxed{\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y k - K_d} $$ > This is the **Lawrence–McCarty fundamental relationship** between SRT and specific substrate removal rate.
