It forms the basis of *all* activated sludge design. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effluent Substrate as a Function of SRT From derivation 2, the SRT equation rearranged: $$ k = \frac{1}{Y}\left(\frac{1}{\theta_c} + K_d\right) $$ From derivation 1, $k$ is also given by: $$ k = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \theta} $$ Equating the two and solving for $S_e$: $$ \frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \theta} = \frac{1}{Y}\left(\frac{1}{\theta_c} + K_d\right) $$ Using the identity $\theta_c / \theta = X / (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Y^{-1}$ (from rearrangement), and substituting $X$ expression from formula 7 of the cheat sheet: $$ \boxed{S_e = \dfrac{K_s (1 + K_d \theta_c)}{\theta_c (Y k - K_d) - 1}} $$ A **simpler, more KTU-friendly form** (assuming $K_s \ll S$ and using $U = 1/\theta_c$ formulation): $$ S_e = \frac{S_0}{1 + k X \theta} $$ Or in terms of design: $$ \boxed{S_e = \dfrac{K_s \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} K_d \cdot \text{(time factor)}\right)}{\theta_c (Y k - K_d) - 1}} $$ **Numerical Example (KTU-type):** **Given:** $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d; $S_0 = 250$ mg/L; $X = 2000$ mg/L; $V = 1500$ m³; $Y = 0.6$; $K_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹. **Step 1 — Compute F/M:** $$ F/M = \frac{Q S_0}{V X} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 250}{1500 \times 2000} = \frac{2{,}500{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000} = 0.833 \; \text{d}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Compute HRT:** $$ \theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1500}{10{,}000} = 0.15 \; \text{d} = 3.6 \; \text{h} $$ **Step 3 — Find $k$ from $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Yk - K_d$** — but SRT is not given. Assume $\theta_c = 10$ d. $$ \frac{1}{10} = 0.6 \cdot k - 0.06 \Rightarrow 0.6 k = 0.16 \Rightarrow k = 0.267 \; \text{d}^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Effluent BOD:** $$ S_e = S_0 - k X \theta = 250 - (0.267)(2000)(0.15) = 250 - 80 = 170 \; \text{mg/L} $$ **Step 5 — Removal Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{250 - 170}{250} \times 100 = 32\% $$ > The low efficiency suggests $\theta_c$ is too low; increasing SRT to 20 d would give a more realistic 80%+ removal. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Question Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---| | Aeration tank volume | Q, S₀, X, F/M | V | $V = \dfrac{Q S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$ | | Effluent BOD | V, Q, X, θc, Y, Kd | Se | Derivation 3 | | Sludge production | Q, S₀, Se, θc, Y, Kd | Px | $P_x = \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S_e)}{1 + K_d \theta_c}$ | | Recycle ratio | X, Xr | R | $R = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | | Oxygen required | Q, S₀–Se | O₂ | $1.42 Q (S_0 - S_e)$ | | Aeration time | V, Q | θ | $V/Q$ | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR INF["Raw Influent
Q, S0"] --> PRI["Primary Clarifier
Removes settleable solids"] PRI --> AER["Aeration Tank
Volume V, MLSS X
DO 1.5-2.0 mg/L
HRT theta = V/Q"] AER --> SEC["Secondary Clarifier
Sludge settles
SVI 80-150 mL/g"] SEC --> EFF["Treated Effluent
Se, X_e approx 0
Discharged to env"] SEC --> SPLIT{"Sludge Split
at Clarifier Bottom"} SPLIT --> RAS["Return Activated Sludge
RAS, Concentration Xr
Flow QR = R times Q"] SPLIT --> WAS["Waste Activated Sludge
WAS, Concentration Xr
Flow Qw
Sent to Thickener"] RAS --> AER AER -.->|"Air Supply"| AER WAS --> DGA["Sludge Handling
Thickener to Digester
to Dewatering"] classDef reactor fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0366a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#032b4d classDef clarifier fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3d8b3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1d401d classDef sludge fill:#f9d6d6,stroke:#a30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#400000 classDef out fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#a37400,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2900 class AER reactor class PRI,SEC clarifier class RAS,WAS,SPLIT,DGA sludge class INF,EFF out ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram of Microbial Reactions ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Wastewater Influent
Contains organic carbon
BOD, COD, N, P"] --> STEP1["1 Aeration Tank
Aerobic Zone
DO greater than 1.5 mg/L
F/M around 0.2 to 0.5"] STEP1 --> STEP2["2 Microbial Metabolism
Substrate plus O2
produces CO2 plus H2O
plus new cells"] STEP2 --> STEP3["3 Biomass Growth
Active MLVSS X
Yield Y 0.4 to 0.8
Decay Kd 0.04 to 0.075"] STEP3 --> STEP4["4 Floc Formation
Bacteria embedded in EPS
Settle in clarifier
SVI 80 to 150"] STEP4 --> OUT["Effluent plus RAS plus WAS
Closed loop system
Sludge age theta_c 5 to 15 d"] classDef reaction fill:#dde7ff,stroke:#1f4ea1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a1e3d classDef output fill:#e6f7d6,stroke:#3a8a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#173d17 class STEP1,STEP2,STEP3,STEP4 reaction class START,OUT output ``` ## 4.3 Mass Balance Nomenclature Map ```mermaid flowchart TB Q_in["Q_in: Influent flow"] --> AER2["Aeration Tank
Volume V, MLVSS X"] AER2 --> Q_eff["Q_eff: Effluent flow
Substrate Se"] AER2 --> Q_RAS["Q_RAS: Return sludge
Concentration Xr"] AER2 --> Q_WAS["Q_WAS: Waste sludge
Concentration Xr"] Q_RAS -.->|"| R = QRAS over Q |"| AER2 classDef eq fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2e00 classDef tank fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0366a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#032b4d class AER2 tank class Q_in,Q_eff,Q_RAS,Q_WAS eq ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Activated Sludge Process. List any four operating parameters. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The activated sludge process is a **suspended-growth aerobic biological wastewater treatment** process in which a **mixed culture of microorganisms** is brought into contact with organic matter in wastewater inside an **aerated reactor (aeration tank)**. The biomass–water mixture is then sent to a **secondary clarifier** where the **biomass is separated**; a portion is **recycled (RAS)** and the excess (**WAS**) is wasted. **Four operating parameters:** 1. **MLSS / MLVSS** (mixed liquor suspended / volatile suspended solids) — typically 1500–4000 mg/L. 2. **F/M ratio** (Food to Microorganism) — 0.2 – 0.5 d⁻¹. 3. **SRT (Sludge Age / θc)** — 5 – 15 days. 4. **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time / θ)** — 4 – 8 hours. *(Also acceptable: DO, SVI, recycle ratio R.)* ### Question 2: What is SVI? State its significance and the desirable range. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** is defined as the **volume (in mL) of settled sludge** obtained from **1 gram of MLSS** after 30 minutes of settling in a 1-litre graduated cylinder. $$ \text{SVI} = \frac{V_{30} \text{ (mL/L)}}{X_{MLSS} \text{ (mg/L)}} \times 1000 \; \text{(mL/g)} $$ **Significance:** SVI is an **indicator of sludge settleability** and a key process control parameter. **High SVI (>150 mL/g)** indicates **bulking sludge** (poor settling, often caused by filamentous bacteria), while **very low SVI (<50 mL/g)** indicates **pin-point floc** or straggler flocs. **Desirable range:** **80 – 150 mL/g**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) With a neat flow diagram, explain the Activated Sludge Process. What is the role of return activated sludge? (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO1, CO2]** **Model Solution:** **1. Flow Description (4 marks):** The activated sludge process consists of the following units: - **Primary Clarifier:** Removes settleable solids and reduces BOD load. - **Aeration Tank (Bioreactor):** Wastewater is aerated continuously using either **surface mechanical aerators** or **diffused-air systems (blowers + fine bubble diffusers)**. The tank is maintained at **DO 1.5–2.0 mg/L** to support aerobic microorganisms. The mixed liquor (MLSS) is kept in **suspension** by aeration-induced turbulence. - **Secondary Clarifier:** The MLSS–effluent mixture enters a quiescent clarifier where **sludge flocs settle** under gravity. The **clarified supernatant** is discharged as **treated effluent**. - **Sludge Recycle System:** The settled sludge is divided into: - **Return Activated Sludge (RAS):** Recirculated to the aeration tank. - **Waste Activated Sludge (WAS):** Removed for further treatment. **2. Role of Return Activated Sludge (3 marks):** - **Maintains a high, constant MLSS concentration** in the aeration tank independent of influent flow variations. - **Provides the "seed" microbial population** to metabolize incoming organic matter — ensures the system remains biologically active. - **Controls the Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) ratio** by diluting the incoming substrate with a dense, active biomass. - **Enables decoupling of HRT (short, ~6 h) from SRT (long, 5–15 d)** — the hallmark feature of activated sludge. #### (b) An activated sludge plant treats 15,000 m³/d of wastewater with influent BOD of 280 mg/L. The MLVSS in the aeration tank is 2500 mg/L and the desired F/M is 0.30 d⁻¹. Calculate: (i) Aeration tank volume, (ii) HRT, (iii) Volumetric BOD loading. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO3, CO4]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $Q = 15{,}000$ m³/d; $S_0 = 280$ mg/L; $X = 2500$ mg/L; $F/M = 0.30$ d⁻¹. **(i) Aeration Tank Volume (V):** The F/M ratio is: $$ \frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q S_0}{V X} $$ Rearranging for V: $$ V = \frac{Q S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \frac{15{,}000 \times 280}{2500 \times 0.30} $$ $$ V = \frac{4{,}200{,}000}{750} = 5600 \; \text{m}^3 $$ **(ii) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT, θ):** $$ \theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{5600}{15{,}000} = 0.373 \; \text{d} = 8.96 \; \text{h} \approx 9 \; \text{h} $$ **(iii) Volumetric BOD Loading:** $$ \text{BOD Loading} = \frac{Q S_0}{V} = \frac{15{,}000 \times 280}{5600} = 750 \; \text{g/m}^3 \cdot \text{d} = 0.75 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot \text{d} $$ **Verification of F/M:** $$ F/M = \frac{750}{2500} = 0.30 \; \text{d}^{-1} \;\checkmark $$ **Final Answers:** - $V = 5600$ m³ - $\theta \approx 9$ h - BOD loading = **0.75 kg/m³·d** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### (a) Derive the relationship between Mean Cell Residence Time (θc) and the specific substrate utilization rate (k) in an activated sludge process. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2022 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO1, CO2]** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (3 marks):** 1. Steady-state, completely mixed aeration tank. 2. Soluble substrate only; no biomass in the influent. 3. Effluent suspended solids $X_e \approx 0$ (clarifier captures biomass). 4. Aeration tank is the **only CSTR** in the system. 5. Substrate utilization follows first-order kinetics w.r.t. biomass. 6. Biomass decay follows endogenous kinetics ($K_d$). **Biomass Mass Balance on Aeration Tank (4 marks):** At steady state: $$ 0 = \underbrace{V \mu_{net} X}_{\text{net growth}} - \underbrace{Q_w X_r - Q_e X_e}_{\text{wastage}} $$ Assuming $X_e \approx 0$: $$ 0 = V X (Y k - K_d) - Q_w X_r $$ Dividing both sides by $V X$: $$ 0 = Y k - K_d - \frac{Q_w X_r}{V X} $$ But the **sludge age** is defined as: $$ \theta_c = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_r} $$ Substituting: $$ Y k - K_d = \frac{1}{\theta_c} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y k - K_d} $$ This is the **Lawrence–McCarty kinetic relationship** fundamental to ASP design. #### (b) A wastewater treatment plant operates with the following data: Q = 20,000 m³/d, S₀ = 300 mg/L, Se = 30 mg/L, X = 3000 mg/L, Y = 0.5, Kd = 0.05 d⁻¹, θc = 10 d. Calculate: (i) Aeration tank volume, (ii) HRT, (iii) Observed yield and excess sludge production per day. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2022 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO3, CO4]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d; $S_0 = 300$ mg/L; $S_e = 30$ mg/L; $X = 3000$ mg/L; $Y = 0.5$; $K_d = 0.05$ d⁻¹; $\theta_c = 10$ d. **Step 1 — Specific substrate utilization rate (k):** $$ k = \frac{1}{Y}\left(\frac{1}{\theta_c} + K_d\right) = \frac{1}{0.5}\left(\frac{1}{10} + 0.05\right) = 2 \times 0.15 = 0.30 \; \text{d}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Specific substrate utilization (U):** $$ U = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \theta} = k \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \cdot k} = \frac{300 - 30}{3000 \times 0.30} = \frac{270}{900} = 0.30 \; \text{d} = 7.2 \; \text{h} $$ **(i) Aeration Tank Volume:** $$ V = Q \cdot \theta = 20{,}000 \times 0.30 = 6000 \; \text{m}^3 $$ **(ii) HRT:** $$ \theta = 0.30 \; \text{d} = 7.2 \; \text{h} \;\checkmark $$ **(iii) Observed Yield:** $$ Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + K_d \theta_c} = \frac{0.5}{1 + 0.05 \times 10} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333 \; \text{mg VSS/mg BOD}_5 $$ **Excess Sludge Production:** $$ P_x = Y_{obs} \cdot Q (S_0 - S_e) = 0.333 \times 20{,}000 \times (300 - 30) \; \text{(in g/d)} $$ $$ P_x = 0.333 \times 20{,}000 \times 270 = 0.333 \times 5{,}400{,}000 = 1{,}800{,}000 \; \text{g/d} = 1800 \; \text{kg/d} $$ **Final Answers:** - $V = 6000$ m³ - $\theta = 7.2$ h - $Y_{obs} = 0.333$; $P_x = \mathbf{1800 \; kg/d}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert mg/L × m³/d → kg/d by dividing by 1000. Many students lose 1–2 marks on this. > 2. **MLSS vs MLVSS:** Most formulas use **MLVSS (X)** for biological activity, not MLSS. Using MLSS directly is acceptable only if specified. MLVSS ≈ 0.7–0.8 × MLSS. > 3. **SRT vs HRT:** They are NOT the same. **θc (SRT) = total biomass / biomass wasted per day** (typically 5–15 d). **θ (HRT) = V/Q** (typically hours). > 4. **Lawrence–McCarty derivation:** Writing the kinetic equation without showing the **biomass balance** explicitly will lose at least 2 marks. > 5. **Distinguish F/M from volumetric loading:** $F/M$ has units d⁻¹ (mass substrate/mass biomass per day); volumetric loading has units kg/m³·d. > 6. **Diagram marks:** In Part (a) of any 7-mark question, a **labeled flow diagram** is mandatory. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Activated Sludge Process** - ✅ **Definition:** Suspended-growth, aerobic biological treatment with biomass recycle. - ✅ **Three main units:** Aeration tank → Secondary clarifier → Sludge recycle loop. - ✅ **MLSS range:** 1500–4000 mg/L; **MLVSS** is the biologically active fraction. - ✅ **F/M ratio:** $F/M = Q S_0 / (V X)$; typical **0.2 – 0.5 d⁻¹**. - ✅ **SRT (θc):** $\theta_c = VX / (Q_w X_r)$; **5 – 15 days** for conventional ASP. - ✅ **HRT (θ):** $\theta = V/Q$; **4 – 8 hours** typically. - ✅ **Lawrence–McCarty equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y k - K_d$ — *the* design backbone. - ✅ **Effluent BOD:** $S_e = S_0 - k X \theta$ (first-order approximation). - ✅ **Observed Yield:** $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + K_d \theta_c}$. - ✅ **Sludge production:** $P_x = Y_{obs} Q (S_0 - S_e)$. - ✅ **Oxygen demand:** $1.42$ kg O₂ / kg BOD₅ removed (theoretical). - ✅ **SVI:** **80 – 150 mL/g** is good; >150 = bulking, <50 = pin floc. - ✅ **Recycle ratio:** $R = X / (X_r - X)$; typically **0.25 – 0.75**. - ✅ **DO in aeration tank:** **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L** (must be aerobic). - ✅ **Variants to know:** Conventional, Completely Mixed, Extended Aeration, SBR, MBR, A²/O. - ✅ **BOD removal efficiency:** **85 – 95%** in well-designed plants. > **Module 1 Anchor Concept:** The **Activated Sludge Process** is the *gold standard* of secondary biological treatment — its **kinetic design (Lawrence–McCarty model)**, **mass balance on aeration tank**, and **sludge recycle logic** are the three most tested elements in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

topics.title: Biofilm Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biofilm Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Biofilm Reactor** is a biological wastewater treatment unit in which the active microbial population is **immobilized on a solid support medium** (stationary or moving) as a thin, gelatinous layer called a *biofilm*, rather than being freely suspended in the bulk liquid as in the conventional activated sludge process. The wastewater flows over, through, or past this support, and dissolved pollutants (substrates) are removed by **diffusion into the biofilm followed by microbial degradation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A biofilm is a **structured consortium of microorganisms** embedded in a self-produced matrix of **Extracellular Polymeric Substances (EPS)** — primarily polysaccharides, proteins, and nucleic acids — that adheres to an inert solid surface. The EPS matrix is responsible for the mechanical stability, adhesion, and protective barrier function of the biofilm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sticky River Pebble" Model

Imagine you are standing by a fast-flowing river. A smooth, clean pebble thrown into the stream stays shiny for a few days, but after a couple of weeks, a slippery **greenish-brown layer** of algae, fungi, and bacteria coats it. That slippery layer is a **biofilm**. The river water brings dissolved food (substrates) which diffuse into this layer and are consumed by the microbes. The pebble acts as the **support medium**, the algae-bacteria consortium acts as the **biological catalyst**, and the river represents the **wastewater feed**. A biofilm reactor works on exactly the same principle at an industrial scale: a high-surface-area support (rocks, plastic media, rotating discs, or floating carriers) hosts the biofilm, and polluted water flowing through it gets cleaned by the attached microbial ecosystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Biofilms?** The EPS matrix protects microbes from toxic shocks, hydraulic washouts, and predators, allowing the system to retain a **very high biomass concentration** (10,000 – 30,000 mg/L VSS equivalent) compared to ~2,000 – 4,000 mg/L in activated sludge. This translates into smaller reactor volumes and higher organic loading rates.

## 1.3 Classification of Biofilm Reactors

| Category | Reactor Type | Key Feature |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Attached Growth — Fixed** | Trickling Filter | Wastewater sprayed over stationary rock/plastic media |
| **Attached Growth — Rotating** | Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) | Discs rotate between wastewater and air |
| **Submerged Fixed Bed** | Biological Aerated Filter (BAF) | Aerated packed bed; dual filtration and biological action |
| **Submerged Moving Bed** | Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR) | Free-floating plastic carriers in aerated tank |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Fluidized Bed Biofilm Reactor (FBBR) | Sand/activated carbon particles fluidized by upflow |
| **Membrane Coupled** | Membrane Biofilm Reactor (MBfR) | Biofilm on gas-transfer membrane for special substrates |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Step Removal Mechanism in a Biofilm

For any substrate (organic carbon, ammonia, nitrate, etc.) to be biodegraded inside a biofilm reactor, it must successfully complete the following three sequential steps:

1. **External Mass Transfer (Bulk Liquid → Biofilm Surface)**
   The substrate is transported from the bulk liquid across the hydrodynamic boundary layer to the outer surface of the biofilm by molecular diffusion and convection. Governed by the **external mass transfer coefficient**, $k_L$ (m/s).

2. **Internal Mass Transfer (Biofilm Surface → Interior)**
   The substrate diffuses through the water-filled pores of the EPS matrix, driven by the concentration gradient. Governed by the **molecular diffusivity in biofilm**, $D_f$ (m²/s), which is typically 50–80% of the diffusivity in pure water due to matrix tortuosity.

3. **Biological Reaction (Microbial Consumption)**
   The substrate is metabolized by the entrapped microorganisms. The rate is described by **Monod kinetics**:
   $$r_s = -\frac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \frac{S \cdot X_f}{K_s + S}$$
   where $r_s$ is the volumetric substrate consumption rate (g/m³·s), $X_f$ is the active biomass density inside the biofilm (g/m³), and $Y$ is the yield coefficient (g biomass/g substrate).

> [!TIP]
> **The Limiting Step is the Slowest One.** In engineering design, the slowest of these three steps controls the overall removal rate. This principle is fundamental to the KTU 2024 question pattern on "rate-limiting biofilm mechanisms."

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation No. | Formula | Description / Application | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $J = -D_f \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law — substrate flux through biofilm | g/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod growth kinetics | 1/s |
| 3 | $q = q_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Specific substrate utilization rate | 1/s |
| 4 | $J = \sqrt{2 \cdot D_f \cdot k \cdot S_s}$ | Steady-state flux for **deep biofilm** (fully penetrated at surface) | g/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $J = k \cdot L_f \cdot \dfrac{S_s}{K_s + S_s}$ | Steady-state flux for **shallow biofilm** (linear regime) | g/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $r_s = -\dfrac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot X_f}{K_s + S}$ | Substrate consumption rate (Monod) | g/(m³·s) |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{J_{actual}}{J_{no\,resistance}}$ | Internal effectiveness factor (ratio of actual to ideal flux) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L_{eff} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D_f \cdot S_s}{k \cdot K_s}}$ | Effective penetration depth of substrate into biofilm | m |
| 9 | $A_{spec} = \dfrac{A_{media}}{V_{reactor}}$ | Specific surface area of media — critical for design | m²/m³ |
| 10 | $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT for biofilm washout prevention | days |

**Where:**
- $S$ = substrate concentration (g/m³), $S_s$ = substrate concentration at biofilm surface (g/m³)
- $D_f$ = molecular diffusivity in biofilm (m²/s) — typically $0.5 \cdot D_w$ to $0.8 \cdot D_w$
- $k = \mu_{max} \cdot X_f / Y$ — maximum volumetric reaction rate constant (g/(m³·s))
- $L_f$ = biofilm thickness (m) — typically 50 – 500 µm
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (g/m³)
- $J$ = substrate flux (g/(m²·s))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The two limiting flux equations (Eq. 4 and Eq. 5) are the **most frequently tested pair** in Part B questions. Students must clearly state the assumption (deep vs shallow) before applying either equation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Biofilm reactors are indispensable in process industries wherever wastewater contains:
- **High organic loads** (food, dairy, brewery, pulp & paper) — MBBR / FBBR preferred
- **Toxic / recalcitrant compounds** (pharma, petrochemical, pesticide) — biofilm's EPS matrix protects microbes
- **Low-strength ammonia** (municipal secondary treatment) — nitrification BAF
- **Specialized gas-fed substrates** (hydrogen, methane) — Membrane Biofilm Reactor (MBfR) for nitrate/perchlorate reduction

In the KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment framework, this topic directly maps to **CO2: "Design biological treatment units for industrial effluents."**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Substrate Flux Through a Biofilm (KTU High-Yield)

**Objective:** To obtain the closed-form expression for the substrate flux $J$ entering a planar, uniform biofilm under steady-state conditions.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Differential Mass Balance

Consider a differential slab of biofilm of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$ from the biofilm surface. Under steady state, the rate of substrate diffusion in = rate of substrate consumption by reaction. Therefore:

$$D_f \cdot \frac{d^2 S}{dx^2} = r_s$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Monod Rate Expression

Using the Monod expression for substrate consumption (where biomass density $X_f$ is assumed constant throughout the biofilm):

$$r_s = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot X_f}{Y} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} = k \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

where the lumped parameter $k = \mu_{max} \cdot X_f / Y$ has units of g/(m³·s).

The governing equation becomes:

$$D_f \cdot \frac{d^2 S}{dx^2} = k \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions

- **BC-1 (biofilm surface):** $S = S_s$ at $x = 0$
- **BC-2 (biofilm base or fully depleted):** Two cases emerge
  - **Deep biofilm:** $S = 0$ and $\dfrac{dS}{dx} = 0$ at some finite depth
  - **Fully penetrated biofilm:** $S = S_s$ at $x = L_f$ (thin biofilm, no depletion)

### Step 4 — Multiply Both Sides by $2 \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dx}$ and Integrate

This is the standard trick to convert the non-linear 2nd-order ODE into a 1st-order integrable form. Multiplying:

$$2 \cdot D_f \cdot \frac{dS}{dx} \cdot \frac{d^2 S}{dx^2} = 2 \cdot k \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{dS}{dx}$$

Recognizing the left side as $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{dS}{dx}\right)^2\right]$:

$$D_f \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left[\left(\frac{dS}{dx}\right)^2\right] = 2k \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{dS}{dx}$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ to depth $x$:

$$D_f \cdot \left[\left(\frac{dS}{dx}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{dS}{dx}\right)_{x=0}^2\right] = 2k \cdot \int_{S_s}^{S} \frac{S'}{K_s + S'} \, dS'$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral on the Right

$$\int_{S_s}^{S} \frac{S'}{K_s + S'} \, dS' = \left[S' - K_s \cdot \ln(K_s + S')\right]_{S_s}^{S} = (S - S_s) - K_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{K_s + S}{K_s + S_s}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Deep-Biofilm Condition

For a **deep biofilm**, the substrate is completely consumed at a finite depth, so the concentration gradient vanishes at the bottom: $\dfrac{dS}{dx} = 0$ where $S = 0$. At the surface, the flux is $J = -D_f \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0}$. Therefore:

$$-D_f \cdot \left(\frac{dS}{dx}\right)_{x=0}^2 = 2k \cdot \left[(0 - S_s) - K_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{K_s + 0}{K_s + S_s}\right)\right]$$

$$D_f \cdot \left(\frac{dS}{dx}\right)_{x=0}^2 = 2k \cdot \left[S_s + K_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{K_s + S_s}{K_s}\right)\right]$$

### Step 7 — Extract the Flux

Since $J^2 = D_f^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{dS}{dx}\right)_{x=0}^2$ and $J = -D_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{dS}{dx}\right)_{x=0}$:

$$\boxed{J = \sqrt{2 \cdot D_f \cdot k \cdot \left[S_s + K_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{K_s + S_s}{K_s}\right)\right]}}$$

### Step 8 — Special Case Simplification (First-Order Limit)

When $S_s \gg K_s$ (typical of high-strength industrial effluents), $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_s + S_s}{K_s}\right) \approx \ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_s}{K_s}\right)$, and for very high $S_s$, the logarithmic term is dominated by $S_s$. The flux simplifies to the **classic deep-biofilm form**:

$$J = \sqrt{2 \cdot D_f \cdot k \cdot S_s}$$

This is the form most commonly required in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum SRT for Biofilm Washout (Sanctuary Effect)

**Objective:** Prove that biofilm reactors can retain slower-growing microbes (e.g., nitrifiers) that would be washed out of a CSTR.

**Step 1.** In a CSTR, the biomass washout condition is $\theta_c = 1/\mu$ (i.e., when HRT equals reciprocal of growth rate). For a biofilm, biomass is **physically retained**; only the substrate is hydraulically washed out.

**Step 2.** The effective SRT for biofilm biomass is decoupled from the HRT:

$$\theta_c^{biofilm} = \frac{X_f \cdot L_f \cdot A_{media}}{Q \cdot S_{removed}/Y} \quad [\text{days}]$$

**Step 3.** Since $L_f$ and $X_f$ are independent of hydraulic loading, the biofilm can operate at very low HRT (high $Q$) while still maintaining $\theta_c > 1/\mu_{max}$ for slow-growing nitrifiers ($\mu_{max} \approx 0.77$/day at 20 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sanctuary" Principle** — Because biofilm biomass is not subject to hydraulic washout, biofilm reactors can host microbial populations with doubling times of **3 – 7 days** (nitrifiers, anammox) at hydraulic retention times of only **2 – 6 hours**. This is the engineering basis of all modern nitrification/denitrification MBBR plants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Substrate Removal Mechanism in a Biofilm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Liquid Wastewater] --> B[External Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer]
    B --> C[Biofilm Outer Surface]
    C --> D[EPS Matrix Zone of Diffusivity Df]
    D --> E[Active Microbial Layer Monod Kinetics]
    E --> F[Inactive / Deep Layer]
    F --> G[Attached Support Medium Rock Plastic Sand]
    G --> H[Reactor Wall or Fluidized Bed Bottom]
    C --> I[Effluent Stream After Consumption]
    B -.->|External Resistance kl| C
    D -.->|Internal Resistance Df| E
    E --> J[Metabolic End Products CO2 H2O Biomass]
    J --> K[Sloughing Detachment Biomass Loss]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an MBBR Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Influent Inlet] --> SC[Screen Chamber Removes Grit and Large Solids]
    SC --> EQ[Equalization Tank Buffers Flow and Load]
    EQ --> MBBR1[MBBR Aeration Tank 1 Carries Moving Plastic Carriers]
    MBBR1 --> MBBR2[MBBR Aeration Tank 2 Nitrification Stage]
    MBBR2 --> SED[Secondary Clarifier]
    SED --> EFF[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    SED --> RAS[Return Activated Sludge]
    MBBR1 --> AIR1[Fine Bubble Diffuser Air Supply]
    MBBR2 --> AIR2[Fine Bubble Diffuser Air Supply]
    AIR1 --> B1[Blower 1]
    AIR2 --> B2[Blower 2]
    RAS --> WAS[Waste Activated Sludge to Sludge Handling]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — Rate-Limiting Regimes in Biofilm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Steady State Substrate Profile] --> check1{Is Lf Greater Than Effective Penetration Depth}
    check1 -- YES --> deep[Deep Biofilm Regime J equals sqrt 2 Df k Ss]
    check1 -- NO --> check2{Is Ss Much Greater Than Ks}
    check2 -- YES --> shallow[Shallow Biofilm Linear Regime J equals k Lf Ss over Ks plus Ss]
    check2 -- NO --> general[General Non Linear Solution J equals sqrt Expression]
    deep --> out[Overall Rate Controlled by Internal Diffusion]
    shallow --> out
    general --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Aid:**
> - In **Diagram 4.1**, follow the solid arrows left → right to trace the path of a substrate molecule from influent to sloughing.
> - In **Diagram 4.2**, the MBBR plant is divided into two biofilm zones: the first (MBBR1) handles **carbonaceous BOD removal** (heterotrophs), while the second (MBBR2) handles **nitrification** (autotrophs like *Nitrosomonas*).
> - In **Diagram 4.3**, the flowchart classifies the reactor into one of three operating regimes — a critical first step before any KTU numerical problem is solved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a biofilm. List any four advantages of biofilm reactors over the conventional activated sludge process.**

**Model Answer:**
A biofilm is a **structured microbial community** attached to a solid surface and embedded in a self-produced matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS). Four advantages of biofilm reactors over activated sludge:
1. **Higher biomass concentration** (10,000–30,000 mg/L VSS equivalent) → smaller reactor volume.
2. **Decoupling of SRT from HRT** → slow-growing microbes (nitrifiers, anammox) can be retained even at short hydraulic retention times.
3. **Better tolerance to toxic shocks** because the EPS matrix acts as a diffusion barrier and protective layer.
4. **No sludge recirculation required** (in MBBR/RBC) → simpler operation and lower pumping cost.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Trickling Filter and a Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC). Mention any three points of difference.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Trickling Filter | Rotating Biological Contactor |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mode of contact | Wastewater sprayed over **stationary** media | Discs **rotate** between water and air |
| Oxygen supply | Natural draft or forced air from below | Atmospheric oxygen absorbed by rotating discs |
| Sloughing | Periodic, often uncontrolled | Continuous, gentle sloughing due to rotation |
| Energy input | Pumping/distribution only | Motor for shaft rotation |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle and constructional features of a **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)**. Discuss the role of each stage in multi-staged RBC systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater with **BOD₅ = 250 mg/L** and **flow rate Q = 1000 m³/day** is to be treated in an RBC. The first-stage disc surface area required is given by the empirical relationship:
$$A_1 = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{N_1 \cdot F}$$
where $N_1 = 2.5$ g BOD/(m²·day) and $F = 0.85$ (a temperature correction factor). Calculate $A_1$ and the total disc surface area for a 4-stage RBC if the loading factors are $N_1 = 2.5$, $N_2 = 1.5$, $N_3 = 1.0$, and $N_4 = 0.8$ g BOD/(m²·day). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):
The RBC consists of a series of **closely spaced circular discs** (polystyrene or PVC) mounted on a horizontal shaft. The shaft rotates slowly (1–2 rpm) with about 40% of the disc area submerged in the wastewater trough. As the discs rotate:
- Microbes grow as a **biofilm** on the disc surfaces.
- When submerged, the biofilm **adsorbs and oxidizes** organic matter (BOD).
- When exposed to air, the biofilm **absorbs oxygen** for aerobic degradation.
- The rotation also causes **gentle sloughing** of excess biomass, which is carried out with the effluent.

**Constructional features:** drive motor, shaft, support bearings, end plates, trough, baffles, and protective enclosure.

**Multi-staged RBC:** Typically 3 to 4 stages in series. **Stage 1** handles the highest BOD load and is dominated by **heterotrophs**. **Stage 2** continues carbonaceous removal and transitions to **nitrification**. **Stages 3 and 4** are dominated by **nitrifiers** (autotrophs) and sometimes **denitrifiers** if anoxic zones are engineered. The staged design prevents the faster-growing heterotrophs from outcompeting the slower nitrifiers in the downstream zones.

#### Model Solution to (b):
**Sub-part (i):** Calculate first-stage area $A_1$.

$$A_1 = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{N_1 \cdot F} = \frac{1000 \cdot 250}{2.5 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{250000}{2.125} \approx 117647 \text{ m}^2$$

**Sub-part (ii):** Total area for 4-stage system.

For a multi-stage RBC, the BOD removal in each stage is calculated sequentially. Using the loading factor relationship, the area for each stage is:

| Stage | $S_{in}$ (mg/L) | $S_{out}$ (mg/L) | $N_i$ (g/m²·day) | Area $A_i$ (m²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 250 | 100 | 2.5 | 117,647 |
| 2 | 100 | 50 | 1.5 | 55,556 |
| 3 | 50 | 25 | 1.0 | 41,667 |
| 4 | 25 | 10 | 0.8 | 17,857 |

**Total disc area = 117,647 + 55,556 + 41,667 + 17,857 ≈ 232,727 m²**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature correction factor $F$** — without it, $A_1$ will be ~15% lower than the correct value.
> 2. **Using the same loading factor for all stages** — the loading factors $N_i$ decrease down the train as BOD drops; this is a frequently missed point.
> 3. **Not mentioning the role of sloughing in RBC** — examiners specifically look for this as it distinguishes RBC from a trickling filter.
> 4. **Confusing RBC with a rotating disc filter** — RBC is a biological, not physical, separation process.

### **Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*
**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of substrate removal in a biofilm reactor**, clearly distinguishing between **external mass transfer**, **internal mass transfer**, and **biological reaction**. Sketch the typical substrate concentration profile across the biofilm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A completely mixed biofilm reactor receives wastewater with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L of glucose. The biofilm has a thickness $L_f = 200 \,\mu m$, diffusivity $D_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s, and biological rate parameters: $\mu_{max} = 0.30$/h, $X_f = 40{,}000$ mg/L, $Y = 0.45$ mg biomass/mg substrate, and $K_s = 20$ mg/L. The bulk-liquid substrate concentration at steady state is $S = 80$ mg/L. Determine whether the biofilm is operating in the **deep biofilm regime or the shallow biofilm regime**, and calculate the steady-state substrate flux $J$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):
The mechanism of substrate removal in a biofilm reactor is a **three-step serial process**:

**Step 1 — External Mass Transfer:** Substrate moves from the well-mixed bulk liquid to the outer surface of the biofilm across the **hydrodynamic boundary layer**. The driving force is the concentration difference $(S_b - S_s)$, and the resistance is the external mass transfer coefficient $k_L$. The flux is:
$$J_{ext} = k_L \cdot (S_b - S_s)$$

**Step 2 — Internal Mass Transfer:** Substrate diffuses through the water-filled pores of the EPS matrix, from the biofilm surface to its interior. Fick's law governs this step. The driving force is the concentration gradient $dS/dx$, and the resistance is the molecular diffusivity $D_f$ (which is lower than in pure water due to matrix tortuosity).

**Step 3 — Biological Reaction:** Within the biofilm, the substrate is metabolized by the entrapped microbial cells according to Monod kinetics, with simultaneous diffusion and reaction. Substrate concentration decreases from $S_s$ at the surface to nearly zero at depth, where no active biomass remains.

**Sketch of the substrate profile:** A steep exponential-like curve starting at $S_s$ on the biofilm surface, dropping through the "active layer" to near zero at the attachment interface. (The liquid-side boundary layer appears as a thin linear drop from $S_b$ in the bulk to $S_s$ at the surface.)

#### Model Solution to (b):
**Step 1:** Calculate the lumped rate constant $k$.

$$k = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot X_f}{Y} = \frac{0.30 \, \text{h}^{-1} \cdot 40000 \, \text{mg/L}}{0.45} = 26666.67 \, \text{mg/(L·h)}$$

Convert to consistent units (g/m³·h): $k = 26666.67$ g/(m³·h).

**Step 2:** Convert $D_f$ to m²/h:

$$D_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s} = 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{h}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the effective penetration depth using the dimensionless modulus.

The criterion for distinguishing regimes is the **dimensionless Thiele modulus analog**:

$$\phi^2 = \frac{k \cdot L_f^2}{D_f \cdot K_s} = \frac{26666.67 \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{3.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 20}$$

Compute numerator: $26666.67 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-8} = 1.0667 \times 10^{-3}$

Compute denominator: $3.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 20 = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}$

$$\phi^2 = \frac{1.0667 \times 10^{-3}}{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 14.81$$

**Since $\phi^2 \gg 1$, the biofilm is operating in the DEEP biofilm regime** (diffusion-limited).

**Step 4:** Apply the deep biofilm flux equation (Eq. 4 of the formula sheet).

Using $S_s \approx S = 80$ mg/L (assuming negligible external resistance for simplicity):

$$J = \sqrt{2 \cdot D_f \cdot k \cdot S_s}$$

$$J = \sqrt{2 \cdot (3.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 26666.67 \cdot 80}$$

$$J = \sqrt{2 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 26666.67 \cdot 80} = \sqrt{15.36 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ g/(m}^2\cdot\text{h)}$$

$$J \approx \sqrt{0.01536} \approx 0.124 \text{ g/(m}^2\cdot\text{h)} = 124 \text{ mg/(m}^2\cdot\text{h)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — $D_f$ is often given in cm²/s but $k$ in g/(m³·h). Convert all quantities to a single consistent set (SI preferred) before substitution.
> 2. **Choosing the wrong regime equation** — the KTU examiner checks whether you computed the Thiele modulus (or compared $L_f$ with $L_{eff}$) before picking Eq. 4 vs Eq. 5.
> 3. **Ignoring the assumption $S_s \approx S$** — if the question gives the external $k_L$ separately, you must first compute $S_s$; otherwise, explicitly state the assumption.
> 4. **Not sketching the substrate profile** — in part (a), the sketch carries ~2 marks and is often left out.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Biofilm = attached microbial consortium + EPS matrix on a solid support.** EPS = polysaccharides + proteins + nucleic acids.
- **Three mass transfer / reaction steps in series:** External → Internal → Biological. The slowest step rate-limits the system.
- **Monod kinetics inside biofilm:** $r_s = (\mu_{max}/Y) \cdot S \cdot X_f / (K_s + S)$.
- **Two critical flux equations (must memorize):**
  - Deep biofilm: $J = \sqrt{2 D_f k S_s}$
  - Shallow biofilm: $J = k L_f S_s / (K_s + S_s)$
- **Steady-state assumption** in derivations: diffusion in = reaction consumption.
- **Typical $D_f$** is 50–80% of diffusivity in pure water due to EPS tortuosity.
- **Typical biofilm thickness $L_f$**: 50 – 500 µm; very thick biofilms become diffusion-limited.
- **Sanctuary effect:** SRT is decoupled from HRT → slow growers (nitrifiers, anammox) can be retained.
- **MBBR** uses free-floating plastic carriers (~70% fill) in an aerated tank; no sludge recirculation.
- **RBC** uses rotating discs (40% submergence) providing natural aeration and gentle sloughing.
- **Trickling filters** use stationary media with intermittent or continuous wastewater dosing; rely on natural/forced draft aeration.
- **BAF** is a submerged packed bed combining biological oxidation with physical filtration in a single unit.
- **Industrial preferences:** MBBR for high BOD loads; BAF for tertiary nitrification; FBBR for toxic industrial effluents.
- **Key design parameter:** Specific surface area of media $A_{spec}$ (m²/m³) — higher $A_{spec}$ → smaller reactor.
- **Minimum SRT** for biofilm (no washout of attached biomass): $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_{max} - k_d)$ — applies to suspended growth in the bulk, not the biofilm itself.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** For any 14-mark biofilm numerical, begin with (1) the regime check, (2) consistent SI units, (3) the correct flux equation, and (4) a clean conclusion with units. Examiners award partial marks generously when the methodology is clearly visible even if arithmetic slips occur.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anaerobic Digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Anaerobic Digestion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is an engineered biochemical process in which a consortium of **facultative and obligate anaerobic microorganisms** decomposes biodegradable organic substrates in the **complete absence of molecular oxygen (O₂)**, converting them into a mixture of **biogas** (primarily CH₄ and CO₂), **digested sludge**, and **trace levels of hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) and ammonia (NH₃)**. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OECHT613 (Pollution Control in Process Industries)**, anaerobic digestion is classified as a **biological treatment technology** under the broader umbrella of **Solid Waste Management and Industrial Effluent Treatment**. The process is governed by the **BOD/COD reduction efficiency**, **methane yield per unit mass of volatile solids destroyed**, and **volatile solids (VS) destruction efficiency**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *“Anaerobic digestion is the controlled microbial degradation of organic matter in an oxygen-free environment, producing biogas and stabilized biosolids.”* ### Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standard Values): | Parameter | Standard Metric | Symbol/Unit | |---|---|---| | Methane yield (theoretical) | **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed** (at STP) | Y_CH₄ | | COD reduction efficiency | **60–90 %** for municipal sludge | η_COD | | VS destruction efficiency | **40–60 %** | η_VS | | Calorific value of biogas | **20–25 MJ/m³** | Q_cal | | Methane fraction in biogas | **55–70 %** | %CH₄ | | Biogas yield (glucose, theoretical) | **0.75 m³/kg glucose** | Y_bg | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Sealed Garbage Bag in Your Kitchen"** > > Imagine you tightly seal a wet pile of kitchen waste (vegetable peels, leftover food) inside a thick plastic bag and leave it untouched for a month. Because no air can enter, the bacteria that thrive without oxygen start to break the food down. Soon, the bag swells up like a balloon as **invisible, flammable gas (biogas)** builds up inside. If you were to puncture it safely, you could actually light the escaping gas — that is **methane (CH₄)**. The mushy, dark, earth-smelling residue left behind is **digested sludge**, an excellent fertilizer. > > This is precisely what happens inside an industrial **anaerobic digester** — only it is carefully engineered (temperature-controlled, well-mixed, pH-balanced) to maximize gas production and pollutant destruction. It is the **opposite of aerobic treatment** (which needs continuous air supply and produces CO₂ + sludge), making AD ideal for **high-strength industrial wastewater** and **organic solid waste**. --- ## 1.3 Why Anaerobic Digestion? (Engineering Relevance) - **Energy recovery:** Produces **renewable biogas** (substitute for natural gas). - **Sludge stabilization:** Reduces pathogens and odor in biosolids. - **Low footprint:** No aeration → **60–90 % lower energy consumption** compared to aerobic processes. - **Greenhouse gas mitigation:** Captures methane that would otherwise be released from open dumps/landfills. - **Carbon-neutral waste management** aligned with **UN SDG 13 (Climate Action)**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Sequential Microbial Stages of AD Anaerobic digestion is **NOT a single reaction** — it is a **syntrophic cascade** performed by at least **four cooperating microbial guilds**. Each stage has distinct intermediate products and reaction kinetics. ### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (Rate-Limiting for Particulate Wastes) - **Microbes:** Extracellular enzymes (cellulase, protease, lipase) secreted by **fermentative bacteria**. - **Function:** Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are hydrolyzed into **monomers** (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). - **Substrate type:** Particulates → Soluble monomers. ### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation) - **Microbes:** **Acidogenic bacteria** (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*). - **Function:** Monomers are fermented into **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** — acetate, propionate, butyrate — plus H₂, CO₂, and alcohols. - **Caution:** Over-acidification here kills methanogens → process failure. ### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (Syntrophic Oxidation) - **Microbes:** **Acetogenic bacteria** (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*) — these live in **obligate syntrophy** with methanogens. - **Function:** VFAs (propionate, butyrate) and alcohols are oxidized into **acetate + H₂ + CO₂**. - **Thermodynamic constraint:** Reactions are endergonic (ΔG > 0) at standard conditions; **only proceed when H₂ partial pressure is kept low by methanogens**. ### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (Final & Critical) - **Microbes:** **Methanogenic archaea** (e.g., *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*). - **Two pathways:** - **Acetoclastic (≈70 % of CH₄):** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ - **Hydrogenotrophic (≈30 % of CH₄):** 4H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Methanogens are **extremely sensitive** to pH, temperature, ammonia, and VFAs. They are the **bottleneck organisms**. The process is called "digester failure" when VFAs accumulate and pH drops below 6.5. --- ## 2.2 Operating Parameters — The "Five Pillars" of AD Design | Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Failure Threshold | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature | **30–40 °C** (optimum 35 °C) | **50–60 °C** (optimum 55 °C) | > 60 °C or < 15 °C | | pH | **6.8–7.4** | **7.0–7.5** | < 6.3 or > 8.0 | | HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time) | 15–30 days | 10–20 days | < 10 days (mesophilic) | | SRT (Solids Retention Time) | 20–40 days | 12–25 days | < SRT_min for methanogens | | C/N Ratio | **20–30 : 1** (optimum) | — | < 15 or > 35 (ammonia inhibition) | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize this table — these equations appear directly in KTU Part B 14-mark numericals.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_m \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | **Monod growth kinetics** | | 2 | $q = \dfrac{q_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | **Specific substrate utilization rate** | | 3 | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}$ | **Substrate depletion rate** | | 4 | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | **CSTR volume from HRT** | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot q - k_d$ | **SRT–growth rate relation** | | 6 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{\theta \cdot (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$ | **Steady-state biomass in CSTR** | | 7 | $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot k - k_d}$ | **Minimum SRT for washout** | | 8 | $V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q$ | **Theoretical CH₄ volume (m³/d at STP)** | | 9 | $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \to \left(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$ | **Buswell Equation** (general stoichiometry) | | 10 | $Y_{bg} = \dfrac{(S_0 - S) \cdot Q \cdot \eta}{M_{substrate}}$ | **Biogas yield (m³/kg substrate)** | > ⚠️ **LaTeX Note for tables:** All absolute values use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Effluent Treatment:** Distilleries (spent wash), dairy, breweries, pulp & paper, slaughterhouses — all use high-rate AD (UASB, EGSB). - **Solid Waste Management:** Municipal sewage sludge, food waste, agricultural residues (rice straw, cow dung). - **Energy Recovery:** Combined Heat and Power (CHP) units; purified biogas → **biomethane** injected into natural gas grids. - **Circular Economy:** AD is the cornerstone of **waste-to-wealth** strategies. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buswell Equation for Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) The Buswell equation predicts the **theoretical methane yield** from any organic substrate. We derive it for glucose — the most common KTU exam substrate. ### Step 1 — Write the general Buswell equation For $C_n H_a O_b$: $$C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2 O \longrightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute values for glucose For glucose: $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$. **Water coefficient:** $$\left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) = \left(6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2}\right) = (6 - 3 - 3) = 0$$ So **no water is required** for glucose → it is already balanced on the oxygen side. **CO₂ coefficient:** $$\left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) = \left(\frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4}\right) = (3 - 1.5 + 1.5) = 3$$ **CH₄ coefficient:** $$\left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) = \left(\frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4}\right) = (3 + 1.5 - 1.5) = 3$$ ### Step 3 — Write the balanced equation $$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3\, CO_2 + 3\, CH_4$$ > **Verification by atom balance:** > - C: 6 = 3 + 3 ✓ > - H: 12 = 0 + 12 (4×3) ✓ > - O: 6 = 6 (2×3) + 0 ✓ ### Step 4 — Convert to molar volume 1 mole of glucose (180 g) yields **3 moles of CH₄ = 3 × 22.4 L = 67.2 L at STP.** $$Y_{CH_4} = \frac{67.2\,L}{0.180\,kg} = 0.373\, \frac{m^3\, CH_4}{kg\, glucose}$$ This matches the experimental value of **0.37 m³ CH₄/kg glucose** — a **standard KTU textbook answer**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Methane Yield Per kg COD Removed The KTU syllabus expects students to derive the **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD** universal constant. ### Step 1 — Molecular oxygen equivalent of CH₄ The complete oxidation of methane is: $$CH_4 + 2\, O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\, H_2 O$$ So **16 g of CH₄ (1 mole) consumes 64 g of O₂ (2 moles)** → COD per g of CH₄ = **64/16 = 4 g O₂/g CH₄**. Therefore, **1 kg of COD consumed corresponds to 0.25 kg of CH₄** produced. ### Step 2 — Convert mass of CH₄ to volume at STP Molar mass of CH₄ = 16 g/mol. Molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol = 0.0224 m³/mol. $$\text{Volume per kg CH}_4 = \frac{1000\,g}{16\,g/mol} \times 0.0224\,m^3/mol = 1.4\, m^3$$ ### Step 3 — Volume of CH₄ per kg COD $$V_{CH_4} = 1.4 \cdot \frac{m^3}{kg\, CH_4} \times 0.25\, \frac{kg\, CH_4}{kg\, COD} = 0.35\, \frac{m^3\, CH_4}{kg\, COD}$$ > **Conclusion (KTU Board Standard Answer):** > $$\boxed{Y_{CH_4} = 0.35\ \frac{m^3\, CH_4}{kg\, COD\ removed}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CSTR Volume from HRT and Substrate Balance For a Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) used as an anaerobic digester: ### Step 1 — Mass balance on substrate at steady state $$\text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumed} = 0$$ $$Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - r_{su} \cdot V = 0$$ Where $r_{su} = -\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}$ from Monod kinetics (using $q = \mu/Y$). ### Step 2 — Substitute and solve for V $$V = \frac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S)}{q \cdot X}$$ But $q = \dfrac{q_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$, so: $$V = \frac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot (K_s + S)}{q_{max} \cdot X \cdot S}$$ ### Step 3 — Express V via HRT (θ = V/Q) $$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{(S_0 - S)(K_s + S)}{q_{max} \cdot X \cdot S}$$ > **KTU Exam Hint:** If $K_s \ll S$ (typical for high-rate digesters), the equation simplifies to: > $$\theta \approx \frac{S_0 - S}{q_{max} \cdot X}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relationship Between SRT and Microbial Washout ### Step 1 — Mass balance on biomass in CSTR $$0 = Y \cdot r_{su} \cdot V - k_d \cdot X \cdot V - Q \cdot X$$ Substituting $r_{su} = q \cdot X / Y$ (steady-state specific rate): $$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot q - k_d$$ Where $\theta_c = V/Q$ is the SRT (= HRT for CSTR). ### Step 2 — Solve for minimum SRT (washout condition) At washout, $X \to 0$, so $q \to q_{max}$: $$\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{Y \cdot q_{max} - k_d}$$ > **Design Rule:** Operate at $\theta_c \approx (2 \text{ to } 10) \cdot \theta_c^{min}$ to ensure safe biomass retention. --- ## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** An industrial wastewater flow of **10,000 m³/d** with influent COD $S_0 = 5000$ mg/L is treated in an anaerobic CSTR. Effluent COD $S = 500$ mg/L. Biomass concentration $X = 3000$ mg/L, $Y = 0.05$ mg X/mg COD, $k_d = 0.03$ d⁻¹. Calculate (a) HRT, (b) SRT, (c) Daily methane production at STP, (d) Digester volume. ### Part (a) — Substrate Removal Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} = \frac{5000 - 500}{5000} = 0.90 = 90\,\%$$ ### Part (b) — Daily COD Removed $$\Delta S \cdot Q = (5000 - 500)\, \frac{mg}{L} \times 10{,}000\, \frac{m^3}{d} \times \frac{1000\,L}{m^3}$$ $$= 4{,}500 \times 10^7\, mg/d = 45{,}000\, kg/d$$ ### Part (c) — Methane Volume per Day $$V_{CH_4} = 0.35\, \frac{m^3}{kg\, COD} \times 45{,}000\, \frac{kg}{d} = 15{,}750\, \frac{m^3}{d}$$ ### Part (d) — Digester Volume (assuming HRT = 20 days) $$V = Q \cdot \theta = 10{,}000 \cdot 20 = 200{,}000\, m^3$$ ### Part (e) — SRT Calculation $$\theta_c = \frac{X \cdot V}{Q \cdot X_{eff}} \approx \frac{3000 \cdot 200{,}000}{10{,}000 \cdot 50} = 1{,}200\, days$$ (Assuming negligible effluent biomass → SRT is effectively controlled by sludge wasting.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always state **assumptions clearly**: complete mixing, steady state, biodegradable COD only, no inhibitory compounds. Marks are deducted if assumptions are missing. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Four-Stage Anaerobic Digestion Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD A["FEED: Complex Organic Waste
