Mass emission per day:
$$E = C \times Q = 939.96 \,\frac{mg}{Nm^3} \times 288{,}000 \,\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

$$E = 270{,}708{,}480 \,\text{mg/day}$$

Converting mg → kg by dividing by $10^6$:
$$E = 270.71 \,\text{kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{NO_2} \approx 270.71 \,\text{kg/day}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **concentration units** used to express pollutant levels in air and water. Compare molarity, molality, and normality with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Air pollutant concentration units:**

1. **ppm (v/v):** Volume ratio — 1 cm³ pollutant per m³ air.
   *Used for:* SO₂, NOₓ, CO, VOCs in ambient air and stack monitoring.

2. **µg/m³ (or mg/m³):** Mass per unit volume — preferred by CPCB for NAAQS compliance.
   *Used for:* PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂.

3. **ppb (v/v):** Used for trace gases — ozone, benzene, dioxins, PAHs.

**Water pollutant concentration units:**

1. **mg/L (≈ ppm for dilute aqueous solutions):** BOD, COD, TDS, TSS.
2. **µg/L (≈ ppb):** Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), pesticides.
3. **% by mass:** Industrial effluents with high pollutant load (e.g., 5% sulfuric acid waste).
4. **Molarity (M):** Used in stoichiometric calculations during treatment.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Molarity (M) | Molality (m) | Normality (N) |
|----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| Definition | moles solute / L solution | moles solute / kg solvent | equivalents solute / L solution |
| Symbol | M | m | N |
| Temperature dependent? | **Yes** (volume expands with T) | **No** (mass-based) | **Yes** |
| Typical use | Lab reagent prep | Colligative properties, high-T processes | Acid-base & redox titrations, COD |
| Example | 1 M NaCl = 58.44 g/L | 1 m NaCl = 58.44 g/kg water | 1 N H₂SO₄ = 0.5 M H₂SO₄ |

**Conversion backbone:** $N = M \times n$ where $n$ = valence factor.

**(b)** A wastewater sample has **COD = 480 mg/L**. Calculate the theoretical oxygen demand in **ppm, % by mass, and kg/day** if the discharge flow is **50 m³/h**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption:** Density of wastewater ≈ 1000 kg/m³ (water-like dilute effluent).

**(i) ppm:**
Since 1 L of wastewater ≈ 10⁶ mg:
$$480 \,\text{mg/L} = 480 \,\text{ppm}$$

**(ii) % by mass:**
$$\% = \frac{480}{10^6} \times 100 = 0.048\,\%$$

**(iii) kg/day emission:**

Hourly flow:
$$Q = 50 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Daily flow:
$$Q_{day} = 50 \times 24 = 1200 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Daily COD mass load:
$$E = 480 \,\frac{mg}{L} \times 1200 \,\frac{m^3}{day} \times 1000 \,\frac{L}{m^3}$$

$$E = 576 \times 10^6 \,\text{mg/day}$$

Converting mg → kg:
$$\boxed{E = 576 \,\text{kg COD/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions explicitly** (e.g., ideal gas, density = 1000 kg/m³ for water). Examiners deduct up to 1 mark per missing assumption.
> 2. **Mixing "Nm³" with "m³"** in stack emission problems. Always confirm the reference state — if not specified, default to **0 °C, 1 atm (Nm³)**.
> 3. **Using 22.414 instead of 24.47** when 25 °C is stated (or vice versa). The temperature reference MUST match the molar volume used.
> 4. **In ppm↔mg/m³ conversions for gases**, students often forget to multiply by **M/24.47** and write a dimensionless number — losing the concentration identity.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in final answer**: writing "kg/h" when asked for "kg/day" or omitting units altogether. **Always write the unit.**
> 6. **Confusing normality with molarity** in COD titration problems — always verify the valence factor (n-factor) of the titrant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SI base units** form the foundation: **m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd**.
- **Molarity (M)** = moles/L of solution; **Molality (m)** = moles/kg of solvent; **Normality (N)** = equivalents/L; relation **N = M × n**.
- **Molar volume** is **22.414 L/mol at STP (0 °C)** and **24.47 L/mol at SATP (25 °C)** — pick the right one based on problem data.
- **Master conversion formula (gases, 25 °C):** $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M}{24.47}$
- **For dilute aqueous solutions:** mg/L ≡ ppm ≡ mg/kg (assuming density = 1 kg/L).
- **Stack emission daily load:** $E_{kg/day} = \dfrac{C_{mg/Nm^3} \times Q_{Nm^3/h} \times 24}{10^6}$
- **Nm³ ≠ m³** — Nm³ refers to gas volume at **0 °C, 1 atm** (normal conditions).
- **Pressure conversions:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 760 torr = 14.696 psi.
- **Temperature conversions:** K = °C + 273.15; °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9.
- **Energy conversions:** 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 8.6 × 10⁵ cal ≈ 3412 BTU.
- **Dimensional consistency** must be verified in every formula before numerical substitution.
- **CPCB/KSPCB** standards are reported in **µg/m³ (air)** and **mg/L (water)** — keep regulator units as the final reporting unit.
- Always state the **reference temperature, pressure, and assumptions** at the start of any unit conversion problem — this is where most KTU marks are won or lost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Sources" in Pollution Control?

In the context of **Process Industries**, a **pollution source** is any point, area, or operation within an industrial facility that releases **physical, chemical, or biological contaminants** into the environment (air, water, or soil). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) classifies these systematically, and KTU follows a parallel framework for evaluating industrial pollution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **pollution source** in a process industry is defined as a *specific unit operation, process stream, or material handling activity* that generates, transforms, or releases a pollutant—whether as a **point source** (discrete, identifiable outlet like a stack or discharge pipe) or a **fugitive source** (diffuse, non-point emissions like leaks, spills, or evaporative losses).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Body" Analogy

Think of a process industry like a **living human body**:
- **Point sources** (stacks, drains) are like the **mouth and nose**—contaminants exit through well-defined openings.
- **Fugitive sources** (valves, flanges, storage tanks) are like **body odor or sweat**—pollutants leak out from everywhere, slowly and often unnoticed.
- **Process units** (reactors, distillation columns) are like the **digestive system**—they consume raw materials and generate both products and **by-product wastes**.

Just as a doctor must identify *all* sources of an illness to cure it, an environmental engineer must identify *all* pollution sources to control them effectively.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Source Classification Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** | varies (e.g., **25 ppm** for SO₂) | Worker exposure limit |
| **Stack Height (H)** | ≥ **30 m** (varies by GLC) | Dispersion optimization |
| **Effluent Discharge pH** | **6.5 – 8.5** | Water quality compliance |
| **BOD₅ (Inland surface water)** | ≤ **30 mg/L** | Receiving water protection |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Students must be able to identify sources **sector-wise** (fertilizer, petroleum, pulp & paper, textile, pharmaceutical, etc.) and classify them as **point, fugitive, or area sources** for full marks in Module 3.

### 1.4 Why Are Sources Important?

A pollution control engineer who does not know **where** the pollutant comes from cannot:
- Design the right **end-of-pipe treatment** (scrubber, ETP, etc.)
- Apply **in-plant modification** (source reduction)
- Comply with **Consent to Operate** requirements under the **Water Act, 1974** and **Air Act, 1981**

Hence, "Sources" is the **foundational first step** in the pollution control hierarchy: **Identify → Characterize → Treat → Dispose**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pollution Sources in Process Industries

Pollution sources in process industries are classified along **three primary axes**:

#### A) By **Environmental Medium**
- **Air pollution sources** — stacks, vents, storage tanks, loading operations
- **Water pollution sources** — process effluents, cooling water blowdown, boiler blowdown, sanitary waste
- **Soil/land pollution sources** — solid waste dumps, sludge disposal sites, accidental spills
- **Noise pollution sources** — compressors, turbines, crushers, conveyors

#### B) By **Spatial Character**
- **Point Source** — a single, identifiable discharge location
  - *Examples*: a chimney stack, an outlet pipe of an ETP, a tanker loading arm
- **Fugitive Source** — diffuse, non-confined emissions
  - *Examples*: leaking valve packings, pump seals, tank breathing losses, landfill gas
- **Area Source** — multiple small point sources aggregated over an area
  - *Examples*: a tank farm with multiple vents, a mining pit

#### C) By **Process Operation Type**
- **Raw material extraction & handling**
- **Reaction & conversion units**
- **Separation & purification units**
- **Storage & transfer operations**
- **Utility generation** (boilers, cooling towers, DG sets)
- **Effluent treatment plants** (sludge handling, vented gases)

### 2.2 Sector-Wise Major Sources

| Industry | Air Pollutants (Major) | Water Pollutants (Major) | Hazardous Waste |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fertilizer (Ammonia-Urea)** | NH₃, urea dust, NOₓ | Ammonical nitrogen, urea, oil | Spent catalysts |
| **Petroleum Refinery** | SO₂, H₂S, VOC, HC | Phenols, sulfides, oil & grease | Sludge, spent caustic |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Cl₂, ClO₂, reduced S compounds | High BOD/COD, lignin, color | Black liquor |
| **Textile** | Particulates, VOCs | High COD, color, salts | Dye sludge |
| **Pharmaceutical** | VOCs, acid mists | API residues, solvents, COD | Spent solvents |
| **Cement** | Particulate matter (PM), NOₓ | Low volume, high pH | Kiln dust |
| **Pesticide** | H₂S, HCl, organics | Toxic, non-biodegradable | Container rinse |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Source Identification

The **Hierarchy of Pollution Control** (used by KTU 2024 and the EPA) places source reduction at the top:
1. **Source elimination** — change the process to not produce the pollutant
2. **Source reduction** — minimize generation
3. **Recycle/Reuse** — close the loop
4. **Treatment** — end-of-pipe control
5. **Disposal** — last resort

Without knowing the **source**, steps 1–3 are impossible.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Emission rate from a stack | $Q = C \times V \times A$ | $Q$ = emission rate (g/s), $C$ = concentration (g/m³), $V$ = velocity (m/s), $A$ = area (m²) |
| Total VOC from storage tank (TANKS model) | $L_{T} = L_{S} + L_{W}$ | $L_{S}$ = standing losses, $L_{W}$ = working losses |
| BOD load from a stream | $L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD}$ | $Q$ = flow (m³/d), $C_{BOD}$ = BOD concentration (mg/L) |
| Equivalent population | $P_{eq} = \dfrac{L_{BOD}}{0.08 \text{ kg/capita/day}}$ | Used when comparing industrial load to domestic |
| Fugitive emission factor | $E_{f} = F \times N \times t$ | $F$ = emission factor (kg/hr/source), $N$ = number of sources, $t$ = time |
| Specific effluent volume | $S = \dfrac{V_{eff}}{P_{prod}}$ | $V_{eff}$ = effluent volume, $P_{prod}$ = production (m³/tonne) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip:** All set-membership and magnitude operators in formulas must use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\mid x \mid$), never the literal `|` character, to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax in KTU PDFs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Identifying sources correctly enables:
- **Emission Inventory Preparation** (mandatory under India's **National Clean Air Programme, 2019**)
- **Consent Application** under Water Act 1974 & Air Act 1981
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** notification 2006
- **Design of treatment plants** (ETP, STP, APCD)
- **ISO 14001 EMS** aspect-impact identification
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Volatile Organic Compound (VOC) Loss from a Fixed-Roof Storage Tank

A **fixed-roof tank** loses VOCs through two mechanisms: **standing losses** (diurnal breathing) and **working losses** (filling/emptying). This is fundamental for KTU Module 3 derivations.

#### Step 1: Define Standing Loss ($L_S$)

Standing loss occurs due to **thermal expansion and contraction** of vapors in the vapor space during day-night temperature cycles.

$$
L_{S} = 0.179 \times \dfrac{M \times P \times D \times K_{N} \times K_{P} \times K_{B}}{T}
$$

**Where:**
- $M$ = molecular weight of the liquid (g/mol)
- $P$ = true vapor pressure (psia)
- $D$ = tank diameter (ft)
- $K_N$ = turnover factor (dimensionless)
- $K_P$ = paint factor
- $K_B$ = breather vent setting factor
- $T$ = average daily temperature (°R)

#### Step 2: Explain Each Term in Plain English

- $M \times P / T$ comes from the **Ideal Gas Law**: $PV = nRT$ → moles of vapor depend on $P/T$, and weight depends on $M$.
- $0.179$ is a **unit conversion constant** combining area units and time.
- $D$ accounts for the **tank cross-section** (larger tank → larger vapor space → more loss).
- $K_N, K_P, K_B$ are **empirical correction factors** for operating conditions.

#### Step 3: Define Working Loss ($L_W$)

Working loss is due to **displacement of vapor** when the tank is filled or emptied.

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{M \times V_{L} \times P \times K_{N}}{R \times T}
$$

**Where:**
- $V_L$ = volume of liquid pumped in/out (barrels/year)
- $R$ = ideal gas constant = **10.73 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)**
- $T$ = liquid temperature (°R)

#### Step 4: Combine

$$
L_{T} = L_{S} + L_{W}
$$

This $L_T$ (in **lb/year**) is the **total source strength** of VOC emissions from the tank.

#### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A petroleum refinery stores **gasoline ($M = 100$ g/mol, $P = 5$ psia)** in a fixed-roof tank of diameter **$D = 30$ ft**. Daily temperature swing is $60$°F – $90$°F, $T_{avg} = 585$°R. Annual throughput $V_L = 50{,}000$ barrels. Assume $K_N = 0.65$, $K_P = 1$, $K_B = 1$. Calculate $L_T$.

**Solution:**

Calculate $L_S$:

$$
L_{S} = 0.179 \times \dfrac{100 \times 5 \times 30 \times 0.65 \times 1 \times 1}{585}
$$

Numerator: $0.179 \times 100 \times 5 \times 30 \times 0.65 = 0.179 \times 9750 = 1745.25$

$$
L_{S} = \dfrac{1745.25}{585} = 2.98 \text{ lb/year}
$$

Calculate $L_W$:

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{100 \times 50000 \times 5 \times 0.65}{10.73 \times 585}
$$

Numerator: $100 \times 50000 \times 5 \times 0.65 = 16{,}250{,}000$

Denominator: $10.73 \times 585 = 6277.05$

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{16{,}250{,}000}{6277.05} = 2588.81 \text{ lb/year}
$$

Total loss:

$$
L_{T} = 2.98 + 2588.81 = 2591.79 \text{ lb/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Working losses dominate ($99.9\%$) over standing losses. This is why **floating roof tanks** (which eliminate vapor space) are mandatory in refineries handling high-VOC liquids — they directly target the dominant source.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source Identification Workflow

| Step | Action | KTU Skill Tested | CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Walk-through survey** of the plant | Identify unit operations | CO1, CO2 |
| 2 | **List all input/output streams** (mass balance) | Quantify each source | CO2 |
| 3 | **Classify** as point/fugitive/area | Apply taxonomy | CO1 |
| 4 | **Characterize** pollutants (C, Q, T, pH) | Use analytical formulas | CO3 |
| 5 | **Map** onto Air Act / Water Act schedules | Regulatory linkage | CO4 |
| 6 | **Prioritize** using source-reduction logic | Hierarchy application | CO5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **"source" with "sink."** A source *generates* a pollutant; a sink *receives* it (e.g., a river is a sink, not a source). Marks are lost when the two are interchanged in Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Whenever a question asks to "draw a flow diagram of pollution sources," examiners award full marks only if the diagram distinguishes between **point, fugitive, and area sources** using different notation (e.g., circle, square, shaded region).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Differentiate between **point source** and **fugitive source** of air pollution in a process industry. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Point Source (1.5 marks):** A single, identifiable, localized discharge of pollutants into the atmosphere from a defined opening. Quantification is straightforward using stack monitoring. *Example: a 60 m refinery stack emitting SO₂.*
- **Fugitive Source (1.5 marks):** Emissions that are not discharged through a defined opening; they escape from equipment leaks, evaporative surfaces, or wind erosion. *Example: VOC leaks from pump seals and flange joints.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

List **any six major air pollution sources** found in a **nitrogenous fertilizer plant** (Urea–Ammonia complex).

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Ammonia synthesis gas vent (NH₃ release)
2. Urea prilling tower (NH₃ and urea dust)
3. Nitric acid plant tail gas (NOₓ)
4. Boiler stack (SO₂, NOₓ, PM)
5. Catalyst regeneration vent (NOₓ)
6. Coal/fuel handling area (particulate matter)

---

### PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **sector-wise classification**, describe the major sources of air and water pollution in **(i) Petroleum Refinery** and **(ii) Pulp & Paper Industry**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fixed-roof storage tank of diameter **40 ft** stores gasoline (M = 110 g/mol, vapor pressure $P = 6$ psia). Average daily temperature $T = 600$°R. Annual throughput $V_L = 80{,}000$ barrels. Take $K_N = 0.7$, $K_P = 1$, $K_B = 1$, $R = 10.73$ psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R). Calculate the **total annual VOC loss** ($L_T$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Sector-wise Sources (7 marks — 3.5 each)**

**(i) Petroleum Refinery:**

*Air Pollution Sources:*
- **Sour water stripper** releases H₂S and NH₃
- **FCC regenerator** emits CO, NOₓ, PM, and catalyst fines
- **Sulfur recovery unit (Claus)** tail gas has residual H₂S and SO₂
- **Storage tanks** and **loading racks** release VOC
- **Flare system** combustion products (CO, HC, soot)
- **Boilers/heaters** emit SO₂, NOₓ, PM

*Water Pollution Sources:*
- **Desalter effluent** — high TDS, oil, suspended solids
- **Sour water** — sulfides, ammonia, phenols
- **Spent caustic** from mercaptan extraction — high pH, COD
- **API separator skimmings** — oil and grease
- **ETP sludge** — hydrocarbons, heavy metals

**(ii) Pulp & Paper Industry:**

*Air Pollution Sources:*
- **Recovery boiler** emits reduced sulfur compounds (H₂S, CH₃SH, DMS)
- **Lime kiln** emits PM, NOₓ
- **Digesters** release reduced sulfur gases
- **Bleach plant** (if using chlorine) releases Cl₂ and ClO₂

*Water Pollution Sources:*
- **Black liquor** from digesters — extremely high BOD/COD, color, lignin
- **Bleach plant effluent** — chlorinated organics, color, AOX
- **White water** from paper machines — suspended solids, fiber
- **Condensates** from evaporators — methanol, terpenes

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate Standing Loss $L_S$ (3 marks)**

$$
L_{S} = 0.179 \times \dfrac{M \times P \times D \times K_{N} \times K_{P} \times K_{B}}{T}
$$

Substitute:

$$
L_{S} = 0.179 \times \dfrac{110 \times 6 \times 40 \times 0.7 \times 1 \times 1}{600}
$$

Numerator: $0.179 \times 110 \times 6 \times 40 \times 0.7 = 0.179 \times 18480 = 3307.92$

$$
L_{S} = \dfrac{3307.92}{600} = 5.51 \text{ lb/year}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Working Loss $L_W$ (3 marks)**

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{M \times V_{L} \times P \times K_{N}}{R \times T}
$$

Substitute:

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{110 \times 80000 \times 6 \times 0.7}{10.73 \times 600}
$$

Numerator: $110 \times 80000 \times 6 \times 0.7 = 369{,}600{,}000$

Denominator: $10.73 \times 600 = 6438$

$$
L_{W} = \dfrac{369{,}600{,}000}{6438} = 57{,}409.13 \text{ lb/year}
$$

**Step 3: Total Loss (1 mark)**

$$
L_{T} = L_{S} + L_{W} = 5.51 + 57409.13 = 57414.64 \text{ lb/year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make three mistakes in this derivation:
> 1. Using **°F** instead of **°R** in the denominator ($T$ in Rankine = °F + 460).
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.179** in the standing loss formula.
> 3. **Mixing units** of $V_L$ (must be barrels/year, not gallons or m³).
> Each of these costs 1–2 marks. KTU expects **consistent SI/IP units** with unit mention in the final answer.

---

#### **OR**

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchy of pollution control** with respect to source identification. Why is **source reduction** preferred over **end-of-pipe treatment**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process industry discharges an effluent of flow rate $Q = 500$ m³/day with BOD concentration $C_{BOD} = 1200$ mg/L. Calculate **(i)** the daily BOD load in kg/day, and **(ii)** the equivalent population in terms of BOD contribution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Pollution Control Hierarchy (7 marks — 1 mark per level + 2 marks for explanation):**

The hierarchy, in **descending order of preference**, is:
1. **Source Elimination** (1 mark) — change the process to not generate the pollutant. *Example:* replace chrome-based cooling water treatment with a non-chrome program.
2. **Source Reduction** (1 mark) — minimize the quantity generated. *Example:* floating roof tanks instead of fixed-roof.
3. **Recycle and Reuse** (1 mark) — close material loops. *Example:* recycle process condensate as boiler feedwater.
4. **Treatment** (1 mark) — end-of-pipe control (scrubber, ETP, ESP).
5. **Disposal** (1 mark) — last resort, e.g., secured landfill.

**Why source reduction is preferred (2 marks):**
- **Lower long-term cost** — no recurring treatment chemical/energy cost.
- **Reduces liability** — less waste means lower risk and regulatory burden.
- **Often simpler** than complex treatment systems.
- Aligns with **sustainable development** and **circular economy** principles.
- Complies with **ISO 14001** and **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** norms.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Daily BOD Load (4 marks)**

Concentration: $C_{BOD} = 1200$ mg/L = $1200 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/L = $1.2$ kg/m³

Flow: $Q = 500$ m³/day

$$
L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD} = 500 \times 1.2 = 600 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Equivalent Population (3 marks)**

Using the standard per-capita BOD contribution of **0.08 kg/capita/day**:

$$
P_{eq} = \dfrac{L_{BOD}}{0.08} = \dfrac{600}{0.08} = 7500 \text{ persons}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** Many students use **0.17 kg/capita/day** (which is the **per-capita sewage generation**, not BOD) and obtain $P_{eq} = 3529$, which is wrong. KTU's Module 3 expects the **BOD equivalent** of **0.08 kg/capita/day**. Mixing these values is the most common error and results in a **0-mark** answer for the population part.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pollution source** = any point/area/operation releasing contaminants. *Always* classify as **point, fugitive, or area** first.
- **Source identification** is the **first step** of the pollution control hierarchy — never skip it.
- **Standing loss** = diurnal thermal breathing; **Working loss** = filling/emptying displacement. Working loss usually **dominates**.
- The **0.179 constant** in $L_S$ is empirical and must be used as-is; do not derive it.
- **Temperature in $L_S$ and $L_W$** must be in **°Rankine** ($°F + 460$), not °F.
- For **fugitive sources**, the **EPA correlation equation** $E_f = F \times N \times t$ applies to component-level estimation.
- **Sector-wise knowledge** of **Refinery, Fertilizer, Pulp & Paper, Textile, and Pharmaceutical** industries is a KTU hot favorite — at least **one question** in every ESE comes from this mapping.
- **End-of-pipe treatment** is the *last* option, not the *first* — examiners reward source-reduction logic.
- For numerical problems, **always state the units** in the final answer (e.g., "57414.64 lb/year") for the unit-mark.
- The **hierarchy** (Eliminate → Reduce → Recycle → Treat → Dispose) is **not optional** in any KTU 14-mark answer on pollution control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Pollution Control in Process Industries**, **Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of pollutants, wastes, and polluting industries based on their **physical, chemical, biological, and toxicological characteristics**. This taxonomic structuring is the foundational step in environmental management because the *type* and *severity* of a pollutant dictate the appropriate **control technology**, **regulatory standard**, **disposal pathway**, and **monitoring protocol** to be employed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Classification is the prerequisite for **"Source Identification → Pathway Mapping → Treatment Technology Selection → Regulatory Compliance."** Without classification, no Abatement technology can be correctly sized or selected.

The major classification domains covered in Module 3 are:

1. **Classification of Air Pollutants**
2. **Classification of Water (Wastewater) Pollutants**
3. **Classification of Industrial Solid Waste / Hazardous Waste**
4. **Classification of Industries (Red / Orange / Green / White category)**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital emergency room**. When a patient arrives, doctors do **not** administer random medicine — they first **classify** the case (cardiac, neurological, traumatic, infectious). Each classification triggers a specific protocol (ECG, MRI, X-ray, blood culture). Pollution control works identically: you cannot install an **electrostatic precipitator** for a **biological oxygen demand (BOD)** problem, nor a **biological wastewater treatment plant** for **particulate matter (PM)** in stack gas.

**Analogy Summary:**

| Hospital Triage | Pollution Control |
|---|---|
| Patient classification | Pollutant classification |
| Severity (Critical / Stable) | Toxicity / Persistence |
| Treatment protocol | Abatement technology |
| Discharge decision | Disposal pathway |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** In KTU examinations, the examiner expects you to **justify** why a pollutant belongs to a particular class — simply listing categories without reasoning scores poorly. Always couple classification with **property-based reasoning** (e.g., "It is biodegradable → Biological treatment").

---

### 1.3 Foundational Metrics Used in Classification

The following measurable parameters define the boundaries of each classification (memorize these — they are heavily tested):

- **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** — airborne concentration (mg/m³ or ppm) below which no adverse health effect is expected for an 8-hour workday.
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50)** — the single dose (mg/kg body weight) that kills **50%** of a test population; used to classify acute toxicity.
- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50)** — concentration in air/water killing 50% of exposed organisms.
- **BOD₅ / COD Ratio** — biodegradability index for wastewater.
- **pH** — hydrogen ion concentration; governs corrosivity classification.
- **Flash Point** — lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor that ignites; governs flammable waste classification.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "TL CL BC PF"** → **T**LV, **L**D₅₀, **C**OD, **B**OD, **P**H, **F**lash point. These six metrics are the **backbone** of nearly every classification system in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Air Pollutants

Air pollutants are classified based on **origin (primary vs secondary)**, **physical state**, and **chemical nature**.

#### 2.1.1 Primary vs Secondary Pollutants

- **Primary Pollutants:** Emitted directly from a source in a harmful form.
  - Examples: CO, SO₂, NOₓ (NO + NO₂), Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅), VOCs, Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg).
- **Secondary Pollutants:** Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and normal atmospheric constituents.
  - Examples: **Ozone (O₃)**, **Smog (PAN — Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)**, **Acid Rain (H₂SO₄ / HNO₃)**, **Secondary organic aerosols**.

#### 2.1.2 Classification by Physical State

| Class | Sub-Class | Examples | Major Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaseous** | Inorganic | SO₂, NOₓ, CO, H₂S, NH₃, HF, Cl₂ | Combustion, fertilizer, chlor-alkali |
| **Gaseous** | Organic | Benzene, Toluene, Formaldehyde, VOCs | Petrochemical, paints, solvents |
| **Particulate** | Solid | Dust, Smoke, Fly ash, Soot | Power plants, cement, steel |
| **Particulate** | Liquid | Mist, Fog, Spray | Acid mists, metallurgical fumes |

#### 2.1.3 Classification by Chemical Nature

1. **Sulphur Compounds** — SO₂, SO₃, H₂S → cause acid rain, respiratory illness.
2. **Nitrogen Compounds** — NO, NO₂, NH₃ → photochemical smog, eutrophication.
3. **Carbon Compounds** — CO, CO₂, hydrocarbons → greenhouse effect, smog.
4. **Halogen Compounds** — Cl₂, HF, HCl → corrosive, toxic to vegetation.
5. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — organic/inorganic solid/liquid droplets.
6. **Radioactive Pollutants** — Radon-222, Iodine-131 → carcinogenic.
7. **Photochemical Oxidants** — O₃, PAN, H₂O₂ → secondary pollutants.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Water Pollutants

Wastewater contaminants are grouped into **eight major categories** (a KTU-favorite enumeration):

#### 2.2.1 The Eight-Class Classification System

| # | Class | Representative Parameters | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Suspended Solids (SS)** | TSS (mg/L), Turbidity (NTU) | Pulp & paper, textiles |
| 2 | **Dissolved Inorganic Salts** | TDS, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻ | Tanneries, mining |
| 3 | **Dissolved Organic Matter** | BOD₅, COD, TOC | Food, pharma, distillery |
| 4 | **Dissolved Gases** | DO, CO₂, H₂S, NH₃ | Fermentation, anaerobic digesters |
| 5 | **Heavy Metals** | Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr(VI), As, Ni | Electroplating, metallurgy |
| 6 | **Nutrients** (N, P) | NH₃-N, Total P, NO₃⁻-N | Fertilizer, detergent |
| 7 | **Oil & Grease** | O&G (mg/L) | Petroleum, refineries |
| 8 | **Microorganisms** | Coliform count (MPN/100 mL) | Domestic + food processing |

#### 2.2.2 Sewage Classification (by Strength)

$$ \text{Sewage Strength Class} = f(\text{BOD}_5, \text{COD}, \text{TSS}, \text{NH}_3\text{-N}) $$

- **Strong Sewage:** BOD₅ > 400 mg/L, TSS > 600 mg/L
- **Medium Sewage:** BOD₅ = 200–400 mg/L
- **Weak Sewage:** BOD₅ < 200 mg/L

> [!NOTE]
> The **BOD₅/COD ratio** is the **master classifier** for selecting biological vs physico-chemical treatment:
> - Ratio > **0.5** → **Biodegradable** → Biological treatment.
> - Ratio < **0.3** → **Recalcitrant** → Physico-chemical (adsorption, oxidation) treatment.
> - Ratio 0.3 – 0.5 → **Borderline** → often requires combined approach.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Solid Waste

#### 2.3.1 By Source

| Source Class | Examples | Generation Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Kitchen waste, paper, plastics | kg/capita/day |
| **Industrial Solid Waste** | Slag, dross, fly ash, red mud | tonnes/day |
| **Hazardous Waste** | Hospital waste, e-waste, chemical sludge | tonnes/year |
| **Biomedical Waste** | Sharps, pathological, pharmaceutical | kg/bed/day |
| **E-Waste** | PCB, batteries, mobile phones | kg/year (global) |
| **Construction & Demolition** | Concrete, steel, wood | tonnes/project |

#### 2.3.2 Hazardous Waste Classification (Basel Convention / Indian CPCB Schedule)

Hazardous waste is identified by any **one** of four characteristics (the **"4-I" rule**):

1. **Ignitability** — Flash point < 60 °C (Class IB / IC liquids).
2. **Corrosivity** — pH ≤ 2 or pH ≥ 12.5.
3. **Reactivity** — Unstable, explosive, water-reactive.
4. **Toxicity** — TCLP (Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure) extract exceeds limits for: Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr, As, Ba, Se, Ag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Context (HWM Rules 2016, amended 2022):** Hazardous waste is classified into **Schedule-I, II, III & IV** with specific management requirements. Schedules define categories such as: **Class A (Sludge), Class B (Acids/Alkalis), Class C (Spent catalysts), Class D (Used oil), etc.**

---

### 2.4 Classification of Industries (Pollution Index-Based)

The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** of India classifies industries into **4 categories** based on the **Pollution Index (PI)** — a composite score of environmental damage potential.

| Category | Pollution Index | Description | Example Industries |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Red** (Highly Polluting) | PI ≥ 60 | Cannot establish without pollution control; strict consent | Petroleum refineries, cement, sugar (large), distilleries, fertilizer |
| **Orange** | PI 30–59 | Moderately polluting; consent with conditions | Dyeing, electroplating, pharmaceuticals, food processing |
| **Green** | Low Polluting | Only intimation required; minimal regulation | Assembly, cold storage, cotton ginning, bio-fertilizer |
| **White** | Practically Non-Polluting | Essentially pollution-free | Electronics testing, IT/ITES, R&D labs |

$$ \text{Pollution Index (PI)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i $$

Where $w_i$ = weight factor (air, water, hazardous waste contribution) and $s_i$ = severity score (0–100) for each emission stream.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Use in Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BOD₅ = (DO_initial − DO_final) × Dilution Factor | mg/L | Biodegradability class |
| 2 | COD = mg of O₂ consumed by strong oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇) | mg/L | Total organic load |
| 3 | BOD/COD ratio | dimensionless | Biological vs physio-chemical decision |
| 4 | LD₅₀ | mg/kg body weight | Toxicity class (highly toxic < 25) |
| 5 | LC₅₀ | mg/L (water) or mg/m³ (air) | Aquatic / inhalation toxicity |
| 6 | Threshold Limit Value (TLV) | mg/m³ or ppm | Workplace air safety |
| 7 | Pollution Index (PI) | 0–100 | Industry category (R/O/G/W) |
| 8 | pH = −log[H⁺] | dimensionless | Corrosivity class |
| 9 | Flash Point | °C | Ignitability class |
| 10 | TSS = (W_final − W_initial) / V_sample | mg/L | Solids classification |
| 11 | Turbidity (NTU) | NTU | Drinking water class |
| 12 | DO saturation % | % | Receiving water body class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Every classification question in KTU exams can be answered by linking one or more of these 12 parameters to a definitional threshold.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engineers designing ETP/STP:** Classification of wastewater determines whether to use **primary clarifier** (suspended solids), **activated sludge process** (BOD removal), or **reverse osmosis** (dissolved salts).
- **Air Quality Engineers:** Classification determines whether to deploy **cyclone separator** (large PM), **baghouse filter** (fine PM), **wet scrubber** (SO₂/NOₓ), or **thermal oxidizer** (VOCs).
- **Solid Waste Managers:** Classification into biodegradable vs non-biodegradable determines **composting vs landfilling vs incineration**.
- **Regulatory Authorities:** Pollution Index classification decides whether an industry gets **Consent to Establish (CTE)** under the Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Core Classification Indicators

#### 3.1.1 BOD₅ Calculation (Bottle Method)

The 5-day BOD is computed by measuring dissolved oxygen depletion in a sealed bottle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD}_5 \,(\text{mg/L}) &= \frac{D_1 - D_2 - B}{P} \\[6pt]
\text{where:} \quad D_1 &= \text{DO of diluted sample immediately after preparation (mg/L)} \\
D_2 &= \text{DO of diluted sample after 5-day incubation at 20°C (mg/L)} \\
B &= \text{DO depletion of dilution water blank (mg/L)} \\
P &= \text{Decimal volumetric fraction of sample} = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A 10 mL wastewater sample is diluted to 300 mL with aerated dilution water.
- Initial DO (D₁) = 8.5 mg/L
- 5th day DO (D₂) = 3.2 mg/L
- Blank depletion (B) = 0.4 mg/L

**Step 1 — Compute decimal fraction P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}} \\
  &= \frac{10 \, \text{mL}}{10 \, \text{mL} + (300 - 10) \, \text{mL}} \\
  &= \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute BOD₅:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD}_5 &= \frac{8.5 - 3.2 - 0.4}{0.0333} \\
             &= \frac{4.9}{0.0333} \\
             &= 147.0 \, \text{mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Classify the wastewater strength:**

Since BOD₅ = 147 mg/L falls in the range 100–250 mg/L → **Medium-Strength Industrial Wastewater** → suitable for **biological treatment** (Activated Sludge Process).

---

#### 3.1.2 Pollution Index (PI) Computation — CPCB Method

The PI is the weighted sum of three sub-indices: Air PI, Water PI, and Hazardous Waste PI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PI}_{\text{industry}} = \frac{(W_A \cdot S_A) + (W_W \cdot S_W) + (W_H \cdot S_H)}{W_A + W_W + W_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $W_A = 0.4$ (weight for air emission) — though CPCB uses specific lookup; values can be 0.40, 0.35, etc.
- $W_W = 0.40$ (weight for water discharge)
- $W_H = 0.20$ (weight for hazardous waste generation)
- $S_A, S_W, S_H$ are sub-indices (0–100) from CPCB lookup tables.

**Worked Example — Pharmaceutical Industry (Bulk Drug Manufacturing):**

From CPCB reference tables:
- $S_A = 65$ (due to VOCs like methanol, acetone)
- $S_W = 75$ (high COD, color, pH variability)
- $S_H = 70$ (spent solvents, catalyst residues)

Weights: $W_A = 0.40, W_W = 0.40, W_H = 0.20$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &= \frac{(0.40 \times 65) + (0.40 \times 75) + (0.20 \times 70)}{0.40 + 0.40 + 0.20} \\
         &= \frac{26.0 + 30.0 + 14.0}{1.00} \\
         &= 70.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **PI = 70 ≥ 60** → Industry falls in the **RED category** → Requires stringent consent, continuous online monitoring, and dedicated ETP/APCR.

---

#### 3.1.3 Classification of Solid Waste by Moisture Content

Moisture content (MC%) drives the classification of biodegradable MSW and is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MC (\%)} &= \frac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $W_{\text{wet}}$ = mass of sample as received (g)
- $W_{\text{dry}}$ = mass of sample after oven-drying at 105 °C for 24 h (g)

**Worked Example:**

A kitchen waste sample weighs 850 g wet. After oven drying, it weighs 320 g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MC (\%)} &= \frac{850 - 320}{850} \times 100 \\
              &= \frac{530}{850} \times 100 \\
              &= 62.35 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (KTU Standard):**

| MC (%) | Class | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| > 50 | **High-moisture / putrescible** | Anaerobic digestion / composting (wet route) |
| 25 – 50 | Medium moisture | Composting (mechanical turning) |
| < 25 | **Low-moisture / non-putrescible** | Incineration / RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel) |

MC = 62.35% → **High-moisture putrescible waste** → Anaerobic digestion preferred (also generates biogas as co-benefit).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Trees

#### 3.2.1 Air Pollutant Treatment Decision Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad &\text{Is the pollutant } \textbf{particulate}? \\
  &\quad \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Is PM size} > 10 \, \mu m? \quad \text{YES} \rightarrow \textbf{Cyclone Separator} \\
  &\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{NO} \rightarrow \textbf{Baghouse / ESP} \\
  &\quad \text{NO} \rightarrow \textbf{Step 2} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Is the pollutant } \textbf{gaseous}? \\
  &\quad \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Is it } \textbf{SO₂ / HCl}? \quad \text{YES} \rightarrow \textbf{Wet Scrubber} \\
  &\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{NO} \rightarrow \textbf{Step 3} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Is it } \textbf{VOC / Odour}? \\
  &\quad \text{YES} \rightarrow \textbf{Thermal / Catalytic Oxidizer} \\
  &\quad \text{NO} \rightarrow \textbf{Activated Carbon Adsorption}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a KTU-favorite for **"Justify your choice of pollution control equipment for XYZ industry"** — it scores full marks when you walk through the classification → decision → equipment logic.

#### 3.2.2 Water Pollutant Treatment Selection Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Class} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \textbf{Primary Treatment} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \textbf{Secondary Treatment} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \textbf{Tertiary Treatment} \\
\hline
\text{Suspended Solids} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Screening + Clarifier} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{—} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{—} \\
\text{BOD / COD} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Sedimentation} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Activated Sludge} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{—} \\
\text{Heavy Metals} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{pH adjustment} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Chemical Precipitation} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Ion Exchange} \\
\text{Nutrients (N, P)} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{—} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Nitrification-Denitrification} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Chemical P Removal} \\
\text{Oil & Grease} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Oil-Water Separator / API} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Dissolved Air Flotation} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{—}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Classify air pollutants into primary and secondary categories with two examples each. Why is this classification important for control technology selection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Primary Pollutants** (3 marks distributed as: 1 mark definition + 1 mark examples + 1 mark importance):

- **Definition (1 mark):** Pollutants emitted directly from identifiable sources in a harmful form. Examples: **CO** (incomplete combustion), **SO₂** (sulphur in fossil fuel), **NO** (high-temperature combustion), **Particulate Matter** (industrial dust).
- **Secondary Pollutants (1 mark):** Formed by atmospheric reactions between primary pollutants and natural constituents. Examples: **Ozone (O₃)** — formed by photolysis of NO₂ + VOCs; **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** — formed from VOCs + NOₓ under sunlight; **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄ mist)** — from SO₂ oxidation leading to acid rain.
- **Importance (1 mark):** Primary pollutants are controlled **at source** using scrubbers, ESPs, and cyclones. Secondary pollutants require **regional/ambient air quality management** (e.g., vehicular emission norms) since they are not emitted directly. Hence, classification determines whether control is **point-source** or **diffuse-source**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Explain the BOD₅/COD ratio as a classification parameter for wastewater. What threshold values indicate biodegradability versus recalcitrance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BOD₅** measures the biodegradable organic fraction (5-day biochemical oxygen demand), while **COD** measures the total chemical oxygen demand including non-biodegradable compounds.
- The ratio BOD₅/COD is therefore a **biodegradability index**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} > 0.5 &\rightarrow \text{Highly Biodegradable (biological treatment recommended)} \\
0.3 < \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} < 0.5 &\rightarrow \text{Borderline (combined physico-chemical + biological)} \\
\frac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} < 0.3 &\rightarrow \text{Recalcitrant (only physico-chemical: adsorption, AOPs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Example (1 mark):** Domestic sewage typically has BOD₅/COD ≈ 0.6 → biological treatment (ASP, RBC). Pharmaceutical effluent has BOD₅/COD ≈ 0.2 → requires advanced oxidation or adsorption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both parts below are **independent** — students can choose any one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) Classify the major categories of water pollutants in process industries. Discuss in detail any four categories with representative parameters, sources, and typical treatment technologies.**  **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the CPCB pollution-index-based classification of industries (Red/Orange/Green/White) with suitable examples. A pharmaceutical bulk drug manufacturing industry has air sub-index 65, water sub-index 75, and hazardous waste sub-index 70. Compute its Pollution Index and classify the industry. Use weights $W_A = 0.40$, $W_W = 0.40$, $W_H = 0.20$.**  **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** *(7 marks)*

**1. Suspended Solids (SS)** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Parameters:** Total Suspended Solids (TSS, mg/L), Turbidity (NTU).
- **Sources:** Pulp & paper, textile sizing, cement, mining slurries.
- **Treatment:** Primary clarifiers, dissolved air flotation (DAF), sedimentation tanks.

**2. Dissolved Organic Matter** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Parameters:** BOD₅, COD, TOC (Total Organic Carbon).
- **Sources:** Food processing, distillery, dairy, pharma fermentation.
- **Treatment:** Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Trickling Filter, Anaerobic Digester (UASB), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR).

**3. Heavy Metals** *(2 marks)*
- **Parameters:** Concentration of Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr(VI), Ni, As (mg/L); regulatory limits under EPA / Indian Standards (IS 2490).
- **Sources:** Electroplating rinse water, tanneries (Cr), battery manufacturing (Pb), metal finishing.
- **Treatment:** Chemical precipitation (pH adjustment + hydroxide/sulphide), ion exchange, electrocoagulation, membrane filtration.

**4. Nutrients (N, P)** *(2 marks)*
- **Parameters:** Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN, mg/L), Ammoniacal Nitrogen (NH₃-N), Total Phosphorus (TP).
- **Sources:** Fertilizer plants, agricultural runoff, detergent effluent.
- **Treatment:** Nitrification-Denitrification (biological), Struvite precipitation, Anammox process.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** *(7 marks)*

**Theory of CPCB Classification (3 marks):**

The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) classifies industries into four colour-coded categories based on the **Pollution Index (PI)**, a composite numerical score (0–100) calculated from:
- Air emission severity
- Water discharge severity
- Hazardous waste generation severity

| Category | Pollution Index Range | Regulatory Action |
|---|---|---|
| Red | ≥ 60 | Strict consent + continuous online monitoring + ETP/STP mandatory |
| Orange | 30 – 59 | Consent with conditions + periodic self-monitoring |
| Green | 15 – 29 | Only intimation required |
| White | < 15 | Practically non-polluting; no consent needed |

**Examples:**
- **Red:** Petroleum refinery, cement, sugar (large), fertilizer, bulk drug.
- **Orange:** Dyeing & bleaching, electroplating, pharma formulation.
- **Green:** Cotton ginning, bio-fertilizer units, cold storage.
- **White:** IT/ITES, R&D labs, electronic testing.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

Given:
- $S_A = 65$, $S_W = 75$, $S_H = 70$
- $W_A = 0.40$, $W_W = 0.40$, $W_H = 0.20$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &= \frac{(W_A \cdot S_A) + (W_W \cdot S_W) + (W_H \cdot S_H)}{W_A + W_W + W_H} \\[6pt]
         &= \frac{(0.40 \times 65) + (0.40 \times 75) + (0.20 \times 70)}{0.40 + 0.40 + 0.20} \\[6pt]
         &= \frac{26.0 + 30.0 + 14.0}{1.00} \\[6pt]
         &= 70.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **PI = 70.0 ≥ 60** → The pharmaceutical bulk drug industry falls in the **RED category** → Requires stringent consent under Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981, online continuous emission/effluent monitoring systems (OCEMS/CEMS), and dedicated ETP/STP.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]*

**(a) Classify solid waste based on source and characteristics. Discuss the four characteristics (Ignitability, Corrosivity, Reactivity, Toxicity) used to classify hazardous waste, with threshold values.**  **(7 marks)**

**(b) Distinguish between primary and secondary air pollutants with examples. Explain photochemical smog formation and the role of VOCs and NOₓ.**  **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** *(7 marks)*

**Source-based Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** Domestic, commercial, street sweeping. Biodegradable + recyclable fraction.
2. **Industrial Solid Waste:** Fly ash (thermal power), slag (steel), gypsum (FGD sludge), red mud (alumina).
3. **Biomedical Waste:** Human/animal tissue, sharps, pharmaceuticals, cytotoxic drugs.
4. **E-Waste:** Printed circuit boards, batteries, displays, mobile phones.
5. **Construction & Demolition (C&D):** Concrete rubble, steel, wood, glass.

**Hazardous Waste Classification — "4-I Rule" (4 marks):**

| Characteristic | Threshold (Indian HWM Rules 2016 / US EPA TCLP) | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignitability** | Flash point < 60 °C | Solvents, waste oils |
| **Corrosivity** | pH ≤ 2 OR pH ≥ 12.5 | Spent acids/alkalis, pickling liquor |
| **Reactivity** | Unstable / explosive / violently reacts with water | Sodium metal waste, cyanide salts |
| **Toxicity** | TCLP extract exceeds limits for Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr, As, Ba, Se, Ag | Spent catalyst, electroplating sludge |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** *(7 marks)*

**Primary vs Secondary (3 marks):**

- **Primary:** Emitted directly. Examples: SO₂ (from coal combustion), CO (vehicles), PM (industries).
- **Secondary:** Formed by atmospheric reactions. Examples: O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄ (acid rain), secondary organic aerosols.

**Photochemical Smog Formation (4 marks):**

Photochemical smog forms in **sunlit urban atmospheres** with high NOₓ and VOCs through the following sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad &\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (\lambda < 430 \, \text{nm}) \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad &\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M \quad (M = \text{third body}) \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad &\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad &\text{VOCs (RH) + OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad &\text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{RO}^{\bullet} \\[4pt]
\textbf{Result:} \quad &\text{NO is regenerated to NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{more O}_3 \text{ and } \textbf{PAN} \text{ formed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate, CH₃COOONO₂) is a **potent eye irritant** and phytotoxin. VOC + NOₓ + sunlight → O₃ + PAN + aldehydes + aerosols = **smog**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BOD with COD** — BOD is *biological*; COD is *chemical* (using K₂Cr₂O₇). Many students write them interchangeably.
> 2. **Photochemical smog ≠ classical (London) smog.** Classical smog = SO₂ + smoke + fog (winter, coal). Photochemical smog = O₃ + PAN + NOₓ + VOCs (summer, sunlight, vehicles). KTU papers regularly test this distinction.
> 3. **In PI computation,** do NOT forget to **divide by sum of weights** if the weights do not sum to 1 (here they do, so PI = 70; but in altered problems the divisor matters).
> 4. **Hazardous waste classification:** Listing "Ignitability, Corrosivity, Reactivity, Toxicity" without **numerical thresholds** loses 1 mark per characteristic in Q.B(a). Always quote the pH/flash point/TCLP values.
> 5. **CPCB categories:** Confusing **Green with White** is a common error. Green = low-polluting (still has emissions, e.g., cold storage using refrigerants). White = essentially zero-emission (e.g., IT offices).
> 6. **BOD/COD ratio:** Do NOT state "BOD > COD" — it is always COD ≥ BOD. COD includes the non-biodegradable fraction that BOD misses.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision — Memorize Before Every Exam:**

- [ ] Classification is the **first step** in pollution control → **identifies pollutant → selects abatement technology**.
- [ ] **Air pollutants:** Primary (direct) vs Secondary (atmospheric reactions). Examples of secondary: **O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄**.
- [ ] **Water pollutants:** 8 major classes — SS, dissolved inorganics, dissolved organics, dissolved gases, heavy metals, nutrients, oil & grease, microbes.
- [ ] **BOD₅/COD ratio:**
  - \> 0.5 → biodegradable → biological treatment.
  - \< 0.3 → recalcitrant → physico-chemical treatment.
- [ ] **Solid waste source types:** MSW, ISW, BMW, E-waste, C&D.
- [ ] **Hazardous waste "4-I":** Ignitability (FP < 60 °C), Corrosivity (pH ≤ 2 or ≥ 12.5), Reactivity, Toxicity (TCLP).
- [ ] **CPCB Industry Categories by PI:**
  - Red ≥ 60, Orange 30–59, Green 15–29, White < 15.
- [ ] **PI formula:** $PI = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot S_i}{\sum W_i}$.
- [ ] **Photochemical smog:** NO₂ + hν + VOCs → O₃ + PAN. Distinct from classical SO₂-based smog.
- [ ] **Heavy metal removal:** chemical precipitation (pH) → ion exchange → membrane filtration.
- [ ] **Moisture content rule for MSW:** MC > 50% → anaerobic digestion; MC < 25% → incineration / RDF.
- [ ] **Six master metrics:** TLV, LD₅₀, LC₅₀, BOD₅, COD, pH.
- [ ] **Always justify technology choice** with **classification logic** in exam answers — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
- [ ] **Always quote numerical thresholds** (pH, flash point, PI value, BOD/COD ratio) — examiners reward quantitative precision.
- [ ] **Indian regulatory anchors** to mention: Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2022), CPCB guidelines, IS 2490 (effluent discharge).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In every classification question, structure your answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Categories with examples → (iii) Parameter-based threshold → (iv) Treatment link → (v) Regulatory reference.** This 5-step framework guarantees full marks under KTU's outcome-based assessment rubric (CO attainment levels 3–4).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Analysis of Air Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Analysis of Air Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling** of air pollutants refers to the systematic collection of a representative portion of ambient air, stack emissions, or indoor air using scientifically validated instruments for subsequent **qualitative identification** and **quantitative determination** of pollutant concentration. **Analysis** is the subsequent physico-chemical measurement of the collected sample using laboratory or instrumental techniques to ascertain pollutant type, concentration, and source contribution.

In the framework of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT613)**, the term encompasses the complete chain of custody from **source identification → sample collection → preservation → instrumental analysis → data interpretation → reporting**, conforming to **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board, India)** protocols and **IS 5182** series specifications.

### Standard Metrics Used in KTU Curriculum

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass concentration | $\rho$ | $\mu g/m^3$ | $0 - 500$ |
| Volume mixing ratio | $\chi$ | $ppm$ or $ppb$ | $0 - 1000$ $ppm$ |
| Gravimetric | $m$ | $mg$ | Variable |
| Volume sampled | $V_s$ | $m^3$ | $0.001 - 100$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Anchor:** $1 \text{ ppm} = 1000 \text{ ppb} = 10^{-6} \text{ mol/mol}$. The mass-to-volume conversion at standard conditions (**$25^\circ C$, 101.325 kPa**) is governed by the **Ideal Gas Law**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **air quality monitoring** as a **"blood test for the atmosphere"**. Just as a doctor cannot diagnose a patient by simply looking at them, an environmental engineer cannot judge pollution levels by visual inspection alone. We must:

1. **Draw the sample** (like drawing blood) — using pumps, filters, and absorbents.
2. **Preserve the sample** (like storing in a vacutainer) — by refrigeration, chemical fixation, or absorption in reagents.
3. **Analyze in the lab** (like running a blood panel) — using spectrophotometers, chromatographs, or electrodes.

The **"representativeness"** of the sample is critical — a single breath-test reading of a smoker does not represent the general population. Similarly, a single air grab at a busy intersection does **not** represent city-wide exposure. Hence we use **composite, time-integrated, and statistical sampling strategies**.

## 1.3 Classification of Air Pollutants for Sampling Purposes

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes this classification because the **sampling strategy**, **preservation technique**, and **analytical method** all depend on the **physical state** of the pollutant.

- **Gaseous pollutants** — $SO_2$, $NO_x$, $CO$, $O_3$, $H_2S$, $NH_3$, $VOC$, $CO_2$
- **Particulate Matter (PM)** — dust, soot, smoke, fly ash, bioaerosols
- **Semi-volatile / Aerosols** — PAHs, dioxins, pesticides (exist in both phases)
- **Biological pollutants** — bacteria, fungal spores, pollen
- **Radioactive pollutants** — $Rn^{222}$, $Cs^{137}$ fallout
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — The Five-Stage Sampling-Analysis Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** When a question asks *"Explain the steps involved in air quality monitoring,"* write these **five stages** with at least one sentence per stage. Marks are awarded stage-wise.

### Stage 1 — **Site Selection & Survey**
- Identify **microenvironment** (residential, industrial, kerbside, background).
- Apply **CPCB NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards)** station siting criteria.
- Use **GPS coordinates** and **land-use regression** for spatial mapping.

### Stage 2 — **Sample Collection (Active / Passive)**
- **Active sampling** — uses a suction pump to draw a known volume through an adsorbent/filter.
- **Passive sampling** — relies on molecular diffusion (Fick's Law); no pump required; cheap and ideal for $NO_2$, $O_3$, $Benzene$.

### Stage 3 — **Sample Preservation**
- Refrigerate at $\le 4^\circ C$ for VOCs.
- Use **Tedlar bags** for reactive gases.
- Store filters in **desiccators** for gravimetric PM analysis.

### Stage 4 — **Sample Analysis (Laboratory / Field)**
- **Wet chemistry** — West-Gaeke method for $SO_2$, Jacob-Hochheiser for $NO_x$.
- **Instrumental** — NDIR, chemiluminescence, GC-MS, AAS, ICP-MS.

### Stage 5 — **Quality Assurance / Quality Control (QA/QC)**
- Field blanks, trip blanks, spiked samples.
- Calibration with **NIST-traceable** standards.

## 2.2 Types of Sampling Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Topic:** This is a recurring **5-mark short question** in KTU ESE papers (2019 – 2024 pattern).

| Sampling Type | Duration | Application | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grab Sampling** | Instantaneous ($< 60$ s) | Peak emissions, accident monitoring | Glass syringe, Tedlar bag |
| **Composite Sampling** | Hours to 24 h | Average ambient concentration | Sequential sampler, timer |
| **Continuous Sampling** | Real-time, 24x7 | $PM_{2.5}$, $O_3$, $CO$ | Beta attenuation monitor, chemiluminescence analyzer |
| **Stack Sampling (Isokinetic)** | Minutes | Source emission testing | USEPA Method 1–5 apparatus |
| **Personal Sampling** | 8 h work shift | Occupational exposure | Personal cascade impactor |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas verified against standard references (Cooper & Alley, "Air Pollution Control", 4th Ed. and CPCB NAAQS 2009 documentation).**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{mass} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_w}{24.45}$ | Converts **ppm** → **$\mu g/m^3$** at $25^\circ C, 1 atm$ |
| 2 | $C_{ppm} = C_{\mu g/m^3} \times \dfrac{24.45}{M_w}$ | Converts **$\mu g/m^3$** → **ppm** at $25^\circ C, 1 atm$ |
| 3 | $M_{adj} = 24.45 \times \dfrac{298}{T_K} \times \dfrac{P}{101.325}$ | Adjusted molar volume at non-standard $T, P$ |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \times v_{stack}$ | Stack gas volumetric flow rate ($m^3/s$) |
| 5 | $v_{nozzle} = v_{stack}$ (isokinetic condition) | Nozzle velocity must equal stack velocity |
| 6 | $C_{stack} = \dfrac{m_{collected}}{\dot{V}_s \times t}$ | Source emission concentration |
| 7 | $Fick's Law: J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Governs passive sampler uptake rate |
| 8 | $\eta_{collection} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{3 L Q}{2 d_c A U}\right)$ | Venturi scrubber collection efficiency (general) |
| 9 | $PM_{2.5} / PM_{10}$ ratio | Indicative of combustion vs. mechanical dust |
| 10 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}10^{L(t)/10}dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous sound level (used in noise monitoring context) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulatory compliance:** Industries must adhere to **CPCB emission norms**; sampling proves compliance or triggers penal action.
- **Epidemiological studies:** $PM_{2.5}$ concentration correlates with **respiratory mortality** (WHO, 2021).
- **Process control:** Real-time analyzers feed back to **DCS / PLC** in refineries for $H_2S$, $CO$, $NO_x$ trim.
- **Carbon trading:** Accurate baseline $CO_2$ sampling underpins **PAT scheme (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** in India.
- **Climate science:** $CO_2, CH_4, N_2O$ sampling towers (e.g., Mauna Loa) track global greenhouse trends.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ppm to $\mu g/m^3$ Conversion (Formula #1)

We begin with the definition of **parts per million by volume (ppm)** under the assumption of **ideal gas behavior**.

### Step 1 — Write the Definition
By definition, the volumetric mixing ratio in ppm is:
$$\chi = \dfrac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{total\ mixture}} \times 10^6$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation
For each component $i$:
$$V_i = \dfrac{n_i R T}{P}$$

Thus, the volume ratio equals the **mole ratio** (since $R, T, P$ are identical for all components at equilibrium):
$$\chi = \dfrac{n_{pollutant}}{n_{total}} \times 10^6 = y_{pollutant} \times 10^6$$

### Step 3 — Express Mass Concentration
Mass concentration of pollutant is:
$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} = \dfrac{n_{pollutant} \cdot M_w}{V_{air}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Using Mole Fraction
From Step 2: $n_{pollutant} = y_{pollutant} \cdot n_{total}$, and $n_{total} = \dfrac{PV_{air}}{RT}$.

Therefore:
$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{y_{pollutant} \cdot \dfrac{PV_{air}}{RT} \cdot M_w}{V_{air}}$$

Simplify $V_{air}$:
$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{y_{pollutant} \cdot P \cdot M_w}{RT}$$

### Step 5 — Insert Reference Conditions ($25^\circ C$, 101.325 kPa)
Molar volume at STP-reference conditions:
$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 298.15}{101.325} = 24.45\ L/mol = 0.02445\ m^3/mol$$

So:
$$C_{mass}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu g}{m^3}\right) = y_{pollutant} \times \dfrac{M_w}{0.02445} \times 10^6$$

Converting $y_{pollutant}$ from ppm:
$$y_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\chi_{ppm}}{10^6}$$

Hence the working formula:
$$\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = \chi_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_w}{24.45}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For $SO_2$ ($M_w = 64$), $1\ ppm = 64/24.45 \approx 2.618\ \mu g/m^3$. ✓ Matches CPCB reference tables.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Isokinetic Sampling Condition (Formula #5)

### Step 1 — Physical Setup
A probe is inserted into a **stack** carrying gas with linear velocity $v_{stack}$. The probe draws gas through a **nozzle of diameter $d_n$** at velocity $v_{nozzle}$.

### Step 2 — Definition of Isokinetic Sampling
> **Isokinetic condition:** the velocity of gas entering the probe nozzle must be **exactly equal** to the local velocity of the gas in the stack at the sampling point.

Mathematically:
$$v_{nozzle} = v_{stack}$$

### Step 3 — Why This Condition is Necessary
If $v_{nozzle} < v_{stack}$: gas streamlines **bend away** from the probe → lighter particles follow streamlines → **under-sampling of larger particles** (low-bias).
If $v_{nozzle} > v_{stack}$: streamlines **converge** into the probe → heavier particles have inertia and enter disproportionately → **over-sampling of larger particles** (high-bias).

### Step 4 — Volumetric Flow Constraint
The volumetric flow through the nozzle must satisfy:
$$Q_{probe} = v_{nozzle} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_n^2}{4}$$

Setting $v_{nozzle} = v_{stack}$:
$$Q_{probe} = v_{stack} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_n^2}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Relate to Stack Cross-Section
If the stack has diameter $D$:
$$Q_{stack} = v_{stack} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

The **isokinetic ratio** must remain **unity**:
$$\dfrac{Q_{probe}}{Q_{stack}} = \left(\dfrac{d_n}{D}\right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** US-EPA **Method 5** explicitly requires this; failure to maintain isokinetic condition can produce $\pm 50\%$ errors in $PM$ concentration. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptually.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — $SO_2$ Concentration Conversion

> **Problem:** Ambient air measurement shows $SO_2$ at $0.075\ ppm$. Express this in $\mu g/m^3$ at $25^\circ C, 1\ atm$. Given $M_w(SO_2) = 64.07\ g/mol$.

**Step 1:** Identify formula:
$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \chi_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_w}{24.45}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$C = 0.075 \times \dfrac{64.07}{24.45}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator–denominator ratio:
$$C = 0.075 \times 2.6205$$

**Step 4:** Final multiplication:
$$C \approx 0.1965\ \mu g/m^3$$

**Step 5:** Sanity check against CPCB NAAQS ($SO_2$ 24-h limit = $80\ \mu g/m^3$):
$$0.1965\ \ll 80 \Rightarrow \text{Site is well within compliance. } \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Methods vs. Pollutants

| Pollutant | Sampling Medium | Flow Rate (L/min) | Analysis Method | Reference Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $SO_2$ | Sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM) impinger | 1.0 | West-Gaeke (spectrophotometry at 548 nm) | IS 5182 (Part 2) |
| $NO_2$ | Saltzman reagent (absorbing solution) | 0.4 | Griess-Saltzman (azo dye, 540 nm) | IS 5182 (Part 6) |
| $O_3$ | Potassium iodide impinger / UV photometry | 1.5 | UV absorption at 254 nm | IS 5182 (Part 10) |
| $CO$ | Direct reading | 1.0 | NDIR (4.67 $\mu m$) | IS 5182 (Part 11) |
| $PM_{10}$ | Quartz filter in cyclone | 1.0 | Gravimetric | IS 5182 (Part 23) |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | Teflon filter in WINS impactor | 16.7 | Gravimetric + XRF | IS 5182 (Part 24) |
| $VOC$ | Tenax / Carbotrap sorbent tube | 0.1 | GC-MS (thermal desorption) | TO-17 (EPA) |
| $Pb$ | Cellulose filter + acid digestion | 1.0 | AAS / ICP-MS | IS 5182 (Part 22) |
| $H_2S$ | Cadmium hydroxide impinger | 1.0 | Methylene blue (670 nm) | IS 5182 (Part 7) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Air Quality Monitoring Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identify Sampling Objective] --> node2[Site Selection per CPCB Criteria]
    node2 --> node3[Choose Sampling Strategy]
    node3 -->|Grab| nodeA[Instantaneous Sample]
    node3 -->|Composite| nodeB[Time-Integrated Sample]
    node3 -->|Continuous| nodeC[Real-time Analyzer]
    node3 -->|Isokinetic| nodeD[Stack Sampling Train]
    nodeA --> node4[Sample Preservation]
    nodeB --> node4
    nodeC --> node6[Data Logger Output]
    nodeD --> node5[Gravimetric / Chemical Analysis]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node7[QA/QC and Validation]
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[Reporting and Compliance Check]
    node8 --> node9[Action / Regulatory Decision]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Continuous Gaseous Pollutant Analyzer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Ambient Air Inlet] --> nodeFilt[Particulate Filter]
    nodeFilt --> nodePump[Suction Pump with Flow Controller]
    nodePump --> nodeReact[Reaction Chamber]
    nodeReact -->|chemiluminescence| nodeDet1[Photomultiplier Detector]
    nodeReact -->|NDIR| nodeDet2[Infrared Detector]
    nodeReact -->|UV absorbance| nodeDet3[UV Photodiode]
    nodeDet1 --> nodeSig[Signal Processor and Amplifier]
    nodeDet2 --> nodeSig
    nodeDet3 --> nodeSig
    nodeSig --> nodeDisp[Digital Display and Data Logger]
    nodeDisp --> nodeCal[Auto Zero Span Calibration Module]
    nodeCal --> nodeReact
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — US-EPA Method 5 Stack Sampling Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeProbe[Sample Probe with Nozzle] --> nodePitot[Type S Pitot Tube]
    nodeProbe --> nodeHeater[Heated Filter Holder 248 F]
    nodeHeater --> nodeCond[Condenser and Impingers]
    nodeCond --> nodeMeter[Dry Gas Meter with Thermometer]
    nodeMeter --> nodePump[Vacuum Pump]
    nodePump --> nodeExh[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    nodePitot --> nodeMan[Inclined Manometer for Stack Velocity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice the **heated filter** in the EPA train — this prevents condensation of water-soluble gases ($SO_3, HCl$) that would otherwise cause **positive bias**. KTU exam questions often ask "why is the filter heated to $248^\circ F$ ($120^\circ C$)?" — answer: to drive off moisture while retaining PM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "isokinetic sampling" and state why it is critical in stack emission testing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Isokinetic sampling is a stack-emission collection technique in which the **linear velocity of gas entering the sampling probe nozzle is exactly equal to the linear velocity of the gas stream in the stack at the point of sampling** ($v_{nozzle} = v_{stack}$).

**Criticality (1 mark):**
- If the probe velocity is **lower** than the stack velocity, the streamlines diverge around the probe and **lighter particles deflect away** → **under-sampling of particulates** (negative bias).
- If the probe velocity is **higher**, streamlines converge and **heavier particles enter disproportionately** due to inertia → **over-sampling** (positive bias).

**Governing standard (1 mark):** US-EPA Method 5, IS 5182 (Part 1).

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "grab sampling" and "composite sampling" of ambient air with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Grab Sampling | Composite Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Single instantaneous sample ($< 60$ s) | Multiple grabs mixed, or continuous over $1-24$ h |
| **Information** | Peak / momentary concentration | Time-weighted average |
| **Equipment** | Glass syringe, Tedlar bag | Sequential sampler, integrated sampler |
| **Example use** | Accident emission, peak traffic hour | $PM_{10}$, $SO_2$ daily average per NAAQS |

(Any 3 distinguishing points = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "passive sampling uses a pump"** — it does NOT. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Always specify the wavelength** when describing UV photometry ($O_3$ at 254 nm).
> 3. **Units matter:** write **$\mu g/m^3$** for NAAQS comparison; writing ppm without specifying is **$-1$ mark**.
> 4. **Always cite IS 5182 / CPCB reference** — a question asking "Indian standard for $PM_{2.5}$" expects **IS 5182 Part 24**, not just "EPA".

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, procedure, and analysis method** of the **West-Gaeke method** for $SO_2$ determination in ambient air. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **US-EPA Method 5 stack sampling train** for particulate matter. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
$SO_2$ from ambient air is absorbed into a **0.04 M sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution, forming a **dichlorosulfitomercurate complex** that is stable and prevents oxidation by atmospheric $O_2$:
$$SO_2 + [HgCl_4]^{2-} \rightarrow [HgCl_2SO_3]^{2-} + 2Cl^-$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Air is drawn at **1 L/min** through an **impinger** containing 10 mL TCM for a defined time (typically 24 h → $1.44\ m^3$).
2. The complex is reacted with **formaldehyde** and **pararosaniline (PRA)** dye, producing a **red-violet pararosaniline methyl sulfonic acid** dye.
3. Absorbance is measured at **548 nm** in a spectrophotometer.
4. Calibration is done using a standard $SO_2$ solution from **sodium sulfite + $HCl$**.

**Calculation (1 mark):**
$$C_{SO_2}\!\left(\mu g/m^3\right) = \dfrac{(A_s - A_b) \times V_t \times 1000}{V_a \times \varepsilon \times l}$$

Where:
- $A_s$ = sample absorbance, $A_b$ = blank absorbance
- $V_t$ = total absorbing volume
- $V_a$ = volume of air sampled (corrected to $25^\circ C, 1$ atm)
- $\varepsilon$ = slope of calibration curve
- $l$ = path length (1 cm)

**Reference (1 mark):** IS 5182 (Part 2) : 2001 (Reaffirmed 2014).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Components in series (5 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Sample Probe** — stainless-steel nozzle, borosilicate glass probe with heating to prevent condensation.
2. **Type S Pitot Tube** — measures stack velocity from differential pressure.
3. **Heated Filter Holder** — holds **glass-fiber filter** at $120^\circ C \pm 14^\circ C$ (248°F ± 25°F) to retain PM while driving off moisture.
4. **Condenser + Impingers** — four impingers: first empty (catch condensate), next two with 100 mL distilled water (absorb $SO_2, HCl$), last empty (safety trap).
5. **Dry Gas Meter + Thermometer** — measures total volume sampled at standard conditions.
6. **Vacuum Pump + Orifice** — maintains constant flow.

**Procedure (1 mark):** The probe is inserted at a centroidal point; nozzle velocity is adjusted to match stack velocity using the Pitot-derived $v_{stack}$; sampling at **isokinetic condition**; weighing of filter before and after gives PM mass.

**Calculation (1 mark):**
$$C_{PM}\!\left(mg/Nm^3\right) = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^3}{V_{sampled,std}}$$

**Reference:** IS 5182 (Part 1) and US-EPA Method 5.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **principle and operation of a chemiluminescence analyzer** for **NO and $NO_2$** measurement. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the various **sampling techniques** for **particulate matter** ($PM_{10}$ and $PM_{2.5}$). (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (3 marks):**
Chemiluminescence is the emission of light from a **chemical reaction without combustion**. NO reacts with **ozone** ($O_3$) to form excited-state $NO_2^*$:
$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$$
$$NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu\ (\lambda > 600\ nm)$$

The emitted photons are detected by a **photomultiplier tube (PMT)**. The signal is **proportional to NO concentration**.

**Measurement of $NO_x$ and $NO_2$ (3 marks):**
- The sample passes through a **pre-reaction chamber** → reads only NO.
- Then through a **molybdenum converter** at $\approx 350^\circ C$ → converts $NO_2 \rightarrow NO$.
- Difference gives $NO_2 = NO_x - NO$.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**$PM_{10}$ (3 marks):**
- Sampler: **Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)** at **$1.1\ m^3/min$ (approx)**.
- Inlet: **$PM_{10}$ size-selective cyclone / impactor** with **50% cut-off at $10\ \mu m$**.
- Filter: glass-fiber / quartz / Teflon.
- Analysis: **gravimetric** (weighing before and after at $24 \pm 2\ hr$ conditioning, $50 \pm 5\%$ RH, $20 \pm 2^\circ C$).

**$PM_{2.5}$ (3 marks):**
- Sampler: **Fine Particulate Sampler (FPS)** at **$1\ m^3/hr$** OR **high-volume sampler at $1.13\ m^3/min$**.
- Inlet: **WINS impactor** (Well Impactor Ninety-Six) or **VSCC** (very sharp cut cyclone) — aerodynamic cut-off at $2.5\ \mu m$.
- Filter: **Teflon** (for gravimetric + elemental by XRF) or **quartz** (for carbon speciation).
- Analysis: gravimetric + subsequent speciation (OC/EC by thermal-optical analysis, metals by ICP-MS).

**Reference (1 mark):** CPCB NAAQS — $PM_{10}$ ($60\ \mu g/m^3$, 24-hr) and $PM_{2.5}$ ($40\ \mu g/m^3$, 24-hr).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — read twice before exam.**

- **Three categories of pollutants** — gaseous, particulate, biological. Sampling strategy differs.
- **Isokinetic sampling** is mandatory for stack PM; equal velocities of nozzle and stack gas.
- **ppm ↔ $\mu g/m^3$** conversion at $25^\circ C, 1 atm$ uses **$M_w / 24.45$**.
- **Active vs Passive sampling** — passive uses Fickian diffusion, no pump, lower cost, lower temporal resolution.
- **West-Gaeke** — for $SO_2$ → TCM absorber → PRA at **548 nm**.
- **Saltzman / Griess** — for $NO_2$ → azo dye at **540 nm**.
- **NDIR** — for $CO$ at **$4.67\ \mu m$** wavelength.
- **Chemiluminescence** — for NO/$NO_x$ → reaction with $O_3$ → red light.
- **$PM_{10}$** — cyclone impactor cut-off, gravimetric; **$PM_{2.5}$** — WINS or VSCC, gravimetric.
- **Reference Indian standards:** IS 5182 (Parts 1-25), CPCB NAAQS-2009.
- **Always cite:** sample volume, flow rate, sampling duration, temperature, and pressure when reporting data.
- **Standard conditions** for reporting stack emissions: **1 atm, 25°C** (corrected to "Nm³").
- **Heated filter at $120^\circ C$** in EPA Method 5 prevents moisture bias.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** "Particulate matter is measured in ppm." — **WRONG.** PM is **always in $\mu g/m^3$** (mass-based) because it is not a gas. Only gaseous pollutants use ppm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Effects of Air Pollution, Global Warming and Ozone Depletion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Health Effects of Air Pollution, Global Warming and Ozone Depletion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
