Cement Industry] K --> R[Small Flow
Sticky Dust] L --> S[Acid Mist
Submicron] M --> T[Air Conditioning
Clean Rooms] style A fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **List the five mechanisms by which fabric filters capture particulate matter from a gas stream.** **Model Answer:** The five collection mechanisms in fabric filters are: 1. **Straining** — Particles larger than fabric pores are mechanically trapped. 2. **Inertial Impaction** — Large/dense particles deviate from gas streamlines and strike fibers. 3. **Interception** — Mid-sized particles following streamlines touch fibers as they pass nearby. 4. **Diffusion (Brownian Motion)** — Submicron particles move randomly and collide with fibers. 5. **Electrostatic Attraction** — Naturally charged particles or fibers attract oppositely charged particles. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** Listing all 5 mechanisms in correct order (largest to smallest particle) scores full marks. Skipping the electrostatic mechanism costs 1 mark. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **State the Deutsch-Anderson equation. What is its significance in ESP design?** **Model Answer:** The **Deutsch-Anderson equation** for ESP collection efficiency is: $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ where $w$ = migration velocity (m/s), $A$ = total collection plate area (m²), and $Q$ = gas flow rate (m³/s). **Significance:** - It relates ESP efficiency to the **migration velocity** and **Specific Collection Area (SCA = A/Q)**. - It is the **primary design equation** for sizing ESP plate areas for a target efficiency. - It is derived from a first-order mass balance on a differential element of the ESP. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a pulse-jet fabric filter. List its advantages over a reverse-air baghouse.** **Model Answer:** **Construction:** A pulse-jet baghouse consists of the following components: 1. **Filter Bags** — Long, cylindrical bags (typically 4–6 m long, 0.12–0.30 m diameter) suspended vertically from a tubesheet. 2. **Cages (Support Frames)** — Internal wire cages prevent bag collapse during filtration. 3. **Hopper** — Conical bottom section for collecting dislodged dust. 4. **Pulse Valves** — Solenoid-operated valves for injecting compressed air. 5. **Compressed Air Reservoir** — High-pressure air source (typically 5–7 bar). 6. **Venturi Nozzle** — Mounted at the top of each bag to amplify cleaning pulse. **Working:** - Dirty gas enters the hopper section, flows **outside-to-inside** through the bag fabric, and clean gas exits through the top. - A **dust cake** forms on the bag surface; pressure drop rises. - When ΔP reaches a critical value, the controller triggers the **solenoid pulse valve**. - A short burst (0.1 s) of **compressed air** is injected into the bag, creating a shock wave. - The bag inflates and snaps, dislodging the dust cake. - Dust falls into the hopper and is removed via a **rotary airlock**. **Advantages over Reverse-Air Baghouse:** 1. Operates continuously (no need to take bags offline for cleaning). 2. Higher air-to-cloth ratio (1.0–3.0 m/min vs 0.5–1.0 m/min). 3. Smaller footprint and lower capital cost for the same duty. 4. Higher cleaning intensity → better for sticky or fine dust. 5. Faster cleaning cycle (frequent, brief pulses vs long reverse-air cycles). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **A baghouse handles 25,000 m³/hr of gas at 150 °C. The filter media is polyester with $K_m$ = 80 Pa·s/m and cake specific resistance $K_c$ = 1.5 × 10⁸ m/kg. The filtration velocity is 1.2 m/min. The dust mass per unit area after one cycle is 0.4 kg/m². Calculate:** 1. The total pressure drop. 2. The required filter cloth area. 3. The number of bags if each bag has an area of 2.5 m². **Model Solution:** **Part (b)(i) — Total Pressure Drop:** **Step 1:** Convert velocity to SI units. $$v_f = 1.2 \ \text{m/min} = \dfrac{1.2}{60} = 0.02 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the pressure drop equation. $$\Delta P = K_m \cdot v_f + K_c \cdot v_f \cdot W$$ $$\Delta P = (80)(0.02) + (1.5 \times 10^{8})(0.02)(0.4)$$ **Step 3:** Calculate each term. - Medium term: $80 \times 0.02 = 1.6 \ \text{Pa}$ - Cake term: $1.5 \times 10^{8} \times 0.02 \times 0.4 = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}$ **Step 4:** Sum the terms. $$\Delta P = 1.6 + 1{,}200{,}000 = 1{,}200{,}001.6 \ \text{Pa} \approx 1.2 \ \text{MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Most students forget to convert **m/min to m/s**. KTU examiners award 1 mark for correct unit conversion alone. Without it, the final answer is **50× off**! **Part (b)(ii) — Required Filter Cloth Area:** **Step 1:** Convert gas flow to m³/min. $$Q = \dfrac{25{,}000}{60} = 416.67 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the filtration velocity relation. $$A_c = \dfrac{Q}{v_f} = \dfrac{416.67}{1.2} = 347.22 \ \text{m}^2$$ **Part (b)(iii) — Number of Bags:** $$N = \dfrac{A_c}{A_{bag}} = \dfrac{347.22}{2.5} \approx 139 \ \text{bags}$$ Always round **up to the next whole number** to provide design margin → **140 bags**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Explain the working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) with a neat block diagram. What is corona discharge? How does it affect ESP efficiency?** **Model Answer:** **Working of ESP (4 stages):** 1. **Corona Discharge Generation:** A high negative DC voltage (**30–100 kV**) is applied between thin discharge wires and grounded collection plates. The strong non-uniform electric field near the wires ionizes surrounding gas, producing a bluish glow (corona). 2. **Particle Charging:** Dust particles entering the ESP collide with negative gas ions and acquire a charge (bombardment/diffusion charging). 3. **Particle Migration:** Charged particles experience a force $F = qE$ toward the positively charged collection plate. The drift velocity is called **migration velocity (w)**. 4. **Collection and Removal:** Particles deposit on the plate. Periodic **rapping** dislodges the dust layer into the hopper. **Corona Discharge:** Corona discharge is a localized electrical discharge that occurs when the electric field strength near a high-voltage electrode (wire) is high enough to ionize surrounding gas molecules (typically air), creating a region of charged ions and free electrons. It appears as a faint bluish glow and hissing sound. **Effect on ESP Efficiency:** - A **stable corona** ensures consistent particle charging → high efficiency. - **Excessive voltage** causes **back corona** (if dust resistivity is high), discharging collected particles and dropping efficiency. - **Low voltage** = weak charging = low efficiency. An optimal operating voltage exists (typically **30–50 kV** for industrial ESPs). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **A plate-type ESP handles 30 m³/s of flue gas. The plate area installed is 5,000 m². The migration velocity is 0.12 m/s. Calculate:** 1. The Deutsch-Anderson efficiency. 2. The required plate area to achieve 99.9% efficiency. 3. Comment on the design implications if the gas flow is doubled to 60 m³/s. **Model Solution:** **Part (b)(i) — Deutsch-Anderson Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Apply the Deutsch-Anderson equation. $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{0.12 \times 5{,}000}{30}\right) = 1 - \exp(-20)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate. $$\exp(-20) \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\eta \approx 1 - 2.06 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999998 \approx 99.9999\%$$ **Part (b)(ii) — Plate Area for 99.9% Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Rearrange the Deutsch-Anderson equation to solve for $A$. $$1 - \eta = \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ $$\ln(1 - \eta) = -\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}$$ $$A = -\dfrac{Q}{w} \cdot \ln(1 - \eta)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $\eta = 0.999$, $Q = 30$ m³/s, $w = 0.12$ m/s. $$1 - \eta = 0.001$$ $$\ln(0.001) = -6.9078$$ **Step 3:** Calculate. $$A = -\dfrac{30}{0.12} \times (-6.9078) = 250 \times 6.9078 = 1{,}726.95 \ \text{m}^2$$ **Answer:** Approximately **1,727 m²** of plate area is required (much less than the 5,000 m² installed — the ESP is overdesigned for the original load). **Part (b)(iii) — Design Implication for Doubled Flow:** **Step 1:** Calculate the new efficiency with $Q = 60$ m³/s (same $A$ and $w$). $$\eta_{new} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{0.12 \times 5{,}000}{60}\right) = 1 - \exp(-10)$$ $$\eta_{new} = 1 - 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 99.995\%$$ **Comment:** Doubling the gas flow **decreases efficiency** (from 99.9999% to 99.995%) because the SCA (A/Q) is halved. To maintain 99.9% efficiency at 60 m³/s, the required plate area would scale linearly: $A_{new} = 2 \times 1{,}727 \approx 3{,}454 \ \text{m}^2$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (ESP):** > - **Forgetting the negative sign** in the rearranged equation: $A = -(Q/w) \cdot \ln(1-\eta)$ — losing this gives a **negative area**, a guaranteed zero. > - **Confusing A/C ratio with SCA** — fabric filter uses $Q/A_c$ (m/min), ESP uses $A/Q$ (s/m). These are **inverses with different units**. > - **Not commenting** on the design implication part in (iii) costs **2 marks** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the practical insight. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist (Module 3 — Fabric Filters & ESP):** ### **Fabric Filters (Baghouses)** - ☐ Definition: Dry device using **porous fabric** to filter particulates from gas streams. - ☐ **5 collection mechanisms**: Straining, Impaction, Interception, Diffusion, Electrostatic. - ☐ **3 types**: Shaker, Reverse-Air, Pulse-Jet (know cleaning method for each). - ☐ **Key equation**: $\Delta P = K_m \cdot v_f + K_c \cdot v_f \cdot W$ — memorize! - ☐ **Air-to-cloth ratio**: 0.5–1.0 m/min (reverse-air), 1.0–3.0 m/min (pulse-jet). - ☐ **Efficiency**: >99.9% for submicron particles. - ☐ **Filter media**: Cotton, Polyester, Nomex, Teflon, Glass fiber (max temp limits matter). - ☐ **Dust cake** improves filtration efficiency over time (non-obvious fact). ### **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)** - ☐ Definition: Device that **charges particles by corona discharge** and collects them on oppositely charged electrodes. - ☐ **4 working stages**: Corona generation → Particle charging → Migration → Collection + Rapping. - ☐ **Corona voltage**: 30–100 kV negative DC. - ☐ **Deutsch-Anderson equation** (THE most important): $\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ — derive it! - ☐ **Migration velocity (w)**: 0.05–0.30 m/s (typical industrial). - ☐ **SCA (A/Q)**: 30–90 s/m for industrial ESPs. - ☐ **Types**: Plate-wire (most common), Pipe-type, Wet, Two-stage. - ☐ **Particle resistivity** optimum: 10⁴ – 10¹⁰ Ω·cm. - ☐ **Back corona** problem at high resistivity (>10¹¹ Ω·cm) reduces efficiency. ### **Numerical Problem Patterns (KTU 2024 Scheme)** - ☐ Fabric filter: Calculate A/C ratio, pressure drop, or number of bags (unit conversion critical). - ☐ ESP: Calculate efficiency, required plate area, or effect of flow/area changes. - ☐ Always show **step-by-step substitution** and **final units** in the answer. ### **Common Exam Traps (Examiner's Perspective)** - ☐ Mixing up $A/C$ (fabric filter) with $SCA$ (ESP) — they are **conceptual reciprocals**. - ☐ Forgetting to convert m/min to m/s in pressure drop calculations. - ☐ Confusing **migration velocity (w)** with **face velocity (v)** — different physical meanings. - ☐ Not commenting on the **practical design implications** in 14-mark answers (KTU 2024 emphasizes application-based questions). - ☐ Failing to mention the **5 mechanisms** in fabric filter theory questions. ---

topics.title: Control Devices for Gaseous Contaminants: Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Devices for Gaseous Contaminants: Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption** is a surface-based mass transfer operation in which molecules of a gas or vapour (the **adsorbate**) accumulate on the surface of a solid material (the **adsorbent**) due to intermolecular forces of attraction at the gas–solid interface. The process is exothermic in nature and is fundamentally a **surface phenomenon**, distinct from absorption which is a bulk phenomenon.

In the context of air pollution control, adsorption is employed as a **gaseous contaminant control technology** in which toxic or odorous gases (e.g., VOCs, SO₂, H₂S, Hg vapour, hydrocarbons) are physically or chemically trapped on the internal micro-porous structure of a high-surface-area solid, thereby cleaning the effluent gas stream.

The reverse process — removal of the adsorbed species to restore the activity of the solid — is termed **desorption** or **regeneration**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Magnetic Sponge in a Stinky Room":** Imagine you walk into a room filled with the smell of paint. If you place a wet sponge inside, the sponge *absorbs* the paint vapour into its bulk water content. Now imagine a charcoal pouch hung in the same room. The VOC molecules do not enter the bulk charcoal — instead, they **stick to the inner walls of the millions of tiny pores** inside the charcoal. This is adsorption. The charcoal is the *adsorbent*; the VOCs are the *adsorbate*.

In simple words:
- **Absorption** = paint enters inside the sponge's water (bulk phenomenon).
- **Adsorption** = paint molecules stick to charcoal pore walls (surface phenomenon).

## 1.3 Types of Adsorption

| Type | Driving Force | Heat of Adsorption | Reversibility | Layers |
|------|--------------|-------------------|---------------|--------|
| **Physisorption** | Weak **van der Waals** forces | Low (5–40 kJ/mol) | Easily reversible | Multi-layer |
| **Chemisorption** | Strong **chemical bonds** (covalent/ionic) | High (40–400 kJ/mol) | Mostly irreversible | Mono-layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For pollution control applications, **physisorption on activated carbon** is the dominant mechanism because it allows easy regeneration and recovery of valuable VOCs.

## 1.4 Common Industrial Adsorbents

| Adsorbent | Surface Area (m²/g) | Typical Application |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Activated Carbon** | 600 – 1500 | VOC removal, Hg vapour, odour |
| **Activated Alumina** | 200 – 400 | HF, water vapour drying |
| **Silica Gel** | 700 – 800 | Moisture removal |
| **Zeolites (Molecular Sieves)** | 500 – 800 | Naphthalene, dioxins, selective separation |
| **Impregnated Carbon (e.g., KI)** | Variable | Hg, H₂S removal |

## 1.5 Why Adsorption is Critical in Pollution Control

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Activated carbon adsorption can achieve **>99 % removal efficiency** for VOCs at low inlet concentrations (typically < 5000 ppmv), which is often unattainable by thermal oxidizers due to flame instability at very lean mixtures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Steps in an Adsorption System

1. **Transport of bulk gas** to the external surface of the adsorbent pellet — governed by **convective mass transfer coefficient, k_g**.
2. **Film diffusion** of the adsorbate through the gas film surrounding the pellet.
3. **Pore diffusion** of the adsorbate through the tortuous internal pore network — governed by **effective diffusivity, D_eff**.
4. **Surface diffusion** along the inner pore walls.
5. **Adsorption** at an active site, releasing the **isosteric heat of adsorption, ΔH_ads**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** In any KTU numerical on breakthrough time, the *slowest* of these steps controls the rate. For activated carbon at industrial flow rates, **pore diffusion** is usually rate-limiting.

## 2.2 Adsorption Equilibrium — Isotherms

Adsorption equilibrium describes the relationship between the **bulk gas-phase concentration, C (or partial pressure, P)** and the **equilibrium loading on the solid, q* (kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent)** at a fixed temperature.

### 2.2.1 Langmuir Isotherm
**Assumptions:** Mono-layer adsorption, uniform surface, no interaction between adsorbed molecules.

$$q^* = \frac{q_m \, b \, C}{1 + b \, C}$$

Where:
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (kg/kg)
- $b$ = Langmuir affinity constant (m³/kg or Pa⁻¹)
- $C$ = bulk gas concentration (kg/m³ or Pa)

### 2.2.2 Freundlich Isotherm
**Assumptions:** Heterogeneous surface, multi-layer adsorption, empirical power-law.

$$q^* = K_F \, C^{1/n}$$

Where $K_F$ and $n$ are empirical constants; typically $n > 1$ for favourable adsorption.

### 2.2.3 BET Isotherm (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller)
**Used for:** Multi-layer physisorption at high relative pressures; the basis for measuring **specific surface area** via N₂ adsorption at 77 K.

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m \, C_{BET}} + \frac{(C_{BET} - 1)}{V_m \, C_{BET}} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

## 2.3 Breakthrough Curve and the Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)

A **fixed-bed adsorber** is operated until the bed is exhausted. The characteristic **S-shaped breakthrough curve** plots outlet concentration $C/C_0$ vs. time.

Three regions along the bed:
- **Saturated zone** (top) — fully loaded, $C = C_0$.
- **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** — active adsorption region, length $L_{MTZ}$.
- **Virgin zone** (bottom) — clean adsorbent still available.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Langmuir | $q^* = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, C}{1 + b \, C}$ | $q_m$ [kg/kg], $b$ [m³/kg] | Equilibrium loading |
| Freundlich | $q^* = K_F \, C^{1/n}$ | $K_F$, $n$ [dimensionless] | Empirical design |
| BET Surface Area | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{N_A \, A_m}{V_{molar}} \cdot V_m$ | $S$ [m²/g], $V_m$ [cm³/g STP] | Surface area measurement |
| Wheeler–Jonas (Breakthrough) | $t_b = \dfrac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \dfrac{\rho_b \, q^* \cdot L}{C_0 \, v}$ | $t_b$ [s], $W$ [kg], $Q$ [m³/s], $v$ [m/s] | Predicting breakthrough time |
| Pressure Drop (Ergun) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \, \mu \, v_s}{d_p^2} \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3} + \dfrac{1.75 \, \rho \, v_s^2}{d_p} \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}$ | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $\varepsilon$ [—] | Bed sizing, fan power |
| Heat of Adsorption (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\Delta H_{ads} = R \dfrac{\ln(C_1/C_2)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)}$ | $\Delta H$ [J/mol], $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K | Temperature-rise prediction |
| Carbon Usage Rate | $CUR = \dfrac{Q (C_0 - C_{out})}{q \cdot \rho_{carbon}}$ | $CUR$ [kg/h] | Operating cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU:** Always state the **regeneration method** (TSA, PSA, VSA, or microwave) when designing any adsorption system — it is a guaranteed 2-mark part in 14-mark questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical plants:** Recovery of gasoline vapours from tanker-truck loading — regenerated by **vacuum swing adsorption (VSA)**.
- **Semiconductor fabs:** Removal of **dopant gases** (AsH₃, PH₃) on impregnated carbon.
- **Power plants:** **Mercury (Hg⁰) capture** on sulphur-impregnated activated carbon in flue-gas ductwork.
- **Municipal waste:** **Olfactometric odour control** at sewage treatment plants.
- **Indoor air quality:** Building HVAC adsorbent cassettes for VOCs and formaldehyde.

## 2.6 Selection Criteria for an Adsorbent (Decision Matrix)

| Criterion | Activated Carbon | Zeolite | Activated Alumina | Silica Gel |
|-----------|------------------|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| Polarity preference | Non-polar | Polar | Polar | Polar |
| Moisture sensitivity | Moderate | Low | High | Very High |
| Temperature stability (°C) | 200 | 600 | 400 | 250 |
| Regenerability | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Relative Cost | Moderate | High | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm (Statistical Kinetics)

### Step 1: Define elementary rates
Let $\theta$ be the **fraction of surface covered** by adsorbed molecules at equilibrium.
The rate of *adsorption* is proportional to the bare fraction $(1 - \theta)$ and the gas concentration $C$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta)$$

The rate of *desorption* is proportional to the covered fraction $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

### Step 2: Apply equilibrium
At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\theta$
Re-arranging:

$$k_a \, C - k_a \, C \, \theta = k_d \, \theta$$

$$k_a \, C = \theta \, (k_d + k_a \, C)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a \, C}{k_d + k_a \, C} = \frac{(k_a/k_d) \, C}{1 + (k_a/k_d) \, C}$$

### Step 4: Substitute notation
Define $b = k_a/k_d$ (equilibrium constant) and $q^* = q_m \, \theta$:

$$q^* = \frac{q_m \, b \, C}{1 + b \, C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** When $bC \ll 1$ (very low concentration), $q^* \approx q_m b C$ → linear (Henry's law region). When $bC \gg 1$, $q^* \approx q_m$ → saturation monolayer.

## 3.2 Derivation of Wheeler–Jonas Breakthrough Equation

### Step 1: Stoichiometric (ideal) adsorption time
If the entire bed reached equilibrium loading $W_{eq}$, the **stoichiometric time** is:

$$t^* = \frac{W_{eq}}{Q \, C_0} = \frac{\rho_b \, q^* \, L}{v \, C_0}$$

Where:
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of bed [kg/m³]
- $L$ = bed length [m]
- $v$ = superficial gas velocity [m/s]

### Step 2: Effective adsorption time (loss of MTZ)
The mass transfer zone uses only a fraction of its capacity. Define:

$$t_b = t^* - \frac{\rho_b \, q^* \, L_{MTZ}}{C_0 \, v}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $L_{MTZ}$ in terms of weight
Using the equivalent bed length formulation:

$$t_b = \frac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \frac{\rho_b \, q^* \, L}{C_0 \, v}$$

### Step 4: Simplification for design
Often rearranged as:

$$t_b = \frac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \tau$$

Where $\tau$ is the **critical bed depth correction term** accounting for MTZ.

> [!TIP]
> **Worked numerical check (typical KTU value):**
> Given: $W = 100$ kg, $X = 0.10$, $Q = 0.05$ m³/s, $C_0 = 0.005$ kg/m³.
> Stoichiometric term = $(100 \times 0.10)/(0.05 \times 0.005) = 40{,}000$ s $\approx 11.1$ hours.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Ergun Equation (Pressure Drop Across Bed)

### Step 1: Viscous (Darcy) term
For low Reynolds number flow through a packed bed:

$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{visc} = \frac{150 \, \mu \, v_s}{d_p^2} \cdot \frac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$$

### Step 2: Inertial (kinetic) term
For higher velocities, kinetic-energy losses dominate:

$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{inert} = \frac{1.75 \, \rho \, v_s^2}{d_p} \cdot \frac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}$$

### Step 3: Superposition (Ergun's postulate)
Total pressure drop = sum of viscous and inertial contributions:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \, \mu \, v_s}{d_p^2} \cdot \frac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3} + \frac{1.75 \, \rho \, v_s^2}{d_p} \cdot \frac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** In 14-mark design problems, $\Delta P / L \times L$ gives total bed pressure drop; this determines the **blower power** $P_{fan} = Q \cdot \Delta P / \eta_{fan}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TSA vs. PSA vs. VSA Regeneration

| Parameter | TSA (Thermal Swing) | PSA (Pressure Swing) | VSA (Vacuum Swing) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Driving Force | Temperature change | Pressure change | Sub-atmospheric pull |
| Operating Cycle | 4–8 hours | 1–10 minutes | 1–30 minutes |
| Energy Form | Steam / hot gas | Electric (compressor) | Electric (vacuum pump) |
| Heat of Adsorption Use | Yes (release during cool) | No | No |
| Typical Application | VOC removal on carbon | Air-drying, H₂ purification | VOC recovery on carbon |
| Capital Cost | High (heaters + exchangers) | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| Recovery Yield | 60–80 % | 85–95 % | 90–98 % |

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step — Mass Transfer Zone Length, $L_{MTZ}$

### Step 1: Use breakthrough data
From breakthrough curve, identify:
- $t_b$ = breakthrough time (when $C_{out}/C_0 = 0.05$).
- $t_s$ = saturation time (when $C_{out}/C_0 \approx 1.0$).

### Step 2: Calculate MTZ length

$$L_{MTZ} = L \cdot \frac{t_s - t_b}{t_s - (1 - f) t_b}$$

Where $f$ = fraction of bed saturation at breakthrough (typically $0.5$).

### Step 3: Calculate equilibrium loading
For a known inlet concentration and flow rate:

$$q^* = \frac{Q \int_0^{t_s} (C_0 - C_{out}) \, dt}{W}$$

### Step 4: Verify material balance
Numerical integration of breakthrough curve should equal $q^* \cdot W$ within ±5 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to convert breakthrough time units (often given in minutes) to **seconds** for SI consistency in the Wheeler–Jonas equation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fixed-Bed Adsorption System — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InletGas[Inlet Polluted Gas Stream] --> PreFilter[Pre-Filter Particulate Removal]
    PreFilter --> Fan[ID Fan Blower]
    Fan --> BottomInlet[Bottom Inlet Distribution Header]
    BottomInlet --> AdsorberBed[Vessel With Carbon Bed Height L]
    AdsorberBed --> TopOutlet[Top Outlet Clean Gas Stack]
    TopOutlet --> CleanAir[Cleaned Air to Atmosphere]

    AdsorberBed -.-> MTZ[Mass Transfer Zone L MTZ]
    AdsorberBed -.-> SatZone[Saturated Zone]
    AdsorberBed -.-> VirginZone[Virgin Zone]

    RegenerationUnit[Steam or Hot Nitrogen Heater] --> RegInlet[Regeneration Inlet Manifold]
    RegInlet --> AdsorberBed

    AdsorberBed --> RegOutlet[Regeneration Outlet To Condenser]
    RegOutlet --> Condenser[Condenser For VOC Recovery]
    Condenser --> Separator[Phase Separator Liquid VOC]
    Separator --> VOCStorage[VOC Storage Tank]

    Control[PLC Based Valves And Sensors] -. regulates .-> BottomInlet
    Control -. regulates .-> RegInlet
    Control -. regulates .-> RegOutlet
```

## 4.2 Breakthrough Curve — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BedFlow[Gas Flow Through Fixed Bed]
        direction LR
        A[Inlet C equals C zero] --> B[Fresh Adsorbent Top]
        B --> C[MTZ Active Region]
        C --> D[Exhausted Zone Near Outlet]
        D --> E[Outlet C equals near zero initially]
    end

    subgraph BCurve[Breakthrough Curve]
        direction LR
        X[Time Axis] --> Y[C out by C zero]
        T1[Time equals t b Breakthrough] --> T2[Time equals t s Saturation]
    end
```

## 4.3 Adsorption Process Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Incoming Gaseous Contaminant] --> Q1{Concentration Level}
    Q1 -- Less than 5000 ppmv --> Q2{Is Recovery Desired}
    Q1 -- Greater than 5000 ppmv --> R1[Consider Thermal Oxidation or Scrubbing]
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Use Carbon Bed with VSA Regeneration]
    Q2 -- No --> P2[Use Carbon Bed with TSA Regeneration]
    P1 --> P3[Typical Removal Efficiency 90 to 99 percent]
    P2 --> P3
    R1 --> Q3{Is Particulate Loading High}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P4[Add Pre-Filter Bag House]
    Q3 -- No --> P5[Direct to Thermal Unit]
    P4 --> P5
```

## 4.4 Detailed Block Diagram — Industrial Carbon Adsorber with Regeneration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ProcessGas[Process Off Gas Inlet] --> Comp1[Compressor K1]
    Comp1 --> Cooler[After Cooler Heat Exchanger]
    Cooler --> Valve1[Three Way Valve V1 Position 1 Adsorption Mode]
    Valve1 --> Bed1[Adsorber Vessel 1 Active]
    Bed1 --> Valve2[Three Way Valve V2 Position 1 Adsorption Mode]
    Valve2 --> Clean[Cleaned Gas to Stack]

    Valve1 -. when saturated .-> Valve1R[Valve V1 Position 2 Regeneration Mode]
    Valve1R --> Steam[Steam Generator at 120 Degree Celsius]
    Steam --> Bed1Reg[Bed 1 Now in Regeneration Mode]
    Bed1Reg --> Valve2R[Valve V2 Position 2 Regeneration Mode]
    Valve2R --> Cond[Condenser]
    Cond --> Sep[Knock Out Drum]
    Sep --> Rec[Recovered VOC Liquid]

    Bed2[Adsorber Vessel 2 Standby or Active Parallel] -. parallel operation .-> Valve1
    Bed2 -. parallel operation .-> Valve2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Two parallel beds allow **continuous operation** — while Bed 1 is in adsorption mode (4–8 hours), Bed 2 is being regenerated. The PLC switches valves (V1, V2) seamlessly, ensuring zero downtime — a critical design feature in refineries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Force | van der Waals | Chemical bond |
| Heat of Adsorption | 5–40 kJ/mol | 40–400 kJ/mol |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Mostly irreversible |
| Layer | Multi-layer | Mono-layer |
| Temperature | Low temperature | Higher temperature |
| Example | VOCs on activated carbon | Hg on Cu-impregnated carbon |

*(1 mark for each of three distinguishing rows — board pattern)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a breakthrough curve? What does it indicate in a fixed-bed adsorber?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **breakthrough curve** is a plot of outlet gas concentration (C/C₀) versus elapsed time (or throughput volume) for a fixed-bed adsorber operating at constant inlet concentration and flow rate. It indicates:
1. **Breakthrough time (t_b)** — point at which outlet concentration reaches the **regulatory or design limit** (typically 5–10 % of C₀).
2. **Saturation time (t_s)** — when the bed is fully exhausted.
3. **Shape of the curve** reveals the **mass transfer zone (MTZ) length** and bed efficiency. A steeper S-curve indicates a sharper MTZ and better adsorbent utilization.

*(1 mark each for definition, t_b significance, t_s significance)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. State its assumptions and explain how it can be simplified for low and high concentration limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **fixed-bed activated carbon adsorber** used for VOC control. Discuss **TSA regeneration** in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Mono-layer adsorption only.
2. All adsorption sites are energetically equivalent.
3. No interaction between adjacent adsorbed molecules.
4. Each site can hold only one molecule.

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage.
Adsorption rate: $r_{ads} = k_a C (1 - \theta)$
Desorption rate: $r_{des} = k_d \theta$
At equilibrium: $k_a C (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$

Solving:

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C}{k_d + k_a C} = \frac{b C}{1 + b C}$$

where $b = k_a / k_d$.

Expressing in loading form $q^* = q_m \theta$:

$$q^* = \frac{q_m \, b \, C}{1 + b \, C}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting Cases (1 mark):**
- Low concentration ($bC \ll 1$): $q^* \approx q_m b C$ → **Henry's Law**, linear.
- High concentration ($bC \gg 1$): $q^* \approx q_m$ → **saturation monolayer**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Vertical cylindrical pressure vessel of carbon steel or SS.
- Internal **distributor plates** at top (gas in during adsorption) and bottom (gas out).
- Packed with **granular activated carbon (GAC)** with typical pellet size 1–3 mm.
- **Bed depth** typically 0.5–2.0 m depending on VOC load.
- Ports for **steam injection** (regeneration) and **condensate drainage**.
- **Pressure gauges**, **temperature sensors**, **sampling ports**.
- **Bed supports** (grating + support balls) to prevent carbon carryover.

**Working – Adsorption Cycle (2 marks):**
- VOC-laden gas enters top → VOCs adsorb on carbon pore surface → clean air exits bottom.
- Operation continues until outlet concentration reaches **breakthrough threshold**.
- Valves switch to **parallel bed** for continuity.

**TSA Regeneration (2 marks):**
- Steam at **120–150 °C** is passed **counter-currently** through the bed.
- Vapour-laden stream leaves the bed → goes to **condenser** → liquid VOC recovered in phase separator.
- Bed is then **dried and cooled** before returning to service.
- Cycle time typically 4–8 hours; total cycle = adsorption + regeneration + cooling.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the **Wheeler–Jonas equation** for predicting breakthrough time in a fixed-bed adsorber. Using the equation, calculate the breakthrough time for a carbon bed with the following data:  
Mass of carbon $W = 500$ kg, working capacity $X = 0.08$ kg VOC/kg C, gas flow rate $Q = 0.1$ m³/s, inlet concentration $C_0 = 0.002$ kg/m³, critical bed depth correction $\tau = 3600$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **TSA, PSA and VSA** regeneration techniques. Discuss any **two industrial applications** of adsorption in pollution control. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Theory of Wheeler–Jonas Equation (3 marks):**
The Wheeler–Jonas equation relates breakthrough time to bed capacity, flow rate, and inlet concentration:

$$t_b = \frac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \tau$$

Where:
- $t_b$ = breakthrough time [s]
- $W$ = mass of adsorbent [kg]
- $X$ = working capacity (kg adsorbate/kg adsorbent)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate [m³/s]
- $C_0$ = inlet VOC concentration [kg/m³]
- $\tau$ = critical bed depth correction term [s]

**Numerical Substitution (3 marks):**

$$t_b = \frac{500 \times 0.08}{0.1 \times 0.002} - 3600$$

$$t_b = \frac{40}{0.0002} - 3600$$

$$t_b = 200{,}000 - 3600 = 196{,}400 \text{ s}$$

$$t_b = \frac{196{,}400}{3600} \approx 54.6 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer (1 mark):** Breakthrough time ≈ **54.6 hours** or **2.27 days** of continuous operation before bed switching is required.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table of TSA/PSA/VSA (4 marks):**

| Parameter | TSA | PSA | VSA |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Driving Force | Temperature | Pressure | Vacuum |
| Cycle Time | 4–8 h | 1–10 min | 1–30 min |
| Energy Use | Steam / heat | Compressor | Vacuum pump |
| Yield | 60–80 % | 85–95 % | 90–98 % |
| Best For | Heavy VOCs | Light gases | VOC recovery |

**Two Industrial Applications (3 marks):**
1. **Mercury Removal in Coal-Fired Power Plants:** Sulphur-impregnated activated carbon injected into flue gas duct reacts with elemental Hg⁰ to form HgS, captured downstream in the particulate control device. Removal efficiencies >90 %.
2. **VOC Recovery from Tank Loading Operations:** Vapours displaced during gasoline/petroleum tanker loading pass through carbon beds; VOCs are recovered via VSA and returned to product tank. Emission reduction >95 %, with revenue recovery of recovered hydrocarbons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in Wheeler–Jonas numericals (kg vs g, s vs h) — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not stating regeneration method** in fixed-bed design questions — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing absorption with adsorption** in Part A — examiners flag this immediately.
> 4. **Omitting assumptions** of Langmuir isotherm in derivations — at least 1 mark lost.
> 5. **No diagram** in 14-mark construction question — minimum 1–2 marks lost as per valuation key.
> 6. **Wrong bed configuration** (co-current vs counter-current regeneration) — must state clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Quick-Recall Points for KTU Module 3 — Adsorption:**

- ✅ **Adsorption = surface phenomenon**, absorption = bulk phenomenon. (Most-tested 2-mark line.)
- ✅ **Physisorption** is reversible and multi-layer; **chemisorption** is mostly irreversible and mono-layer.
- ✅ **Langmuir** assumes mono-layer; **Freundlich** is empirical; **BET** is for multi-layer / surface-area measurement.
- ✅ **Activated carbon** is the workhorse for VOC control — surface area **600–1500 m²/g**.
- ✅ **Breakthrough curve** is S-shaped; plot of $C/C_0$ vs time at constant inlet conditions.
- ✅ **MTZ (Mass Transfer Zone)** is the active region in a fixed bed; short MTZ = sharp breakthrough = better design.
- ✅ **Wheeler–Jonas Equation:** $t_b = \dfrac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \tau$ — must know by heart.
- ✅ **TSA** uses heat (steam); **PSA** uses pressure; **VSA** uses vacuum — draw comparative table in exams.
- ✅ **Ergun Equation** for bed pressure drop; determines blower/fan power.
- ✅ **Two-bed parallel configuration** is the industrial standard for **continuous operation**.
- ✅ **Mercury removal** on sulphur-impregnated carbon is a high-impact flue-gas application.
- ✅ Always convert breakthrough time to **seconds** in SI-based numericals.
- ✅ Industrial **VOC recovery** with VSA offers both emission control and **product recovery revenue**.
- ✅ **Adsorbent selection** depends on: polarity, moisture sensitivity, thermal stability, regenerability, cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Absorption # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Absorption** is a mass-transfer operation in which one or more soluble components of a gas mixture are selectively dissolved into a liquid solvent. In the context of *Pollution Control in Process Industries*, absorption is the most widely used **end-of-pipe control technology** for removing gaseous pollutants (e.g., SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, NOₓ, HCl, NH₃, VOCs, and particulate-bound gases) from industrial exhaust streams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *"Absorption is a diffusional mass-transfer process governed by the transfer of a solute (pollutant gas) from a bulk gas phase across a gas-liquid interface into a bulk liquid phase, driven by a concentration (or partial pressure) gradient and described quantitatively by Henry's Law and the two-film theory."* **Classification of Absorption:** | Type | Description | Example in Pollution Control | |------|-------------|------------------------------| | **Physical Absorption** | Solute dissolves physically; no chemical reaction | Removal of SO₂ with water | | **Chemical Absorption** | Solute reacts with liquid/solute in the liquid | SO₂ + NaOH → Na₂SO₃ + H₂O | | **Single-Stage Absorption** | One equilibrium contact | Simple spray towers | | **Multi-Stage (Counter-current)** | Multiple equilibrium stages in series | Packed towers, plate columns | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Sponge Bath for Polluted Air":** > Imagine your kitchen is filled with the strong smell of burnt toast (the *pollutant gas*). You open a window and let fresh air in, but a more effective method would be to hold a wet sponge (the *liquid solvent*) near the smoke. The smelly molecules in the air cling to the wet surface of the sponge and dissolve into it. After a while, the air is clean. > - The **smelly gas** = the pollutant (e.g., SO₂, H₂S). > - The **wet sponge** = the absorbing liquid (e.g., water, NaOH solution, amine solution). > - The **clean air** = the treated gas leaving the stack. > - The **dirty sponge water** = the spent solvent (which must be regenerated or treated). > > In engineering terms, we build tall columns where thousands of "sponge surfaces" (trays or packing) ensure thorough contact between the polluted gas rising upward and the scrubbing liquid flowing downward. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) - **Henry's Law Constant (H or m):** typically reported in **atm·m³/mol** or **Pa·m³/mol**. - **Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient (K_G or K_L):** units **mol/(m²·s·Pa)** or **kmol/(m²·h·atm)**. - **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** **T = 273.15 K**, **P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa**. - **Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**. - **Molar Volume at STP:** **22.414 m³/kmol**. - **Typical L/G ratio in scrubbers:** **0.5 to 5.0** (depending on solubility). --- > [!TIP] > **Why this topic matters for Pollution Control KTU exams:** Absorption is the *heart* of industrial flue-gas treatment (FGD — Flue Gas Desulfurization). Examiners frequently test Henry's Law calculations, two-film theory derivations, and Kremser equation-based design of packed towers. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" and "How" ### A. Driving Force for Absorption Mass transfer occurs whenever the **bulk gas partial pressure (p_A)** exceeds the **equilibrium partial pressure (p_A\*)** at the gas-liquid interface. - **Why?** Nature seeks equilibrium; molecules spontaneously migrate from high chemical potential to low. - **How?** Through molecular diffusion across a thin gas/liquid boundary (the *film*). ### B. Henry's Law — The Equilibrium Anchor For dilute gas absorption, the solute partial pressure in the gas is linearly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid: $$p_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x_A = \frac{p_A}{H}$$ A *low* Henry's constant **H** implies **high solubility** (good for absorption). ### C. The Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923) The gas-liquid interface is visualized as two stagnant films: 1. A **gas film** of thickness δ_G. 2. A **liquid film** of thickness δ_L. Solutes must diffuse through BOTH films in series. The film offering the **greater resistance** controls the overall rate. > [!NOTE] > **For highly soluble gases (NH₃, HCl):** gas-film resistance dominates. > **For sparingly soluble gases (CO₂, O₂):** liquid-film resistance dominates. > **Pollutant relevance:** SO₂ absorption falls in a transition regime — both films matter. ### D. Operating Line & Material Balance For a counter-current packed/plate absorber with: - **L** = molar flow rate of solute-free liquid (kmol/s) - **G** = molar flow rate of solute-free gas (kmol/s) - **Y** = mole ratio in gas (kmol solute / kmol solute-free gas) - **X** = mole ratio in liquid (kmol solute / kmol solute-free liquid) The **operating line** from overall material balance: $$G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X_1$$ Rearranged into the **classical y = mx + c form**: $$Y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)X + \left(Y_2 - \frac{L}{G}X_2\right)$$ where the slope **L/G** is the **absorbent-to-gas flow ratio**. ### E. Minimum L/G Ratio The minimum operating slope corresponds to the pinch point where the operating line touches the equilibrium curve at one end. Real design uses: $$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{actual} = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) \times \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min}$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical:** All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on absorption. | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units / Conditions | |---|--------------------|---------------|--------------------| | 1 | $p_A = H \cdot x_A$ | Henry's Law (equilibrium) | Pa or atm | | 2 | $Y_A = \dfrac{y_A}{1-y_A}$ | Mole ratio (gas) | kmol A / kmol B | | 3 | $X_A = \dfrac{x_A}{1-x_A}$ | Mole ratio (liquid) | kmol A / kmol B | | 4 | $y = mx + c$ with slope $L/G$ | Operating line equation | Slope dimensionless | | 5 | $N_A = k_G(p_A - p_{A,i})$ | Flux through gas film | mol/(m²·s) | | 6 | $N_A = k_L(c_{A,i} - c_A)$ | Flux through liquid film | mol/(m²·s) | | 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Overall resistance (gas basis) | m²·s·Pa/mol | | 8 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m \cdot k_G}$ | Overall resistance (liquid basis) | m³·s/mol | | 9 | $H_{OG} = \dfrac{G}{K_G \cdot a \cdot P}$ | Height of a gas transfer unit | m | | 10 | $H_{OL} = \dfrac{L}{K_L \cdot a \cdot \rho_L}$ | Height of a liquid transfer unit | m | | 11 | $N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \dfrac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$ | Number of gas transfer units | dimensionless | | 12 | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | Packed tower height | m | | 13 | $\dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1 - m X_2} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | Kremser (gas absorption) | A = L/(mG) | | 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency | % | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Never confuse **mole fraction (y)** with **mole ratio (Y)**. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you use them interchangeably in material balances. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD):** Limestone slurry scrubbers absorb SO₂ from coal-fired power plant emissions. - **Claus Process Tail-Gas Treatment:** Amine absorption of residual H₂S. - **Carbon Capture:** MEA/MDEA absorption of CO₂ from flue gas. - **Vapor Recovery:** Absorption of VOCs (benzene, toluene) from petrochemical vents. - **Acid Mist Control:** Water scrubbing of HCl fumes from waste incinerators. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating Line from Overall Material Balance **Statement:** Derive the operating-line equation for a counter-current gas absorber. **Assumptions:** - Steady-state, isothermal operation. - Negligible heat of absorption (or use of cooling coils). - Solute-free gas and liquid flow rates L and G are constant (dilute system). **Step-by-step derivation:** Let the column have a top (gas exit) and bottom (gas inlet). Number streams from BOTTOM (1) to TOP (2). For an arbitrary stage n: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(In by gas at stage n)} &= G \cdot Y_{n+1} \\ \text{(In by liquid at stage n)} &= L \cdot X_{n-1} \\ \text{(Out by gas at stage n)} &= G \cdot Y_n \\ \text{(Out by liquid at stage n)} &= L \cdot X_n \end{aligned} $$ **Total Material Balance over the entire column:** $$G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X_1$$ **Algebraic manipulation (rearrange to y = mx + c form):** $$ \begin{aligned} G \cdot Y_1 - G \cdot Y_2 &= L \cdot X_1 - L \cdot X_2 \\ G(Y_1 - Y_2) &= L(X_1 - X_2) \\ \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1 - X_2} &= \frac{L}{G} \end{aligned} $$ A material balance over the *upper* portion (from stage n to top) gives the operating line passing through (X_n, Y_{n+1}): $$G \cdot Y_{n+1} + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X_n$$ Rearranging for Y: $$ \begin{aligned} G \cdot Y_{n+1} - G \cdot Y_2 &= L \cdot X_n - L \cdot X_2 \\ Y_{n+1} &= \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X_n + \left(Y_2 - \frac{L}{G} X_2\right) \end{aligned} $$ This is a straight line with **slope = L/G** and **intercept = (Y₂ − (L/G)X₂)**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The operating line connects the gas inlet condition (X₂, Y₁) to the gas outlet condition (X₁, Y₂) on the X–Y diagram. The closer the operating line lies *above* the equilibrium curve, the larger the driving force and the more feasible the separation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient from Two-Film Theory **Step-by-step derivation:** At steady state, the flux through the gas film equals the flux through the liquid film (continuity): $$ N_A = k_G(p_A - p_{A,i}) = k_L(c_{A,i} - c_A) $$ From Henry's Law at the interface: $p_{A,i} = H \cdot x_{A,i}$ and $c_{A,i} = p_{A,i}/H \cdot \rho_L/M_B$ (or equivalently $c_{A,i} = (p_{A,i}/H)$ if H is in concentration units). Express the liquid concentration in terms of its gas-equilibrium partial pressure $p_A^* = H x_A$: $$ c_A = \frac{p_A^*}{H} \cdot \rho_L/M_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_{A,i} - c_A = \frac{(p_{A,i} - p_A^*)}{H} \cdot \rho_L/M_B $$ So: $$ N_A = \frac{k_L \rho_L}{H M_B}(p_{A,i} - p_A^*) $$ The total driving force across BOTH films is $(p_A - p_A^*)$. Adding the two resistances in series: $$ \begin{aligned} N_A &= \frac{p_A - p_A^*}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H M_B}{k_L \rho_L}} \\ &= K_G (p_A - p_A^*) \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{H M_B}{k_L \rho_L} $$ Or in slope form **m = H·M_B/(ρ_L·P)** for dilute systems, the standard textbook form becomes: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick:** If the gas is **highly soluble** → m is small → $1/K_G \approx 1/k_G$ → **gas-film controls**. > If the gas is **sparingly soluble** → m is large → $1/K_G \approx m/k_L$ → **liquid-film controls**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kremser Equation (Analytical Design Equation) For an N-stage counter-current absorber operating with constant L and G and linear equilibrium Y* = mX: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stage-by-stage balance:} \quad & L X_n + G Y_{n+1} = L X_{n-1} + G Y_n \\ \text{Rearranging:} \quad & Y_{n+1} - Y_n = \frac{L}{G}(X_n - X_{n-1}) \\ \text{Equilibrium at each stage:} \quad & X_n = Y_n^* / m = Y_n / m \quad \text{(if linear)} \\ \text{Define } A = L/(mG): \quad & Y_{n+1} - Y_n = A(Y_n - Y_{n-1}) \end{aligned} $$ This is a linear first-order difference equation whose solution after N stages yields the **Kremser equation for gas absorption**: $$ \frac{Y_{N+1} - Y_1}{Y_{N+1} - m X_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} $$ In standard textbook notation (Y₁ = gas inlet, Y₂ = gas outlet, X₂ = liquid inlet): $$ \boxed{\frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1 - m X_2} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}, \quad \text{where } A = \frac{L}{mG}} $$ **Special cases:** - When **A = 1** (L = mG), the equation reduces to a linear form: $\frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1 - mX_2} = \frac{N}{N+1}$. - For **large N**, the right-hand side → 1, meaning maximum achievable removal approaches the equilibrium limit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently asks:** *"Calculate the number of ideal stages required for 95% removal of SO₂ from a flue gas using water, given m = 30 and L/G = 100."* This is a textbook Kremser problem. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style) **Problem:** A flue gas containing 5 mol% SO₂ is scrubbed counter-currently with water in a packed absorber. The gas flow rate is G = 0.020 kmol/s (solute-free basis), and the water flow rate is L = 1.50 kmol/s (solute-free basis). The inlet water is solute-free (X₂ = 0). The gas exits with Y₂ = 0.0010. The equilibrium relation is Y* = 30X. Find (a) the exit liquid composition X₁, (b) the operating slope L/G, and (c) the SO₂ removal efficiency. **Solution:** **(a) Exit liquid composition X₁:** Use the overall material balance: $$ \begin{aligned} G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X_2 &= G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X_1 \\ Y_1 &= \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = 0.05263 \text{ (mole ratio)} \\ 0.020 \times 0.05263 + 1.50 \times 0 &= 0.020 \times 0.0010 + 1.50 \times X_1 \\ X_1 &= \frac{0.020(0.05263 - 0.0010)}{1.50} \\ X_1 &= \frac{0.020 \times 0.05163}{1.50} \\ X_1 &= \frac{0.001033}{1.50} \\ X_1 &= 6.88 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Operating slope:** $$ \frac{L}{G} = \frac{1.50}{0.020} = 75 $$ **(c) Removal efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1} \times 100\% = \frac{0.05263 - 0.0010}{0.05263} \times 100\% \approx 98.1\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check:** Equilibrium at exit liquid: Y₁* = 30 × 6.88×10⁻⁴ = 0.0206. Since Y₁ (= 0.0526) >> Y₁*, the gas is far from saturation — operation is feasible and the high removal efficiency is realistic. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Counter-Current Packed Absorption Tower — Block Functional Flow > [!NOTE] > **Note on diagrams:** A physical packed-column schematic involves fluid-flow geometry that is best represented as a labelled functional block diagram in Mermaid (compliant with V10 alphanumeric node rule). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hot Polluted Flue Gas
e.g., 5 percent SO2 + N2 + CO2] --> B[Inlet Gas Distributor

Removes Entrained Droplets] F --> G[Clean Treated Gas to Stack
SO2 less than 50 ppm] H[Fresh Scrubbing Liquid
e.g., Water or NaOH Solution] --> I[Liquid Distributor
at Tower Top] I --> J[Packed Section 3
Counter-current Contact] J --> K[Packed Section 2

Counter-current Contact] L --> M[Lean Solvent Collection Sump] M --> N[Solute-Rich Spent Solvent
to Regeneration or Waste Treatment] C --- J D --- K E --- L ``` ## 4.2 Two-Film Theory — Conceptual Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GasFilm[Gas Film Region] GAS[Gas Bulk
Partial Pressure pA] end subgraph Interface[Gas Liquid Interface] INT[Interface
Equilibrium: pAi = H times xAi] end subgraph LiquidFilm[Liquid Film Region] LIQ[Liquid Bulk
Concentration xA or cA] end GAS -- kG flux N_A --> INT INT -- kL flux N_A --> LIQ GAS --- GASNOTE[Highly soluble gas: kG small, gas film controls] LIQ --- LIQNOTE[Sparingly soluble gas: kL small, liquid film controls] ``` ## 4.3 X–Y Diagram — Operating Line vs Equilibrium Curve ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Plot X axis: Mole Ratio in Liquid] --> P2 P2[Plot Y axis: Mole Ratio in Gas] --> P3 P3[Draw Equilibrium Curve Y asterisk equals mX] --> P4 P4[Mark Inlet Gas Condition: X2, Y1] --> P5 P5[Mark Outlet Gas Condition: X1, Y2] --> P6 P6[Draw Straight Line Joining These Two Points
Operating Line slope equals L over G] --> P7 P7[Vertical Distance Y minus Y asterisk
equals Local Driving Force] --> P8 P8[Step Off Stages Between Operating Line
