# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define combustion and explain the difference between complete and incomplete combustion. How does incomplete combustion contribute to air pollution?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion** is a rapid exothermic oxidation reaction between a fuel and an oxidizer, releasing heat, light, and combustion products.
>
> **Complete Combustion:** When fuel reacts with sufficient oxygen, products are fully oxidized to CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂. The reaction is energy-efficient and clean. Example: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.
>
> **Incomplete Combustion:** Occurs when oxygen supply is insufficient, temperature is too low, or mixing is inadequate. Products include **CO**, **soot (C)**, **unburned hydrocarbons (UHC/VOC)**, and **PAHs**. These are toxic and contribute to smog, ground-level ozone formation, and respiratory illness. Industrial sources include poorly tuned boilers, vehicle engines, and improperly operated incinerators.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
What is excess air? Why is it required in industrial combustion? State the typical range for coal-fired and natural-gas systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Excess air is the air supplied beyond the stoichiometric (theoretical) requirement, expressed as:
> $$EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{theoretical}}{A_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$
>
> It is required because **perfect mixing is never achieved** in real burners, and incomplete combustion produces CO, soot, and unburned hydrocarbons. A controlled excess ensures complete burnout of all combustible species.
>
> Typical values:
> - **Natural gas burners: 5–10%**
> - **Coal-fired boilers: 15–25%**
> - **Solid waste incinerators: 50–100%**
>
> Trade-off: Excess air lowers flame temperature and increases NOₓ. Optimization is essential.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]**

A fuel oil has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 3%, O = 1%**, with no nitrogen or ash. The fuel is burned with **30% excess air**.

**Solve:**
**(a)** Calculate the **theoretical air required** per kg of fuel and the **actual air supplied**.
**(b)** Determine the **complete flue gas analysis** (both wet and dry basis) by volume percent.
**(c)** Calculate the **mass-based A/F ratio** and discuss why this parameter matters in pollution control.

#### Model Solution — Question A:

**Part (a) — Theoretical Air Calculation (7 Marks):**

Basis: **1 kg of fuel oil**.

**Step 1 — Convert mass to kmol:**

$$n_C = \frac{0.84}{12} = 0.0700\,\text{kmol}$$
$$n_H = \frac{0.12}{1} = 0.1200\,\text{kmol H}\,(= 0.0600\,\text{kmol H}_2)$$
$$n_S = \frac{0.03}{32} = 0.000938\,\text{kmol}$$
$$n_O = \frac{0.01}{16} = 0.000625\,\text{kmol O}\,(= 0.000313\,\text{kmol O}_2)$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement:**

$$n_{O_2,\,th} = n_C + \frac{n_H}{4} + n_S - \frac{n_O}{2}$$

$$n_{O_2,\,th} = 0.0700 + \frac{0.1200}{4} + 0.000938 - \frac{0.000625}{2}$$

$$n_{O_2,\,th} = 0.0700 + 0.0300 + 0.000938 - 0.000313 = 0.1006\,\text{kmol O}_2$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical air (moles):**

$$A_{th} = \frac{0.1006}{0.21} = 0.4790\,\text{kmol air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of theoretical air:**

$$m_{air,\,th} = 0.4790 \times 28.97 = \mathbf{13.88\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Actual air (30% excess):**

$$A_{act} = 1.30 \times 0.4790 = 0.6227\,\text{kmol air/kg fuel}$$

$$m_{air,\,act} = 1.30 \times 13.88 = \mathbf{18.05\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel}$$

---

**Part (b) — Flue Gas Analysis (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Combustion reaction (per kg fuel):**

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2:\,0.0700\,\text{kmol CO}_2$$
$$\text{H}_2 + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}:\,0.0600\,\text{kmol H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2:\,0.000938\,\text{kmol SO}_2$$

**Step 2 — O₂ supplied and used:**

O₂ supplied = 0.1006 × 1.30 = **0.1308 kmol**.
O₂ used = 0.1006 kmol.
Excess O₂ in flue gas = 0.1308 − 0.1006 = **0.0302 kmol**.

**Step 3 — N₂ from air:**

$$n_{N_2} = 0.1308 \times 3.76 = 0.4918\,\text{kmol N}_2$$

**Step 4 — Wet Flue Gas Composition:**

| Component | kmol | Volume % (Wet) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 0.0700 | 11.78% |
| SO₂ | 0.000938 | 0.16% |
| H₂O | 0.0600 | 10.10% |
| O₂ | 0.0302 | 5.09% |
| N₂ | 0.4918 | 82.87% |
| **Total Wet** | **0.5933** | **100%** |

**Step 5 — Dry Flue Gas Composition (water excluded):**

$$\text{Total Dry} = 0.0700 + 0.000938 + 0.0302 + 0.4918 = 0.5929\,\text{kmol}$$

| Component | kmol | Volume % (Dry) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 0.0700 | 11.81% |
| SO₂ | 0.000938 | 0.16% |
| O₂ | 0.0302 | 5.10% |
| N₂ | 0.4918 | 82.93% |
| **Total Dry** | **0.5929** | **100%** |

---

**Part (c) — Mass A/F Ratio and Pollution Control Significance (3 Marks):**

$$A/F = \frac{18.05}{1} = \mathbf{18.05\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel}$$

**Significance in Pollution Control:**
- The mass A/F ratio is a direct indicator of combustion stoichiometry. A higher A/F (more excess air) reduces **CO** and **soot** but increases **NOₓ** and lowers flame temperature.
- Operating too close to stoichiometric (low A/F) creates reducing conditions favorable to CO, VOC, and dioxin formation.
- Modern low-NOx burners maintain A/F near 1.15–1.20 (i.e., 15–20% EA) as an optimal balance between pollutant species.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Three T's of Combustion** (Temperature, Time, Turbulence) and discuss how each affects the formation of major air pollutants in process industries. Provide typical operating values for a hazardous waste incinerator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the operation of an **incinerator** for hazardous waste. Discuss the EPA's **Four Nines Standard (99.99% DRE)** and the role of combustion modification techniques in achieving it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B:

**Part (a) — The Three T's (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Definition | Effect on Pollutants | Typical Value for Hazardous Waste Incinerator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Mean combustion chamber temperature | T > 850 °C destroys most organics; T > 1000 °C for chlorinated wastes; T > 1300 °C increases NOₓ sharply | **980–1200 °C** |
| **Residence Time** | Time flue gas spends at combustion temperature | Longer time = more complete destruction; < 2 s may permit dioxin/CO escape | **≥ 2 seconds** |
| **Turbulence (Mixing)** | Reynolds number and intensity of mixing in the chamber | Better mixing = lower CO, UHC; poor mixing = fuel-rich pockets, soot | **Reynolds > 10,000**, swirl number > 1 |

**Pollutant-Three T's Relationships:**

1. **CO** is highly sensitive to all three T's. It is **kinetically controlled**; at low T and short residence time, the reaction 2CO + O₂ → 2CO₂ freezes, leaving CO. Improving mixing (Turbulence) is the most effective single intervention.
2. **NOₓ (thermal)** is exponentially sensitive to **Temperature**. Above 1300 °C, the Zeldovich mechanism becomes very fast. Lowering peak flame temperature (via FGR, OFA) is the key control.
3. **Unburned Hydrocarbons (VOC/UHC)** depend strongly on **Time** and **Turbulence**. Wall quenching and short-circuiting flow reduce residence time and produce UHC slip.
4. **Soot/PM** is controlled by **Turbulence** and the local equivalence ratio (φ). Fuel-rich pockets yield soot even when global A/F is correct.

**Typical Hazardous Waste Incinerator Operating Window (EPA):**
- T ≥ **1000 °C** (for chlorinated wastes with ≥ 1% Cl).
- Residence time ≥ **2 seconds**.
- Turbulence: Reynolds > 50,000, mixing achieved via swirl burners.
- Excess air: **50–200%** for waste variability.

---

**Part (b) — Incinerator Operation and Four Nines Standard (7 Marks):**

**Incinerator Schematic (Text Representation):**

```
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   WASTE FEED (Solid/Liquid/Sludge)     │
  └────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                   ▼
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │   PRIMARY CHAMBER    │  ◀── Auxiliary Burner
        │  (Rotary Kiln or     │      T = 1000–1200 °C
        │   Fluidized Bed)     │      Residence ≥ 2 s
        └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │ Hot flue gas
                   ▼
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  SECONDARY CHAMBER   │  ◀── Overfire Air
        │  (Afterburner)       │      Complete burnout
        │  T ≥ 1100 °C         │      of CO and UHC
        └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  HEAT RECOVERY       │
        │  BOILER              │
        └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │ Cooled gas
                   ▼
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  AIR POLLUTION       │
        │  CONTROL TRAIN       │
        │  (Quench → ESP →     │
        │   Scrubber → SCR)    │
        └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
                 STACK
```

**EPA Four Nines Standard (DRE = 99.99%):**

For principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs), the EPA mandates:

$$DRE = \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100\% \geq 99.99\%$$

This translates to a **maximum 0.01% (100 ppm) of the POHC** allowed to escape the stack, measured continuously.

**Role of Combustion Modification (COM):**

| Technique | Mechanism | Typical NOₓ Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Excess Air (LEA)** | Reduces O₂ available for thermal NOₓ | 20–30% |
| **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)** | Recycles cool flue gas to lower flame T | 50–70% |
| **Overfire Air (OFA)** | Stages combustion; fuel-rich zone reduces peak T | 30–50% |
| **Low-NOx Burners (LNB)** | Aerodynamic flame shaping and staging | 40–60% |
| **Burners Out of Service (BOOS)** | Reduces number of active burners; redirects fuel | 20–40% |
| **Water/Steam Injection** | Lowers flame temperature directly | 30–50% |

**Integration with APCD (post-combustion controls):**

| Device | Target Pollutant | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) | Particulate matter | 99.5–99.9% |
| Baghouse (Fabric Filter) | PM, heavy metals | 99.9% |
| Wet Limestone Scrubber (FGD) | SO₂, HCl, HF | 90–98% |
| Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) | NOₓ | 70–90% |
| Activated Carbon Injection | Dioxins, mercury, VOCs | 90–99% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert H mass to H₂ moles** in stoichiometry — this is the #1 error in fuel-oil and coal combustion problems. Always remember 2H + ½O₂ → H₂O, so 1 kg of H needs 8 kg of O₂, not 16 kg.
> 2. **Wet vs. Dry Flue Gas confusion** — the Orsat analyzer gives dry-basis values. For wet-basis calculations (e.g., dew point of acid gases), you must include H₂O. KTU board often tests this distinction.
> 3. **N₂/O₂ ratio** — always use **3.76** (not 3.73 or 3.79) for the molar N₂-to-O₂ ratio in dry air.
> 4. **Excess air sign convention** — excess air is always positive. A negative "excess air" indicates **deficient air** or fuel-rich operation, not a "negative excess."
> 5. **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** — students often forget to account for **dissociation above 1700 K**, leading to overestimation of AFT. For board exams, use constant $C_p$ for simplicity unless specified.
> 6. **DRE calculation units** — ensure both $W_{in}$ and $W_{out}$ are in the same units (typically kg/h of the POHC). Mixed units lead to zero marks.
> 7. **NOₓ vs. NO** — flue-gas analyzers often measure NO, but regulations are quoted as **NO₂ equivalent**. A conversion factor of **1.53** (ratio of mol. wts. 46/30) is required for reporting.
> 8. **Sutton's Equation limitation** — applies only to ultimate analysis on a dry-ash-free basis. Apply moisture and ash corrections before using.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Combustion**

- ✅ **Combustion Definition:** Rapid exothermic oxidation; produces heat + light + flue gas.
- ✅ **General Combustion Reaction:** CₓHᵧOᵤSᵥ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + SO₂ (complete).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric O₂ formula:** $n_{O_2} = x + y/4 + v - z/2$ (kmol O₂/kmol fuel).
- ✅ **Theoretical Air** = $n_{O_2}/0.21$; uses **N₂/O₂ = 3.76** molar ratio in air.
- ✅ **Excess Air (EA)** is essential to ensure complete combustion in real burners.
- ✅ **Three T's:** Temperature, Time, Turbulence — the master variables of combustion quality.
- ✅ **HHV vs LHV:** HHV includes latent heat of water condensation; LHV does not.
- ✅ **Sutton's Equation:** Empirical HHV from ultimate analysis (kJ/kg).
- ✅ **DRE ≥ 99.99%** — EPA "Four Nines" standard for hazardous waste incineration.
- ✅ **Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich):** Dominant above **1300 °C**; controls = LEA, FGR, LNB, OFA.
- ✅ **Fuel NOₓ:** From fuel-bound nitrogen; control = fuel switching, staged combustion.
- ✅ **CO** indicates incomplete combustion; sensitive to all Three T's.
- ✅ **SO₂** controlled via flue gas desulfurization (wet FGD with limestone slurry).
- ✅ **Dioxins/Furans** form via **de novo synthesis** in ESP at **200–400 °C**; control by rapid flue-gas quenching.
- ✅ **Adiabatic Flame Temperature:** Theoretical max temperature; iterative energy balance solution.
- ✅ **Orsat Analysis:** Dry-basis flue-gas composition; does not include water.
- ✅ **Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio φ:** φ < 1 (lean), φ = 1 (stoichiometric), φ > 1 (rich).
- ✅ **Mass A/F Ratio** for typical fuel oil ≈ 18–20 kg/kg; natural gas ≈ 17–20 kg/kg.
- ✅ **Typical Combustion Efficiency** = [CO₂]/([CO₂] + [CO]) × 100%; > 99% for well-tuned systems.
- ✅ **Incinerator Operating Window:** T ≥ 1000 °C, t ≥ 2 s, EA = 50–200%, Reynolds > 10⁴.
- ✅ **APCD Hierarchy:** Combustion modification → primary measures; ESP/baghouse → secondary; SCR/FGD → tertiary.
- ✅ **Autoignition temperature** of fuel must be exceeded for sustained combustion.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Mass Air Formula** (kg/kg): $34.56(C/12) + 8(H/1) + 4.32(S/32) - O/16$ — memorize for board problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automobile Emission Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automobile Emission Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automobile Emission Control** refers to the systematic engineering of internal combustion engines (SI and CI) and their after-treatment systems to minimize the release of regulated toxic pollutants — **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**, **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **Sulphur Oxides (SOx)**, and **Lead Compounds** — into the atmosphere, in compliance with statutory emission standards such as **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)**, **Euro 6**, and **EPA Tier 3** norms.

These emissions originate from three principal pathways in a vehicle:

1. **Tailpipe exhaust** (≈ 60–80% of total emissions)
2. **Crankcase blow-by gases** (from the engine block)
3. **Evaporative emissions** (from the fuel tank and carburettor)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Lung Test" of a Vehicle**
> Imagine a human smoker. With every exhale, the smoker releases **CO (a poison)**, **tar (particulates)**, and **toxins (HC & NOx)** into the room. Over time, this pollutes the air and harms others (passive smoking). An automobile is identical to this smoker — it **inhales clean air + fuel** and **exhales a toxic cocktail**. Automobile emission control is essentially the engineering equivalent of placing a **high-efficiency medical filter, oxygen mask, and detox unit** on the smoker's mouth, ensuring the exhaled air is clean enough to breathe. The catalytic converter acts as the "detox liver," the EGR valve as the "rebreathing lung," and the DPF as the "mucus filter" for soot.

### 1.3 Classification of Mobile Source Pollutants

| Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Primary Source | Health Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion of fuel | Binds haemoglobin → hypoxia |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Wall quenching, crevice volumes | Carcinogenic (Benzene, PAH) |
| Nitrogen Oxides | NO, NO₂ | High-T combustion (>1800 K) | Smog, acid rain, respiratory |
| Particulate Matter (PM2.5/PM10) | C, soot | Incomplete fuel vaporization (Diesel) | Lung cancer, cardiovascular |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur in diesel fuel | Acid rain, bronchitis |
| Lead Compounds | PbClBr | Leaded petrol additives | Neurological damage |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Complete combustion | Greenhouse gas (GHG) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** CO, HC, and NOx are the three **regulated tailpipe pollutants** under BS-VI norms. Particulate Number (PN) limit of **6 × 10¹¹ per km** is now enforced for diesel cars.

### 1.4 Statutory Emission Standards (India — BS-VI)

| Standard | CO (g/km) | HC (g/km) | NOx (g/km) | PM (g/km) | Effective Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BS-IV | 1.00 | 0.10 | 0.08 | 0.025 | 2017 |
| BS-VI | 0.50 | 0.05 | 0.035 | 0.0045 | 2020 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition from BS-IV to BS-VI is **a near-70% reduction** across all pollutants. KTU examiners frequently test these numerical limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Pollutant Formation — A Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Carbon Monoxide (CO) Formation**
- **Why:** Inadequate oxygen supply → fuel cannot fully oxidize to CO₂.
- **How:** Governed by the **equilibrium reaction**: CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ (Water-Gas Shift).
- **Engineering lever:** Leaner air-fuel mixtures and improved flame propagation reduce CO.

**Step 2: Hydrocarbon (HC) Emissions**
- **Why:** Flame cannot reach every fuel molecule.
- **How:** Four mechanisms → (a) Wall Quenching (cold cylinder walls extinguish flame), (b) Crevice Volumes (gap between piston/head), (c) Oil Layer Absorption, (d) Fuel Trapping in deposits.

**Step 3: NOx Formation (The Thermal Mechanism — Zeldovich)**
- **Why:** At temperatures **above 1800 K**, atmospheric N₂ and O₂ react to form NO.
- **How:** Three reactions (Extended Zeldovich):
  - O + N₂ → NO + N
  - N + O₂ → NO + O
  - N + OH → NO + H
- **Engineering lever:** Lower peak flame temperature using **EGR, retarded timing, water injection**.

**Step 4: Particulate Matter (PM) in Diesel**
- **Why:** Heterogeneous combustion in fuel-rich zones creates soot nuclei.
- **How:** Local equivalence ratio (φ) > 2 → pyrolysis of fuel → solid carbon particles.

### 2.2 Engine Modification Techniques (In-Cylinder Control)

| Technology | Function | Target Pollutant |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** | Atomizes fuel uniformly | CO, HC |
| **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** | Optimizes spark & injection timing | All |
| **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** | Re-circulates 5–15% exhaust back to intake | NOx |
| **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** | Optimizes volumetric efficiency | HC, CO |
| **Turbocharging + Intercooling** | Increases air density → lean burn | PM, CO |

### 2.3 After-Treatment Control Devices (Post-Combustion)

**1. Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) — Used in Petrol/Gasoline SI Engines**
- Contains a **ceramic honeycomb** substrate (cordierite, 2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂) washcoated with **Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), and Rhodium (Rh)**.
- Performs three simultaneous reactions:
  - **Oxidation:** CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ ; HC + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O
  - **Reduction:** NO + CO → ½N₂ + CO₂ ; NO + HC → ½N₂ + CO₂ + H₂O
- **Critical operating window:** Air-Fuel ratio must remain within ±1% of stoichiometric (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01).

**2. Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)**
- Oxidizes HC and CO using Pt/Pd.
- Cannot reduce NOx (insufficient reductant in lean exhaust).

**3. Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)**
- Wall-flow ceramic filter with **alternating plugged channels** → forces exhaust through porous walls → soot is trapped.
- Regeneration occurs at **~ 600 °C** by burning accumulated soot.
- **Filtration efficiency > 99%** for PM2.5.

**4. Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)**
- Injects **AdBlue (32.5% aqueous urea solution)** into exhaust.
- Reaction: (NH₂)₂CO + H₂O → 2NH₃ + CO₂, then 4NH₃ + 4NO + O₂ → 4N₂ + 6H₂O.
- Reduces NOx by **80–95%**.

**5. Lean NOx Trap (LNT)**
- Adsorbs NOx as nitrates during lean operation.
- Releases and reduces NOx during short rich-burn purge cycle.

**6. Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)**
- Recirculates blow-by gases back to the intake manifold.
- Eliminates **crankcase emissions** (eliminated by 1984 US standards).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline) | $(A/F)_{sto} = 14.7$ | Mass of air per kg fuel | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel) | $(A/F)_{sto} = 14.5$ | Mass of air per kg fuel | kg air / kg fuel |
| Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (A/F)_{sto} / (A/F)_{act}$ | φ < 1: lean; φ > 1: rich | dimensionless |
| Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = (A/F)_{act} / (A/F)_{sto}$ | λ = 1: stoichiometric | dimensionless |
| Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = (C_{in} - C_{out}) / C_{in} \times 100$ | Catalyst effectiveness | % |
| Space Velocity | $SV = Q / V_{cat}$ | Volumetric flow per catalyst volume | h⁻¹ |
| NOx Formation Rate (Zeldovich) | $d[NO]/dt = k \cdot e^{-E/RT} \cdot [O][N_2]$ | Temperature-dependent | mol/m³·s |
| Mass Emission Rate | $\dot{m}_{poll} = V_{exh} \times C_{poll}$ | Emission per unit time | g/s |
| Emission per Distance | $E_{dist} = m_{poll} / D$ | g/km for chassis dyno | g/km |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Trick:** When the question says "rich mixture," the value of **λ < 1** and **φ > 1**. When "lean mixture," **λ > 1** and **φ < 1**. A common 2-mark board question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEM design** — Engine calibration engineers use these formulas to map ECU injection tables.
- **Emission certification labs** — ARAI (India), USEPA, and EU JRC use chassis dynamometer cycles (MVEG, WLTP, WHTC) to certify compliance.
- **Urban air quality management** — CPCB's **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** targets a 40% PM reduction in 131 Indian cities by 2026.
- **Tunnel ventilation design** — Vehicle emission rates govern CO/NO₂ set-points for jet fans.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Iso-Octane (C₈H₁₈)

The complete combustion reaction for iso-octane is:

$$C_8 H_{18} + a \cdot (O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow b \cdot CO_2 + c \cdot H_2 O + d \cdot N_2$$

**Step 1: Carbon balance**
- Left side: 8 C atoms; Right side: $b$ C atoms
- Therefore: $b = 8$

**Step 2: Hydrogen balance**
- Left side: 18 H atoms; Right side: $2c$ H atoms (in H₂O)
- Therefore: $c = 9$

**Step 3: Oxygen balance**
- Left side: $2a$ O atoms; Right side: $2b + c = 16 + 9 = 25$ O atoms
- Therefore: $2a = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5$

**Step 4: Mass calculation**

Molecular weights:
- $C_8 H_{18} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 114$ kg/kmol
- $O_2 = 32$ kg/kmol; $N_2 = 28$ kg/kmol; Air = 29 kg/kmol
- $CO_2 = 44$ kg/kmol; $H_2 O = 18$ kg/kmol

Mass of fuel: $m_f = 114$ kg
Mass of air required: $m_a = 12.5 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)$

$$m_a = 12.5 \times (32 + 105.28) = 12.5 \times 137.28 = 1716 \text{ kg air}$$

**Step 5: Stoichiometric AFR**

$$(A/F)_{sto} = \frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05$$

Experimental value used in engineering practice: **14.7 : 1** (accounts for incomplete dissociation at high T).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1: Equivalence Ratio Calculation

**Problem:** A petrol engine operates with an air mass flow of 0.0735 kg/s and fuel flow of 0.005 kg/s. Determine (a) Actual AFR, (b) Equivalence Ratio φ, and (c) Lambda λ. State whether the mixture is rich, lean, or stoichiometric.

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.0735$ kg/s
- $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.005$ kg/s
- $(A/F)_{sto} = 14.7$ for gasoline

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Actual Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$(A/F)_{act} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} = \frac{0.0735}{0.005} = 14.7$$

**Part (b): Equivalence Ratio**

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{sto}}{(A/F)_{act}} = \frac{14.7}{14.7} = 1.00$$

**Part (c): Lambda**

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{act}}{(A/F)_{sto}} = \frac{14.7}{14.7} = 1.00$$

**Conclusion:** Since φ = λ = 1.00, the engine is operating at **stoichiometric conditions** — the ideal operating point for a Three-Way Catalytic Converter.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2: Catalytic Converter Efficiency

**Problem:** A three-way catalytic converter is tested on a chassis dynamometer. The inlet concentration of CO is 8000 ppm and the outlet concentration is 80 ppm. Calculate the conversion efficiency.

**Given:**
- $C_{in} = 8000$ ppm
- $C_{out} = 80$ ppm

**Solution:**

$$\eta_{CO} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{CO} = \frac{8000 - 80}{8000} \times 100 = \frac{7920}{8000} \times 100 = 99.0\%$$

The catalyst is performing at the upper regulatory benchmark (BS-VI mandates >95% conversion efficiency for new vehicles after 160,000 km).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem #3: Emission per km Computation

**Problem:** A car travels 100 km in the New European Driving Cycle (NEDC). The cumulative mass of CO emitted is 25 g. Calculate emission in g/km and check if it complies with BS-VI (CO limit = 0.5 g/km).

**Given:**
- Distance $D = 100$ km
- $m_{CO} = 25$ g

**Solution:**

$$E_{dist} = \frac{m_{poll}}{D} = \frac{25 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ km}} = 0.25 \text{ g/km}$$

Since $0.25 \text{ g/km} < 0.5 \text{ g/km}$, the vehicle **complies with BS-VI norms** with a 50% safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Three-Way Catalytic Converter." List the three pollutants it targets and the active metals used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** is an after-treatment device used in petrol (SI) engines that simultaneously oxidises CO and HC while reducing NOx to N₂. It uses **Platinum (Pt)** and **Palladium (Pd)** as oxidation catalysts and **Rhodium (Rh)** as the reduction catalyst, supported on a **ceramic (cordierite) honeycomb substrate** washcoated with γ-Al₂O₃. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is EGR? Why is it used in automobile engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** is the technique of routing a controlled fraction (typically 5–15%) of the exhaust gas back to the engine's intake manifold. Since exhaust gas is inert (mainly CO₂ and H₂O), it dilutes the intake charge, lowers the peak flame temperature **below the 1800 K NOx formation threshold**, and thus significantly reduces thermal NOx emissions. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — Full ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the major pollutant emissions from a Spark Ignition (SI) petrol engine. Describe the formation mechanism of CO, HC, and NOx. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) for emission control in petrol vehicles. State its key operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pollutants from a SI Engine — 7 marks**

**1. Carbon Monoxide (CO):**
- Formed due to **incomplete combustion** of hydrocarbons when local air-fuel ratio is rich (φ > 1).
- Equation: $C_x H_y + (x/2 + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x CO + (y/2) H_2 O$ (under fuel-rich conditions)
- Increases during idling, acceleration, and cold-start when mixture is rich.
- CO concentration in exhaust can range from **0.5% (warm engine) to 13% (cold start)**.

**2. Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC):**
- Caused by **flame quenching** at the cold cylinder walls (Wall Quenching Effect), fuel trapped in **crevice volumes** (head gasket, spark plug threads), and **oil layer absorption** on cylinder walls.
- HC emissions are highest during cold start and deceleration fuel cut-off.
- A typical regulated limit is **0.05 g/km** (BS-VI).

**3. Nitrogen Oxides (NOx):**
- Formed by the **Extended Zeldovich Mechanism** at peak combustion temperatures **> 1800 K**.
- Key reaction: $N_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 NO$
- Maximum formation in the **post-flame zone** where temperature is highest.
- Reduced by **EGR, retarded ignition timing, and lean-burn strategies**.

**(b) Three-Way Catalytic Converter — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram)

**Working Principle:**
- The TWC consists of a **ceramic monolith honeycomb** (cordierite, 400 cells/in²) coated with a porous washcoat of γ-Al₂O₃, impregnated with **Pt + Pd (oxidation)** and **Rh (reduction)**.
- **Three simultaneous reactions:**
  1. CO oxidation: $2 CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2 CO_2$
  2. HC oxidation: $C_x H_y + (x + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2 O$
  3. NO reduction: $2 NO + 2 CO \rightarrow N_2 + 2 CO_2$ ; $2 NO \rightarrow N_2 + O_2$

**Key Operating Conditions:**
- Operates only when **light-off temperature (≥ 250–300 °C)** is reached.
- Requires **stoichiometric A/F ratio (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01)** for all three reactions to occur efficiently.
- Closed-loop control via **upstream and downstream Lambda (O₂) sensors** with ECU.
- Conversion efficiency: **> 95%** for CO, HC, and NOx at steady state.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU Model – 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** and **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** system used in BS-VI diesel vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data was obtained from a chassis dynamometer test of a BS-VI petrol car:
- Distance covered: 200 km
- CO emitted: 60 g, HC emitted: 8 g, NOx emitted: 5 g
Calculate the emissions in g/km and verify compliance with BS-VI norms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DPF and SCR — 7 marks**

**DPF Working (Block Diagram from Section 4.3):**
- The DPF is a **wall-flow ceramic (SiC) filter** with alternating channels plugged at opposite ends.
- Exhaust enters the open channels, is forced through **porous walls**, and soot particles are **trapped on the wall surface**.
- Filtration efficiency > 99% for PM2.5.
- **Regeneration:** When soot loading reaches ~ 8 g/L, ECU triggers regeneration by injecting extra fuel to raise exhaust temperature to **600 °C**, oxidizing trapped soot into CO₂.
- Two types: **Passive regeneration** (continuous, uses NO₂ from upstream DOC) and **Active regeneration** (forced, uses diesel post-injection).

**SCR Working:**
- Aqueous urea solution (**AdBlue, 32.5% by mass**) is injected into the exhaust stream ahead of the SCR catalyst.
- Reactions:
  - Thermal decomposition: $(NH_2)_2 CO \rightarrow NH_3 + HNCO$
  - Hydrolysis: $HNCO + H_2 O \rightarrow NH_3 + CO_2$
  - SCR reaction: $4 NH_3 + 4 NO + O_2 \rightarrow 4 N_2 + 6 H_2 O$
  - Fast SCR: $2 NH_3 + NO + NO_2 \rightarrow 2 N_2 + 3 H_2 O$
- NOx conversion efficiency: **80–95%**.
- An **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** placed downstream prevents NH₃ release to atmosphere.

**(b) Emission per km Verification — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $D = 200$ km
- $m_{CO} = 60$ g, $m_{HC} = 8$ g, $m_{NOx} = 5$ g

**Calculations:**

$$E_{CO} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 \text{ g/km}$$

$$E_{HC} = \frac{8}{200} = 0.040 \text{ g/km}$$

$$E_{NOx} = \frac{5}{200} = 0.025 \text{ g/km}$$

**Compliance Check (BS-VI Limits):**

| Pollutant | Calculated (g/km) | BS-VI Limit (g/km) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 0.30 | 0.50 | ✅ Pass |
| HC | 0.040 | 0.05 | ✅ Pass |
| NOx | 0.025 | 0.035 | ✅ Pass |

**Conclusion:** The vehicle **complies with BS-VI emission norms** for all three regulated pollutants, with comfortable safety margins of 40%, 20%, and 28% respectively. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to write the full chemical equation** of combustion or catalytic reaction → 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Confusing equivalence ratio (φ) and lambda (λ)** — they are reciprocals. Markers deduct 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning the light-off temperature** (~ 250 °C) for TWC → a 14-mark answer feels "incomplete" without it.
> 4. **Skipping the lambda sensor role** in TWC operation → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up BS-IV and BS-VI numerical limits** in calculation problems — KTU papers specifically test BS-VI limits.
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram** in (b) parts → mandatory 1–2 mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **three regulated tailpipe pollutants** are **CO, HC, NOx** (BS-VI norms).
- ✅ **PM (Particulate Number) limit of 6 × 10¹¹ / km** is a new BS-VI addition for diesel.
- ✅ **TWC requires λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** — that is the *only* regime where all three reactions occur.
- ✅ **Diesel engines cannot use TWC** because they run lean (excess O₂).
- ✅ **EGR reduces NOx**, not CO or HC. Common 1-mark conceptual trap.
- ✅ **SCR uses AdBlue (32.5% urea)** and reduces NOx by 80–95%.
- ✅ **DPF filtration efficiency > 99%**; regenerates at ~ 600 °C.
- ✅ **Crankcase emissions** are eliminated by **PCV valve** — earliest emission control.
- ✅ **Evaporative emissions** controlled by **activated carbon canister**.
- ✅ **Euro 6 ≡ BS-VI** in India (adopted 2020).
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio φ = (A/F)_sto / (A/F)_act**; **λ = 1/φ**.
- ✅ **Catalyst light-off temperature ≈ 250–300 °C**; efficiency > 95% above this.
- ✅ **WLTP (Worldwide harmonized Light vehicles Test Procedure)** replaced NEDC in 2017 for Euro 6.
- ✅ **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics)** mandatory in all BS-VI vehicles for emission monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Odour Pollution Management - Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Odour Pollution Management - Sources # MODULE 3: ODOUR POLLUTION MANAGEMENT — SOURCES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Odour Pollution** is defined as the presence of one or more volatile chemical compounds (odorants) in the ambient air at concentrations high enough to be detected by the human olfactory system, thereby causing annoyance, discomfort, reduced quality of life, physiological stress, or triggering community complaints. It is a non-conventional, sensory-based air pollutant where the "harm" is defined by human perception rather than by mass concentration alone. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme OECHT613 syllabus (Module 3), odour pollution is treated as a **secondary air pollutant** arising primarily from biochemical and industrial process operations, and is governed by two key parameters: - **Odorant Concentration (cᵢ)** — expressed in **ou/m³** (Olfactometry Units per cubic meter) or ppb/ppm by volume. - **Olfactory Perception Threshold (Pₜ)** — the minimum concentration at which 50% of an odour panel can detect the substance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Odour is the *only* air pollutant regulated primarily by its **sensory impact on humans**, not by toxicity or mass emission. This unique characteristic drives the entire management discipline. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing near a busy fish market in the early morning. The smell hits you within seconds — not because of any "toxic" gas, but because your nose detected **volatile amines and sulfur compounds** (like trimethylamine, H₂S). The smell itself is the pollution. Now consider this: the same concentration that makes one person wrinkle their nose might be completely imperceptible to another (e.g., a perfume factory worker). This **subjectivity** is what makes odour management fundamentally different from chemical pollutant control. Think of odour like **noise pollution** — you cannot "see" it, you cannot always "measure" it with a single chemical probe, but it absolutely affects well-being. Odour control engineers are essentially **chefs masking bad smells** or **plumbers fixing a sewage leak** — they work at the intersection of chemistry, psychology, and process engineering. ### 1.3 Why Odour is a "Tricky" Pollutant | Property | Conventional Pollutant (e.g., SO₂) | Odour Pollutant | |---|---|---| | Detection | Instrumental (continuous monitors) | Human nose (olfactometry) | | Regulatory Limit | Mass concentration (µg/m³) | Dilution-to-threshold (ou/m³) | | Health Impact | Toxicological dose-response | Annoyance, nausea, stress | | Synergism | Additive | Hyper-additive (mixture effect) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics (must memorize):** > - **1 ou/m³ =** the odorant concentration that produces a sensory response equivalent to **1 European Reference Odour Mass (EROM)** evaporated in 1 m³ of neutral gas. > - **Olfactometer** — the standard instrument (EN 13725 compliant) used for quantifying odour. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Odour Sources in Process Industries Odour sources are classified across **three hierarchical axes** in the KTU 2024 framework: ### Axis A: By Origin (Industrial Sector) 1. **Petroleum Refineries & Petrochemicals** - Sulfides: H₂S, CH₃SH (methyl mercaptan), DMS, DMDS - Hydrocarbons: benzene, toluene, xylene (BTEX) fractions - Source units: hydrodesulfurizers, crude storage tanks, API separators 2. **Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTPs)** - Inlet works (lifting stations), primary clarifiers, sludge thickening, dewatering - Primary odorants: H₂S, NH₃, indoles, skatoles, volatile fatty acids (VFAs) 3. **Food & Agro-Processing Industries** - Slaughterhouses, fish processing, tanneries, breweries, dairy, coffee roasting - Primary odorants: putrescine, cadaverine, trimethylamine, aldehydes 4. **Pulp & Paper Mills** - Kraft process — TRS (Total Reduced Sulfur): H₂S + CH₃SH + DMS + DMDS 5. **Pharmaceutical & Fermentation Industries** - Solvent vapors (MIBK, acetone, ethanol), fermentation by-products 6. **Rendering Plants & Solid Waste Processing** - Composting, MSW transfer stations, landfills - Odorants: ammonia, VFAs, biogenic amines 7. **Agricultural Operations** - Animal husbandry (piggeries, poultry farms), manure storage - Key odorants: NH₃, H₂S, p-cresol, skatole ### Axis B: By Chemical Family | Chemical Family | Representative Compounds | Odour Character | Typical Threshold (ppb) | |---|---|---|---| | **Reduced Sulfur Compounds (RSC)** | H₂S, CH₃SH, DMS, DMDS, COS | Rotten egg, cabbage, garlic | 0.5 – 100 | | **Amines** | NH₃, MMA, TMA, ethylamine | Pungent, fishy | 5 – 50 | | **Aldehydes** | Acetaldehyde, formaldehyde, butyraldehyde | Pungent, fruity | 20 – 200 | | **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** | Acetic, propionic, butyric, valeric | Sour, rancid, sweat | 10 – 1000 | | **Aromatics** | Indole, skatole, pyridine | Fecal, nauseating | 0.001 – 1 | | **Terpenes & Ketones** | MIBK, pinene, carvone | Mouldy, musty | 0.1 – 50 | | **Hydrocarbons** | BTEX, styrene | Solvent-like, sweet | 50 – 5000 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Threshold Rule (KTU High-Yield):** *Detection threshold* (50% panel detection) is always lower than *Recognition threshold* (50% panel can name the smell). Recognition is typically **3×–10× the detection value**. ### Axis C: By Generation Mechanism - **Biological / Biogenic** — anaerobic decomposition (H₂S, NH₃, VFAs, indoles) - **Chemical** — process reactions (sulfidic spent caustic, oxidation off-gases) - **Thermal** — incomplete combustion, rendering cookers - **Storage/Volatilization** — fugitive emissions from tanks, sumps, drains ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_{od} = \dfrac{V_{od}}{V_{n}} \times 10^{6}$ | Odour concentration (from dilution olfactometry) | ou/m³ | $V_n$ is neutral gas volume | | 2 | $Z_{50} = \dfrac{1}{P_{50}}$ | Dilution factor at 50% detection threshold | dimensionless | $P_{50}$ = panel response proportion | | 3 | $OU = C_{od} \times Q$ | Odour emission rate (strength) | ou/s | $Q$ = volumetric flow, m³/s | | 4 | $C_{od}^{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{c_i}{P_{t,i}}$ | Mixture odour concentration (simplified) | ou/m³ | Assumes additive perception | | 5 | $ERI = \dfrac{OU}{A_{site}}$ | Emission rate intensity per unit area | ou/s·m² | Used for area-source mapping | | 6 | $H = f(c, t, F)$ | Hedonic tone (pleasantness) | scale $-10$ to $+10$ | Negative = malodour | | 7 | $\log \left(\dfrac{c_t}{c_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$ | First-order decay of odorant | — | $k$ = decay constant (1/s) | | 8 | $D_{50\%} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot K \cdot x}{u}}$ | Gaussian plume centreline dilution (≤10 km) | — | Pasquill-Gifford class | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical bar $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value should NEVER be typed as `|x|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In a typical **WTP/WWTP** engineering consultancy project, an odour engineer uses these formulae to: 1. Calculate the **OU emission rate** from a sludge thickener (Equation 3). 2. Predict **ground-level concentration** at the nearest receptor using Equation 8 (Gaussian dispersion). 3. Compare predicted concentration (in ou/m³) against the local regulatory limit (commonly **5 ou/m³ at the property line** in Indian/EU standards). 4. Size the **odour treatment unit** (biofilter, activated carbon, wet scrubber) accordingly. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Olfactometer Dilution to Odour Concentration The cornerstone of odour quantification is the **triangular forced-choice olfactometer** (per EN 13725). Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation of odour concentration from raw dilution panel data. ### Step 1: Dilution Setup An air sample of odorous gas is mixed with **neutral, odor-free air** at a known volumetric ratio. If the original odorant volume is $V_{od}$ and the neutral diluent volume is $V_n$, the **dilution factor** $Z$ is: $$ Z = \dfrac{V_n}{V_{od}} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each successive dilution halves the perceived intensity contribution from that odorant in an additive manner. ### Step 2: Panel Response at Each Dilution Step A panel of $n$ assessors (typically $n \geq 8$ in EN 13725) is presented with three ports — two containing neutral gas, one containing the diluted sample (randomized). Each assessor responds with a binary outcome (Yes/No). The proportion of positive responses at dilution $Z_k$ is: $$ P_k = \dfrac{Y_k}{n} $$ where $Y_k$ is the number of correct identifications at the $k^{th}$ dilution step. ### Step 3: Logistic Curve Fit (Probit / Logit Model) The dilution–response relation follows a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve. Using the logit model: $$ P(Z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\left[-a \cdot \ln(Z) - b\right]} $$ where $a$ is the slope and $b$ is the intercept, both fitted by Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). ### Step 4: Solve for the 50% Detection Threshold Setting $P = 0.5$: $$ 0.5 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\left[-a \cdot \ln(Z_{50}) - b\right]} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + \exp\left[-a \cdot \ln(Z_{50}) - b\right] &= 2 \\ \exp\left[-a \cdot \ln(Z_{50}) - b\right] &= 1 \\ -a \cdot \ln(Z_{50}) - b &= 0 \\ \ln(Z_{50}) &= -\dfrac{b}{a} \\ Z_{50} &= \exp\left(-\dfrac{b}{a}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** At exactly half the panel detecting, the logit expression in the denominator becomes zero, giving us the inflection point of the sigmoid — this is the standard "detection threshold" by definition. ### Step 5: Convert Threshold to Odour Concentration By the EN 13725 convention, the **odour concentration** equals the dilution factor at the 50% threshold: $$ C_{od} = Z_{50} \quad \text{[dimensionless]} $$ Then, converted to the standard SI-referenced unit: $$ C_{od} \left[\dfrac{\text{ou}}{m^3}\right] = Z_{50} \times \dfrac{1 \, \text{EROM}}{1 \, m^3} $$ where **1 EROM** = 1 European Reference Odour Mass = the mass of odorant that a reference panel (n=44) detects at the standard threshold in 1 m³. ### Step 6: Derivation of Emission Rate (OU) — Engineering Use For a process stream with volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) and measured concentration $C_{od}$ (ou/m³): $$ \begin{aligned} OU \; \left[\dfrac{\text{ou}}{s}\right] &= C_{od} \times Q \\ \text{Example: } OU &= 5000 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{m^3} \times 0.25 \; \dfrac{m^3}{s} \\ OU &= 1250 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{s} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source–Pollutant–Control Mapping | Industrial Source | Primary Odorant | Generation Mechanism | Typical Concentration (ou/m³) | First-Choice Control | |---|---|---|---|---| | Crude oil storage tank | H₂S, RSC | Standing/breathing losses | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | Floating roof + vapour recovery | | WWTP inlet well | H₂S, indole | Anaerobic septicity | $10^4$ – $10^5$ | Chemical scrubbing (NaOH/NaOCl) | | Sludge dewatering (centrifuge) | NH₃, VFAs | Mechanical shear + biological release | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | Enclosed ventilation + biofilter | | Kraft pulp digester | TRS | Kraft cooking liquor | $10^4$ – $10^5$ | Dedicated TRS incinerator | | Rendering cooker | Amines, VFAs | Thermal hydrolysis | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | Afterburner + 2-stage scrubber | | Pig farm finishing barn | NH₃, H₂S, p-cresol | Manure decomposition | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | Frequent flushing + biofilter pit | | Coffee roaster | Aldehydes, pyridines | Maillard reaction | $10^4$ – $10^5$ | Thermal oxidizer + RTO | | Landfill (active face) | H₂S, VFA, NH₃ | Anaerobic waste decomposition | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | Daily cover + landfill gas extraction | > [!NOTE] > **Concentration Note:** The wide range $10^3$ – $10^6$ ou/m³ reflects measurement variability based on olfactometer, panel training, and source activity. Always use site-specific sampling. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Odour Source Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Industrial Process Source] --> B[Source Identification] B --> C{Odour Generation Pathway} C -->|Biological| D1[Anaerobic Digestion] C -->|Chemical| D2[Process Reactions] C -->|Thermal| D3[Combustion / Rendering] C -->|Volatilization| D4[Storage / Fugitive] D1 --> E1[H2S, NH3, VFAs, Indoles] D2 --> E2[RSC, Mercaptans, Solvents] D3 --> E3[Aldehydes, Pyridines, PAHs] D4 --> E4[BTEX, VOCs, Hydrocarbons] E1 --> F[Olfactometric Measurement] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Concentration in ou per m3] G --> H[Emission Rate Calculation OU] H --> I[Atmospheric Dispersion Modelling] I --> J{Receptor Impact?} J -->|Exceeds Limit| K[Odour Treatment Train] J -->|Within Limit| L[Periodic Monitoring] K --> K1[Biofilter] K --> K2[Activated Carbon Adsorber] K --> K3[Wet Chemical Scrubber] K --> K4[Thermal Oxidizer RTO] K1 --> M[Compliance with KTU / CPCB Norms] K2 --> M K3 --> M K4 --> M L --> N[Annual Reassessment] M --> N ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Odour Sources ```mermaid graph LR ROOT[All Odour Sources] ROOT --> PET[Petroleum Sector] ROOT --> WAT[Wastewater Sector] ROOT --> FOOD[Food and Agro Sector] ROOT --> PULP[Pulp and Paper Sector] ROOT --> PHARMA[Pharmaceutical Sector] ROOT --> AGRI[Agricultural Sector] PET --> PET1[Refinery CDU] PET --> PET2[Sour Water Stripper] PET --> PET3[Tank Farm] WAT --> WAT1[Inlet Works] WAT --> WAT2[Primary Clarifier] WAT --> WAT3[Sludge Thickener] WAT --> WAT4[Bioreactor Off Gas] FOOD --> FOOD1[Slaughterhouse] FOOD --> FOOD2[Fish Processing] FOOD --> FOOD3[Dairy Effluent] PULP --> PULP1[Kraft Digester] PULP --> PULP2[Recovery Boiler] PHARMA --> PHARMA1[Fermenter Off Gas] PHARMA --> PHARMA2[Solvent Recovery] AGRI --> AGRI1[Pig Farm] AGRI --> AGRI2[Poultry Shed] AGRI --> AGRI3[Manure Lagoon] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Wastewater Treatment Plant — Typical Odour Source Hotspots ```mermaid flowchart TB INF[Influent Sewer
Lift Station] -->|Hotspot 1: H2S| SCR1[Inlet Scrubber] SCR1 --> SCREEN[Screening and Grit Removal] SCREEN -->|Hotspot 2: VFA| PC[Primary Clarifier] PC -->|Hotspot 3: Sludge Gas| SD[Sludge Thickener and Digester] SD -->|Hotspot 4: NH3, Mercaptans| DEW[Dewatering Centrifuge] PC --> BIO[Aeration Tank] BIO --> SC[Secondary Clarifier] SC --> DIS[Disinfected Effluent Discharge] SD --> BIOGAS[Biogas Holder
H2S Removal] DEW --> BF[Enclosed Biofilter
Master Treatment Unit] SCR1 -.-> BF SD -.-> BF DEW -.-> BF BF --> STACK[Odour-Free Exhaust Stack
Compliance Verified] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The dotted arrows show **odour-laden air streams** being captured at the source (a key KTU 2024 design principle — *"capture-at-source" before dispersion*) and routed to a centralised biofilter. This avoids diluting the problem by treating ambient air. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Odour Pollution. List any four major industrial sources of odour pollution. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** Odour pollution refers to the discharge of one or more volatile compounds into the atmosphere in concentrations sufficient to produce an unpleasant or objectionable olfactory response in a significant fraction of the exposed population. Four major industrial sources: 1. Petroleum refineries and petrochemical complexes (H₂S, mercaptans). 2. Wastewater treatment plants (H₂S, indoles, VFAs). 3. Food processing industries — fish, slaughterhouse, dairy (amines, fatty acids). 4. Pulp and paper mills using the Kraft process (Total Reduced Sulfur — TRS). ### Question 2: Differentiate between *Detection Threshold* and *Recognition Threshold* with respect to odour measurement. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** - **Detection Threshold (DT):** The lowest concentration of an odorant at which **50% of an odour panel** can perceive *some* difference between the sample and neutral air, but cannot necessarily identify the smell. DT is the basis of the standard olfactometric concentration measurement. - **Recognition Threshold (RT):** The concentration at which **50% of the panel** can both detect and *correctly describe/identify* the nature of the smell. - **Numerical Relationship:** Recognition threshold is typically **3 to 10 times higher** in concentration than the detection threshold. This ratio is odorant-specific and is critical in setting regulatory standards. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice A]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **various sources of odour pollution** in process industries, classifying them by industrial sector and chemical family. Highlight the role of **biological decomposition** in generating odorants. [7 Marks] **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **forced-choice olfactometer** and explain how the **50% detection threshold (Z₅₀)** is determined from panel data. [7 Marks] #### Model Solution to (a): **Classification by Industrial Sector:** 1. **Petroleum Refineries:** Crude storage, hydrodesulfurization, sour water stripping → release H₂S, CH₃SH, DMS, DMDS, BTEX. 2. **Wastewater Treatment Plants:** Inlet works, primary clarifiers, sludge handling → produce H₂S, NH₃, indoles, skatoles, volatile fatty acids (VFAs). 3. **Food Processing:** Slaughterhouses, fish markets, dairies → biogenic amines (putrescine, cadaverine), trimethylamine, organic acids. 4. **Pulp & Paper (Kraft):** Digesters, evaporators → Total Reduced Sulfur (TRS) emissions. 5. **Pharmaceutical/Fermentation:** Solvent vents, fermenters → MIBK, ethanol, esters. 6. **Rendering Plants:** Cookers, dryers → amines, fatty acids, aldehydes. 7. **Agricultural:** Piggeries, poultry, manure lagoons → NH₃, H₂S, p-cresol, skatole. **Classification by Chemical Family:** | Family | Examples | Character | |---|---|---| | Reduced Sulfur Compounds | H₂S, CH₃SH | Rotten egg | | Amines | NH₃, TMA | Fishy, pungent | | Aldehydes | Acetaldehyde | Pungent | | VFAs | Acetic, butyric | Sour, rancid | | Aromatics | Indole, skatole | Fecal | **Role of Biological Decomposition:** Under **anaerobic conditions** (absence of O₂), sulfur-containing proteins in waste are reduced by *Desulfovibrio* and *Desulfobulbus* bacteria, generating H₂S. Simultaneously, nitrogenous matter yields NH₃ and amines, while carbohydrates produce VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric). This explains why **WWTPs and landfills** dominate the odour source list. #### Model Solution to (b): **Working Principle of the Forced-Choice Olfactometer (per EN 13725):** - A known volume of odorous air $V_{od}$ is injected into a stream of **odor-free neutral air** $V_n$ drawn through activated carbon filters. - The dilution ratio $Z = V_n / V_{od}$ is varied across a **logarithmic series** (e.g., 2:1 steps). - The diluted mixture is presented to an assessor at **three sniffing ports** — two with neutral gas, one with the sample — randomized in position. This is the "**forced choice**" — the assessor MUST select one port. **Determining the 50% Detection Threshold (Z₅₀):** For each dilution step $Z_k$, the proportion $P_k = Y_k / n$ of correct panel responses is calculated (where $n \geq 8$ assessors). A logistic (logit) function is fitted: $$ P(Z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\left[-a \cdot \ln(Z) - b\right]} $$ Setting $P = 0.5$ and solving: $$ Z_{50} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{b}{a}\right) $$ By definition: $$ C_{od} = Z_{50} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{ou}}{m^3}\right] $$ **Neat Sketch:** (Refer to the olfactometer schematic in Section 4 of these notes — three-port sniffing panel connected to dilution manifold fed by carbon-filtered neutral air and metered sample injection.) --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Retest, Module 3, Choice B]** **(a)** What are the **chemical families of odorants** commonly encountered in process industries? Give two representative compounds and their characteristic odours for each family. [7 Marks] **(b)** Calculate the **odour emission rate (OU)** from a sludge thickening tank having a measured odour concentration of **8500 ou/m³** and an off-gas flow rate of **0.45 m³/s**. If this tank is replaced with an **enclosed, ventilated centrifuge** having 4× higher concentration but only **60% of the volumetric flow**, what is the new emission rate? Comment on the engineering implications. [7 Marks] #### Model Solution to (a): | # | Chemical Family | Representative Compound 1 (Odour) | Representative Compound 2 (Odour) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reduced Sulfur Compounds (RSC) | H₂S (rotten egg) | CH₃SH — methyl mercaptan (cabbage / garlic) | | 2 | Amines | NH₃ (sharp, pungent) | Trimethylamine — TMA (fishy) | | 3 | Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) | Acetic acid (vinegar) | Butyric acid (rancid butter) | | 4 | Aldehydes & Ketones | Acetaldehyde (pungent, fruity) | MIBK (musty, mouldy) | | 5 | Nitrogen Heterocyclics | Indole (fecal at high conc.) | Skatole (fecal, nauseating) | | 6 | Aromatic Hydrocarbons | Benzene (sweet, solvent) | Styrene (sweet, plastic) | | 7 | Terpenes | α-Pinene (pine, turpentine) | Limonene (citrus) | #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1: Emission rate from existing sludge thickening tank** $$ \begin{aligned} OU_{old} &= C_{od}^{old} \times Q_{old} \\ OU_{old} &= 8500 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{m^3} \times 0.45 \; \dfrac{m^3}{s} \\ OU_{old} &= 3825 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Emission rate from new enclosed centrifuge** New concentration: $C_{od}^{new} = 4 \times 8500 = 34000 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{m^3}$ New flow rate: $Q_{new} = 0.60 \times 0.45 = 0.27 \; \dfrac{m^3}{s}$ $$ \begin{aligned} OU_{new} &= 34000 \times 0.27 \\ OU_{new} &= 9180 \; \dfrac{\text{ou}}{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Comparison and Engineering Comment** $$ \text{Ratio} = \dfrac{OU_{new}}{OU_{old}} = \dfrac{9180}{3825} \approx 2.40 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:** > - Students often report the *enclosed* system as "better" simply because flow rate is reduced. **This is wrong** — although the volume of contaminated air is smaller (cheaper to duct), the **concentration rises much faster (4×)** than the flow drops (40%), giving a **net 2.4× higher emission rate**. This is a classic KTU trap question. > - **Engineering implication:** The enclosed centrifuge MUST be connected to a dedicated **odour treatment unit (e.g., biofilter with ≥95% removal efficiency)** to bring the *net emitted* OU down to acceptable levels. Otherwise, you have concentrated the pollution rather than eliminated it. **Conclusion:** Enclosure alone is insufficient; it must be paired with high-efficiency treatment. The increased treatment cost is justified by the lower ducting and ventilation cost. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 – Sources of Odour Pollution):** - **Definition:** Odour pollution is sensory pollution regulated by human perception, not toxicity. - **Standard Units:** **ou/m³** (odour units per cubic metre), **OU/s** (odour emission rate), **Z₅₀** (dilution factor at 50% detection). - **Seven Major Industrial Source Categories:** Petroleum, WWTP, Food, Pulp & Paper, Pharma, Rendering, Agriculture. - **Dominant Chemical Families (memorize thresholds!):** RSC → Amines → VFAs → Aldehydes → Heterocyclics → Aromatics → Terpenes. - **Biological Decomposition** is the *single largest source-generation pathway* in WWTPs and landfills — anaerobic bacteria convert S, N, and C into H₂S, NH₃, indoles, and VFAs. - **Detection Threshold vs Recognition Threshold:** DT < RT; typically RT ≈ 3–10 × DT. - **EN 13725 Olfactometer:** Triangular forced-choice, logit curve fit, $Z_{50} = \exp(-b/a)$. - **Emission Rate Formula:** $OU = C_{od} \times Q$ (multiply concentration by flow rate). - **KTU Pitfall — Enclosure Trap:** Concentrating the source (enclosure) does NOT reduce emission rate unless paired with treatment; often emission rate INCREASES. - **Hedonic Tone (H):** Ranges from $-10$ (extremely unpleasant) to $+10$ (pleasant); malodours have negative H values. - **First-Choice Treatment Mapping:** Biological → Biofilter; VOC → Carbon; Acid gases → Wet scrubber; High-temp → Thermal oxidizer (RTO/RCO). - **Regulatory Benchmark (India/EU):** Typically **5 ou/m³ at the property line** for sensitive receptors (residential zones). ---

topics.title: Health Impacts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Health Impacts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Health Impact** in the context of pollution control in process industries refers to the adverse biological, physiological, biochemical, or psychological effects on human beings (and ecosystems) caused by exposure to physical, chemical, or biological agents released during industrial operations. According to the World Health Organization (WHO) framework, a *health impact* is the measurable alteration in health status of an individual or population that can be directly or indirectly attributed to an environmental stressor arising from a process industry.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, health impacts are classified along three principal axes:

1. **Nature of Effect** — Acute (short-term, high-dose) vs. Chronic (long-term, low-dose) vs. Sub-chronic (intermediate).
2. **Route of Exposure** — Inhalation, Ingestion (oral), Dermal (skin) contact, and Parenteral (through wounds).
3. **Target System** — Respiratory, Cardiovascular, Nervous, Hepatic, Renal, Reproductive, and Immune systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A health impact is *not* merely the presence of a pollutant — it is the **dose × exposure** interaction between a stressor and a biological receptor. Pollutants may be present in the environment but only become "impacts" when they reach a living system in a biologically active concentration.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Toxicity** | Inherent property of a substance to cause harm |
| **Hazard** | Probability that a substance will cause harm under specific conditions |
| **Risk** | Quantitative combination of hazard and exposure (Risk = Hazard × Exposure) |
| **Dose** | Amount of substance that enters the body (mg/kg body weight) |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose for 50% of the test population (mg/kg) |
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration for 50% of the test population (ppm or mg/m³) |
| **TWA** | Time-Weighted Average exposure over 8 hours (OSHA standard) |
| **STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit (15-minute average) |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA regulatory standard) |
| **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value (ACGIH recommended standard) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toxic Load" Bucket

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Filled Bucket:**
> Imagine your body is a **bucket** with a small drain at the bottom. Every pollutant you breathe, eat, or absorb is a **drop of dirty water** poured into this bucket. Your liver and kidneys (the drain) work continuously to flush it out. Health impacts appear when:
> - The **bucket overflows suddenly** (acute poisoning — e.g., chlorine gas leak) → immediate damage.
> - The **drops never stop coming** (chronic exposure — e.g., living near a refinery) → the bucket stays full, the drain gets clogged, and the dirty water corrodes the bucket walls (organ damage, cancer, neurological decline).
> 
> The *Health Impact* is the visible damage — corroded walls (lungs), clogged drain (kidney failure), or contaminated water (blood poisoning).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

- **Body weight reference adult:** **70 kg**
- **Inhalation reference volume:** **20 m³/day** (adult)
- **Drinking water intake:** **2 L/day**
- **Standard LD₅₀ test species:** **Rat (Rattus norvegicus)**
- **Conversion:** **1 ppm (gas) = 24.45 × MW / 298 × mg/m³** at 25°C, 1 atm
- **Carcinogen classification:** IARC Group 1, 2A, 2B, 3, 4

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exposure → Dose → Response Continuum

The complete chain of a health impact in a process industry follows the **Exposure-Dose-Response Paradigm**:

1. **Source** — Process plant releases pollutant (e.g., sulfuric acid plant emits SO₂).
2. **Pathway/Medium** — Pollutant moves through air, water, soil, or food chain.
3. **Receptor** — Human (worker or community) at the other end.
4. **Exposure** — Contact between receptor and pollutant (concentration × time).
5. **Dose** — Amount that actually enters the body through the route of exposure.
6. **Response** — Biological effect (cough, asthma, cancer, death).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Any of the six links can be broken to prevent the health impact — this is the foundation of *pollution control strategy*. Contain the source (emission control), block the pathway (buffer zones), or protect the receptor (PPE, ventilation).

## 2.2 Routes of Exposure — Detailed Mechanism

### A. Inhalation (Most Common in Process Industries)
- Pollutants enter via the **alveoli** of the lungs.
- **Gases and vapours** (SO₂, NO₂, CO, VOCs) → diffuse directly into blood.
- **Particulates** (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) → deposit in different regions based on aerodynamic diameter:
  - **> 10 μm** → trapped in nasal hair and upper airways
  - **2.5–10 μm** → deposit in bronchi
  - **< 2.5 μm** → reach alveoli (most dangerous)

### B. Ingestion (Oral)
- Through contaminated water/food.
- Major route for **heavy metals** (Pb, Hg, Cd, As) and **persistent organic pollutants** (POPs).
- The **bioconcentration factor (BCF)** determines accumulation up the food chain.

### C. Dermal Contact
- Liquid splashes, vapour absorption through skin.
- Critical for **organic solvents** (benzene, toluene), **phenols**, **acids/alkalis**.

### D. Parenteral
- Through cuts/wounds.
- Occupational hazard in **pharmaceutical**, **biotech**, and **chemical** industries.

## 2.3 Classification of Health Effects

| Type | Onset | Duration | Example |
|------|-------|----------|---------|
| **Acute** | Minutes to hours | Reversible | SO₂ → bronchospasm; H₂S → death at 700 ppm |
| **Sub-chronic** | Days to months | Partially reversible | Solvent exposure → liver enzyme elevation |
| **Chronic** | Months to years | Often irreversible | Asbestos → mesothelioma; Benzene → leukemia |
| **Carcinogenic** | 10–40 yr latency | Permanent | PAHs, benzene, vinyl chloride, asbestos |
| **Mutagenic** | Generational | Heritable | Ionizing radiation, certain heavy metals |
| **Teratogenic** | In utero | Permanent | Mercury → Minamata disease; Thalidomide |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU BOARD EXAM**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dose (mg/kg)} &= \frac{C \times IR \times ET}{BW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Added Lifetime Cancer Risk (ALCR)} &= \text{CDI} \times \text{CSF}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CDI (mg/kg·day)} &= \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT \times 365}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hazard Quotient (HQ)} &= \frac{\text{ADD}}{\text{RfD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ppm to mg/m}^3 &: \quad C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mortality Rate (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Deaths from pollutant}}{\text{Total exposed}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Variable Definitions

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|------|---------------|
| $C$ | Pollutant concentration | mg/L or mg/m³ | — |
| $IR$ | Intake rate | L/day or m³/day | 2 (water), 20 (air) |
| $ET$ | Exposure time | hours/day | 8 (worker), 24 (resident) |
| $EF$ | Exposure frequency | days/year | 250 (worker), 350 (resident) |
| $ED$ | Exposure duration | years | 25 (worker), 30 (resident) |
| $BW$ | Body weight | kg | 70 (adult), 15 (child) |
| $AT$ | Averaging time | days | ED × 365 (non-cancer), 25550 (cancer) |
| $CSF$ | Cancer Slope Factor | (mg/kg·day)⁻¹ | Benzene = 0.015 |
| $RfD$ | Reference Dose | mg/kg·day | Pb = 0.0035 (interim) |
| $ADD$ | Average Daily Dose | mg/kg·day | — |
| $MW$ | Molecular weight | g/mol | — |
| $CDI$ | Chronic Daily Intake | mg/kg·day | — |

### KTU Rule of Thumb (For Numerical Problems)

> **If HQ < 1** → No significant non-cancer risk expected.
> **If HQ ≥ 1** → Non-cancer risk is of concern; intervention required.
> **If ALCR > 1 × 10⁻⁴** → Cancer risk is unacceptable (USEPA standard: 1 × 10⁻⁶ to 1 × 10⁻⁴).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Health impact quantification is the **backbone of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports submitted to the Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB). Every new process plant in Kerala — be it a **rubber processing unit in Kottayam**, a **tile factory in Thrissur**, or a **petrochemical complex in Kochi** — must submit an EIA that quantifies the *health impact* on the surrounding population using the very equations above. Without this, the plant cannot receive consent to operate under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Air Act, 1981**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ppm to mg/m³ Conversion

The conversion between **parts per million by volume (ppm_v)** and **mass concentration (mg/m³)** is one of the most frequently asked numerical problems in KTU board exams.

### Starting Point — Ideal Gas Law

At standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm), 1 mole of an ideal gas occupies **24.45 L**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ mole of gas} &= 24.45 \text{ L (at 25°C, 1 atm)} \\
\text{Volume occupied by 1 ppm_v} &= 1 \text{ part in } 10^6 \text{ parts} \\
&= 10^{-6} \times 24.45 \text{ L/mol} \times \text{mole fraction basis}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Express 1 ppm_v as a Volume Fraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ ppm}_v = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6} \text{ (dimensionless mole fraction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Mass of Pollutant in 1 m³ of Air

If we have $C_{ppm}$ ppm of a pollutant with molecular weight $MW$ g/mol in 1 m³ (= 1000 L) of air:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of air in 1 m}^3 &= \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{24.45 \text{ L/mol}} = 40.90 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Moles of pollutant} &= C_{ppm} \times 10^{-6} \times 40.90 \\
\text{Mass of pollutant} &= \text{Moles} \times MW \\
&= C_{ppm} \times 10^{-6} \times 40.90 \times MW \text{ g} \\
&= C_{ppm} \times 10^{-6} \times 40.90 \times MW \times 1000 \text{ mg} \\
C_{mg/m^3} &= C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Working Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Mnemonic:** *"Mass Per Mole"* — Multiply ppm by MW, divide by 24.45.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Daily Dose Calculation (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A worker in a fertilizer plant drinks 2 L/day of water contaminated with **lead (Pb)**. The Pb concentration is **0.05 mg/L**. The worker weighs 70 kg and is exposed 250 days/year for 25 years. Calculate the **Chronic Daily Intake (CDI)**.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 0.05 \text{ mg/L} \\
IR &= 2 \text{ L/day} \\
EF &= 250 \text{ days/year} \\
ED &= 25 \text{ years} \\
BW &= 70 \text{ kg} \\
AT &= 25 \times 365 = 9125 \text{ days (non-cancer averaging)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply CDI Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
CDI &= \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT \times 365} \\
CDI &= \frac{0.05 \times 2 \times 250 \times 25}{70 \times 9125 \times 1} \text{ mg/kg·day} \\
CDI &= \frac{625}{638750} \text{ mg/kg·day} \\
CDI &= 9.785 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mg/kg·day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Hazard Quotient

The RfD (Reference Dose) for lead (interim) is **0.0035 mg/kg·day** (USEPA, oral pathway):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HQ &= \frac{CDI}{RfD} = \frac{9.785 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
HQ &= 0.2796
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
HQ &= 0.28 < 1 \Rightarrow \text{No significant non-cancer health risk, but in the precautionary range.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mark Allocation Tip:** Always show units in *every* line. Marks are deducted in steps where units are missing.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Inhalation Cancer Risk (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A community near a petroleum refinery is exposed to **benzene** at a concentration of **0.005 mg/m³** continuously. Calculate the **Added Lifetime Cancer Risk (ALCR)**.

### Step 1: Compute CDI (Inhalation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CDI_{inh} &= \frac{C \times IR \times ET \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT \times 365} \\
C &= 0.005 \text{ mg/m}^3 \\
IR &= 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
ET &= 24 \text{ hr/day (continuous)} \\
EF &= 350 \text{ days/year} \\
ED &= 30 \text{ years} \\
BW &= 70 \text{ kg} \\
AT &= 70 \text{ years} \times 365 = 25550 \text{ days (cancer averaging)} \\
CDI_{inh} &= \frac{0.005 \times 20 \times 24/24 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 25550} \\
CDI_{inh} &= \frac{0.005 \times 20 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 25550} \\
CDI_{inh} &= \frac{105}{1788500} \\
CDI_{inh} &= 5.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mg/kg·day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Cancer Slope Factor

For benzene, **CSF = 0.015 (mg/kg·day)⁻¹**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALCR &= CDI_{inh} \times CSF \\
ALCR &= 5.87 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.015 \\
ALCR &= 8.81 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALCR &= 8.81 \times 10^{-7} < 10^{-6} \Rightarrow \text{Negligible cancer risk (acceptable).}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Process Industry Pollutants vs. Health Effects

| Pollutant | Source Industry | Acute Effect | Chronic Effect | IARC Class |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|----------------|------------|
| **SO₂** | Sulfuric acid, smelters | Bronchospasm | COPD, aggravated asthma | 3 |
| **NO₂** | Nitric acid, combustion | Pulmonary edema | Emphysema | 2A |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion | Headache, death at >1000 ppm | Cardiovascular damage | — |
| **PM₂.₅** | Boilers, kilns | Eye irritation | Lung cancer, CVD | 1 |
| **Pb** | Battery, smelting | Colic, encephalopathy | Neurotoxicity (children) | 2A |
| **Hg** | Chlor-alkali, coal | Tremor, gingivitis | Minamata disease | 3 |
| **Cd** | Zinc smelting | Pulmonary irritation | Itai-itai disease | 1 |
| **Benzene** | Petrochemical | CNS depression | Leukemia | 1 |
| **Asbestos** | Insulation, brakes | Asbestosis | Mesothelioma | 1 |
| **H₂S** | Refineries, tanneries | Knock-down at >700 ppm | Olfactory fatigue, neuritis | — |
| **CS₂** | Viscose rayon | Narcosis | Atherosclerosis | — |
| **Vinyl Chloride** | PVC plants | Dizziness | Hepatic angiosarcoma | 1 |

## 3.5 LD₅₀ Interpretation — Step-by-Step

Toxicity rating based on oral LD₅₀ in rats (USEPA classification):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Super-toxic} &: LD_{50} \leq 5 \text{ mg/kg} \quad \text{(e.g., dioxin, botulinum)} \\
\text{Extremely toxic} &: 5 < LD_{50} \leq 50 \text{ mg/kg} \quad \text{(e.g., nicotine)} \\
\text{Very toxic} &: 50 < LD_{50} \leq 500 \text{ mg/kg} \quad \text{(e.g., phenol)} \\
\text{Moderately toxic} &: 500 < LD_{50} \leq 5000 \text{ mg/kg} \quad \text{(e.g., aspirin)} \\
\text{Slightly toxic} &: 5000 < LD_{50} \leq 15000 \text{ mg/kg} \\
\text{Practically non-toxic} &: LD_{50} > 15000 \text{ mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Example:** A pesticide has oral LD₅₀ = 250 mg/kg in rats. Toxicity = "Very toxic." For a 70 kg human, the **estimated lethal dose** would scale allometrically (× 7 rat-to-human factor, but for exam, use direct scaling):
> 
> $$LD_{human} = \frac{250 \times 70}{1000} = 17.5 \text{ g}$$

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete cause-effect chain** of a health impact from a process industry, including intervention points (pollution control strategies).
